# Igros Kodesh

## Volume 18

## 6105

B"H, Erev Rosh Hashanah, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the branches of N’shei U’Bnos Chabad
In every place
May Hashem grant them life

Blessings and peace!

Standing on the threshold of the new year, which approaches for us and for all of Israel for good and for blessing, I wish you all, collectively and each of you individually, a *shanah tovah u’mesukah* (a good and sweet year).

This naturally includes success in the work of your branch, both in quantity (an increase in the number of members) and in quality (a deepening of the work).

And in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*, of goodness and holiness, there is—no matter how good it may be—always room to add more and more.

***

Since the foundation of this work is the concept of *ahavas Yisrael*, as is discussed extensively in many places in the literature of Chassidus, and specifically in the *sichos kodesh* of our Rebbes, I bring here an excerpt from a *sicha* of the Rebbe Rayatz—a story that was told in its time by the Tzemach Tzedek, whose *yom huledet* (birthday) is today, Erev Rosh Hashanah—*zechuso yagen aleinu v’al kol Yisrael*.

***

The grandfather, the Tzemach Tzedek, heard from his grandfather—our great Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe—who heard it in Mezritch, a story from the Baal Shem Tov:

Before his *hisgalus* (revelation), and also in the first years after his *hisgalus*, the Baal Shem Tov would travel through Jewish towns. He would gather the simple Jews—men, women, and children—around him in the middle of the marketplace and tell them stories. These stories were primarily *aggados* of our Sages. He would elaborate and explain the story, or he would connect a story to a concept mentioned in the *aggados* of our Sages, so that the concept would be fixed and absorbed by the listeners.

Once, at such a gathering, the Baal Shem Tov spoke about the concept of *ahavas Yisrael*, how great it must be among Jews—knowing the love of the Ribono shel Olam for Jews—and he explained it with an example:

In that town, there was a Jew named R' Yaakov. He was fluent in the entire *Shas*, *Gemara*, *Rashi*, and *Tosafos* by heart, and he would study by heart—in those times, this was not a rarity in Jewish towns. When one studies by heart, the mind must be more connected to the study than when one studies from a text. Once, R' Yaakov was studying—as mentioned, by heart—a difficult and lengthy *Tosafos*. A small child came over to him and said some clever remark to him. R' Yaakov was so deeply impressed by this wisdom that he interrupted his study. This is what a child can accomplish.

So too, *kivayachol*, Hashem—the Baal Shem Tov continues—is occupied, as our Sages say, "The first three hours [of the day] He sits and occupies Himself with Torah," etc. But when a Jew davens and comes with a request to the Ribono shel Olam, He interrupts and occupies Himself with the *tefillos* and requests of Jews.

When Hashem told the angels that He wished to create man, the angels asked what he would look like. Upon hearing the answer, they said, "What is man that You remember him?"—why is such a human being necessary?

But when a Jew wakes up in the morning, runs to daven with a *minyan* at *vasikin*, and then is busy and burdened the entire day, yet he leaves all his business and runs to daven *Mincha* in shul, and between *Mincha* and *Maariv* he hears a lesson in *Ein Yaakov* and davens *Maariv*, and brings home the content of the *Ein Yaakov* and recounts it to his family—at that moment, Hashem gathers the angels and boasts of the human being He has created, saying to them:

"You angels do not have a millstone around your neck (a yoke), no wife and children, no burdens, no taxes and levies. This human being has a millstone around his neck—and the millstone is one I commanded him to take upon himself; according to Torah, he must provide for his wife and children—and he is busy and burdened, and has taxes and levies, the *ol hagalus* (yoke of exile), and yet, look at how he conducts himself."

When one reflects and imagines this with every good thing that a Jew does—the Ribono shel Olam boasts of it—it will accomplish a great deal.

***

As has been explained several times, the words of the *Nesi’im* and the stories one hears from them are a directive and a lesson for every one of the listeners. Simultaneously, they are also a *nesinas koach* (empowerment), a strengthening, and a blessing, so that one may be able to realize this. It depends only on the listener to fulfill it according to the intent.

May Hashem grant that, as mentioned, one should act in this, and it should be with success in all respects.

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* to everyone, together with your families—*shlita*.

## 6406

B"H, Erev Rosh Hashanah, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Dr. Yosef Goldschmidt
Director of the Department of Religious Education
Jerusalem, Holy City, may it be rebuilt and established

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 22 Elul, which has just arrived.

I begin by expressing my gratitude for your interest and assistance in the past, and even more so—which is the main point—for your promise of assistance in the future. This surely refers not only to the distant future but also to the near future; that is, in these coming months at the start of the new school year. Although someone of your stature surely does not require expressions of gratitude, I nonetheless offer my warm thanks, as it is fitting according to the teaching of our Sages: "One only urges those who are already diligent." Gratitude serves to encourage further diligence in good works, and it is my hope that this will be the result in your case as well—even regarding points that require an approach of *lifnim mishuras hadin* (beyond the letter of the law), and even more so, *lifnim lifnim mishuras hadin*.

Regarding the main point you raise in your letter concerning the necessity for teachers to possess a minimum of knowledge and educational and pedagogical skill, it is surely superfluous to explain that I agree with your opinion. A proven demonstration of this, which you also mention in your letter, is that in the Lubavitcher Yeshivos here, this perspective and requirement are realized in actual practice. However, surely for a person who has been in Eretz Yisrael for several years and has seen how all matters were created and brought into being almost *me'ayin l'yesh* (from nothingness into existence), it is understood that for a long time they were forced to forgo several essential and most important matters, if only to begin vital activities and continue them. Even now, ten years after 5708, a significant portion of the most essential matters is still in such a state. Yet, there is no regret over the situation, because it is more essential and important that the activities continue, even in their current state, with their shortcomings and deficiencies. Therefore, I am confident that this approach and perspective will also be applied to the matter you discuss in your letter regarding the Chabad schools. This is especially true since even in the United States, the change did not occur overnight but over the course of several years, and in Eretz Yisrael, too, they are moving in the aforementioned direction, even though it is naturally difficult to determine exactly the day and hour when all the justified requirements regarding pedagogical skill, knowledge, and so on, will be fulfilled.

What adds strength to this hope of mine is the very fact that we are dealing with *avreichim* and young people who demonstrate the greatest dedication to their roles as teachers and educators. For the departure to distant places was considered impossible—not only for them, but for the entire circle to which they belong—and not for purely economic reasons, but primarily for religious ones. Nevertheless, they agreed to go to relatively remote places and to a foreign environment, even though, in the end, they were not promised treasures of silver and gold, nor even the greatest achievements regarding religion, as each of them was already active in their own environment in the dissemination of Yiddishkeit. This means that their dedication and enthusiasm for the very idea of traveling to distant places in order to engage in the education of the sons and daughters of Yisrael was so great that it nullified all the negative and, in their eyes, most essential considerations. It is therefore natural that this enthusiasm will also overcome all the difficulties in acquiring knowledge, pedagogical training, and so on. I am confident that almost all of them have the ability and the talents for the aforementioned, and those who currently lack them do so only because until now they did not require them and did not feel a need for them. I understand that the acquisition of knowledge is not yet an official certificate, but for someone of your stature, and especially in the situation in Eretz Yisrael, surely what matters is not a piece of paper called a "certificate," but the actual knowledge, and most importantly, the dedication to the benefit of the student.

I take the liberty of enclosing herewith a copy of my general letter for Rosh Hashanah of this year. It discusses the matter of placing trust in the children of Yisrael regarding all religious matters. In my opinion, the same applies to placing trust in those about whom you write in your letter regarding the field of education and pedagogy. For them, it is one and the same, as their engagement in this field is motivated solely by the fact that they view their role as a command of our holy Torah, the Torah of life.

Approaching the new year that is coming upon us and upon all of Yisrael for good and for blessing, I send my blessing to you and to all those associated with you: a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually, both in your private affairs and in your general affairs, which serve as channels and vessels for the blessing of Hashem, which enriches without sorrow.

With respect and blessing.

## 6,507

6,507

B”H, 3 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Avreich, Yitzchak, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from 26 Elul. Enclosed is the response to the group of Charedi youth, which you will surely explain to them—if they require it—in a manner suited to their understanding and grasp. It is certainly superfluous to note that the effort regarding adding members is a major principle, especially among those who have already participated in this group at some point, as you write in your letter.

May it be the will of Hashem that, just as you write that you have already tasted the fruits of your efforts in the aforementioned, Hashem will add His assistance both in your efforts and in the "tasting," so that you may go from strength to strength in Torah and mitzvos, and likewise in your influence in the aforementioned direction.

With blessings for good tidings and a *chasima v’gmar chatima tova*.

P.S.

Regarding your question in your letter concerning the statement of our Sages, of blessed memory, that "whoever is greater than his fellow, his *yetzer* is greater," which is difficult to understand, for when one rises, the other falls:

The explanation of the aforementioned is that when a person is given a great *neshamah*—a *nefesh Elokis* that is lofty in its level—it is fitting that the *nefesh habahamish* also be great in its traits and its essence, i.e., in its opposition [and this is for several reasons, so that there should be no claims that the victory in the battle was achieved because he was given a lofty *nefesh Elokis* and a very weak *nefesh habahamish*]. **And after this**, when he fights with the powers of his *nefesh Elokis* to illuminate with the "candle of mitzvah and Torah light," the process begins where "light repels darkness," and when one rises, the other falls, until ultimately he can even reach the level where he has no *yetzer hara*, for he has "slain it with fasting," similar to what is explained in *Tanya Kadisha* regarding Dovid Melech Yisrael.

## 6508

B"H, 4 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Elul, with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It gave me pleasure to read in your letter of your participation in the *farbrengens* over the past weeks. It is a well-known saying of our Alter Rebbe: "What a Chassidishe *farbrengen* can accomplish, the Angel Michael cannot accomplish."

Regarding what you wrote concerning the discomfort, that a drowsiness falls upon you, etc. — one of the pieces of advice for this is to study in a place that your heart desires, and to vary your study from time to time; that is to say, that the study should not be for hours on end on one subject or in one style. It is understood that the above is in addition to strengthening one's physical health in general, and in particular, as this is a lofty matter in its own right, as the Rambam states: "Maintaining a healthy and sound body is among the ways of Hashem..."

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah* and for good tidings.

#### ***6508***

**Saying:** See also above, Vol. 17, Letter 5899, and the references in the notes there. Vol. 17, 6492.

## 6419

B"H, 4 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I was distressed by the report that reached here regarding the emergence of an issue concerning the lack of acceptance of authority by you at the school...

Now, in the aforementioned matter, one does not touch upon or enter at all into evaluations or comparisons of the person who is to accept the discipline and the administrator, for this is not a comparison between individuals, but between the positions. A parallel to this, albeit in a very subtle way, is the well-known story regarding Rabbi Yehoshua and Rabban Gamliel. Moreover, in the case at hand, the necessity is even greater, since the lack of acceptance of the yoke and discipline acts in direct opposition to the intended purpose for the students—may they live.

There is no need at all to elaborate on these matters between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, especially since the very essence of these days is the acceptance of authority and discipline without any involvement of personal opinion or intellect, beginning with the *kabbalas ol* of Rosh Hashanah for the entire year. I hope to receive good news in the return mail that, to begin with, the aforementioned lack did not occur, or that at the very least, from here on out, the matter has been fully rectified.

With blessings for good tidings and a *chasimah v’chasimah tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6510

B"H, 4 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, which was received in due course, in which you write of your distress when you recall where you were at the time of writing your letter three years ago, and where you find yourself now.

You should look into what is explained in Chassidus regarding the Mishna, "One hour of teshuvah and maasim tovim is better [than the whole life of the World to Come]," and specifically in the Hemshech "V'Kacha" [5637].

The central point is that teshuvah and maasim tovim are the matters that cause *nachas ruach* **to Hashem**, whereas the life of the World to Come is what causes *nachas ruach* **to man**.

From this, one can understand the magnitude of the distance and the magnitude of the difference between them.

This concept also applies to your situation from three years ago, when the primary focus was *nachas ruach* for man, compared to your situation now, where the primary focus is *nachas ruach* for Hashem through your activities in *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah*. This is understood, and it is also simple.

And yet, regarding this change—which is for the better—is it possible that one would, Heaven forbid, feel distressed?

Regarding what you wrote about the distance of the location, etc.: in these days of ours, "it is not hidden from you, nor is it far away." Rather, "G-d has made everything beautiful in its time."

Regarding what you wrote about *shidduchim*: the ruling of our Sages is well known, that "the daughters of Israel are beautiful," and it is simple that [the women in your area] are also included in this, especially in these very recent years, where a great change for the better in matters of Yiddishkeit is evident even to the physical eye.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and for a *chasimah v'gmar chatimah tovah*.

6111

6111

B”H, 5 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn

To the avreich, whose work is the work of Heaven, Yosef, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of 27 Elul, in which you write regarding your holy position at the Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch school in Lod.

Regarding the magnitude and value of this matter, there is surely no need for explanation or elaboration. I only wish to emphasize, based on the statement of our Sages, that “a name is a sign,” especially when it concerns a Tzaddik, the Nasi of Klal Yisrael. A faithful shepherd does not abandon his flock even when he ascends on high; he stands and arouses Divine mercy for all their affairs. As the holy Zohar states—and as is cited in the Iggeres HaKodesh of our Alter Rebbe—a Tzaddik who has departed is found in all worlds even more than during his lifetime. This is all the more true regarding a Tzaddik who is the Nasi of Klal Yisrael, a general soul who connects with himself the individual souls who are bound to him and follow in his footsteps—especially those who fulfill his instructions in the very matters to which he dedicated his life. Regarding my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, this is the matter of holy education, specifically education illuminated by the light and warmth of Toras HaChassidus and its teachings.

From the above, it is understood that every effort and labor is worthwhile in this endeavor and in a holy institution named after him, which brings nachas ruach to the soul of the Tzaddik, the Nasi. Through this, he further increases his arousal of Divine mercy for the success of the institution and the success of all who are involved in it.

As for the difficulties and opposition that appear—sometimes even from those who, on the contrary, should be assisting with all their might—this itself is proof that “Hashem, your G-d, is testing you to know, etc.”

As is known regarding the test in this: any such action or opposition that prevents a Jewish person from a matter of Torah and mitzvos does not originate from the side of holiness (the “right side”). See *Hayom Yom*, 23 Sivan.

May it be the will of Hashem that you fully utilize the success and blessing that are being bestowed from Above, and that this Chabad school be firmly established among the other institutions of the Reshet and Chabad institutions in general. Surely, the blessings of Hashem will be added, many times over, for each of them, both materially and spiritually.

It is understood and simple that all the above is directed to the entire staff of teachers, the administration, and all those who assist and strive in the most exalted matter mentioned above, may they live and be well.

With blessings for a *Chasimah V’Gmar Chasimah Tovah* and for good tidings in all the above.

***

6111

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 11, p. 230, and completed according to the secretariat’s copy.

**Yosef, may he live:** Marton, Lod.

## 6512

B"H, 7 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the auspicious day of 18 Elul, along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein, which will be read at a time of favor at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory:

I do not find the reasoning acceptable that he should study in a school with mixed classes or the like, for this provides a stamp of approval, and furthermore, it is akin to breaching the boundaries of the conduct of the circle in which he finds himself. After all, He who sustains and provides for all has provided his livelihood until now, and surely in this new year, too, He will provide his livelihood with grace, kindness, and mercy. And although in our current era, work in the field of holy education is the most exalted and vital matter, there is still a vision for the future when it will be possible to arrange the matter in a way that there will be no complications regarding what was mentioned.

With blessings for a *chasimah v’gmar chatimah tovah*.

## 6513

B"H, 7 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Rosh Hashanah.

It is surely unnecessary to emphasize that all thoughts contrary to joy and a good heart do not come from the *yetzer tov*. This is explained at length in many places, including in the *Tanya Kadisha*, as noted in the subject index at its conclusion. From the words of the Rambam at the end of *Hilchos Lulav*, it is understood that even matters of *teshuvah* must be performed specifically with joy and a good heart. As it is written there, this is one of the important points of *avodas Hashem*—and not merely a matter of *hiddur* (embellishment), but rather, in the language of the Rambam, a "great *avodah*." Further elaboration on such a simple matter is unnecessary.

Enclosed is a check from one of the funds of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *Nasi Yisrael*. You should invest the money in one of his enterprises (*unternehmungen*), and it is best to do so in several of them. May Hashem fulfill the statement of our Sages (*Bava Basra* 15b) that "he who takes a *prutah* from Iyov is blessed," for it is said of him, "a man whole and upright, etc." All these qualities are present in my father-in-law, the Rebbe, and this is well understood.

Your *pidyon nefesh* will be read at an *eis ratzon* at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nbg"m, zya*. May Hashem help that very soon you will bring good news regarding all the above, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed—and above all, with joy and a good heart.

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*.

## 6363

B"H, Between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received the good news regarding the Bar Mitzvah celebration of your son, may he live and be well, which will take place on Shabbos Shuvah. May it be the will of Hashem that his entry into the observance of mitzvos be at a fortunate and auspicious time, and may his parents derive from him much nachas—true nachas, which is Jewish nachas—such that all who see him will recognize that he is the seed blessed by Hashem.

It is surely unnecessary to emphasize to your son, the Bar Mitzvah, may he live and be well—though such emphasis is appropriate in these matters—that upon reaching the age of thirteen, for long and good years, he enters a new order of life. It is a full life of a Jew who is commanded and performs, living according to our holy Torah, the Torah of life, and its mitzvos, regarding which it is said, "and he shall live by them." Since the Giver of the Torah and the Commander of the mitzvos is the Creator, may He be blessed and exalted, who requires all of this, there is no doubt that He also grants the strength and the means to every single person to fulfill what is incumbent upon him; the matter depends solely on the will of man.

Everything is surely by Hashgacha Pratis, that the Bar Mitzvah celebration will take place on Shabbos Shuvah, which is between Rosh Hashanah—the time of the good inscription—and Yom Kippur—the time of the good sealing. And for a Jew, a good life is a life according to the Torah, in both spirituality and physicality alike.

May it be the will of Hashem that you and your wife, may you live and be well, see true pleasure and nachas from your son, the Bar Mitzvah, and from your other children, may they live and be well, and may you always share only good news, as is the wish of your friend.

With the blessing of a good inscription and sealing.

## 6515

B"H, 7 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Elul, with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory,

In which you write regarding your idea to learn the craft of *STaM* (scribal arts),

This is a very proper thing, for several reasons.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that a *sofer STaM* must increase his *yiras shamayim*. However, Hashem does not deal tyrannically with His creations; He grants the ability and the strength for this from the outset. The matter depends solely upon the person, and on the contrary, one is granted additional assistance, as our Sages of blessed memory said: "One who comes to purify himself is assisted." See *Likutei Torah*, *Devarim* 89d.

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*.

#### ***6515***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 375.

## 6,516

B"H, 9 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, man of many accomplishments and fine character,
R' Levi, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter, and its contents regarding the visit of the students of our holy yeshiva to the sanatorium during the two days of Rosh Hashanah, to enable those staying there to fulfill their obligation of the mitzvah of blowing the shofar.

And since our Sages have said that one of the aspects of blowing the shofar is the crowning of the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He, in this physical and material world of ours and all its affairs—and since Hashem is the essence of good and the source of good, and the kingdom of heaven is like the kingdom of earth, where on the day of coronation [the king] grants amnesty to the provinces—

May this amnesty be extended to the matters of improving the health of the body and the health of the soul for all those present in the sanatorium where you serve in holiness, within the entirety of Klal Yisrael, in a good that is visible and revealed.

May you be able to share good news regarding your grandchildren, may they live, whom you mention in your letter, and likewise regarding all the people of the institution and their affairs.

With respect and with blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chatimah tovah*.

***

**R' Levi:** Bilitzky, Brooklyn.

## 6,517

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the participants of the first farbrengen
of the Shemitah year, the year 5719,
in the city of Toronto,
May Hashem keep them alive.

Peace and Blessing!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter from Motzaei Shabbos Shuvah, informing me of the aforementioned first farbrengen.

May it be Hashem’s will that this serves as a good beginning for the entire year—the seventh year, which is a Shabbos to Hashem.

This is alluded to and connected to the "day that is entirely Shabbos and rest for eternal life" (see Ibn Ezra and Ramban on the concept of the day of Shabbos, etc., Vayikra 25:2).

It is explained in the teachings of Chassidus that this is the most exalted level of the reward in the time to come, which is divided in its entirety into two periods: the period where there is no rest for the world to come, as they go from strength to strength, and from that, they ascend to the period of "Shabbos and rest for eternal life."

And since the ultimate perfection of the world at that time depends upon our deeds and our avodah throughout the entire duration of the galus (Tanya, beginning of Chapter 37), it is understood from this that in our avodah, too, there must be a reflection of this.

It is simple: this is achieved by completely diverting oneself from all worldly matters—not only the physical world, but even the spiritual world; and not only from human intellect and emotion, but even from the intellect and emotion of kedushah. The avodah is beyond intellect and emotion, reaching the avodah of "with all your might" (*b'chol me'odecha*). This is alluded to in the statement of our Sages regarding avodah not for the sake of receiving a reward—not even a spiritual reward.

This is also the concept of the verse, "Whom have I in heaven? And besides You, I desire nothing on earth," and as explained by the Alter Rebbe, who would say in these words: "I do not want anything at all; I do not want Your Gan Eden, I do not want Your Olam HaBa, etc.; I want nothing but You alone" (Shoresh Mitzvas HaTefillah by the Tzemach Tzedek, Chapter 40, at the end).

And regarding this, too, it is said, "It is not hidden from you... it is not in heaven," and as our Sages say there, that if it were in heaven, you would have to ascend after it to learn it (Devarim 30:12).

And since we have been promised that the Holy One, blessed be He, does not deal tyrannically with His creatures, it is certain that the kochos for this have been granted. And this is easy to understand.

With the blessing of a happy Yom Tov.

#### ***6,517***

**And as explained by the Alter Rebbe:** See also above, Vol. 5, Letter 1,554; Vol. 6, 1,549.

## 6,518

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 15 Elul, the month of mercy, in which you write regarding what you read in a *sicha* concerning the matter of *pruzbul*, the essence of which is the handing over of one's promissory notes to the *beis din*. You cite the dispute between Rashi and Tosafos on this matter.

You should examine the *Hilchos Halva'ah* of our Alter Rebbe, author of the *Tanya*—who is the authority on the *nistar* (hidden) dimension of Torah—and the *Shulchan Aruch*—the authority on the *nigleh* (revealed) dimension of Torah—in section 34. This serves as the foundation for what is stated in the *sicha*, that it is as if he had handed his note to the *beis din*, etc., and that even if he has no note at all, but rather a debt by word of mouth, a *pruzbul* is effective.

On this occasion, I would like to encourage you regarding the observance of the three daily *shiurim*—*Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*—which are well-known. We have heard many times from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that these are a *segulah* for many matters.

With blessings for good tidings and a happy holiday.

#### ***6,518***

**In the sicha:** Shabbos Parshas Re'eh 5712 (*Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 2, p. 628).

6519

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos Shuvah, in which you write your reasoning regarding the scheduling of the wedding, and what you wrote therein regarding the month of Teves:

Since you are asking me about this, it is not our custom in our circles to hold weddings during that month. However, in the month of Kislev, we hold them throughout the entire month.

Whatever you decide, may it be in a good and auspicious hour in all respects.

Regarding what you wrote about preparation, and the limited time for proper preparation:

Although Chazal spoke at length about the great importance of *hachnasas kallah* and the preparation for it, in our current era, in my opinion, the *hiddur* (embellishment) should be in spiritual preparation, and the extra expenditure should be dedicated to matters of Torah and *mitzvos* for the merit of both the *chassan* and the *kallah* together.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, with expanded livelihood and true peace of mind, and for a happy holiday.

*6519*

**That one knows:** That through this, it is possible to reveal many hidden things to help one's fellow, to discover the source of illnesses and methods of treatment, to discover water or metals in the ground, and much, much more.

## 6519*

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After the longest of intervals, your letter of 4 Tishrei was received.

Regarding what you wrote about a pendulum, and that you are copying what you know, etc. — for my own part, I do not believe in the entire matter. Nevertheless, "we have not heard and we have not seen" is not proof. Therefore, you should turn to a Rav who is a *moreh hora'ah*, and he will instruct you whether the matter is permitted according to the *Shulchan Aruch*. As I believe I have already written to you: why must you search for new paths and new matters, at a time when there is a paved path—the path of *Torah* and *mitzvos*? There is a great principle in this: "In all your ways, know Him," which encompasses all of a person’s affairs, as explained in the *Rambam* and ruled as *halacha* in the *Tur Shulchan Aruch*, *Orach Chaim*, siman 231. If only you would utilize all your spare time for the study of *Torah* in general, and within that, the study of the *pnimiyus haTorah* (inner dimension of Torah), which in our days has been revealed in the teachings of *Chassidus*.

With blessings for good tidings and a *chag sameach*,

On behalf of *k'vod kedushas Admur shlita*,

Secretary

## 6520

B"H, 12 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the avreich Naftali Tzvi, may he live,

Shalom u'Bracha!

We acknowledge receipt of the pan from the 5th of Tishrei, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding what you wrote about the custom to recite the verse corresponding to one's name at the conclusion of the Shemoneh Esrei prayer, etc.:

Several Chassidim have the custom to recite the verse corresponding to their own name before "Yehi Ratzon," as well as the verse corresponding to the name of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Certainly, this adds to the avodah of spreading the wellsprings (until they spread also) outward—in one's own "outward" and especially in that of others. There is nothing that can stand before the will, especially regarding this matter, which is the demand and necessity of the hour and a personal obligation.

With blessings for a happy holiday and for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6,521

B"H, 12 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from before Rosh Hashanah, which was received during days when time did not permit a reply to a matter requiring deliberation, I offer my sincerest apologies for the delay in responding.

In it, you write regarding your avodah and your holy position in [city name omitted], and regarding the difficulties you are encountering, etc., as if you are in doubt as to whether to continue your work there or to relocate to another city, etc.

My opinion is clear [based on what we heard many times in such matters from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory]: you must continue your holy work in your current location. This is especially true since there are already signs of movement for the better—and not just movement, but actual change. Furthermore, it reinforces the obvious point that leaving there is liable to destroy and undo all the achievements made thus far. Certainly, there are those who would interpret a departure from there as a retreat and a flight from the battlefield. Surely, for someone like you, the nature of baalei batim and the rulings regarding them are known (see Sma and Nesivos HaMishpat, Shulchan Aruch, Choshen Mishpat 63:4). Let your trust be strong that, in the end, you will succeed in establishing the community on its proper footing, as our Sages say (in the Zohar and the Midrash): "I am asleep in exile, but my heart is awake" to Hashem, to His Torah, and to His mitzvos.

We have now entered, may it be for a good year, a new year that is a complete year, a year and its leap month, the year of Shemitah, regarding which it is said, "a Shabbos to Hashem" (see the commentary of the Ramban there).

With respect and with blessings for success in all the aforementioned, for good tidings, and for a happy holiday.

## 6522

6522

B"H, 18 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in the work of Heaven,
Shimon Gad, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding the meeting concerning activities to awaken our brethren, the Sephardic Jews in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established), to matters of Yiddishkeit, Torah, and mitzvos, and you ask regarding obtaining letters of support from Rabbonim, etc.

It is well known that the opinion of our Torah, the Torah of life, regarding the success of matters of holiness, is to begin in a manner of "though your beginning was small" [and as the Baal Shem Tov emphasizes, "then your latter end shall greatly increase"]. From this, it is understood that one should not currently occupy oneself with publicizing or acquiring letters from Rabbonim, etc., but rather to proceed step by step. Through this very process, various details, methods of activity, and the aptitude for this will become clarified, and then you will be able to decide regarding your questions, etc.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6522***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 23, p. 503, and completed according to a photocopy of the letter.

**Shimon Gad, may he live:** Alitov, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 15, 5713, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6,523

6,523

B"H, 18 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Dovid Avraham, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letter of 11 Tishrei...

It is surprising that you do not mention in your recent letters whether you have met again with those relatives of yours whom you met previously in Kfar Chabad, and upon whom you had an influence—drawing them closer to Chabad, even if they are still lacking in the actual performance of mitzvos. My hope is strong that you are succeeding in this effort, and that you are doing so from time to time—with an increase in both quantity and quality. Please provide an update on this in your next letter, and do not wait until you feel that you are once again in a "siege" or similar situation.

Certainly, you are utilizing your influence regarding the school where you teach, and regarding the residents of the moshav. In both places, you are surely working with great energy, as the matter is of utmost importance. Although it is understood that the method of action differs—that is, in the school, the activity is within the content of your duties, whereas regarding the local residents, one must contemplate and estimate in advance the reaction of the shochet and others in the place. Furthermore, one must be careful not to give room for any claim that you are neglecting the instruction of the students for the sake of propaganda among the adults. Everything must be according to the conditions of the place and the individuals involved. Surely you will consult with other anash who are there and know the local conditions. It is understood that this consultation must also be done discreetly; a word to the wise is sufficient.

With blessings for success in your work—the work of Heaven—and regards to the entire teaching staff, may they live.

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed, and I am signing on his behalf,

A. Quint
Secretary

***

**R' Dovid Avraham, may he live:** Of Selaubim, Moshav Brosh. Further letters to him—above, Vol. 16, 5,907, and in the references noted there.

**Met again:** See above, ibid.

## 6,524

6,524

B"H, 19 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After a long interval, your letter from Erev Sukkos has arrived. At an auspicious time, I will mention you and your children, may they live, at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever each of you is in need of, in accordance with the contents of your letter.

Regarding what you write about your manner of speaking: it is understood and simple that it is nothing more than a nervous matter. The advice for this is *hisbonenus* in matters that strengthen the *middah* of *bitachon* in Hashem Yisbarach, and primarily in the matter of *hashgacha pratis*—that Hashem Yisbarach oversees everyone in every single detail, and in every moment. Furthermore, specifically in the matter of *krias haTorah*, this is done for the honor of Hashem Yisbarach and for the honor of the Torah, and it makes no difference what people think. All of this reduces the *shimas lev* (attention) toward the people standing around you, and consequently, the agitation diminishes and goes away.

If it happens that a certain word is difficult to pronounce, the simple advice is to re-read a few words from before it (if there is no *shem* for it), and then the word will come out more clearly along with the others. Certainly, the doctor who is treating you will be able to find and advise you on more details regarding this.

And when the state of health in general becomes stronger, a feeling of improvement is also felt in the nerves and their consequences.

With blessings for good tidings.

Due to the sanctity of the festival, the Rebbe shlita did not sign, and I am signing on his behalf, The Secretary

## 6,525

B"H, 19 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter of 11 Tishrei, in which you ask whether to continue your studies in the Kollel, given that there is no other organization [available].

Regarding a matter such as this, one must be careful not to let the situation become a habit of inaction concerning an organization. For the body is naturally inclined toward the approach of "sit and do nothing," and you must contemplate [the importance of] a stable organization. You should consult with those who know you and are familiar with the local conditions regarding this concern, and act accordingly.

It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding increasing your study of Chassidus, both for yourself and for others; after all, the dissemination of the wellsprings outward is a requirement and a necessity of the hour.

With blessings for good tidings,

Due to the sanctity of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed personally, and I sign on his behalf,

The Secretary

6526

B"H, 25 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the first day of Chol Hamoed Sukkos. Regarding what you write—that you are afraid to take upon yourself to be a Chabad Chossid because it imposes the heavy burden of studying Chassidus, etc.—there are several responses to this, the primary ones being:

a) This is not the imposition of a new burden. After all, every person accepts upon themselves the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven and the yoke of the mitzvos twice daily when reciting the Shema, as stated in the Mishnah (Berachos, Chapter 2, Mishnah 2). This study is simply a part of the yoke of the mitzvos; once one accepts the yoke in its entirety, the whole is comprised of its individual parts.

b) The aforementioned burden is not heavy, because the study of the teachings of Chassidus, like the study of all parts of the Torah, proceeds from the easy to the difficult.

c) As one continues in this progression, and comes to recognize more of the content of this Torah—its depth, and alongside it, its clarity—the desire to study it increases, as does the taanug in grasping these concepts. These two factors transform the matter of a "burden" into a matter of will and desire.

The story is well-known regarding our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, the author of the Tanya. When he first began to disseminate his teachings—the teachings of Chabad—in the lands of Reisin and Lithuania, he passed through Shklov, which was then the fortress of the opponents of these teachings and their principles. He ascended the bimah in the Great Synagogue there, and in his holy, melodic tone, he recited the verse: "Taste and see that Hashem is good" (*ta'amu u're'u ki tov Hashem*—"taste and see how Hashem [may He be blessed] is good"). Because his words emanated from the heart, carving out flames of fire, they entered the hearts of the listeners. As it is recounted from Chossid to Chossid, generation after generation, down to our own day: "Dozens of young men ran after him," who subsequently engaged in the study of the teachings of Chassidus and, in time, became the pillars of the Chassidic community.

Regarding your additional concern—that through the study of Chassidus you will not be able to concentrate as much on your research—it is within your power to dedicate to this study [and in the initial period, this will suffice] at least the time you have free from that research.

The reason I reiterate this matter of study and its necessity several times is because each of us is commanded to follow the aforementioned conduct, as our Sages have said regarding such matters: "even a hundred times."

May it be Hashem's will that, in accordance with the point made in my general letter for this year’s Rosh Hashanah, you will see the obstacles to the aforementioned as they truly are—that is to say, extremely minor—and that a firm decision of the mind in the aforementioned direction will have the power to nullify them entirely.

With respect and blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6,527

6,527

B"H, 25 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished [individual],
R' Shmuel, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter and the enclosed [item], which has been delivered to the *Lishkas Chashaim*.

May Hashem Yisbarach help that for long, good days and years, your heart’s desire for good should be fulfilled—to be able to serve Hashem Yisbarach with *simchah* and *tuv levav*. For in the *avodah* of Hashem in its literal sense, as our Sages of blessed memory expound on the verse "Honor Hashem with your wealth" (*Mishlei* 3:9)—"Do not read it as 'your wealth' (*mehoncha*), but rather 'with your throat' (*migroncha*)" (*Rashi* ad loc.), and in the language of Moshe Rabbeinu, the *Ra'aya Meheimna* (*Zohar* Vol. II, 93a), "with the joy of a *nigun*."

And this should be as discussed in the *maamarim*: with an abundance of *simchah*, which is indicated by the doubling of the expression, "with *simchah* and *tuv levav*."

May you merit the true abundance in all matters, as per the promise, "And you shall spread out to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south" (*Bereishis* 28:14)—and to an inheritance without boundaries.

With blessings.

## 6228

B"H, 25 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter and the three *pidyonos nefesh* in due time. Regarding your moving from there to..., surely Mr... *shiy* has detailed to you what we discussed regarding this. The point is that whether you are moving entirely or only for a period of time, it is understood and simple that it is impossible to leave the place vacant, *v'kal v'd'l*. Therefore, well in advance [I mean, some time **before** you depart from there], it is worthwhile for a suitable person from *anash* to arrive there, so that they may gradually become accustomed to him, and he may become accustomed to the situation there. It is imperative that until that time, the *baalei batim* and the public should not know about your departure from there, so that they do not begin to come forward with suggestions, bringing someone of their own choosing to the position. Conversely, in the manner described above, the interpretation of the matter will be that this is a continuation of your stay there, and there is nothing new here; there are many advantages to this. As mentioned, the success of this strategy requires that you delay the announcement of your desire to travel for some time from... as much as possible. Please inform me here as soon as possible when you plan to travel, so that the search for a suitable person and his travel—which require some preparation—will have sufficient time, and as mentioned above, the sooner you provide details to me here, the better it will be for the matter.

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

6229

6229

B"H, 25 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished man of many talents and sciences, etc., etc., Mr. Eliezer Aryeh HaLevi, known as Dr. Finkelstein,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge with thanks receipt of your letter from the 17th of Elul, as well as the books you were kind enough to send for my library. It is my hope that you will continue to provide us with the books you publish from time to time. Please forgive the delay in my response, which was due to the busy days that intervened.

Some find the statement of our Sages, "Rabbi honored the wealthy," puzzling.

To me, however, it is understood and explained according to one of the fundamental points of the Baal Shem Tov’s approach: that every matter in the world, even the most minute, is by Divine Providence—and especially general matters that affect many people.

Wealthy individuals—people whom Divine Providence has placed in a position to accomplish much good in the world of the Ribono shel Olam—certainly possess the soul-powers suited to fulfilling this role, a role that far exceeds the role of an average person, and certainly that of a poor person. Our holy Rabbi [Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi], whose era was a transitional period in the life of the Jewish people—from relatively peaceful times to times of decrees and persecutions—had to mobilize all his strength so that the Jewish people would withstand this trial and emerge whole and fortified. His mission was to observe each and every person and strive to utilize all the potential within them. Therefore, he expressed a feeling of honor toward those whom Above had granted greater possibilities, which were surely also utilized to protect everything holy in Israel, just like all the other actions of our holy Rabbi.

However, it is consistent with the general conduct of the world that every Jew is given free choice, and Hashem Elokim tests him to know whether he loves Hashem, etc., and keeps His mitzvos and listens to His voice, etc. In this regard, too, the choice is given to every wealthy person, as it is written, "See, I have set before you today life and good, etc.," to fulfill through his own choice, as the verse concludes, "And you shall choose life," or, G-d forbid, the opposite.

It is simple that if this applies to those wealthy in gold and silver, it applies all the more so to those wealthy in the power of influence over their immediate and distant surroundings.

It is also understood that since there is a Leader for this world and nothing in it is in vain, an active and positive utilization of wealth is mandatory. It is not at all sufficient that they simply not use their wealth in an undesirable direction, because even the absence of utilization disrupts the order of Creation, since its purpose is that **all** its parts must actively assist in the general and unified goal.

It is surely unnecessary to emphasize that my intention is the true purpose of our people, the people of the G-d of Avraham, both the collective and the individual: life according to our Torah, the Torah of life—a complete Torah without compromises. The greatness of Torah is that "great is study, for it leads to action"—the performance of mitzvos in practice in daily life, regarding which it is said, "And he shall live by them."

Please forgive me for touching upon this fundamental point immediately in my first letter. Seemingly, you might be surprised: "Is this really so?" Yet, it is my hope that these lines will bring about the hoped-for result. There are several reasons for this:

First, we live in an era pregnant with events that some time ago would have been considered impossible. Anyone who observes, even with slight contemplation, sees at every step matters that are beyond the intellect—and how true are the words of our Sages: "Do not dismiss anything."

Second—and this is a point whose belief is absolute and founded in the teachings of Chassidus—is the explanation of the title that every Jew is called "a garden spring, a well of living waters." It is the nature and soul-characteristic of every Jew that when one speaks to him about matters of Torah and mitzvos to the maximum degree, there is no need to innovate anything within him, for in his innermost being, he agrees with this. He only needs to remove the thing that is preventing and hindering it somewhere, and then he becomes "a garden spring, a well of living waters." Like this well that flows from itself, and according to the style of the Zohar, this can happen in one moment and in one instant.

And another primary point: since you have been graced by Divine Providence with the power of influence and have a significant number of people who are influenced by you—some of whom are themselves influencers and leaders in certain circles—if these lines bring about any change in practice, it will spread and be multiplied many times over through those you influence.

I have elaborated somewhat on the aforementioned points in a general letter for Rosh Hashanah, a copy of which is enclosed here, and I hope that you will find interest in this as well.

With respect and blessing.

## 6730

B"H, 26 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, etc.,
R' Schneur Zalman, may he live, Halevi

Greetings and blessings!

I received the news regarding the request for a blessing for the boy... may he live, and I was pleased to learn that everything concluded peacefully and well. May it be His will that he returns to full health, and that his parents, may they live, raise him and all their children, may they live, to Torah, to the chuppah, and to good deeds, illuminated and permeated with the light and warmth of Chassidus.

It is explained in many places regarding the verse, "This is the Torah of man," that this is based on the connection and equivalence between the Torah and the Jewish person. This is also alluded to in the statement of our Sages that the 248 limbs of a person correspond to the 248 positive mitzvos, and the 365 sinews correspond to the 365 negative mitzvos. Based on this, it is understood—and it is also simple—that just as an increase in the inwardness and essence of the life-force of the human body is the best medicine and the simplest path to strengthening and increasing the health of the body, so too is it exactly the same for the health of the neshamah: an increase in the inwardness and essence of the life-force of the neshamah brings about the greatest strengthening and increase in the health of the neshamah. And the life-force of the neshamah is dependent upon Torah and its matters; that is to say, the aforementioned is achieved through the inwardness of Torah and the inwardness of the mitzvos, and the path to arrive at this and to them is also through the study of the inwardness of the Torah. And since the health of the Jewish body is dependent upon the health of his neshamah, which is a part of Elokus from above, literally—

Since the Jewish body is also holy,

And as explained in Tanya, Chapter 49, that regarding the material body it is said, "And You have chosen us," and the place of choice above is specifically in His essence and being, may He be blessed, as explained in many places—

Therefore, an increase in the study of the inwardness of the Torah, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus, and in accordance with the statement of our Sages, "The study is not the essential thing, but the deed"—walking in its guidance and conduct—this is the straightest and best path, also regarding the health of the body.

And for children of the age of the aforementioned, all that has been said applies to his parents, may they live, each according to their respective matters, and this is understood.

May it be His will that just as you brought the aforementioned request and news, so too may you bring good tidings in the future regarding all that has been said.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6,531

6,531

B"H, 26 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding the conclusion of your letter, that you have not yet found any source of livelihood—surely, with all due respect, you were not precise in that expression. For it is clear—and we say this with the mention of Hashem’s Name and Kingship—that "Hashem our G-d, King of the world, feeds the entire world in His goodness, with grace, with kindness, and with mercy," etc. And even though the obligation of Birchas Hamazon is from the Torah, while the specific text is not, in addition to the fact that the words of the Sages are sweet and there is stringency in the words of the Sages, the text of the first blessing of Birchas Hamazon was established by Moshe Rabbeinu, the first recipient of the Torah, the *Ra'aya Mehemna* (faithful shepherd) of all Bnei Yisrael in every generation until today—read not "until the sea" (*yam*), but "until today" (*yom*), the last [generation] (Sifrei on the verse there).

However, one can find a merit for your expression, in that Yaakov Avinu also "lifted his eyes to the mountains, from where will my help come?" But, of course, this is not sufficient, for the narrative regarding Yaakov Avinu is told in order to instruct all those who follow him that if anyone poses this question, the answer is already prepared by our father Yaakov: "My help is from Hashem."

May it be the will [of Hashem] that very soon, the blessing of Hashem Yisbarach will be drawn down in the manner of the aforementioned blessing, below ten *tefachim*.

With respect and blessing for good news regarding the above.

P.S.
Although you do not mention your study of Chassidus, it is my hope that you set aside times for it, and in any event, you should establish such times from now on, in accordance with the well-known directive to "increase in holiness," as we discussed regarding this when we last met.

## 6,532

B"H, 28 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Agudas Gemilus Chasadim...
May Hashem keep them and grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the report for the year 5719.

It is somewhat surprising that, according to the report, only 55 loans were distributed throughout the entire year.

May it be the will of Hashem that in the near future the prophecy will be fulfilled, "However, there shall be no needy among you."

And may it be that if, as the verses continue, this is delayed—Heaven forbid—and "there shall be a needy person among you," you will have the ability to increase your good work in Gemilus Chasadim. As is well known, the Sages stated that Gemilus Chasadim is greater than Tzedakah (even though Tzedakah is generally considered equal to all the mitzvos).

Furthermore, this year is the seventh year, the year of Shemitah, and therefore this matter is even more precious, as there is an additional special commandment in the aforementioned verses: "Beware for yourself... you shall surely give."

With blessings for success in this work, and as our Torah requires, with joy and a glad heart.

P.S.

As is my custom—bli neder—I am enclosing my check as a symbolic contribution for the coming year.

## 6,733

6,733

B”H, 29 Tishrei, 5719

Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

As I traveled today to the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I received your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, along with the enclosure.

...One should note the Yerushalmi, Berachos, end of chapter 8, regarding "If he blessed you, etc." The response in the second *maamar* is seemingly puzzling, where he says to him, "Your word has already been spoken twice." On the face of it, it is difficult to understand why he did not explain the matter. Seemingly, one could say that since "man looks at the eyes and hears with his ears," it is possible that it could be along the lines of the blessing of Bilaam (Sanhedrin 105b), and therefore he left the matter to Heaven. And one should not raise a difficulty as to why he answers "Amen" after him, for "Amen" is said regarding the speaker, whereas "Your word has already been spoken" relates to his *kavanah*, as is understood from the text. And although it is not explained therein why, when another encountered him and cursed him as well, he answered, "Your word has already been spoken" [for it is obvious that regarding a non-Jew, one should not insert an interpretation into his speech, which is along the lines of what is recounted in Moed Katan 9b, "These, etc."—see there in *Chiddushei Aggados*, and in *Likutei Torah* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe (Bechukosai 48b)], and in this regard, it must be that it is because "to punish the righteous is not good," whoever it may be—yet after all this, the above is forced.

It could be said that the aforementioned response was in order to sharpen his students, but this too is forced.

Therefore, it seems to me to say [and this was also explained at the farbrengen of Shabbos Bereishis, which is Shabbos Mevarchim], that the blessing of a person, whoever he may be, is limited. Not so when one says, "Your word has already been spoken"—the blessing comes from the Source of Blessings*, and similarly in the opposite case, so that "you shall eradicate the evil" as much as is required. And it still requires further study.

With blessings for good tidings.

*) See on this the *maamar* beginning "Rabbi Yehuda bar Ilai: Any Kohen who blesses"—in *Sefer HaMaamarim 5629*, which has just been published.

## 6,534

6,534

B"H, 29 Tishrei, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Following the longest of interruptions, I received your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you ask about the short days of winter: due to the pressures of your job, you are unsure if you will have enough time to study the daily shiur of Chumash and Chok L'Yisrael during the day, and you ask if you may study it at night.

I have not heard an explicit instruction regarding this. It seems to me that regarding the shiur of Tehillim, if you know the chapters by heart, you should recite them after chatzos halailah (midnight) or before tzeis hakochavim (nightfall), but not between tzeis hakochavim and chatzos halailah.

Regarding the shiur of Chumash and Chok L'Yisrael, you do not have to study them all at once; rather, you can divide them into segments throughout the day—and, of course, this can be done even while at your place of business. If you do not have the time even for that, then you may study them with a commentary on each and every verse. In times of duress, one can explain that this is included in the category of Torah Sheba'al Peh, especially since in our times, many are lenient regarding the matter of studying Mikra at night, even though it is mentioned in several sefarim...

#### ***6,534***

**If you may study this at night:** See also above, Vol. 5, Letter 1,502, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6531

6531

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Adar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday, regarding your sister, Mrs. ..., who has been married for several years and has not yet been blessed with children.

It is surprising that you do not mention whether they have sought the opinion of a specialist physician. In any case, they should do so now, in accordance with the instruction of our Sages, "And he shall surely heal," for the Torah has granted the physician permission to heal.

However, since for a Jewish person, the physical and the spiritual go hand in hand—for "they are one nation on earth," and in the words of the Alter Rebbe in his Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 9, that even in worldly matters they are not separated from the One Truth—the aforementioned couple should increase their involvement in Torah and mitzvos and strengthen their trust in Hashem, the Creator and Guide of the world, who oversees each and every individual with Divine Providence regarding all that has been mentioned.

In a situation such as this, it is appropriate to clarify whether there was any slight to the honor of a Jewish man or woman during their shidduch process that would require asking for forgiveness, whether in the presence of the person offended or in their absence. Furthermore, they should have the husband’s tefillin and the mezuzos in their home checked to ensure they are all kosher. The wife should observe the custom of Jewish women to set aside coins for tzedakah before lighting the candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov.

With blessings for good news regarding all that has been mentioned.

Regarding your note on what is written in Daas Zekenim (Bereishis, at the beginning) that the world is open from the northern side—this is also found in Bava Basra (25b), Pirkei D’Rabbi Eliezer (Chapter 3), Zohar Chadash on Shir HaShirim, the beginning of Braisa D’Shmuel, and elsewhere.

The explanation—according to astronomy—can be found in Sefer Toras HaOlah by the Rema, Part II; in the commentary Shvilei HaMeoros to the aforementioned Braisa; and elsewhere.

6536

6536

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After a long interval, your letter from the 24th of Tishrei was received, in which you also included your daily schedule.

May it be Hashem’s will that you fulfill and infuse this schedule with ahavas Hashem and yiras Hashem. For all the matters in your plan can be conducted in this manner—for those who use their right hand [in Torah study] —and this is achieved by prioritizing ahavas Hashem and yiras Hashem. As the Mishnah states, one’s fear [of Hashem] must precede one’s wisdom; the same applies regarding the attribute of love. This is also as our Sages of blessed memory explain at the beginning of Maseches Berachos regarding the reason why the first paragraph of the Shema precedes the second. See also Tanya, beginning of Chapter 4: love is the root of the 248 positive mitzvos, and fear is the root of the 365 negative mitzvos. The path to attaining His love and fear is explained in the Rambam’s Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, beginning of Chapter 2. In our times, these matters are attained through the study of Chassidus, and I believe we spoke of this when you were here.

May it be His will that you succeed in the above, and primarily, that you emphasize that you are located in a land that is called by all—even by the nations of the world—by the name "the Holy Land." This necessitates an increase in the holiness of the person, similar to what is written, "You shall be holy, for I am holy," and there is nothing that can stand before one’s will.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

*) See there in Rashi, s.v. *limasmilin*: "If so..." and in Rashi, Yoma (72b), s.v. *zachah*.

## 6,737

6,737

B”H, 1st of MarCheshvan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 28th of Tishrei, in which you write regarding the situation at the school, and specifically regarding the issue of separation [segregation], noting that your opinion is a minority opinion and that you lack the strength, etc.

It is surely unnecessary to elaborate for someone like you that the aforementioned opinion is also the opinion of the "Only One of the World." According to the well-known saying of our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch, that one must "live with the times" (*m'darf leben mit der tzeit*)—meaning, to live with the lessons of the weekly parsha (printed in *Kuntres Bikur Chicago*, *Kuntres* 45)—and that "the deeds of the fathers are a sign for the children," in this week’s parsha [Noach] it is written: "And the whole earth was of one language and of one speech," and as explained in Rashi’s commentary, they were so unified that there was concern even Above, "and now nothing will be withheld from them." Yet the end of the matter was that "Avraham was one," and "he inherited the land," etc. It is a well-known saying of our Rebbes, the *Nesi’im*, that there is a *chazakah* (presumption) that *ta'amulah* (outreach/propaganda) does not return empty. May it be His will that this occur speedily and with a minimum of *he'lem* and *hester* (concealment and hiddenness). Fortunate is the merit of anyone who advances this matter by even one month, one week, or one day.

With respect and blessings.

#### ***6,737***

**Regarding the issue of separation:** See on this above, Vol. 17, Letter 6,177, and the references in the notes there. Below, 6,929, 6,945.

**The well-known saying:** See also above: Vol. 15, 5,476*, and the references in the notes there. Below, 6,743.

## 6,538

B"H, 3rd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Miriam, may she live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 27th of Tishrei, in which you ask about the obligation of lighting candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov prior to marriage.

In general, there are various customs regarding this. There are communities where women begin lighting candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov at a very young age, even several years before becoming a bas mitzvah, and there are communities where they are accustomed to begin the lighting only after marriage.

The custom of the Rebbe’s house is to begin lighting at a **very** young age, as mentioned.

In any event, regarding yourself, since you have already become a bas mitzvah and have the ability to fulfill this great mitzvah—the lighting of candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov, including the recitation of the blessing—it is proper that you do so, *bli neder*. The reward is explained in the Midrashim of our Sages: that the lighting of the candle, in the literal sense, adds light to "the candle of Hashem, the soul of man"—the holy soul that is within every Jewish man and woman. Through this, there is an increase in all of one's matters regarding the mitzvos of Hashem, and from this comes a blessing with an increase also in one's physical matters.

It is my hope that you are engaged in the dissemination of Yiddishkeit by organizing Shabbos parties and the like, and that even between one party and the next, you strive to influence your environment to bring their hearts closer to our Father in Heaven through the fulfillment of the mitzvos of Hashem, may He be blessed. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

#### ***6,538***

**Miriam:** Schechter, Winnipeg.

## 6,539

6,539

B"H, 3rd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent scholar, engaged in communal affairs,
R' Yitzchak, may he live.

Shalom u’vrachah!

Following a long interval, your letter from the conclusion of Shabbos, Parshas Bereishis, was received. [And in a style befitting the stories of Chassidim, according to the custom of their ancestors in Russia of old, who would declare at the conclusion of Simchas Torah, "And Yaakov went on his way," this is also] the *Bereishis* of a new period in a person’s *avodah* in general, and specifically in the fulfillment of his inner and essential mission—fitting the statement of our Sages in their *Mishnah* to serve my Creator and to make for Him, may He be blessed, a dwelling place in the lower worlds. And according to what is explained in the *Iggeres HaKodesh* of our Alter Rebbe, *siman* 14, from year to year the *avodah* is renewed in a more sublime manner. In the language of the aforementioned *iggeres*, a light descends that had not yet shone since the days of old—such a supernal light—to the supernal earth and to those who dwell upon it, namely all the supernal and lower worlds. And for the lower worlds, it is through their deeds and *avodah* that they reveal the light from the concealment.

In your letter, you write regarding the choice given to you: to choose between giving *shiurim* in Be’er Sheva or in the place where you have always worked among the youth. You chose the latter, and you ask my opinion on this.

My opinion is the same as yours, as I have said in the past: working with the youth is an influence on the building of their future homes in Israel. That is to say, any change in the recipient is a change in the foundation of the home he will build in its time and season, and his entire future is before him. But together with this, it would be worthwhile to contemplate and find some way for you to have an influence in Be’er Sheva as well. For all matters are by *Hashgacha Pratis*, and when it becomes known to a Jewish person that there is a place where he can exert influence, the very fact indicates that he must exert influence there. Especially given what you mentioned, that they requested you for this purpose—meaning that the connection to this is strong to the extent that even the other party, who possesses free choice, feels it (and as is explained in *D'ch* that choice—free choice—is only for *Bnei Yisrael*, who are a "part of G-d above," where the place of choice and the true reality exist. Since their reality is from His Essence and is not caused by any prior cause, *chas v'shalom*—for this is the primary and true reality—it is understood that their choice is truly free. This is not the case for all who come after this, except for the part of the Essence; for when you grasp the part, you grasp the Essence, and *kal v'chomer*). And even if it is impossible to give frequent and fixed *shiurim* there, surely one can find a way to visit at set intervals—once every two or three weeks. The lecture then would be comprehensive and general, in a way that provides material for thought and study until the time of your next visit. The main thing is to provide a way to maintain a connection in writing or through visits to you, and thereby it will be within your power to influence them in the main thing—*maaseh b'poel*—similar to what you write that you have arranged regarding *iyunim*.

Regarding what you wrote that you yourself are in need of "good taste and knowledge"—surely you also know that no one is exempt from this need. This is similar to what is recounted in the *reshimos* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, printed in the notes to *Tanya* regarding the study of *Tanya* and the *Chumash*: that the greatest of the great and the smallest of the small are equal in this, that each has a share in them, and one is already found not at the beginning, and none of them have reached their ultimate goal. I believe I have already written to you that this is the *psak din* in *Shulchan Aruch*: that every Jew is obligated in the *mitzvah* of *tzedakah*. It is simple that this does not only refer to *tzedakah* of physical silver and gold, but also regarding spiritual silver and gold—which are *ahavah* and *yirah*, and the things that lead to them. These are *hisbonenus*, of which the *ohr* and *yirah* are the offspring, and the main thing is the results of this: the 248 positive *mitzvos* and 365 negative *mitzvos*, as explained in *Tanya*, beginning of chapter 4.

Regarding what you wrote about a *Tanach* quiz—in my opinion, the ground is already prepared for you to suggest that they organize a *Tanach* and *Mishnah* quiz together. The chances are good that it will be accepted, since everyone prefers a *chiddush*. A *Tanach* quiz has already taken place, and although they naturally did not exhaust the matter and there is still room to build and add, when they add *Mishnah* together with it, both will be upheld. And in the style of "from *lo lishmah* [one comes to *lishmah*]," one should emphasize the value of the language of the *Mishnah*, its eternity, and the fondness that exists for it even among those circles, etc., and also emphasize the aforementioned point that there is a *chiddush* and addition in this.

My inner intention in the above is understandable enough, as I wrote at the beginning of my letter, for this is a stage closer to action. And another inner point in this, according to the explanation of our Sages regarding the difference between the Written Torah and the Oral Torah, is that it is specifically through the latter that "we are distinguished from all the peoples of the earth" (*Shemos Rabbah*, ch. 47:1). Especially since the previous *Tanach* quiz left an entirely undesirable impression due to the participation of missionaries, etc., and a word to the wise is sufficient.

Incidentally, to the above, one should add another point that will be of interest: regarding the first *Seder* of the *Mishnah*, which is *Seder Zeraim*, which is made in the style of the world now, most relevant through the settlement of *Bnei Yisrael* in the Holy Land, *tizkeh l'mitzvos*, in the field of agriculture, etc.

As is customary in such things, surely the aforementioned quiz will be connected with the distribution of prizes, and in this there are matters of publication. It is understood that this too can be made into a channel for introducing true *Yiddishkeit* into the circles about which you write.

It was pleasant for me to read in your letter what you write about your son and daughter, may they live. May it be His will that you derive from both of them together *Yiddishe nachas*, which is physical and spiritual *nachas* combined.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6540

6540

B"H, 6 MarCheshvan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive the news regarding the first meeting of the new year, may it come upon us and upon all of Israel for good.

May Hashem Yisbarach help that this beginning should be in a good hour, and that you should continue with success in your highly important work throughout the entire year,

And in accordance with the demand of our holy Torah, Toras Chaim, in a manner of *ma'alin bakodesh*—ascending in holiness—increasing in this work in all respects.

For this is also the sign of vitality: to grow and to ascend.

And may Hashem Yisbarach help that the work should proceed without difficulties, and be done in good health and good spirits by each and every one of the members and her entire family—*shichyu*.

In accordance with the instruction of Rabbeinu HaZaken that one must live with the times—with the *sedra* of the Torah that is read this week—

I conclude with the theme of today's *sedra*: the blessing that Hashem Yisbarach gave to Avraham Avinu, and through him to his children and children's children, Jews wherever they may be:

"And I will bless you, and make your name great, and you shall be a blessing" (*Bereishis* 12:2).

And as our Sages, of blessed memory, explain the content of "and you shall be a blessing": to bring the distant ones closer, to purify them, and to bring them to our Father in Heaven (*Bereishis Rabbah*, Chapter 39:11).

And it is self-understood that this also follows the primary and literal meaning of the verse: to be blessed in everything one needs, both *b'gashmius* and *b'ruchnius*.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6540***

A similar form was sent to the branches of Agudas N'shei U'Bnos Chabad.

## 6,541

B"H, 6 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the 1st of Cheshvan and the one preceding it, in which you write about matters that you perceive as unusual, and it seems that a thought is creeping in that perhaps this is connected to the matter of the school named after my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Yisrael. In this regard, the saying of our Alter Rebbe is also applicable: to live with the parsha of the week, as our Sages explain regarding the verse "And there was a famine in the land" —see the commentary of the Ramban there.
