The Giver of the Torah and the mitzvos, who sustains and provides for the entire world in His goodness, grace, kindness, and mercy, will surely fulfill His promise: "If you walk in My statutes (and our Sages explained this to mean that you should be toiling in Torah) and keep My commandments, etc., then I will give, etc.," with all the blessings enumerated in the parsha, and success in all that a person requires.

May it be His will that you succeed in your studies, in the study of Torah and the fulfillment of mitzvos, and that you go from strength to strength. It is also my firm hope that, in the end, your parents will also derive nachas from your conduct, and may it be His will that this happens soon.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6,529

B"H, 4 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, involved in communal needs,
R' Michael Aryeh Yehuda Leib, may he live, HaKohen

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 27 Cheshvan and those preceding it, I am sure you have already received my letter of response to your wife, may she live and be well.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the matter of plowing during the seventh year, which is the Shmittah year:

It is understood that it is alarming that a Jewish person would involve himself in this, for even those who permit [certain agricultural activities] did not permit work such as this to be performed by a Jew. The fact that so-and-so is involved in this—to our great sorrow in our era of double and redoubled darkness—is, by that logic, grounds to transgress, G-d forbid, the entire Torah.

Many thanks, many thanks for your blessing at the conclusion of your letter. Surely the promise of the Creator of the world will be fulfilled: "And I will bless those who bless you," as the great virtue of this is explained in the maamar entitled *Amar Rabbi Yitzchak*, "Every Kohen who blesses is blessed" (*Sefer HaMaamarim 5629*, which has been published recently. See there).

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S.
Surely you are preparing a detailed plan for utilizing the days of Kislev in general, and the bright day of 19 Kislev in particular, for the dissemination of the wellsprings—until they reach even the "outside."

## 6580

B"H, 5 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Moshe, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 2 Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption.

It gave me pleasure to read in your letter that a committee has been established with the goal of strengthening and expanding the education of children and youth in your area on the basis of *taharas hakodesh*.

May it be the will of Hashem that every member of the committee, and indeed all those who fear the word of Hashem in your surroundings, work toward this stated goal with *mesiras nefesh* commensurate with the magnitude and importance of the matter, and in a manner that is constantly increasing. This is especially pertinent as we approach the auspicious days of 10 and 19 Kislev—the redemption of the *Toras HaChassidus* and all its matters—and subsequently the days of Chanukah, whose essence is an increase in light, "going and increasing" and enduring forever (as per the commentary of the Ramban at the beginning of *Parshas Beha'aloscha*). Certainly, this is an opportune time to increase in "the candle is a mitzvah, the Torah is light," and the "light of the Torah"—which is *Toras HaChassidus*.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

P.S.
Regarding what you wrote about a visit from Paris to your camp in connection with the auspicious day of 19 Kislev, you should contact them directly at the address provided.

## 6,581

B"H, 6 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After a long hiatus, your letter dated Wednesday was received, containing the good news regarding the receipt of the funds from the restitution payments.

May it be Hashem’s will that just as you bring good tidings regarding matters that are inherently physical, you should bring good tidings regarding matters that are inherently spiritual. Furthermore, may this extend to the very purpose of the soul’s descent—that physical matters should be transformed into vessels for spiritual matters, until a semblance of the reward of the future is revealed, where the soul will receive its vitality from the body, and likewise, in a general sense, spirituality will be drawn from physicality.

In connection with the above, I must remark on the inappropriate style of the beginning of your letter—where you write to announce that you received such-and-such a sum, purchased machinery, etc., and paid a down payment on a house, etc., and that you hope to take out an additional loan for the house, and nothing more regarding this matter.

May it be Hashem’s will that in your next letter, the style will be that since you received such-and-such a sum, it is self-understood that you immediately set aside a maaser for tzedakah. Moreover, based on what is explained in Iggeres HaKodesh, which is founded upon the ruling of Chazal, you added to this allocation such-and-such a sum, and your hand is still outstretched. And furthermore, in accordance with what is explained and stated, "four parts shall be yours" (see Likutei Torah, Shir HaShirim 6, 4)—that with a portion of this money, you purchased machinery and also made a down payment, etc.

It was pleasant for me to read in your letter about the dissemination of the takanah to recite Tehillim on Shabbos Mevarchim, before davening. May it be Hashem’s will that soon there will also be a full minyan for this, as is explained in Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 23, that in every matter of holiness, it is a superior thing to have a group of ten.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

## 6,582

B"H, 6 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rav, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, involved in communal needs,
R' Efraim Eliezer HaKohen, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I was already concerned by the absence of your letter, as is your custom on the eve of Rosh Chodesh Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption. It seems the reason for this delay is the content of your letter, which was received just now, regarding the state of your health.

May it be the will of Hashem that just as you report an improvement in this regard, the situation will continue to improve until you return to your full strength in every respect. See also the *maamar* "Maskil L'Eisan" by our Alter Rebbe (printed in the *Kuntres Limud HaChassidus*).

Thank you for what you wrote at the end of your letter regarding the content of the dream. May it be the will of Hashem that from this month—the third month from Tishrei, which corresponds to the "third month" beginning from the month of Nissan, the *nigleh* of Torah and the *pnimiyus* of Torah—a threefold blessing will be drawn down for each and every one, within the "third people" (the Jewish nation), in the three matters of children, life, and sustenance, and may there be abundance in all of them. After all, we are currently in a *shanah temimah* (a complete year), a leap year.

This is especially so according to what is explicitly stated in the Rambam’s commentary on the Mishnah, *Erchin* 9:3, although for the time being, I have not found a source that corroborates this.

With blessings for good tidings, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed in all the aforementioned matters.

***

**R' Efraim Eliezer:** Yalles, Philadelphia. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 17, 6,449, and the references in the notes there. Below: 6,662; 6,700; 6,752; 6,831; 6,886.

**What is explicitly stated in the Rambam’s commentary on the Mishnah:** "Rabbi says: For what is stated in the Torah, 'a complete year,' refers to a solar year, which is 365 days, etc."

## 6,583

B"H, 6 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Following a very long hiatus, your letter of 28 Cheshvan was received, in which you write that you have accepted a teaching position at an educational institution dedicated to pure holiness.

May it be the will of Hashem that you succeed in your teaching, and most importantly, that this study fulfills the teaching of Chazal: "Great is study, for it leads to actual deed," in the daily lives of the students, and that through this, there will be an increase in matters of yiras shamayim, even regarding their parents, may they live.

Regarding what you wrote about your davening, that the minyan where you daven rushes excessively:

The custom in such a situation is to begin the davening with the tzibbur, and afterwards to daven according to your ability—that is, with kavanah according to your ability. If your davening takes longer than the tzibbur, the essence of davening is the focus and the kavanah. By beginning together with the tzibbur, you achieve, in this regard, a semblance of davening with the tzibbur, even if it is, of course, only a semblance.

As for your question regarding skipping sections of Pesukei D’Zimra: although many permit this in order to daven with the tzibbur, in addition to the fact that there are opinions that it is forbidden, even those who permit it do not hide the fact that skipping is improper. This is especially true according to what is explained in the seforim of Kabbalah and Chassidus, which are too numerous to count. Therefore, as mentioned above, you should do what is in your power—that is, begin your davening with the tzibbur and afterwards daven in order, with an understanding of the words, etc. The great reward for one who directs his heart to Heaven is already well known, and this is sufficient for the wise.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6584

6584

B”H, 6 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, occupied with communal needs, etc., R’ B.A. [Gorodetzky], may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

The esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, a man of fine character, etc., R’ Gershon Mendel, may he live, Gerlik, is prepared to depart in these coming days for Milan. It has transpired that he will need to obtain his visa for Italy in one of the countries along his route—namely, London or Paris.

Therefore, I have advised him to choose Paris, in order to utilize this opportunity to speak at length and in brief with you, and to receive your detailed instructions regarding his work in Milan.

Now, if it were at all possible, it would be most appropriate if you, too, would travel with him to Milan to introduce him to his community. It is certainly understood by you that such a gesture would surely establish and strengthen the entire endeavor, with the help of Hashem.

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

On the day of R’ G.M.’s [Gerlik] departure from here to Paris, send a telegram regarding this...

#### ***6584***

**R’ B.A.:** Gorodetzky, Paris. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. 17, 6364, and the references in the notes there.

**Prepared to depart:** See below, letter 6614.

## 6,585

B"H, 7 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After a long interval, your letter from Rosh Chodesh Kislev was received, in which you share the good news that you have been approved for a good apartment.

May it be Hashem’s will that it be in a good and auspicious hour, and may it broaden your mind, as per the well-known teaching of our Sages (Berachos 57b), in a true sense of expansion. May you soon share good news regarding this expansion of mind through the three matters mentioned there, as explained in the Tikkunei Zohar (Tikkun 6, see there).

It is most astonishing that you do not mention anything regarding your activities in the holy work of *hafatzas hamaayanos*. Furthermore, you write explicitly in your letter that "there is nothing more to innovate," as if we were already in that era of which it is said, "a Shabbos and **rest** for eternal life." If only we had at least reached the level where we go "from strength to strength" in matters of holiness—and not as in our current period, which is one of double and redoubled darkness, where one must still struggle with the line of "turn from evil." And this is understood.

And although it has already been said that "there is nothing new" under the sun, and that one should not innovate, it has already been refuted by our Sages in many places—among them the Tanya, beginning of Chapter 40—that this applies specifically "under the sun," as mentioned above, even regarding matters of holiness, even *d'oraysa* and that which is learned with toil. However, the study of Torah according to the instruction of *Toras HaChassidus*, which is above the sun, certainly allows for—and indeed requires—innovation. Moreover, this is the true task of every single one of the children of Israel. There is no need to elaborate on such a simple matter. May you share good news regarding this, in a manner that is "increasing and continuing." We are approaching the auspicious days, the bright days of Yud and Yud-Tes Kislev, and the days of Chanukah that follow immediately thereafter, where the *halacha* follows Beis Hillel: to increase and continue from day to day.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6,589

B"H, 7 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the avreich Yehoshua Moshe Yosef
and the entire family, shlita,

In response to your letter from Rosh Chodesh Kislev.

I was shocked to hear the news a few days ago regarding the terrible tragedy that has befallen you with the passing of the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, a man of character and many accomplishments whose work was work for the sake of Heaven, R' Yisrael Yitzchak Zelig, a"h.

May it be the will of Hashem that from here on out you know no further sorrow, G-d forbid, and that you may find only goodness and kindness, in a way that is good and revealed, speedily and in our days.

And since consolation includes matters of rectifying the past—the order of which should be twofold—may it be the will of Hashem that this also apply to your situation, and that matters of goodness and delight increase and continue to grow (as is explained in other places, that the concept of "twofold" in this regard is far greater than merely double in quantity).

I was pleased to read in your letter about your work in teaching at the yeshiva in Kfar Chabad, where "Hashem commanded the blessing." May it be the will of Hashem that you engage in this with the greatest possible dedication and self-giving; through this, there will also be great nachas ruach and merit for the soul of the departed, and it will certainly serve as an added blessing and success in all your affairs and in the affairs of the family in general.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6,589***

**Yehoshua Moshe Yosef:** Halperin, Kfar Chabad.

## 6,587

B"H, 7 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 4 Kislev, in which you write that you are seventeen and a half years old and wish to continue your studies even after marriage, but are worried because the expenses of an apartment after marriage are very high, and therefore the idea has arisen that you should travel abroad, etc.

It is surely a great wonder that you are worried **now** about how you will manage **after the wedding**, and worried to such an extent that it disturbs your *hasmadah* and *shekidah* in your studies!

If you ask for my opinion, you should study in one of the yeshivos that are on the purity of holiness in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) with the greatest *hasmadah* and *shekidah*, while dismissing all thoughts that distract from the study of Torah and from *avodas Hashem* through *tefillah*. It is understood and also simple that in your study schedule there must be a fixed *shiur* in the study of *pnimiyus haTorah*, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus [which, especially in our time, is a vital necessity—this study, and with consistency, not only in time, but—and this is the main thing—consistency in the *nefesh* as well, as is explained at length in the *Kuntres Eitz Chaim* by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, based on the *maamarim* of our Sages in several places; and see the wonderful and astounding words in the introduction of Rabbeinu Chaim Vital, student of the Arizal, printed in the additions to the aforementioned *kuntres*]. The *hasmadah* and *shekidah* in the aforementioned study will also increase the blessings of Hashem in general, and in particular regarding what is needed for you and for all those you write about, who will be remembered at the *tziyon* of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

You surely know of the three *shiurim* of *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*, and at the very least, you should observe them from now on.

## 6,588

B"H, 8 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Kislev, regarding your questions:

a) How to conduct oneself with those who speak against the study of Chassidus, etc.

In your current situation and standing, you should not enter into arguments with them at all. Instead, you should increase your consistency and diligence in your studies, both in Nigleh and in Chassidus. The promise is well-known: even a little light pushes away much darkness, and certainly a great deal of light.

b) Regarding your question about the study of the teachings of Chassidus.

It is a great wonder that you ask this, after it is clearly written in the holy Zohar and explained in the introduction to *Shaar HaHakdamos* by R. Chaim Vital, the student of the Arizal, and in the teachings of our Rebbe HaZaken, and also in the collected writings of the Gra of Vilna [ftnref_63637_#&a], that the study of the inner dimension of Torah is **mandatory**. In our generation, this has been revealed only through the teachings of Chassidus. It is known what R. Chaim Vital wrote in the name of the Arizal: that the delay in this study prolongs the Galus and delays the Geulah. See there for the startling words (in the introduction to the aforementioned *Shaar HaHakdamos*).

c) A Tikkun for the blemish, etc.

First and foremost, you must stop thinking about this for the time being and fix your thoughts on the letters of Torah and Tefillah. Furthermore, be careful with *tevillas Ezra*, and every weekday morning, set aside a few coins for Tzedakah. Also, keep to the established *shiurim* of Chitas (Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya).

d) How to arrange your studies going forward.

It is understood and simple that you must study within a framework that includes both the study of Nigleh and the study of the inner dimension of Torah, in accordance with the words of the Arizal and R. Chaim Vital mentioned above, which are founded upon the holy mountains, the words of the holy Zohar—among them, Zohar Part III, 124b, 285b, and in many other places.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

***

**And also in the collected writings of the Gra of Vilna:** See also above, Vol. 17, Letter 6,586, and the references in the notes there.

## 6,589

6,589

B"H, 8 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 6 Kislev, regarding your questions:

1) How to conduct oneself with those who speak against the Torah of Chassidus, etc.

In your current standing and situation, you should not enter into arguments with them at all. Instead, you should increase your persistence and diligence in your studies, both in Nigleh and in Chassidus. The promise is well-known: even a little light pushes away much darkness, and certainly a great deal of light does so.

2) Advice on how to keep one's thoughts concentrated on study, so they do not wander here and there.

The well-known advice is to study from inside the sefer, and also to daven from the Siddur, as the letters hold the thoughts in place.

I am sure you are aware of the three daily shiurim—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and in any case, you should keep them from here on out.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6,590

B"H, 8 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 3 Kislev, in which you outline the main points of your current life situation.

It is understood, and indeed simple, that what you write—that you are unable to participate in a shiur or study holy sefarim—is not correct at all. After all, the teaching of the Mishnah is well known: one who fulfills the Torah out of hardship is guaranteed that, in the end, he will fulfill it in comfort. Since this is required of you, it is certain that you have the ability to fulfill the requirement, for the Ribono shel Olam does not make unreasonable demands of His creations.

Nevertheless, it is appropriate—in addition to the above—that you memorize a few chapters of Mishnayos, or at the very least, one chapter of the Tanya Kadisha. While traveling to your place of physical livelihood and upon your return, you should fill your time with Torah by reciting these chapters by heart, or at the very least, by reviewing them in your thoughts.

It is also appropriate that every weekday, before davening Shacharis, you set aside a few coins for tzedakah, as this is also a segulah for the expansion of one’s livelihood in the literal sense. Furthermore, you should maintain the takanah to recite the monthly Tehillim shiur—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—every day after morning davening.

It is my hope that you will participate in the farbrengens of anash during the month of Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption, and especially on the auspicious day of 19 Kislev, the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all the above.

6591

6591

B.H., 8 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the distinguished, G-d-fearing, learned, and possessing noble character traits, scion of a holy lineage, etc., R’ Shazar, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I trust you have received the personal regards I requested be conveyed to you by the distinguished, G-d-fearing, learned, and active communal worker, a man of energy, etc., Rabbi Binyamin Eliyahu Gorodetzky, and the distinguished, G-d-fearing, learned, and active communal worker, etc., Rabbi Dovid Chanzin.

It was with sorrow that I learned that the state of your wife’s health, may she live, was not as it should be. It is my hope that the medical treatment has been successful and that her peace of mind has been restored, for I was also informed that you were in a state of sorrow and mental distraction—

Now, the matter of sorrow and mental distraction is a contradiction to the entire life of a person, insofar as he is a person. In the words of our Sages, of blessed memory: one should not stand to pray while in a state of sadness, and the same applies to discussing Torah law. Yet, the entire life of a Yid on this earth consists of tefillah and Torah. Tefillah is an elevation from below to above, reaching the point of connection, dveikus, and unification with the Creator. Torah is an extension from His Essence and Being, drawing down holiness and goodness from above to below. The purpose of both is that they be "in a congregation" (b’tzibur), as explained in Iggeres HaKodesh by the Alter Rebbe, Epistle 23. The inner meaning of this, as taught by the Alter Rebbe, is known: tefillah b’tzibur means to gather all the clarifications and elevate them through tefillah (a letter from the Rebbe Rayatz, printed in Kovetz HaTmimim, Issue 2, p. 46).

From this, one can also understand the concept of "congregation" as it pertains to Torah.

A person, being an intellectual being, must certainly conduct all his affairs with intent and for a significant purpose, gathering his actions—his deeds, speech, and thoughts—and the powers of his soul toward a lofty goal. That is to say, elevating them from below to above, and subsequently drawing down into them a supernal light from Heaven, even greater than what is within the power of a human being to attain through his own strength.

The teachings of Chassidus come to unite and unify Torah and tefillah through the instruction that tefillah comes after contemplation of the greatness of Hashem, in the manner of study and comprehension within the Chochma, Binah, and Da’as of the soul—"Know the G-d of your father"—such that the tefillah is a continuation of the verse, "And serve Him with a whole heart." Similarly, regarding the study of Torah, one precedes it with ahavah and yirah—love and fear of Hashem—which come as the results of the avodah of tefillah. At that point, the Torah unites the person who is studying with the Giver of the Torah, reaching the level where "Yisrael, the Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, are all one." And there is no joy greater than the joy of the unification of the created being and the Creator—a twofold joy: the joy of the Creator and the joy of the created being, as explained in Tanya, Chapter 33.

With respect and blessings for good tidings in a revealed and manifest way regarding all the above.

Surely, as has been your good custom for many years, you will visit Kfar Chabad on the auspicious day of the 19th of Kislev, which marks one hundred and sixty years since the day of the redemption of our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe. With him came the redemption of his Torah—Chabad Chassidus—and all those who walk in its ways and paths, they, their children, and their children’s children, until our generation: "We who are all here alive today" (Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 27).

## 6,592

B"H, 8 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter dated Tuesday, regarding your brother. Indeed, I have learned that he has accepted a contract for a rabbinical position at a shul in New York. However, I must ask his forgiveness, as I must say that I received this news with a mixture of sorrow and astonishment.

For one such as him, my sorrow is surely understood, in line with what I discussed with him regarding [the individual] shlita—and how much more so do these matters apply to your honor, and to an even greater degree.

For in [the city mentioned], the state of Yiddishkeit depends upon a select few individuals, and it is liable to collapse, chas v’shalom, even with the departure of a single person from there. Public success there requires unique conditions that cannot be recreated through a few months of preparation. This is not the case in New York, where the situation is the polar opposite.

It is possible that from the perspective of a private individual, New York has more advantages than other places, in that there are a greater number of chareidi people here, and so on. But even if you were to say that this is true one hundred percent, in the style of our Sages, "Between an individual and the many, the halacha follows the many" (the interests and gains of the individual versus the interests and gains of the many). However, in the present case, I do not see any doubt even regarding the gains of the individual. For while in his current location he is one-of-a-kind, which naturally provides him with unique strengths, possibilities, standing, and status, here in New York—even with the fullest appreciation of his talents—there is competition from many others. Especially since not everyone delves into who excels over whom within the circle of rabbis, who all appear similar on the surface.

I will add one more point: knowing the mindset of the Jewish public in the United States, even in the realm of influencing American Jewry, one can exert more influence through a visit as the Chief Rabbi of [the city mentioned] than as a local rabbi, even in one of the most prominent shuls.

In truth, I wanted to write all of the above to your honor when I first received the news regarding the offer of a rabbinical position at a shul in New York, but I refrained, in accordance with the well-known adage transmitted in the name of the Alter Rebbe: "One does not force advice upon someone." But upon receiving your honor’s letter, which also included this news, I saw it as my duty—from which I have no justification to exempt myself—to express my clear view that it is proper for him to remain in his current position. May it be the will of Hashem that these words have their intended effect, for the priority belongs to the demands of the many—the members of his current community whom he has led on the path of Torah and mitzvos, and whose future conduct surely depends to a most significant degree upon his influence and guidance. And even if a young, energetic, and G-d-fearing rabbi were to be found, it is a known ruling of our Torah that "not from everyone is a person privileged to learn," and in a case of "certainty versus doubt"—especially the certainty of the many versus the doubt of the individual—the certainty takes precedence.

With respect and blessings for a good decision in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6,592***

**Well-known adage:** See also above, Vol. 13, Letter 4,727, and the references in the notes there.

## 6,593

9 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 6/11.

I read with great astonishment that you are no longer studying in yeshiva, even though you are only 20 years old. It has been explained in the Seforim Hakedoshim how vital the study of Torah is throughout a person’s entire life—and all the more so during the years when one does not yet bear the yoke of providing for a family. As the well-known statement of the Mishnah says, "If not now, when?" Regarding the question of one's purpose, there is still plenty of time, for ultimately, one’s livelihood depends on Hashem Yisbarach, who alone sustains the entire world with His goodness, grace, kindness, and mercy. He, Blessed be He, has commanded that one should study Torah and fulfill mitzvos. Even when the time comes that one must occupy oneself with a trade, it is sufficient to spend a few hours learning that trade before marriage, while the remaining time can still be spent studying in yeshiva.

What you write about studying electronics at home is incomprehensible, as such study is connected with various machines and instruments that can only be used in a school; perhaps "electronics" is not the correct term.

At an auspicious time, you will be mentioned at the Tziyun Hakadosh of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, as will your mother, tichyeh, for whatever she is in need of. Naturally, any increase in matters of Torah and mitzvos by any member of the family—and especially by you, who are making this request—will add to the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach in the areas where they are needed.

It would be a proper thing for you to observe the takanah of reciting the monthly Tehillim shiur every day after davening in the morning—as the Tehillim is divided according to the days of the month—and to give a few cents to tzedakah every weekday before davening. Your mother, tichyeh, should observe the good custom of the daughters of Israel to give to tzedakah each time before lighting candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6494

B"H, 9 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of November 18, in which you write about the difficulties you are experiencing with your speech.

Generally, this stems from the state of one’s nerves; therefore, it is advisable that you consult with a specialist physician regarding this and follow their instructions. From the above, it is understood that as you strengthen your bitachon in the Creator of the world and its Guide—who oversees every individual with hashgacha pratis, and therefore there is no place for worry, agitation, and the like, for Hashem stands over him and guards him, and Hashem is the essence of good, and it is the nature of the good to do good—your agitation and fear will diminish, and through this, your manner of speech will also improve.

Furthermore, among the technical pieces of advice for this is that if you feel that a word you are about to say is difficult for you to pronounce, do not try to force yourself to say it right then. Instead, start from the word or two preceding it, or speak about a different subject; when you let go of the tension, an improvement naturally occurs.

And since for every matter one needs siyata d'shmaya, you should keep the practice of reciting the monthly Tehillim—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—every day after morning davening. Also, every weekday before morning davening, set aside a few coins for tzedakah, and have your tefillin checked to ensure they are kosher according to halacha.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
In my office, we do not write in French, which is why this response is in Lashon HaKodesh. However, you may continue to write in French, as I am able to read that language.

## 6,495

B”H, 9 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R’ Meir Shechna, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

My secretariat has conveyed to me the contents of your letter and your activities regarding the matters of Taharas HaMishpacha in your camp, as well as the fact that you stem from a distinguished family, the Reich family from Warsaw, and that your grandfather was the brother of the esteemed R’ Meshulam Reich, of blessed memory.

May it be the will of Hashem that the rock from which you were hewn add vitality and inner enthusiasm to your soul—as it does for every Jew—in a way that will bear fruit in matters of Yiddishkeit, Torah, and mitzvos in general, and specifically in spreading them throughout your surroundings wherever your reach extends. For in our times, it is the holy duty of every single person to disseminate Torah and mitzvos throughout their entire environment, in a manner that leads to actual practice. This is the whole of man, as it is written: “Fear G-d and keep His mitzvos.” We have been promised by Chazal, “If you have labored and found, believe it.” This is especially true since you are in a place where, according to reports, there are currently not many who fear G-d and tremble at His word, which only increases the value of each individual’s efforts. Surely, the Holy One, Blessed be He, who creates the world and leads it, provides the ability and the opportunity to fulfill this great mission.

Since you stem from a family of Chassidim and are a relative by marriage to the families of our Rebbes, the Nesi’im, it is my hope that you have fixed times for the study of Chassidus, in addition to your fixed times for the study of the revealed Torah.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all that has been mentioned, and with regards to all your colleagues who are engaged in the holy work of strengthening the pillar of Taharah in the camp. May one mitzvah lead to another in all matters of the mitzvos of our holy Torah.

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita.

***

**R’ Meir Shechna:** Frankel, Wellington.

## 6,496

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding the issue of the separation, although my hope is strong that you continue to work on this with the energy appropriate to the importance of the matter. For in this regard, the situation is as mentioned above—that is to say, if they do not separate today, it is possible that by tomorrow, literally, it will already be, etc. Surely, for someone like you, there is no need to elaborate on this.

It is understood that I am also aware of the arguments that one should not cast aspersions on the Children of Israel, etc. But it has already been said that even regarding this, it is stated, "Do not be overly righteous." For our Sages—and of each one of them it is said that he is a lover of Israel (for they are connected from generation to generation, all the way back to Moshe Rabbeinu, who was a lover of Israel)—nevertheless warned about the aforementioned, and in expressions that are like the piercing of a sword, and the wise will understand.

Regarding your notification concerning the condition of your wife, may she live and be well: May the Ribono shel Olam fulfill the days of her pregnancy properly and with ease, and may she give birth to a healthy child in its proper time, properly and with ease.

With respect and blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6597

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...It is understood that my intention is not to cry over the past or to cause anyone distress, but rather to awaken [you] for the future and to point out—as is visible even to the physical eye—that one of the most reliable conduits and vessels for matters of material livelihood is involvement in matters of spiritual livelihood. On the contrary: material livelihood is in the hands of Heaven, as in the text of the Grace After Meals, which is *min haTorah*: "He who sustains the entire world in His goodness, with grace, with kindness, and with mercy" [though man must make vessels within nature]. This is not the case with spiritual livelihood, which is entirely in the hands of man, for "everything is in the hands of Heaven except for the fear of Heaven." It is said, "See, I have placed before you [life and good, and death and evil]," and nothing more—the "and you shall choose" is dependent upon man [though one is granted assistance from Above, as "he who comes to purify himself is assisted"].

May it be the will of Hashem that very soon we hear good news regarding a significant shift in the above.

Especially as we approach the auspicious days of Yud-Tes Kislev—the victory of the light of the teachings of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus—and the days of Chanukah that follow, whose essence is to increase light from day to day. For the *halacha* is according to Beis Hillel, that one increases and continues, through the "mitzvah candle," the "Torah light," and the "light of Torah," which is the teachings of Chassidus and its matters.

With blessings for good news in all that has been mentioned.

## 6,198

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, the content of which is a question upon which there are apparently differences of opinion between you and your wife, may she live and be well.

Regarding your question specifically, and regarding such questions in general, where the customs of communities differ—and in a city like New York, where there are many communities, each following its own custom—the instruction of our Sages is well-known: in a place where there is a Rav, one follows the Rav.

You should turn to a Rav who is a *moreh hora'ah* in the community to which you belong, and he will instruct you.

There are matters in which all Bnei Yisrael are equal, and I will take this opportunity to urge you concerning one of them: the necessity of studying *pnimiyus haTorah*, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus.

Even though there are many who do not yet do so, there are several reasons for this, but in any case, this does not in the least diminish the necessity of setting times for the study of this part of Torah for all those to whom the report of the teachings of Chassidus, its practices, and its guidance has reached. And you are certainly among them, since you have turned to me in your letter. See *Hilchos Talmud Torah*, Chapter 1, end of Halacha 4, by Rabbeinu HaZaken—the author of the *Tanya*, the *posek* in the *nistar* of Torah—and the *Shulchan Aruch*—the *posek* in the *nigleh* of Torah—as well as the introduction of Rav Chaim Vital to *Shaar HaHakdamos* and the *Kuntres Eitz Chaim* by the Rebbe (Rashab) *n.e.*, who explained at great length the necessity of studying the teachings of Chassidus. May it be His will that, at the very least, from here on you will establish set times for this, and as is the saying of Rabbeinu HaZaken: "Establishment" (*kevi'us*) not only in time, but also "establishment" in the soul.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
You surely know of the *takanah* to recite the monthly *shiur* of Tehillim—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—every day after *davening* Shacharis. In any case, you should observe this from here on.

## 6,599

6,599

B”H, 13 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In due time, I received the news regarding your state of health and the doctors' opinion. It was pleasant for me to learn subsequently that the surgery was successful and that your health is improving. May it be the will of Hashem that this be followed by many more blessings of a complete and speedy recovery.

This is especially pertinent as we are currently in the days of the month of Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption, leading up to the bright day of 19 Kislev—the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus. This is followed, in close proximity, by the days of Chanukah, where the halacha is like Beis Hillel, that one adds and increases in light until Zos Chanukah, which is the illumination of the essential taanug, etc., a continuation from the "seven days of Atik" to the revelation of Malchus—for this is Malchus, as is known (see the maamar beginning *Baruch She’asah Nissim*, which is based on the maamar of the Alter Rebbe, the master of the celebration of 19 Kislev; his Chanukah maamar, with additional notes by the Tzemach Tzedek, was printed in the year 5711).

With respect and blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6363

B"H, 13 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 8 Kislev and its enclosure, in which you write about your state of mind and health, and that confusing thoughts, etc., are occurring.

The advised counsel in such a case is well known: *hesech hada'as* (diverting one’s attention). It is understood that *hesech hada'as* does not mean to fight against these thoughts, for the very act of fighting a matter is the opposite of diverting one's attention from it. Rather, it means to pay no attention to the thoughts at all. Since the power of thought is always wandering, *hesech hada'as* from any given thought is achieved by thinking about a different matter—one that is not of that category at all. How much better it is to do so with a matter of Torah, for then the principle is fulfilled that "a little light pushes away much darkness," and all the more so with a great deal of light.

Based on what is explained in many places (Likutei Torah by the Alter Rebbe, Vayikra [30d, 52d], and elsewhere) that matters engraved in the memory are constantly with a person—and furthermore, they influence the person, albeit as a *makif* (encompassing force)—it is appropriate that you know by heart some chapters of Mishnayos, or at least one chapter of Tanya, and from time to time repeat them in speech or thought. Above all else, there is the *hisbonenus* (contemplation) regarding the concept of *hashgacha pratis* (Divine Providence) according to the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov: that it applies to every individual down to the finest details of their life. Since this providence is from Hashem, who is the essence of good, and it is the nature of the Good to do good, this contemplation will certainly result in true joy and the negation of sadness, as it is written, "Hashem is with me, I will not fear." These matters are also explained in the Tanya in many places, as indicated in the index of subjects in the Kehot edition of the Tanya.

On the occasion of the bright day of 19 Kislev that is approaching, I will conclude my letter with a blessing for good tidings in all that was mentioned, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed. Especially since Divine Providence has placed you in a position of light, to occupy yourself with true good—good for Heaven, good for people, and good that produces fruit (Kiddushin 40a)—and the measure of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is many times over, to bestow upon you as well, good in abundance.

With respect and blessing for good tidings.

## V'Tira

B"H, 14 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

The Administration of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch
Detroit Branch
May Hashem grant them life

Shalom u'Bracha!

It was with pleasure that I received the announcement regarding the first farbrengen being organized by you—on the auspicious day, the upcoming 19th of Kislev.

I hereby send my blessing that it should be successful, and lead to constant success in your work in the field of chinuch al taharas hakodesh.

True and constant success in this means the dissemination of authentic Yiddishkeit, the Torah of Hashem and His mitzvos, in the daily lives of ever-expanding circles.

And we "bring about merit through a meritorious day"—the day of the Geulah, 19 Kislev—so that from the farbrengen on this day, there will be an increase in the energy, vitality, and light of your branch's work, and that this increase will lead to a manifold increase in all matters of proper chinuch throughout the entire year.

The significance of this festival of the Geulah is well-known among the Bnei Yisrael. Its celebration each year, and now, one hundred and sixty years later, must remind and awaken each and every one of us to the necessity—especially in these days of ours—to serve Hashem through the study of His Torah, both the revealed and the inner dimensions—Chassidus—a study that unites all parts of the soul, the revealed and the inner—and the fulfillment of His mitzvos with vitality and enthusiasm.

These are founded upon the love of Hashem, the love of Torah, and the love of every single Jew, which are all one—

As explained at length in the teachings of our Rabbeinu HaZaken, the Baal HaTanya and Shulchan Aruch—the Torah whose righteousness was brought to light and revealed to the eyes of all on the 19th of Kislev.

With blessings to each and every one of the participants in the farbrengen, and to each and every one of those taking part in your holy work in matters of chinuch al taharas hakodesh.

#### ***V'Tira***

Printed in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 18, p. 232, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

## 6,202

B"H, 14 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

To the Administration of the Machon L'Hotzaat Sefarim named after the Chasam Sofer,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge with thanks receipt of the books published by you, which you sent here.

May it be the will of Hashem that you go from strength to strength in magnifying Torah and making it glorious—both the revealed Torah and the inner dimension of Torah—for they walk together; indeed, the Torah is a complete Torah, which in our generation has been revealed through the teachings of Chassidus.

It is explained in many places how essential this study is, as it leads to ahavas Hashem and yiras Hashem, and also serves as a preparation for the study of Torah in general. As the Gemara states (Pesachim 50b), "Great is the Torah... [but only] for those who engage in it lishmah." And one who engages in it lishmah does so out of love for the Master of all the earth (Rambam, end of Hilchos Teshuvah; see there).

It is also well known that the conduct of the Chasam Sofer in this regard was to preface his Torah study with a matter of Aggadah and to extend his davening. Indeed, davening comes specifically before study, as per the request of Abba Binyamin (Berachos 5b) [and regarding the difficulty raised by Tosfos there, see Likutei Torah by our Rebbe HaZaken, Devarim 92b].

With respect and blessing.

6363

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, man of action, distinguished scholar, R' Menachem Tzvi, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

It is with pleasure that I acknowledge receipt of your book—the first volume of the Ramban’s commentary on the Torah. Many thanks.

May it be the will of Hashem that you soon celebrate the completion of the remaining volumes, for all of Israel to see, to explain that which Moshe (ben Nachman) has done. May it also be that this work—an elucidation of the Torah of these Rishonim, who synthesized the *gali* and *satim* of the Torah—will hasten the expansion of the dissemination of the *ma'ayanos* outward. And, in the style of the Ramban at the end of his commentary on the Torah, may this be established and seen before the eyes of all the people, and may the prophecy be fulfilled: "For He will bring that which our heart asks" (and see the *maamar* in *Zohar Chadash*, *Bereishis* 8a; see also *Zohar* Vol. I, 253a; *Sanhedrin* 99a, in Rashi there, regarding the matter of the *ketz*—that it is hidden from the heart, from above, and from the *gali*, but it is not revealed to the mouth) speedily in our days.

With respect and with blessings for success in the aforementioned.

P.S.
As a token of affection, I have transcribed here a few notes, written in the margins of the book, albeit in haste.

**Opening. 1a.** *Ha-El... v'ha-nora* (The G-d... and the Awesome). — Note the reference in *Yoma* 69b: Moshe said "Ha-El... v'ha-nora."

*B'eimah... b'zie b'mora* (With awe... with trembling, with fear). — In accordance with the language and the instruction of our Sages (*Berachos* 22a): "Here, too, with awe... and with trembling." It is highly probable that he also holds that this is in the secret of the four letters of the name *Havayah* (as written in *Likutei HaShas*—from the Arizal—to *Maseches Moed Katan*). The addition of **"b'mora"** does not contradict the above, for in many matters of the *Toras HaSod*, five elements are specified in the name *Havayah*: the four letters and the *kotzo shel yud*. He chose this manner here as well—to meet the requirement of the rhyme of this introduction, the movement of **"rah."**

**By the way:** In all the editions of *Likutei HaShas* that I have seen, there is a clear omission in the aforementioned *maamar* (before the words "malbush hachitzon"). Furthermore, it is puzzling that the aforementioned *Arizal* was included in the *Likutim* for *Moed Katan* and not for *Berachos*.

**1b.** I noticed that you have appended the verses *Tzerufah...* to the opening (as you call this beginning).

With your permission, in my humble opinion, it is clear that they are an introduction to the introduction. The opening concludes with the subject of the *Geulah*, as is customary in many books (and as is the conclusion of the Ramban’s commentary on the Torah as a whole).

**By the way:** In the facsimile of the Adler manuscript at the beginning of your book, there is an addition at the end of the opening: "Amen... v'ametz." This also proves that the verses continue into what follows them.

**Introduction. 1c.** Its beginning—it seems to have been influenced by the beginning of the Rambam’s introduction to his book.

**There:** *Im ha-Torah kulah* (With the entire Torah) — perhaps he comes to negate the distorted interpretation in *Megillah* (31b) that [Moshe wrote it] from his own mind.

And in *Zohar* Vol. III (261a): "The *Mishneh Torah*—Moshe said them from his own mind." See further on: "And let the matter of *Mishneh Torah*... not be difficult for you." Note also *Bava Basra* 15a: "Yehoshua wrote them..."

Regarding this *Zohar* passage on *Mishneh Torah*—see also *Ohr HaChayim* at the beginning of *Devarim*; *Likutei Torah* of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, *Parshas Bamidbar* (17c), and references there.

**Introduction, at its end. 1e.** A *Sefer Torah* in which one letter is wrong... is invalid. — This is in *Tikkunei Zohar* 425. It is cited there in *Responsa Avnei Nezer*, *Yoreh Deah*, section 371. I do not have it at hand. See *Shulchan Aruch*, *Orach Chayim*, section 143, subsection 4.

**Commentary, at its beginning. 1b.** He thinks that the world is eternal... entirely. Note the reference to *Moreh Nevuchim*, Part II, chapter 25.

## 6404

6404

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, etc., R' Nosson Dovid, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

... Regarding what you wrote concerning the matter of "opening with a jug (*kad*) and concluding with a barrel (*chavis*)" — see *Ohr Torah* by the Maggid of Mezritch at the end (in the *Remzei Torah* on the *Aggados Chazal*). Also, note the introduction to *Tikkunei Zohar* (14a), where the *kad* is the *Shechinah Tatta'ah* (the lower *Shechinah*). And in *Zohar Chadash* (245b), the *chavis* is the *Shechinah Ila'ah* (the higher *Shechinah*), *Malchus* and *Binah*. See also *Likutei Torah* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, at the beginning of *Shir Hashirim*. And this is not the place to elaborate.

#### ***6404***

**R' Nosson Dovid:** Rosenblum, Buenos Aires. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 17, 6350, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6,305

B"H, 18 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Tziporah, may she live,

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of 12 Kislev, in which you write that you have scheduled your medical treatment for the auspicious day of Zos Chanukah.

May it be Hashem’s will that it be successful. In the days leading up to it, may you remain confident in Hashem, the Healer of all flesh who performs wonders, that He will send His word and heal you through this specific treatment and this specific doctor. May you return to your full strength and increase your vigor in the dissemination of Yiddishkeit and its matters, and specifically in the central point: chinuch al taharas hakodesh. Divine Providence has granted you the ability and the opportunity to accomplish great things in this area, together with your husband, may he live and be well.

Regarding what you wrote at the end of your letter, that you are prepared to take upon yourself any additional responsibility:

It is understood that the aforementioned—[chinuch]—is the most important task, one that requires the investment of great strength. Nevertheless, since you wrote those lines—which surely also came about through Hashgacha Pratis—I wish to emphasize the great importance of working within Agudas N'shei U'Bnos Chabad and the affairs of Kfar Chabad. There are several reasons for this, one of which is that, after all, this is the source from which your husband’s [spiritual] roots were hewn. The home—that is, the mistress of the house and the members of the household—must serve as a living example of a Chabad home, one of whose foundations is the dissemination of the wellsprings of Chabad wherever their reach extends. It is a known tradition from our Rebbes that when one truly engages in this, they succeed beyond the natural order.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S.
As you requested, this response is being sent without waiting for the regular turn.
I trust your husband, may he live and be well, has already received my letter of response.

## 6176

B"H, 18 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your notification regarding your birthday:

You should certainly follow the custom of Anash in recent times regarding one's birthday. May it be the will of Hashem that this be a year of success for you in the study of Torah and the performance of mitzvos with hiddur.

With blessings for Torah study with yiras shamayim.

Regarding what you wrote about reducing your nigleh studies to fit the yeshiva schedule — it is understood that you should not do so. Rather, you should study Chassidus during all your free time. May you succeed in both nigleh and Chassidus.

***

**6176**

**To reduce:** In order to add to the study of Chassidus.

## 6,707

6,707

B"H, Motzaei Shabbos, Parshas Vayishlach, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Committee of Kfar Chabad,
May Hashem keep them and grant them life,

Peace and Blessing!

Transcribed below is the text of the telegram that the Rebbe shlita was pleased to send tonight in connection with the upcoming farbrengen of 19 Kislev:

To all participants in the farbrengen of the Day of the Light and the Luminary, 19 Kislev, shlita.

I join your farbrengen with the hope and prayer that the hisorerus, vitality, and light will be drawn down throughout the entire year, and at the central point: to disseminate, with abundance in both material and spiritual matters, the wellsprings of Chassidus and a Chassidic way of life throughout the entire Holy Land. May it be fulfilled regarding the Kfar and its affairs, and regarding all the affairs of the Alter Rebbe, "And you shall spread out to the west and to the east and to the north and to the south." And may the prophecy (Michah 2:13) be fulfilled very soon: "The breaker—Moshiach—shall go up before them; they shall break through—the exiles—and pass on, and Hashem shall be at their head." Lechaim, lechaim and blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

The telegram was surely received in due time. And surely you will inform us here of what is done, and the more detailed the report, the more praiseworthy it is.

With respect and blessing,

Secretary

#### ***6,707***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 15, p. 521.

## 6108

6108

B"H, 19 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To our brothers, the Children of Israel,
Lovers and cherishers of Torah and Mitzvos,
May Hashem keep them alive.

Greetings and Blessings!

On this luminous day of 19 Kislev, the day of the liberation of the Alter Rebbe, the Baal HaTanya and Shulchan Aruch, who illuminated the eyes of Klal Yisrael with a double light—*shnei-ohr*—the light of the revealed Torah and the light of the inner dimension of Torah, I turn with my call to all those who hold Torah and Mitzvos dear, in connection with the annual celebration of the central Yeshiva, Tomchei-Tmimim Lubavitch, which is scheduled to take place, G-d willing, on Sunday, 2 Shevat, upcoming.

The concept of 19 Kislev, which brought about the liberation of the Alter Rebbe and the expanded flourishing of his *shita*—which relies upon the great principle that **Ahavas Hashem, Ahavas HaTorah, and Ahavas Yisrael are all one**—reminds, demands, and encourages every one of us regarding our obligations to ourselves and to Klal Yisrael. These are obligations that must be expressed in *maaseh b’poel* (actual deed), and in an ever-increasing measure.

In the Lubavitcher Yeshivos and their center—the central Lubavitcher Yeshiva Tomchei-Tmimim in Brooklyn, which my father-in-law, the Rebbe, transplanted here upon his arrival in America nineteen years ago—generations are being raised who are permeated with Ahavas Hashem, Ahavas HaTorah, and Ahavas Yisrael, to the point of *mesiras nefesh*.

It is a holy obligation upon all Jews, lovers of Torah and Mitzvos, to support the Lubavitcher Yeshivos. Especially now, in light of the annual celebration, it is a duty to participate and help ensure the success of this annual undertaking, which holds such significant importance for the strengthening and further development of the central Yeshiva Tomchei-Tmimim Lubavitch in Brooklyn.

The *zechus* of the *baal-hasimcha* of this day, and the *zechus* of our Rebbes, the *Nesi’im*, his holy successors—may the merit of the righteous protect us—will surely stand by every one of the supporters, *shichyu*, in whatever they are in need of. May it be for them: *Padah b’shalom*—redeemed from distress through the merit of *tzedakah*, which brings peace to the world.

And every good action in this regard, in a manner of *mosif v’holech* (ever-increasing), will bring down the blessings of Hashem in an ever-increasing measure as well.

With blessings for all good, in body and soul,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***6108***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 18, p. 260.

## 6909

B"H, 20 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt—in due time—of your letter of blessing for the thirtieth anniversary of our wedding day.

In the style of our Sages—the matter has already been stated, that whoever blesses is blessed with the blessing of Hashem, as it is written: "And I will bless those who bless you."

It is explained that the blessing of Hashem possesses a wondrous quality, in that it draws down a new *ratzon* and the most sublime blessing.

This is explained in many places, including in the teachings of *Chassidus* in the *maamar* beginning with the words "Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi..." [regarding] "I will bless those who bless you" (*Sefer HaMaamarim 5629*).

With blessings for good tidings in the near future.

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***6909***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 20, p. 573. A similar version was sent to several individuals.

## 6,510

6,510

B"H, 21 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs,
R' Avraham Dober, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

I wish to thank you for presenting me with the book *Toldos R' M.A. Kramer*, a biography of your father, *a"h*, in Yiddish and English.

Surely this book is found among all the family members, and especially among the younger generation. As explained in many places—and it is also understood logically—the documentation of the homes of previous generations, and memories of the past in general, is not intended merely to tell a beautiful story about a fine person. Rather, the intent is that it should awaken and elicit in the reader—especially when read by a family member—a strong desire to walk in the path paved by the subject of the memoirs, and in general to conduct oneself in that way. In doing so, one causes *nachas ruach* to his *neshamah*. If this is expected of everyone, it is certainly expected to a much greater degree from the family members.

Seeing how you and your brothers, may they live, take part in all the matters that were so dear to the subject of the memoirs, your father, *a"h*, this serves as an iron bridge upon which your children and your brothers' children can also travel. According to the principle that "the deed is the essential thing," it is vital that the grandchildren of the subject of the memoirs follow in his path, supporting all the matters and institutions that were so dear to him, and in general, being a living example in whom the *dyukno* (image) of the subject of the memoirs is reflected. It is well known what *Chazal* say: if one truly desires, one succeeds.

With blessings for a happy Chanukah, to you and your brothers and your families, may they live.

Letter just received.

#### ***6,510***

**R' Avraham Dober:** Kramer, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him: above Vol. 2, p. 410; Vol. 4, 895; Vol. 6, 1,543.

## 611

611

B”H, 21 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Kislev, in which you write regarding the health of the children, may they live, and conclude with a question as to how to act regarding a food that is not *mehudar* (not of the highest standard of kashrus/refinement), noting that this food might increase the children’s appetite.

In general, *hiddur* in food (as explained in many places) is relevant not only to the observance of the mitzvah itself; rather, since every food eventually becomes the blood and flesh of the body, which in turn connects with the *neshamah*, the kashrus and refinement of the food affect the character and *midos tovos* of the person who consumes it. Therefore, every addition in the *hiddur*, refinement, and purity of the food should also be viewed as an addition to the formation, delicacy, and refinement of character and *midos*. If this is true for adults, it is true to an even greater extent for children, whose character is currently in the process of formation. Therefore, the direction in which one leads them in this regard is of the utmost importance.

Based on the above, my opinion is understandable: since this does not involve, *chas v’shalom*, a matter upon which their entire health depends, and it is nothing more than adding to the taste and strengthening the appetite, one should not diminish the *hiddur* of the food. Certainly, one can find other advice and methods to strengthen the appetite and make the children healthier, without doing so at the expense of kashrus and *hiddur*.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

#### ***611***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 7, p. 299.

## 6,412

B"H, 21 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Yosef Chaim, may he live, HaKohen

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 13 Kislev, and with it, the cane was received.

May it be the will of Hashem that in your activities, you and your wife, the Rabbanit, may they live, will succeed with great success in leading with a "staff of pleasantness" (*makel noam*). This is especially true as this is the Chabad approach, and it is also consistent with the Holy Epistle of our master, the Baal Shem Tov, that the spreading of the wellsprings outward is the preface, preparation, and vessel for the matters of Moshiach. As is explained at length in the *sicha* of Simchas Torah 5690 by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, and as is explained in many places [among them, in the *hemshech* of *VeHecherim* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe Maharash], the concept of Moshiach is leadership with a "staff of pleasantness." From this, it is also understood that this is the core of the leadership of our Rebbes, the Nesi'ei Chabad, and of all those who go on their mission to spread the light of the *Maor* and the wellspring in the places to which they have been sent.

May it be the will of Hashem that all the above be in a manner of "increasing and continuing." The strength for this is also provided by the days of Chanukah, for the *halacha* is according to Beis Hillel, that one increases from day to day, and in the matters of the "oil of the secrets of the secrets of the Torah," the Tree of Life, from which the light is drawn into the entire world and all its affairs, and this is well understood.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and a happy Chanukah.

Surely you are aware of my custom, *bli neder*, to refrain from accepting gifts. Therefore, I am enclosing a check in exchange for the aforementioned.

Use it in good health.

## 6,613

B"H, 21 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Yehoshua, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Isru Chag of the Geulah, 19 Kislev, containing a report of what was done in connection with this auspicious day.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that the influence of this day be drawn down throughout the entire year, and into one's daily life.

At a time of favor, please mention your wife and your young son, Yaakov Dovid—may he live and be well—for whatever they may need, in accordance with the content of your letter.

With the blessing of a happy Chanukah,

On behalf of the Rebbe, may he live,
Secretary

Regarding what you wrote concerning the order of naming—it has already been noted in the parsha from which we follow (Vayikra 38:3-5): "And he called," "And she called," and "And she called." See our Sages in the Ba'al HaTurim and the commentary of the Ramban there.

***

**R' Yehoshua:** Yozewitz, Jerusalem. Another letter to him—above, Vol. 15, 5,608.

## 6414

6414

B”H, 26 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R’ Gershon Mendel, may he live,
And his wife, the Rabbanit, Mrs. Batya, may she live,

Peace and blessing!

With pleasure, I received your letter of 21 Kislev, in which you write the main points of your activities in Paris and during your travels.

It is surprising that you do not mention your arrangements in Milan, aside from a few words regarding the journey; surely you will fill this in at the next opportunity.

May Hashem, blessed be He, help that it should be with great success in all details, utilizing to the fullest extent the blessing and success granted from Above, and may you do so with joy and a glad heart, in the manner of the instruction of the days of Chanukah, for we are commanded to increase in candles and light from day to day. I await good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

With a blessing for outstanding success, good tidings, and "the more one details, the more it is praiseworthy..."

And with a blessing for a happy Chanukah.

Enclosed is Chanukah gelt, and it is as if this is also money in the physical sense. May it be the will of Hashem that it be a "complete shekel" in the *shekel hakodesh*, as explained in *Torah Or* and in many places in the *maamarim* on the topic of *Tisa Es Rosh*.

## 6159

B"H, 28 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Parshas Vayishlach, which was received in due time, in which you write regarding the state of your health and the results of the treatment.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you, as well as your family members—may they live and be well—at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, each according to their needs, as appropriate to the content of your letter.

Some time ago, I drew the attention of your father-in-law, shlita, when he wrote to me regarding your health, that it is very proper and highly beneficial to establish a time for studying the "light of the Torah"—which is the inner dimension of the Torah that has been revealed in our generation through the teachings of Chassidus—on the holy Shabbos, which is sanctified to Hashem, as well as several times during the week, and how much better if it is done every day. It is explained in many places how this study is essential, especially in our current era. Furthermore, regarding you personally, the study of the "light of the Torah" adds (in the measure of the Holy One, blessed be He, which is "measure for measure" and even more) to the light of a person's eyes.

May it be His will that you not only set aside times for the aforementioned, but that you also increase in them, in accordance with the commandment to "increase in matters of holiness." This is the mitzvah and concept of Chanukah, for the miracle occurred with oil—the "secrets of secrets of the Torah"*—which increases and continues until it becomes very great.

With blessings for health and for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

*) See Devarim Rabbah 7:3: "The Torah is compared to five things... to honey and milk, as it is stated: 'Honey and milk are under your tongue' [the secrets of the Torah—Chagigah 13a; and according to the version of the Rambam, Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, end of chapter 2]... to oil... just as oil is superior to all other liquids..."

## 6156

B"H, 28 Kislev, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing individual, who engages in communal needs, Moreinu HaRav Moshe, may he live, HaKohen.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Monday, along with the enclosure, and as per your request, I am enclosing the receipt for the contribution.

Since we are currently in the days of Chanukah—where the Halacha follows Beis Hillel, that we increase and continue to add from day to day in light and flame—and since the miracle occurred through the Kohanim, and the candles are never nullified, may it be G-d’s will that you likewise increase, going from strength to strength in the "candle of mitzvah and the Torah of light," and in the "light of Torah." For the miracle occurred through the oil, which represents the secrets of the secrets of the Torah, and in all that is necessary for the "candle of Hashem, the soul of man."

With blessings for a happy Chanukah and for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6,517

B"H, the fifth night of Chanukah, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished [individuals], etc., R' Chaim Yosef, may he live, and his wife, Mrs. Itte Rachel, may she live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I was pleased to receive greetings through the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who is occupied with communal needs with energy, etc., R' Aryeh Leib, may he live, Kramer, that you have had a new Sefer Torah written and have dedicated it to the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva in Montreal.

It is superfluous to emphasize the importance and holiness of the mitzvah of writing a Sefer Torah, especially dedicating it to a place where they know how to appreciate the holiness of a Sefer Torah, which is the holiest thing that Jews possess.

This is, after all, the essence of the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva — to raise students who will know how to appreciate the holiness of the Torah, the Giver of the Torah, and the holy nation, and to integrate within themselves Ahavas Hashem, Ahavas HaTorah, and Ahavas Yisrael in their daily lives.

May the merit of this holy mitzvah stand by you, such that *mitzvah goreres mitzvah* — one mitzvah should lead to another in an increasing measure — and may this bring you and all your family members the blessings of the Ribono shel Olam in an increasing measure, as is indicated by the Chanukah lights, which are kindled in a manner of **mosif v’holech** (increasing and continuing), to bring ever more light of Torah and mitzvos into the Jewish home and the surrounding environment.

With blessings,

## 6,618

6,618

B"H, Fifth Night of Chanukah, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned man, a man of many talents and knowledge, a man of fine character, etc., R' Tzvi, may he live, known as Dr. Harkavy

Peace and blessing!

I read your letter of the 22nd of Kislev with great astonishment, in which you write as if there has been a change in my perspective regarding traveling on Shabbos on Israeli ships.

It is surely understood and self-evident that there is no room to change my opinion in the slightest. As I have explained at length in my previous letters to you, my opinion is clearly and absolutely that it is forbidden, and the reasons and arguments for this are explained in those aforementioned letters.

Regarding what you write about technical arrangements, etc., I have already written clearly on this as well: given the current state of technology, it is impossible in any way for a ship to be operated "automatically" in the true sense of the word—meaning, that it would not require human intervention throughout the entire twenty-four hours of the holy Shabbos day. Furthermore, it is not enough to have one or two people; rather, many people are required, and a significant portion of them must be expert engineers of a quite high level.

To my great regret, I must point out a change for the worse that has occurred regarding this issue. According to reports that have reached me, it happened several times this past summer that several of these ships sailed even on the holy Shabbos day itself (not from the New York shore). Furthermore, there are plans in the near future to arrange a **pleasure** trip (a "cruise," in the vernacular) lasting about two weeks. As I hear it, those participating in these trips will include the *mashgichim* for kashrus and those who lead the davening, etc. To tell the truth, I have not investigated the veracity of these rumors, because, as mentioned above, my opinion is clear that even when sailing on the first day of the week, the matter is absolutely forbidden. I have only mentioned this change to point out the trend of events, which, to my great regret, is not in the desired direction of improving the situation, but rather in the opposite direction. May Hashem enlighten the eyes and hearts of Yisrael.

With respect and blessing.

***

6,618

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 17, p. 642, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

## 6,319

B"H, 1 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs, R' Dovid Nosson, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

Before my departure to the holy gravesite, I received the telephone message and the names, and I mentioned them yesterday, Erev Rosh Chodesh Teves, at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding the month of Teves, our Sages taught that in this month "the body derives pleasure from the body." This is understood according to the teachings of our Sages that "Achashverosh" refers to the Ribono shel Olam—He who is the "end" (acharis) and the "beginning" (reishis) (as brought in *Meorei Ohr* and in the commentary of the Rema on Megillas Esther). "Esther" refers to Knesses Yisroel—every single one of the Bnei Yisroel. This means that the Atzmus and Mahus of the Ein Sof, Baruch Hu, derives pleasure from the essence and the inwardness of every single one of the Bnei Yisroel. This is specifically in the month of Teves, which is associated with the "other side" (sitra achra), as explained in the holy Zohar (Vol. III, 12a; Vol. III, 259a; Zohar Chadash, Eichah 92d). Through their avodah, Bnei Yisroel transform darkness into light and bitterness into sweetness, which is the purpose of the creation of all the worlds, as explained in the holy Tanya, Chapter 36, that the Ribono shel Olam desired to create a dwelling place for Himself.

May it be His will that all the blessings which Bnei Yisroel bless one another be fulfilled in their entirety, and in a manner that is "increasing and continuing," just as the instruction of these days of Chanukah—increasing and continuing, until it becomes very great.

With blessings for a happy Chanukah and for good tidings.

Regarding what was written about Moshe and Aharon—who is in Netzach and who is in Hod—see *Etz Chaim*, Shaar 32 (Illumination of the Mochin), end of Chapter 6. And in Zohar Vol. III, 27a, in the glosses of the Maharach Vittal there.

***

6,319

R' Dovid Nosson: Lesser, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him—see above 6,195, and the references in the notes there.

Regarding the month of Teves: See also above, Vol. 14, Letter 5,041, and the references in the notes there. Below, 6,335.

## 6220

B"H, 3 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Mr. Yitzchok, may he live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of 19 Kislev, along with the enclosure, and the receipts are enclosed herewith, in accordance with your instructions.

I was particularly delighted to read in your letter about your visit to Kfar Chabad in our Holy Land—may it be built and established by Moshiach Tzidkeinu speedily in our days—and about the satisfaction you derived from your visit and the impression it made upon you.

And merit is brought about through a meritorious day, the bright day of 19 Kislev, the day of the redemption of the founder of the Chabad philosophy, whose cornerstone is that love of Hashem, love of Torah, and love of a fellow Jew are all one. And in continuation of the above regarding Kfar Chabad, it is well known how highly the Alter Rebbe spoke of the matter of tzedakah for our Holy Land, as is evident from several of his Igros Kodesh printed in the Tanya, and specifically in Iggeres HaKodesh, end of section 4, where he writes: "And especially the tzedakah of Eretz Yisrael... and it is this which has stood for us to redeem the lives of our souls..."

With respect and blessing for good news always, all the days.

Please convey my regards to your daughter and son-in-law, may they live, who visited me together with you.

#### ***6220***

**Mr. Yitzchok:** Rabinowitz.

## 6211

B"H, 3 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 1/21:
