1) Regarding what you wrote concerning your sister, may she live, and the family dynamics in her home: In such matters, it is tangibly evident that the words of friends and acquaintances are more effective than those of relatives. Therefore, you should ask friends and acquaintances to speak about this, and it is understood that this should be done in a way that she does not know the request came from you.

It is also understood that your sister should not be particular, even about matters where she believes she is one hundred percent in the right. Peace is great, especially *shalom bayis*, and it is worth it to overlook one’s personal feelings and exercise restraint, if only to maintain peace.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that she should increase her observance of the laws of *taharas hamishpachah* (niddah, *hefsek taharah*, immersion in a kosher *mikveh*, etc.), for closeness that is not according to the law leads to distance. I hope that you will find the appropriate words to explain the above to your sister, may she live.

2) Regarding what you wrote about forgetfulness: Among the advised methods for this is to change the subject of study from time to time—that is, not to study one topic or in one style for hours on end. Rather, after studying for about an hour or half an hour, switch to a different topic or a different style of study, such as alternating between *bekius* (breadth) and *iyun* (depth), and so on. Another piece of advice is to write down the content of what you are studying or an outline of the main points.

Since one needs *siyata d'shmaya* (Heavenly assistance) for everything, your mother, may she live, and your sister should maintain the custom of the daughters of Israel to set aside coins for *tzedakah* before lighting candles every *Erev Shabbos* and *Erev Yom Tov*. They should also have the *mezuzos* in their homes checked to ensure they are all kosher according to the law, as well as the *tefillin* of the husband, may he live. You (the writer) should set aside a few coins for *tzedakah* every weekday before morning *davening*.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all of the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe,

Secretary

## 622

622

B”H, 3 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 23rd and 24th of Kislev, detailing your holy activities over the past weeks, as well as the farbrengens held during those times.

May it be Hashem’s will that the impression of these farbrengens be evident in the days that follow, for that is the very purpose of a farbrengen, and this is well understood.

At an opportune time, you should mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, each according to their needs, as appropriate to the content of your letter.

Regarding what you wrote—that sometimes when speaking with others, you hear words that are not at all desirable, etc.:

The established practice in such matters is well known: one should not enter into arguments at all. Instead, simply present to them what is discussed in our holy Torah. Those who will listen, will listen. If the listener has questions, or is looking for reasons to argue, you can direct them to the address of the Rabbonim and elders of Anash in the holy city of Jerusalem—may it be rebuilt and established—and they can turn to them.

Regarding the content of your letter of the 25th of Kislev, my letter to your father, shlita, has surely been received by now.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6263

B"H, 3 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

After the longest of pauses, your letter from Tuesday—the day that was doubled with "it is good"—has been received, along with your questions:

1) Regarding outreach to others to bring them closer to our Father in Heaven, to His Torah and His mitzvos:

If only each of us would utilize **all of our free time**—beyond our set study sessions—for this holy work, we would accomplish great and wondrous things. This does not detract from the performance of a mitzvah; on the contrary, the Mishnah rules that one mitzvah **drags** another along with it.

2) Based on the above, the answer to your second question is also understood. You mention feeling nervous because you feel the weight of your study schedule and personal avodah as a burden on your mind. Aside from the fact that this is the opposite of the proper manner of avodas Hashem [as it is explained in many places that it must be done specifically with joy and a good heart, and it is codified as halacha in the Rambam, end of Hilchos Lulav], there is no reason to be nervous. There is enough time in the day to maintain your study sessions, your personal avodah, and so on. In fact, this approach actually helps one utilize time properly, allowing one to grasp the material being studied with greater ease.

3) Regarding kavanah in tefillah: You are correct that there is no single piece of advice that works every time, as it depends on a person’s mood, which changes from time to time. Nevertheless, there are general points that are helpful at all times. Among them is giving tzedakah before tefillah, as our Sages said (Bava Basra 10a), "Rabbi Elazar would give a coin to a pauper and then pray," and as it is written, "I will behold Your face in righteousness (tzedek)." Similarly, be careful with the immersion of Ezra, and keep your Siddur open in front of you at the place where you are davening. This means that even if you are davening with your eyes closed due to depth of thought or hispaalus, the very fact that the Siddur is open to that place holds (halt tzu) your focus.

4) Regarding guarding one's speech and strengthening the quality of bitachon: A piece of advice that serves both is the contemplation of how Hashem stands over him and examines his kidneys and heart, etc., as explained in the holy Tanya, Chapter 41. It would be appropriate to know that chapter by heart, from the beginning until the word "the King" (hamelech) on the second page. From time to time, review it.

5) Regarding what you wrote—that you might fall and not rise, G-d forbid: It is understood that this is the opposite of our Torah, the Torah of life. It is explained in many places that the performance of a mitzvah refines the person performing it, even if he does not always feel it. In truth, even through simple logic, this is understood: by performing a mitzvah, one fulfills the will of the Creator, and this connects the person with the Creator. How could it be that this does not have its effect? To what extent does this reach? Even regarding those who are in the category of whom it is said, "What right have you to recite My statutes?" our Sages commanded that a person should always study. This is explained in the Hilchos Talmud Torah of our Rebbe, the author of the Tanya (who rules in the hidden parts of Torah) and the Shulchan Aruch (who rules in the revealed parts of Torah), in Chapter 4, subsection 3, and in the Kuntres Acharon there. See the wonderful things written there, and in the Tanya in many places, as indicated in the index at the end.

6) Regarding your question at the end of your letter, about your study partner with whom you have a set time to learn: you fear his influence on you, even though you see clearly that you are influencing him for the good.

In such situations, it is appropriate to share with him what you have learned in the books of Chassidus and mussar without entering into arguments. In this manner, you should certainly continue the study session.

I hope you participated in the farbrengen of the auspicious day of 19 Kislev, and may it be Hashem's will that the inspiration continues throughout the entire year.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6284

B”H, 4 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the honorable Rabbonim, the Dayanim,
Members of the Beis Din Tzedek of London and the country,
May Hashem preserve them and keep them alive.

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 27 Kislev, regarding the letter now being sent from Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) concerning the registration of children of non-Jewish mothers, etc.

It is understood and quite simple that you are correct in saying it would be highly desirable for the position of the Rabbonim being consulted to be uniform and consistent in its foundation, etc. However, it is surely known to your honors that there are difficulties inherent in such a matter in general.

Regarding the matter mentioned, it appears that several of those consulted have already responded and sent their answers there; if so, there is no room for the suggestion you mention in your letter that perhaps it would be worthwhile to refrain from answering altogether.

However, I must add that even if the matter were in the initial stages, my opinion is clear that one must respond to the question. Aside from my general approach—that in such matters, "sit and do nothing" can lead to entirely undesirable results, and if only those who fear the word of Hashem would take an open stance on every phenomenon in the religious life of our people, the Bnei Yisrael, wherever they may be, many absolutely undesirable matters would be avoided—there is an additional, primary point regarding this matter: the very act of turning to the Jews abroad negates, from here on, the argument heard in many circles that Jews abroad should not express an opinion on matters concerning our Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established).

May it be the will of Hashem that from here on, the avodah of each and every one, within the entirety of Klal Yisrael, will not necessarily be limited to the line of "turn from evil," but rather to the line of the right, "do good," to increase light—and there is no light other than Torah and its mitzvos, as it is said, "For a mitzvah is a candle and Torah is light." This is the instruction of the days of Chanukah, where we proceed in a manner of "increasing and continuing." Even though we are in a generation of sunset, where "darkness covers the earth," a double and redoubled darkness, we have been promised that these candles will never be nullified, for "ours is the victory." In place of the situation where they call darkness light and light darkness, "the night will shine like the day," and in the language of the Zohar, "the transformation of darkness into light and bitterness into sweetness." The Bnei Yisrael will go out openly to defend the Torah and its mitzvos, until even the people who walk in darkness will see a great light, the light of Hashem.

With respect and blessing.

As you requested, the letter is being sent as soon as possible, fast and urgent.

***

**With the letter now being sent from Eretz Yisrael:** See below, letters 6214-15.

## 6225

B"H, 5 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write that you are sometimes afraid of lightning and the like:

You should memorize several chapters of Mishnayos and at least one chapter of Tanya. From time to time, you should review them while walking in the street or similar situations. Since the words of our Torah—which was given by Hashem, the Creator of the world—will be engraved in your mind, this will increase the blessings of Hashem and His protection in all your affairs.

Regarding what you wrote about having difficulties in studying Tanya and the like, you should ask your Rebbe or your mashpia in Chassidus to explain the concepts you find difficult.

The main thing is that you should study with consistency and diligence, in accordance with the schedule of the yeshiva and the instructions of your teachers, and then you will succeed.

You surely know of the takanah to recite the monthly Tehillim portion every day after davening Shacharis; you should be careful to keep this.

With blessings for good news regarding all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 626

B"H, 5 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the fifth night of Chanukah.

It is **obvious** that the content of my letter, to which you are responding in your aforementioned letter, was not, Heaven forbid, intended to cause anyone distress. Rather, it was intended to clarify the matter as I see it, regarding those points that concern conduct in the present and the future.

It is likewise obvious that it was not my intention to instruct anyone that they must live in a state of *tzimtzum* (constriction). That is not my role at all, especially in light of the well-known statement of our Sages that the world was not worthy of using gold except by virtue of the fact that gold is necessary for the construction of the Beis HaMikdash. The same applies to every Jewish home, regarding which it is said, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them"—within each and every Jew. Any existence of abundance in the world exists by virtue of the fact that it is appropriate for every Jewish home. While, of course, this must be without a pursuit of physicality and material expansion, the thing itself must be so, similar to the words of the Rambam at the end of *Hilchos Teshuvah* and the end of *Hilchos Melachim* regarding conduct in the days of Moshiach concerning physicality.

What was my intention, however, was to urge involvement in the Chabad institution in your camp. My opinion on this is absolute: this is the channel for material sustenance as well for *anash* who are there. On the other hand, I emphasized the necessity of minimizing worry regarding *parnassah* (sustenance). While, of course, there must be *hishtadlus* (effort) in this, it should not be in a manner of worry; rather, on the contrary, it should be from a position of strong *bitachon* (trust) in Hashem, who sustains the entire world in His goodness, with grace, kindness, and mercy. When one acts with such trust, the action is more effective and successful. Furthermore, it also adds to the health of the body and its response to the surrounding situation and to the *hishtadlus* in matters of *parnassah* in particular—which is not the case when one is burdened by worry for *parnassah*, and in the manner of, etc.

May it be the will that, at the very least, from here on in, things will be in the manner of the instruction of the days of Chanukah, where we go from them "increasing and adding" in light. May it be so also in general matters and in private matters, and may you bring good tidings of revealed and manifest good.

With blessings for good news in all that was mentioned.

## 627

B"H, 6 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of the Beis Rivkah Institution,
May Hashem keep them alive.

Peace and Blessing!

Transcribed below is the text of the telegram that the Rebbe shlita was pleased to send:

My blessing to all the participants in the Beis Rivkah celebration in Montreal. May it be the will that the celebration be successful and lead to the establishment and development of this holy institution, to fulfill its purpose and role: to educate and raise a generation of daughters of Yisrael—each of whom is called a daughter of Sarah, Rivkah, Rachel, and Leah—who are worthy of their names, that they may build and continue to build the house of Yisrael.

With respect and blessing for success,

**Menachem Schneerson**

I am certain you will inform us here of what transpired, and the more detailed, the better.

With respect and blessing,

Secretary

## 6228

B"H, 6 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Teves, in which you write about your davening and the state of mind associated with it, and how this affects the limitation of your parnassah, etc.

We have heard several times a story from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that it was the custom of the early Chassidim to divide their davening into several parts. That is to say, each time they would, of course, daven the entire davening, but the hisbonenus regarding the matters and the intent of the meaning of the words—or the explanation thereof—they would divide over several days, such that over the course of a week or two they would complete the explanation of the words once, and then begin again. Therefore, you too should act in this manner. Given your situation, you should arrange it so that on weekdays, the time for your davening in the morning is approximately two hours from beginning to end.

Regarding what you wrote, that sometimes you repeat and double a word—the advised counsel for this is to daven from inside the Siddur. In any case, this is the proper way, and it is advice for many matters.

It is my hope that every weekday, before Shacharis, you set aside a few coins for tzedakah, and also that after davening, as well as on Shabbos and Yom Tov, you keep to the monthly Tehillim schedule—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—to recite it. It is also appropriate that your tefillin be checked, as well as the mezuzos in your home, to ensure they are all kosher according to the law.

It is also appropriate that you memorize from the holy Tanya, from the beginning of Perek 41 until the second page, the word "HaMelech." When you contemplate the content of the matter—that Hashem stands over him, and He is the essence of good, and He has promised that one who comes to purify himself is assisted, and that when a person sanctifies himself a little below, they sanctify him much from above—surely your worries will diminish and decrease until they are nullified entirely. After all, we have been commanded to serve Hashem with joy and gladness of heart specifically, as is explained in many places.

Since you are writing to me, it is my hope that you have fixed times for the study of Chassidus as well, which is what brings vitality and joy even to the study of the revealed parts of Torah and the fulfillment of mitzvos.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***6228***

**Divide over several days:** See also below, letter 6255.

629

629

B"H, 6 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch
in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established,
May Hashem grant them life.

Peace and Blessing!

A note has been received here from Anash in Eretz Yisrael, stating that since some of the Reshet schools are located at a distance from the central hubs in Eretz Yisrael, and there are specific difficulties in connection with the Shmittah year,

It is requested [and perhaps even more than a request] that from time to time, food supplies and the like be brought to them via a van or similar means, so that they will not need to travel from there and waste time, energy, and effort in obtaining these provisions, which certainly also costs more. Since the details regarding this are not known here, I cannot provide specific instructions, but in general, the proposal is most correct for several reasons.

As the aforementioned write, the intention is not that the provisions be given to them for free, but rather that the effort regarding obtaining the food, etc., and bringing it to the remote location be done wholesale. Surely you will provide notification regarding this and thank you in advance.

With respect and blessing,

Secretary

The report regarding the meetings with the participation of the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, etc., etc., Gorodetsky, may he live — have been received.

## 6370

B"H, 6 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' Moshe Chaim Ephraim, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the fifth night of Chanukah.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the righteous, chassidic, and learned Rabbi, R' Shemaryahu Noach Schneersohn of Bobruisk:

What is known to me is as follows: His name was as you mentioned; he was a grandson of the Admur the Tzemach Tzedek. The day of his passing was 15 Teves, 5683, and he is interred in Bobruisk. His grandchildren, may they live and be well, who now reside in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established) in Tel Aviv, are preparing for publication a volume of his responsa, as well as discourses on Chassidic thought.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the righteous, chassidic, and learned Rabbi, R' Shneur Zalman of Kopust: See the book *Beis Rebbe*, Part 3, end of Chapter 8 [the author of the book knew the aforementioned person personally]. It states there: "He bore the holy name of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe (referring to the Baal HaTanya) and of his grandfather, the chassid R' Shlomo Freides, of blessed memory. In every place, he would sign only with one name, Shlomo Zalman or Sh. Zalman; in one place we saw his signature as 'Shlomo Sh. Zalman'." His passing was on 26 Iyar, 5660, and he is interred in Kopust. His only son, the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Yehuda Leib, did not serve in the rabbinate at all, and his livelihood was from commerce.

Under separate cover, I am sending you the volume *Hayom Yom*, at the beginning of which is printed the genealogical lineage of the aforementioned family of the Admur [Baal] HaTanya in general.

With respect and blessing.

#### ***6370***

**R' Moshe Chaim Ephraim:** Bloch, Bronx.

## 6,331

B"H, 6 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, who engages in the needs of the community, whose work is the work of Heaven, R' Yehuda Zarchia Mordechai Leib Chaim, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the fifth night of Chanukah.

At a time of favor, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for each one according to their needs, as appropriate to the content of your letter.

Regarding what you noted in *Torah Or*, *Vayeshev* 29d, in the *maamar* beginning "And from now we will come to explain," that a multitude of gifts serves to draw down the *Ohr Ein Sof* (blessed be He) below into all the subsequent sacrifices—and you found it difficult to understand what "multitude of sacrifices" has to do with drawing down the *Ohr Ein Sof*, before Whom all is as naught.

It is already well-known in many places in our holy Torah regarding such matters, and in the general *avodah* of man, that *avodah* of "with all your heart" and "with all your soul" is not sufficient; it must specifically be "with all your *me'odecha*." In the language of our Sages, this is called "not **doing** the will of the Omnipresent" (*Berachos* 35b), because it is specifically the *me'od*—the "veryness"—of man that draws down the status from Above. As is explained in many places, this is the measure of the Holy One, blessed be He: a measure of the Holy One, blessed be He, corresponding to the measure of man. It is the same in the aforementioned case: when one adds and multiplies in the matters of man, one adds from Above to the *hemshech* (drawings) from Above that are beyond the ordinary.

As for your question regarding what advantage there is generally in the sacrifices of the *Nesi'im* (Princes) that is called a "multitude of gifts"—the matter is explained in the aforementioned *maamar*: the silver bowls, the bulls, the sheep, and the goats. See also regarding this entire matter in *Likutei Torah* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, at the end of *Parshas Naso*, for further details on the multitude of the *Nesi'im's* gifts.

With blessings for good tidings.

 See there in *Torah Or* before the aforementioned *maamar*.

## 6732

6732

B"H, 7 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, etc., R' Gershon Mendel, may he live, and his wife, the Rabbanit, may she live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter, in which you write a summary of your activities over the past days. Surely, in your next letter, you will also include the points of the *shakla v'tarya* between you and the *baalei batim*, may they live, and the subsequent transition from potential to actual, and the manner of your work in your community at this time.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that all of this be with great success and with organized *avodah* regarding the men, women, and children. Indeed, the merit of the many assists you, as do the blessings of our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im*—may their merit shield us.

You are surely also interested in the possibilities of publishing in the newspapers read by our brethren, the Children of Israel, various stories regarding matters of Yiddishkeit and Chassidus, etc.—it is understood that these must be adapted appropriately to the character of the readers of each specific newspaper.

With blessings for good tidings, with joy and goodness of heart,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***6732***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 23, p. 483, and completed based on a photocopy of the letter.

**R' Gershon Mendel, may he live:** Gerlik, Milan. Additional letters to him—above, 6714, and in the references noted there.

## 6733

B"H, 8 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, a man of many accomplishments, engaged in communal needs,
R' Naftali, may he live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of 3 Teves: It is surprising that you do not mention receiving my response to your previous letter.

In it, you write regarding the organization of another trip during the upcoming Chol Hamoed, which, according to the plan, will last for two days. You express the hope that this will assist in the dissemination of the sichos to the youth, as well as the spreading of this matter in general.

Regarding this, in my opinion, a trip during the days of Pesach is generally associated with the greatest difficulties, due to the stringencies (*hidurim*) of the matters of Pesach. Since, as you hope, a large number of youth will participate, this makes the aforementioned even more difficult—especially in a Shmittah year. Therefore, if it is decided to organize another trip, one should ensure that it does not take place during the days of Pesach.

Regarding the question itself, surely there are already summaries of the results of the previous trip, or at the very least, such a summary should be made. I am sure you will inform me, and then there will be room to express an opinion on the matter.

Regarding what you wrote about the sichos for the youth, I believe a letter has already been sent to you from the Merkaz L'Inyonei Chinuch regarding this.

Fortunate is his portion that he stands at the head of this endeavor, which, without a doubt, brings about the drawing near of the hearts of many of our Jewish brethren to our Father in Heaven, to His Torah, and to His mitzvos. The magnitude of this matter cannot be sufficiently explained, especially according to the saying of our Alter Rebbe, that "Love your fellow as yourself" is a vessel for "Love Hashem your G-d" [for the love of Hashem is the foundation and root of all the mitzvos, as love is the foundation and root of all 248 positive mitzvos, within which is also the mitzvah of the fear of Hashem, which is the foundation and root of the 365 negative mitzvos].

I reiterate my blessing that you and your wife, may she live, raise your daughter, may she live, to Torah, to the chuppah, and to good deeds, with proper health and expansiveness in all your affairs.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***6733***

**R' Naftali, may he live:** Kraus, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 17, 6375, and in the references noted there.

## 6734

B"H, 8 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, the chossid, descendant of holy lineage, etc.,
R' Moshe Halevi, shlita

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Sunday, and I believe that during these days a response was sent to your honor from the administration of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim.

On this occasion, I acknowledge receipt of your book, *Eish Das*, Volume 6. May it be Hashem’s will that for many more years you continue to increase soldiers for the Torah, and may it be His will that you emphasize the central point regarding the great necessity for every single member of Bnei Yisroel to study the *pnimiyus haTorah*—which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus. The emphasis here should be on the aspect of study through *hasagah* and *havanah* (comprehension and understanding), for the law regarding the study of Torah Shebe'al Peh is that it is considered "study" only if one understands and grasps it. See the *Magen Avraham* in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim*, siman 50, which is cited in *Likutei Torah* by our Rebbe HaZaken in *Vayikra*, in the *maamar* beginning *Lo Tashbit* (5b). Furthermore, the fiery, soul-stirring words of R' Chaim Vital in his introduction to *Shaar HaHakdamos* are already well-known, and these matters are explained at length by Admur Moharashab in his *Kuntres Eitz Chaim*. Surely, for one such as yourself, this matter requires no elaboration whatsoever.

With respect and blessing.

#### ***6734***

**R' Moshe:** Epstein, Tel Aviv.

## 6375

6375

B”H, 8 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished,
R’ Avraham Zusha, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the *pidyon nefesh* along with its enclosure.

At a time of favor, it will be read at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

May it be the will of Hashem that, since we are in the month of Teves—concerning which our Sages of blessed memory said that "the body derives pleasure from the body"—and as discussed at the *farbrengen* (at which I believe you were present), based on the teaching of our Sages that "Ahasuerus" refers to the Holy One, Blessed be He, whose "end" (*acharis*) and "beginning" (*reishis*) are one, and "Esther" refers to the *Knesses Yisrael*, the Jewish people of every generation—that it be fulfilled in practice: that His very Essence and Being, may He be blessed, will derive pleasure, as it were, from the body and the affairs of each and every Jew, in the literal sense. See also the *sicha* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (the Rashab), of blessed memory, in *Toras Shalom* (Simchas Torah 5669), page 108 and onwards, regarding the concept that "the Essence has to do with the body." All of the above further reinforces the ruling of the Rambam that maintaining a healthy and sound body is among the ways of Hashem.

It may be said that this is also the concept of "And Yehudah approached him" (*eilaiv*—to Him, and not to His attributes), etc., as explained in the *maamarim* of *Torah Or* on this verse, regarding the virtue of the inanimate, the deed, and the body.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the health of the body and the health of the soul, for you and for each and every member of your household, may they live.

***6375***

**R’ Avraham Zusha:** Ziskind, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. 17, 6304, and the references in the footnotes there.

**In the month of Teves:** See also above, letter 6319, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 6336

B"H, 8 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and accomplished, R' Tuvia, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 1 Teves.

At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that each may receive what they need, in accordance with the content of your letter.

May the merit of your involvement in *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah* stand by you. As explained in other places, *chutzah* (the outside) begins below *Chochma* and *Binah* [the *mochin* as they are in themselves], and continues from level to level until it reaches *chutzah* in the simplest sense. Fortunate is your portion, for your *chutzah* consists of *yeshiva* *avreichim*. Yet, on the other hand, this increases the responsibility, for through appropriate *hashpaah* upon them, one must ensure that each one becomes a *tofeach al menas hatfiach*—one who is saturated and can in turn saturate others in his surroundings over time. See *Tosefta Eduyos*, end of chapter 3, regarding the great significance of "students of students," and this is well understood. May it be the will of Hashem that you engage in all the aforementioned with joy and a glad heart, in a manner of "spreading out to the west, east, north, and south," and to an inheritance without boundaries.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6336***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 20, p. 612; completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Tuvia, may he live:** Blau, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 17, 5886, and in the references noted there. Below, 6450.

## 6737

B"H, 11 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 5 Teves.

After all the calculations—the "calculation of the world," etc.—you finally conclude your letter regarding the rent, and that you are indebted in dollars, etc.!

Is it truly your opinion as well that one should "shake the world" over such matters? Especially since, with a little effort, you can settle your debt in *gashmius*, and with a little more effort, it is in your hands to settle your debt in *ruchnius* as well [for when a person sanctifies himself a little from below, they sanctify him greatly from above]. If so, everything is as if it is already placed in a box; it is only that **you** need to open the box. It is enough to open an opening the size of the eye of a needle, and then the *Ribono shel Olam* will open it like the entrance to a hall.

Regarding what you wrote—that you do not know what your purpose is—our Sages of blessed memory have already informed us in their *Mishnah* that one is created to serve his Creator. There are many ways to do this, and what difference does it make to you if you accomplish this by drawing the hearts of your students closer to our Father in Heaven, or by being a *shaliach* for the *tefillos* of *Bnei Yisrael*, etc.? The main thing is that you fulfill your role with tranquility, joy, and a glad heart.

May it be His will that you do not desire to *einreiden zich* (convince yourself) that these matters are confusing you, for there are no confusions; they are only waiting for you to open an opening the size of the eye of a needle, and then it will be as mentioned above.

I was pleased with the report of your visit with your students. May it be His will that, in line with what was said, you do not remain satisfied with your current standing, but rather expand in pleasant ways, for there is nothing that can stand before the *ratzon*.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6178

B"H, 11 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 5th of Teves, in which you write that some time ago you met a young woman, etc., and you had already discussed marriage and even set a date for the wedding, and subsequently she retracted her words and you parted ways, etc.

As it appears from your words, there was not even a formal engagement (*tenaim*) between you. Furthermore, it is not entirely clear whether the young woman’s older sister has become engaged in the meantime, and whether there was a full, explicit forgiveness, or as you write, that she told her sister not to wait for her, etc.

Regarding the practical course of action: If the older sister of the aforementioned young woman has become engaged, or if she at least forgives [you] with a whole heart, then it is advisable to add a name [to the young woman] from among the Matriarchs, because I see in your letter that the name of the young woman’s mother and the name of your mother are the same. After that, you should speak with the young woman once again, since the reason she gave for the separation is too flimsy.

Another point in the aforementioned—and this is also fundamental—is whether, at the time you spoke regarding the connection, you both agreed that you would build your home on the foundations of Torah and mitzvos, a traditional home. As mentioned, this is the essence of the success of a shidduch.

Since, as you write in your letter, your role is to oversee kashrus in..., I would like to hope that you are not only doing this as required, but that you are also putting all your ability into your role to include the supervision of the kashrus of the mind and heart of the immigrants, and not only the kashrus of food. My intention is regarding the general conduct of the places where the immigrants are located, the shul, etc., and matters of education there, and connecting with those who fear the word of Hashem in the vicinity of these places. Certainly, for one who is on-site, there are several more ways to act in the aforementioned direction, and this, too, will serve as a channel and vessel for an increase in the blessings of the Ribono shel Olam in what you need.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6379

B.H., 11 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 21/3, in which you describe the various circumstances that you and your husband (may he live and be well) have gone through, and conclude that things have always been difficult and remain so now, etc.—from which your outlook on the future is understandable.

I am surprised by your conclusion, when you yourself write that out of your entire family, only a few remain, and you are among them. You also write about various deficiencies and illnesses that you have endured, and regarding your husband (may he live and be well), you write that one could not have imagined [the outcome], that he was involved in various matters, and that you both find yourselves in an apartment, etc.

Looking into all of this—what is happening, and not only externally, but what is happening within the home—how can it be that this is the conclusion you draw?!

Understandably, one must pray to Hashem Yisbarach that things should be better and even better still, for He is the Essence of Good, and it is the nature of the Good to do good. However, one must also not ignore the kindnesses of Hashem Yisbarach that one has already witnessed, especially since, as you write, you have seen manifest kindnesses and open miracles.

I wish to emphasize that I do not mean to say that one should not look into what is lacking, nor do I mean to say that you do not lack in the essential matters mentioned above. I only wish to highlight the good—the good in great measure—that you and your husband have seen even with physical eyes.

There is another point in this (which is no less important), which is explained in our holy Torah: that the measure of blessings from Hakadosh Baruch Hu depends, to a certain extent, on how a person receives them. Conduct that is in accordance with this, and the recognition of these kindnesses, expands the channel and the vessels to receive His kindnesses in the near future and in the future in general.

Among the pieces of advice for this, when one desires an increase in the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach, is to do a favor for another Yid, whether in material or spiritual matters. Just as everyone has the possibility to give material tzedakah, so too is there the possibility to do a favor in spiritual matters. The more one adds in this, beyond the fact that one is fulfilling a commandment of the Torah—"Love your fellow as yourself," which Rabbi Akiva says is a great principle of the Torah—and the fact that this adds to the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach, there also comes the inner satisfaction of seeing the good fruit of one's labor. This acts directly and indirectly toward the improvement of health and toward the assessment and perspective of everything that is being done within oneself and around oneself. May it be His will that you report good news regarding all of the above.

You should have the tefillin of your husband checked, as well as the mezuzos in your apartment. And you should maintain the good custom of the daughters of Yisrael to give tzedakah each time before lighting candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6440

6440

B"H, 12 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Friday, in which you write about what you have been through, and conclude that you wish to return to the path of Yiddishkeit.

Fortunate is your portion for having decided upon the true good—good both in a physical sense and in a spiritual sense—to live your life according to the guidance of the Torah HaKedosha, the Torah of life. You may be certain, based on the words of our Sages, that even when a person sanctifies himself a little from below, they sanctify him greatly from above, especially when he increases his own efforts in the aforementioned matters.

Regarding what you wrote about those who laugh, etc.: It is appropriate that you do not enter into any arguments with them at all. For sometimes, the reason these people laugh is that they feel deep within themselves that their own conduct is not as it should be. After all, their fathers and forefathers, for three thousand five hundred years, have walked in the path of Torah and mitzvos, and only they have deviated from it. They feel—even if they do not always understand it intellectually—that this deviation occurred because they did not want to struggle against the yetzer. They attempt to cover up this feeling with laughter and mockery, etc. You can see tangibly that when one does not enter into arguments and continues on the path of Torah and mitzvos without paying attention to the mockers, eventually, even the mockers and those who laugh will cease doing so, and they too will draw closer, step by step, to the Torah and its mitzvos, of which it is said, "and he shall live by them."

It is my hope that you will meet often with the Tzeirei Agudas Chabad members you mentioned in your letter, and participate in their farbrengens; regarding such things it is said, "Each man shall help his fellow."

Regarding your financial situation and the debts you have:

Every weekday, before the morning davening, set aside a few coins for tzedakah, and it is also good to do so before the Mincha davening. Maintain the practice of reciting each day, after the Shacharis davening, the daily portion of Tehillim—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month. It is also appropriate that you have your tefillin checked to ensure they are kosher according to the law.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6141

B"H, 12 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Eshel,
May Hashem preserve them and grant them life,

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from 7 Teves, along with the enclosure. I have also received in recent days several copies of the maamar "Amar Rabbi Shimon ben Lakish" and the explanatory pamphlet.

It is somewhat surprising that the explanatory pamphlet was not proofread as thoroughly as it should have been. For example, the birth year of the Tzemach Tzedek is listed as 5748, whereas it should be 5749, among other things. Although on the face of it this is a minor matter, nevertheless, in a publication of four pages distributed in thousands of copies, it is understood that one must strive for the greatest possible degree of correction and improvement, and the wise will understand.

I have already written in my letter to Tzeirei Agudas Chabad that the time has long since arrived for the activities of anash in general, and especially those of the students of Tomchei Temimim, to move beyond the stage of plowing, sowing, and planting, and to reach the stage of harvesting and seeing the good fruit of their labor in matters of Torah and mitzvos. I will not repeat these points here, as they are understood; I only wish to emphasize their connection to them (not **also** to them, for they are the activists working in this area, and the wise will understand). And there is nothing that can stand before the will. Especially in light of the known ruling of the Heavenly Beis Din, that because of this mesiras nefesh (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, they ruled in the Heavenly Beis Din that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of his followers and those who walk in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

With blessings for success in your holy work and for good tidings regarding all the above,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6842

B"H, 13 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished, a man of action, R' Michel Yehoshua, may he live.

Shalom and Bracha!

I inquired about your well-being through your brother, may he live, and about your state of health, and most importantly, your state of mind. It is my hope that by the time you receive this letter, there has already been a tangible improvement. Especially knowing your love for our Holy Land—may it be built and established speedily in our days through Moshiach Tzidkeinu—surely the air of Eretz Yisrael must influence you in the desired direction, not only in health and added strength for the neshamah, but also in health and added strength for the body, for the two are interdependent. It would give me great pleasure to receive good news from you regarding all of the above.

It is surely unnecessary to prompt you, especially considering the connection between your printing press here and the printing affairs of Lubavitch, but it is appropriate and also correct that you visit the printing press that has been established in Kfar Chabad, in addition to your visits to other Chabad institutions. Beyond the matter itself, there is no doubt that you will be able to bring great benefit to this new institution in Kfar Chabad through your advice and guidance, etc., especially since this printing press is in its initial stages, and who is as expert and experienced in this profession as you?

Please convey my regards to your father, may he live, even though I do not know him personally.

I look forward to hearing good news regarding all of the above.

With blessings for good tidings, in joy and goodness of heart.

#### ***6842***

**R' Michel Yehoshua:** Shulsinger. Another letter to him – Vol. 12, 3887.

## 6443

B"H, 13 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

After the longest of interruptions, I received your letter, which you finished writing on Zos Chanukah and which has just arrived.

In it, you write of your holy work in *chinuch* on *taharas hakodesh* in the yeshiva, as well as in the women’s group that you teach.

Certainly, the immense value of the aforementioned involvement requires no explanation, for it is explicitly stated in our holy Torah—*Toras Chaim*—in many places. Surely your wife, may she live and be well, is also aware of this, as you write that she agrees with several details of your work. You conclude your letter by stating that there are complaints regarding this study, namely that you dedicate too much time to this work, and that this affects the attention required for your children, may they live and be well, as well as for household matters in general, and you ask for my opinion on this.

Now, in general, it is understood and also simple that one cannot set hard and fast rules—that is, a fixed schedule for every single day—since circumstances change, and in connection with this, responsibilities also change, both regarding communal matters and regarding household matters.

However, it is simple that a person must dedicate some of their time to communal work, even those who need to dedicate much of their time to household matters, and at the central point, the *chinuch* of their children. Based on the above, it is understood that exactly how to divide the time between communal matters and private work requires the opinion of those who are on the scene. Since he himself is "closest to himself," as our Sages have said, it is impossible to judge truly as is necessary. As our Sages explain, because of one’s closeness to oneself, one is not deemed credible regarding oneself, whether when speaking of one’s virtues or when speaking of one’s faults, and the rest is understood.

Therefore, you should present the matter before three of your friends who are in your location, for it has already been said, "In the multitude of counselors there is salvation."

It remains for me only to reiterate once more that work for the benefit of the community is a necessity for everyone, especially for those whom Divine Providence has privileged and who have already seen good fruit from their labor in this area.

I trust you are aware of the three daily *shiurim*—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and in any case, you should observe them from here on out. Your wife, may she live and be well, should observe the good custom of the daughters of Israel: to set aside money for *tzedakah* before lighting the candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6,344

B"H, 13 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Students of Yeshivas Erev Shaarei Chesed, under the auspices of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in the Holy City of Jerusalem, may it be rebuilt and established,
May Hashem protect them and may they live.

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter in due time regarding the completion of two years since the establishment of the yeshiva, and subsequently, I received with pleasure the news regarding the gathering you held for all the students of the evening yeshivos and the examinations, which took place during the days of Chanukah.

I hereby send my blessing to each and every one of you, based on what is explained in several places: that the matter of Chanukah was [the Greeks' attempt] "to make them forget Your Torah and force them to stray from the laws of Your will." This means that the intention of the wicked Greek kingdom was not merely to forbid the study of Torah as a form of wisdom, but specifically to make them forget "Your Torah"—the perfect Torah of Hashem. Similarly, regarding the performance of mitzvos, they did not intend to forbid the rational mitzvos, but specifically the *chukim* (statutes) which have no [apparent] reason; and even regarding these, they would not have cared had they not been "the laws of Your will"—the will of the Creator. Through the **mesiras nefesh** of the Children of Israel—men, women, and children—the wicked were delivered into the hands of the righteous, and the wanton into the hands of those who occupy themselves with Your Torah. A Yom Tov was added in Israel to thank and praise His great Name, in a manner of "increasing and continuing" to illuminate the darkness of the galus through the Chanukah light, and the *halacha* follows Beis Hillel.

And based on what is said, "The candle of Hashem is the soul of man," every Jew is a candle of the Ribono shel Olam, and his task—as the Mishnah states, "I was created to serve my Maker"—is to illuminate his portion in the world. And this applies not only to his own **nefesh habehamis**, but also to his surroundings and beyond, as is the concept of the mitzvah of the Chanukah light, [placed] at the entrance of his house, outside. May it be the will [of Hashem] that all the above be fulfilled in each and every one of you, in a manner of "increasing and continuing," adding and shining.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

It is understood that all that has been said applies and is directed also to their friends, may they live and be well, in the other branches of the evening yeshivos. Surely you will convey these words to them, and most importantly—serve as a living example for them of a good and successful student, in accordance with the hopes and intentions of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im.

## 6,345

B"H, 13 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the Tenth of Teves—may it soon be transformed into joy and gladness—in which you write regarding your teaching a class of students at the Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in [location], and that because of this, you have almost no time to study for yourself.

The greatest astonishment is understandable: how does teaching a class of students preclude the possibility of studying on one’s own? Especially since you write that they are young, and while preparation for study is indeed necessary for any learning—as is known from our Sages [and as is known from our Rebbes, even regarding the recitation of a Haftorah and the like]—the time required for appropriate preparation is minimal in quantity. If only you would utilize all the time free from teaching the students to study for yourself!

Regarding the core question of whether you should continue teaching, this depends on the results: if you have succeeded with your students, it is understood that you should continue in this, and Hashem will guide you in all that has been mentioned, for the good before Him, in all details.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

Since a person is biased toward himself, it is also understood that in evaluating the aforementioned results, you should rely on the administration of Tomchei Temimim.

## 6363

B"H, 15 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

Mrs. Zlata, may she live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

After a long interval, your letter from 10 Teves—may it be transformed into joy and gladness soon—arrived, along with the enclosed photo of the seminary and vocational school.

May Hashem Yisbarach help you that, with joy and a good heart, you should not only continue this highly important work but also increase it and influence your surroundings to do the same, and in ever-increasing measure. It is surely unnecessary to dwell on the importance of this, and it is surely also unnecessary to dwell on the great wonder and pain caused here by the news of the "indifferent" attitude of many of Anash toward this institution, despite the numerous sichos of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which one has heard orally and which also exist in writing, regarding the supreme importance of such institutions.

May it be His will that, at the very least from now on, people will reflect more deeply upon the aforementioned sichos, in a way that will lead to concrete actions to the greatest possible extent. For the full potential is there, and unfortunately, only the will is lacking, as the well-known saying goes: "You can, but you do not want to."

At a time of favor, I will mention all those you write about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May the merit of your work for the aforementioned institutions stand by you and your family, may they live and be well, to report good tidings in all that you write.

With blessings,

## 6,287

B"H, 15 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After the longest of interruptions, I received your letter dated 21/12/58, in which you write about the incident—may Hashem spare us—that occurred to you.

May it be the will of Hashem that from here on out, you report only good, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed, both in your personal matters and in communal matters. For with a Jewish person, one is dependent upon the other. As is known from the saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, every individual is a "multitude" and has the capacity to influence the many—especially in your case, as you have already attempted this and succeeded, as is known to me.

Regarding your request for my opinion specifically concerning the aforementioned incident that occurred on the road: It is well known what is explained in many places, that the matters of our physical world evolve from matters in spirituality, and especially from the matters contained in the Torah. This is also understood from the statement of our Sages in Bereishis Rabbah at the beginning, that the Torah is the ledger and blueprint according to which the entire world and all its affairs were built. Every physical event is a hint—and sometimes even more than a hint—regarding one’s spiritual state.

The concept of a "road" (derech) in spirituality is, as it is written, "To keep the way (derech) of Hashem, to do tzedakah and justice"—that is, the Torah and the mitzvos in general, and specifically the matters of tzedakah and justice. It is explained in the teachings of Chassidus that these two matters are connected: tzedakah must be performed after one has exercised "justice" regarding oneself—determining what is appropriate and necessary for one’s own needs—at which point one sets aside a large portion of what remains for tzedakah. This is the same regarding spiritual tzedakah: one must evaluate one’s own standing and situation, and how one can influence others in one’s immediate and even distant surroundings. At that point, one sets aside a large portion of one’s time, energy, and ability for tzedakah, to influence those who are far away and bring them closer to the life of the World to Come (which is also the life of this world). Surely, for you, further elaboration on this is unnecessary.

May it be the will of Hashem that you report good news in all the aforementioned, as well as regarding livelihood in tranquility and with abundance, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings.

#### ***6,287***

**The incident:** That his car overturned on the road.

**Saying:** See also above, Vol. 17, Letter 6,508, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 648

B"H, 17 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

... Regarding the conclusion of your letter, where you mention that you are in a low state of spirits and express surprise at this: both matters are puzzling. Your surprise is puzzling, for the Alter Rebbe writes that this [the state of sadness] is one of the stratagems of the Yetzer Hara. He writes about this at length, from which it is understood that anyone who has not reached the level of King David—whose heart was "hollow" within him, having no Yetzer Hara—is tested by the *Sitra Achra* with this tactic as well. 

The second point of surprise is that you allow the Yetzer Hara to succeed, Heaven forbid, in this tactic—that not only does he entice you to be in a low state of spirits, but you are actually in one. If everyone has been given the strength not to be enticed and to nullify this tactic from the outset, then all the more so is this true for those whom Divine Providence has privileged to be involved in the holy institutions belonging to the community of Chassidim, whose primary point is the service of Hashem specifically with joy and gladness of heart. 

I look forward to hearing good news regarding all of the above, and may it be His will that this happens very soon.

With blessings for good news.

## 6489

B"H, 17 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 12 Teves.

I read with great astonishment that he began to recite a maamar of Chassidus but was not permitted to finish it.

Although they may have advocated for this custom [so as not to cause embarrassment], even in this matter we have only the instruction of our Nasi, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who instructed that the entire maamar be completed [and for one who wishes to fulfill the custom of the surrounding environment — he should begin again and stop during the second time]. There are several of those currently in Eretz Yisrael who were present at that same gathering in Poland in the year 5696, and it is strange that they did not publicize this instruction. In any event, it is worthwhile to publicize it now, and a word to the wise is sufficient...

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

#### ***6489***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 23, p. 521.

**Instruction of our Nasi:** See also above, Vol. 17, Letter 5872, and the references in the notes there.

## 6550

B"H, 18 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, Boston Branch
May Hashem grant them life!

Greetings and blessings!

It was with great pleasure that I read your report, especially regarding the *taharas hamishpacha* branch.

It would be a fitting step for you to connect with the Merkaz Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad and inform them specifically about this part of your work. Surely, this or a similar approach can be implemented in many branches of Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad. From your experience and knowledge, others can learn how each branch should approach this work in its own city, and how to increase success in this, with the help of the *Ribono shel Olam*.

May Hashem Yisbarach help you to be successful in the aforementioned, as well as in the other departments of your work, and in a manner of "ever higher," with health, joy, and a glad heart.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6551

B"H, 18 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Kfar Chabad Committee,
May Hashem protect them and grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I trust my previous letter has already been received. In response to your letter of 11 Teves, in which you write regarding the organization of committees, etc., I will reiterate that:

May it be the will of Hashem that the desire of Hashem succeeds in their hands to establish Kfar Chabad, upon the foundation and under the leadership of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, on its own basis and structure. Fortunate is the portion of each and every one who participates in the communal affairs of Kfar Chabad,

For this is a connection and unification with our Nasi, my father-in-law, the Rebbe,

Who, just as during his lifetime in this world he stood and served, so too now he stands and serves, and awakens abundant mercy for all who take part in his activities and affairs,

To add to them an increase of blessing and success in physical health, in spiritual health, and in all that they require—which is included in the three lines of children, life, and sustenance—and in all of them, specifically in abundance.

And may it be the will that even if at times there was some error, our Sages have said, "A person does not stand upon them unless he has stumbled," and "The Torah was not given to ministering angels."

From here on, may each and every one of them walk their path securely—the path of the King, the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He. And the Tzaddikim are similar to their Creator; "Who are the kings? The Rabbis." And in the place of the Rav—this is my father-in-law, the Rebbe.

And from a place of joy and goodness of heart, and the proper health of each and every one and their households, may they add strength to the establishment of Kfar Chabad and its development in all general matters and in all specific matters, both in physicality and in spirituality.

With blessings for good tidings in all that is mentioned.

P.S.
It is understood that it is your merit to publicize the above in an appropriate manner.

#### ***6551***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 16, p. 506, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

## 6,152

B"H, 20 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Mr. S. Dardak, may he live.
Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 19 Teves, regarding its contents:

1) Regarding the prayer of Yishtabach, "Melech Yachid Kel" (King, Unique, G-d), whether the word "Yachid" should be connected to the word preceding it or the word following it.

It is understood that "Yachid" here is connected to the word "Melech" that precedes it, or it is a descriptor in its own right, and it does not flow either to what precedes it or to what follows it, similar to the phrasing in Boruch She'amar.

2) Regarding the pronunciation of the Name A-donai, as there are those who end it with a kamatz under the nun without pronouncing the yud.

It is obvious that this is a fundamental error, and there is no relevance here to the question posed in your letter—that it is better that they remain in ignorance—for certainly, once their error is explained to them, they will strive to correct it, as it is within their power to do so.

I will take this opportunity to remind you to observe the three daily study portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—which we have heard many times from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which are appropriate for every soul and are a segulah for many matters.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

 The Arizal’s version is "Melech Yachid Chai HaOlamim," omitting the word "Kel." This also proves that the word "Yachid" is not connected to the word "Kel." And our Rabbeinu HaZaken explains the Arizal’s version: "This King is in the aspect of Yachid and Meyuchad, etc." (Torah Or 40c).

 See Pri Megadim (Shulchan Aruch Orach Chaim 265, in Eshel Avraham) that, on the contrary, the yud must be emphasized.

## 63637

B"H, 20 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the Fast of the Tenth [of Teves], which will soon be transformed into joy and gladness, in which you write that you have been for a month and several days at Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in... and that there are details regarding the situation there with which you are not satisfied, and you detail them in your letter, etc.

After requesting your forgiveness, and prefacing with what is explained by our Sages, that although the *yetzer hara* is called a "fool," he is nonetheless cunning in his craft of how to entice the Jewish person. Since people do not share the same opinions, it is understood that each and every one has his own approach, suited to the nature and character of that individual.

Regarding an *avreich* who has decided for himself, despite various difficulties, that he will study Torah with *yiras shamayim* and ascend in the fulfillment of *mitzvos* and in *hiskashrus* to all matters of Torah and its *mitzvos*, and has even begun to carry out this decision by entering an environment where the emphasis is on matters of *yiras shamayim* and the study of the inner dimensions of the Torah—it is understood that if [the *yetzer hara*] were to approach him with the argument that he should retract his decision and return, *chas v’shalom*, to his previous conduct, he would certainly not listen to his enticement. Therefore, he attempts a method in which his (the *yetzer hara’s*) hope is that perhaps he will be enticed through this, by explaining that this is not the place and this is not the way through which he will reach his goal. It is understood that he will endeavor to invent reasons that have a foothold in the intellect, and it is possible he will find some, since there is no perfection in the world, and therefore all matters are a mixture of good and evil (although, of course, there are differences in this from one extreme to the other, to the point where at the extreme of good, the evil is only one part in a thousand and ten thousand, and is completely *batel*, and up to the other extreme, *r’l*). And the more the *yetzer hara* sees that the person will succeed in the path he has chosen and in the place where he is found, and is attempting to bring his decision into action, he also intensifies his enticements—similar to what is explained in the *Tanya*, that foreign thoughts and the enticements of the *yetzer hara* intensify during *tefillah* more than during the rest of the day, and this is well known.

It is possible that the arguments about which you write (at least in part) are correct—meaning, that the *avreichim* who are in *chavrusa* with you lack the points you detail. But a parable for this: to a merchant working for his material livelihood—when customers come to buy his merchandise, he endeavors to find in the customers those qualities that can **assist** him in selling the merchandise and thereby achieve the greatest profit, and he will not emphasize the part of the customers' traits that are in contradiction to this. And similarly in the analogy: when a person connects with his friend, he must emphasize, highlight, and endeavor to strengthen the good part of his friend, and not the other side.

**I am certain** that among the *avreichim* friends in the Yeshiva, most of them do not possess several of the undesirable points you write about in your letter, although, like every human being who is not perfect, they have some other point that requires correction and improvement. The advised counsel is to be one who learns from every person the good side within him, and to connect with the good side within him, and not the opposite.

The conclusion from all that has been said is that you must study with *hasmadah* and *shekidah*, and be assisted by the *avreichim* in your surroundings at Tomchei Temimim—those who are *masmidim* in study (even if they do not prolong their *tefillah*) regarding your own *hasmadah*, and from the *avreichim* who prolong their *tefillah* and *regesh* (even if they are not *masmidim*) regarding the manner of your own *tefillah*, etc. And it has already been promised by our Sages: "You have labored and you have found."

May it be the will that these few lines of mine, small in quantity relative to the importance of the matter, will perform their function—by your contemplating them and expanding upon them in your own understanding and grasp. For they describe reality as it **is**, and certainly everything you absorb in Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim you will be able to utilize—over the course of time—for your influence upon others. The merit of the many will assist you in overcoming the imagined obstacles, and in your own success during your study in the Yeshiva.

The *sicha* from the Rebbe Rashab *n.e.*, printed in *Toras Shalom*, is well known: that one whose role is to be a *mashpia*, and who wishes to succeed in this role, must first be a *mekabel* in all details. In the style of our Sages, to be a vessel empty of all side-interests and all intellectual confusion of his own in his reception of matters, through which he will become a *mashpia* over the course of time.

And to add further: it is certainly known to several of your friends regarding your decision to change your path in essence, and that you have already begun to ascend in this direction. Any retreat backward will give them the foothold and the opening to come to you with various and strange proofs that you are not correct in your decision, etc. For, like all the Children of Israel, their *nefesh elokis* certainly reproaches them: why have they not made a decision like yours, to return to the path of true life, which is the path of our Torah—the Torah of life—and the fulfillment of its *mitzvos* upon which it is said, "And he shall live by them"? And in order to justify their own lack of decision, the easiest way is to cast aspersions on your decision to return to the path of our ancestors, to the Torah and the *mesorah*.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6,454

B"H, 21 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Sunday.

It gave me pleasure to read that you participated in the farbrengen of the auspicious day of 19 Kislev, the Rosh Hashanah of the Torah of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus. May it be the will [of Hashem] that the purpose of every farbrengen be fulfilled in you as well—that is, that it should have an effect, and its impression should be noticeable throughout all the days, weeks, and months that follow, leading to actual deed, as it is written: "Fear Hashem and keep His mitzvos, for this is the whole person."

Regarding what you wrote about how to strengthen one's davening:

The halacha has already been decided in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, siman 98, se'if 2, and in the Rema, that before every davening—that is, three times a day—there must be hisbonenus in the greatness of Hashem and also in the lowliness of man [though it is understood that the nature of the hisbonenus is not the same before the morning davening, which is at the beginning of the day and is lengthy, as opposed to the davening of Mincha and Maariv; for this reason, Pesukei D'zimra was instituted at length in the morning, etc.]. Likewise, one should daven from a Siddur. If the schedule of the yeshiva does not allow for hisbonenus in the meaning of the words every single day, it is proper and good that, at the very least, one should contemplate one concept or one verse each day—naturally, using different verses on different days—so that over time, the hisbonenus will encompass all the chapters and parts of davening. Furthermore, our Sages have commanded us to set aside tzedakah before davening, as it is written: "I, through tzedakah—then—will behold Your face."

According to what is explained in many seforim, being careful with the tevilas Ezra is highly relevant and has a great influence on the manner and level of one's davening. To such an extent that, even though strictly speaking the requirement for tevilas Ezra was abolished, the opinion of Rav Hai Gaon (the last of the Geonim and the first in stature) is that this applies only regarding Torah study—to words of Torah—but not to davening (see the R"I to the Rif, Chapter 3 of Berachos, where he concludes that even though the law is not definitively binding according to halacha, etc., nevertheless, all agree that davening is more readily accepted with the immersion). Much has been written on this in the seforim of Kabbalah, Chassidus, and mussar.

I trust you know of and keep the three daily shiurim of Chitas (Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya), and at the very least, you should keep them from here on in. May you have good news regarding all that has been mentioned.

With blessings.

## 6555

B"H, 23 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, distinguished, and learned, whose work is the work of Heaven,
Rabbi Yosef E. shlita,

Peace and blessing!

After a very long interval, I received your letter from Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Mikeitz, as well as the one preceding it, in which you write regarding your holy work in the Gibraltar community.

May it be Hashem’s will that you continue to increase this work further and further, for we are commanded to "ascend in matters of holiness." Furthermore, regarding every command of Hashem—the Creator and Guide of the world—the strength and the potential to fulfill the command are granted from the outset, especially in communal matters, where the merit of the many assists the individual. May it be His will that you proceed with vigor, joy, and a glad heart, for this, too, adds to the success of the avodah. May you share good news regarding all that was mentioned.

I have a strong hope—even though you do not mention it—that you are setting times for the study of the inner dimension of Torah, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus. If this is required of everyone in our era, it is certainly required of those who have an influence on the souls of the Yidden in their community; for any growth within themselves is immediately evident in the members of the community, both them and their families shlita, and this is simple to understand.

With respect and with blessings of Mazal Tov on the birth of your son, Mordechai shlita, of whom you informed me in your letter. May you and your wife raise him, along with your other children shlita, to Torah, to the chuppah, and to maasim tovim.

With blessings for good tidings.

...Regarding what you wrote about the efforts to establish a high school—but then they would learn in a mixed environment, unlike now.

If the curriculum in the public schools does not force the students to desecrate the holy Shabbos, rachmana litzlan, then "sit and do nothing" is preferable for the time being.

## 6,556

B"H, 24 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, who engages in the work of Heaven,
R' Ze'ev, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 15 Teves, in which you raise a point regarding the words of the Rashbam on the verse "Dan will judge" (*Bereishis* 49:16), noting that his commentary—which states that the interpretation regarding Shimshon does not know the depth of the *peshat*—seems difficult, given that in several *Chazal* Midrashim, the verse is interpreted in that very manner.

The precision of the Rashbam is that those interpretations mentioned above belong to the *drush* (homiletical) dimension of the Torah, not the *peshat* (literal) dimension. The Rashbam’s approach to this is explained further in his commentary at the beginning of *Parshas Vayeishev*; see there.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

#### ***6,556***

**R' Ze'ev:** Weber, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 15, 5,386, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6557

B"H, 24 Teves – The Hillula of Rabbeinu HaZaken, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch.
5719 – Brooklyn.

Mr. Yitzchak, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

It was a pleasure to receive your letter and to see in it your interest in the songs of the Jewish people in general, and in Chassidic and Chabad nigunim in particular, as well as your description of the situation where, in recent years, the portion of these songs in the public programs of various choirs has diminished.

It is understood that you are correct in your response to this, that it is a regrettable matter. It is regrettable in two respects: that something good is missing, and that its place is being taken by something trivial, and at times even harmful. Yet, it is precisely in the teachings of Chassidus Chabad that the importance of song and melody has been emphasized, as is explained in many places, a significant portion of which is collected in the introduction to the *Sefer HaNigunim* published by Kehot, which I am certain you possess.

Based on the above, it is also understood that, in principle, the proposal to arrange a number of Chabad nigunim in a way that they could be used in choirs and the like seems very appropriate. However, given what is done in these areas in our confused generation, there is room for great concern that the arrangement will alter the form and the content—possibly to the extent that the loss will outweigh the gain. Unfortunately, in certain areas where various matters of our holy Torah and various matters from the realm of Chassidus have been "arranged," the arrangement has, so to speak, turned these matters into something harmful. Surely, there is no need for me to provide you with evidence or examples of this, as your letter indicates that you are in close contact with the younger generation.

In any case, it is understood that my words do not constitute a rejection of the proposal at all, but rather a question and a request for a specific proposal and plan through which the aforementioned concern could be ruled out.

Regarding what you wrote about suitable text for the songs, this complicates the matter further. For the text must be appropriate to the content of the song and the state of mind of the one singing it—which cannot always be aligned with the text. In some places, this intention is also explained, albeit with an explanation from a different perspective: that speech limits song—meaning, that song is more flexible and often encompasses several interpretations that no single text can contain.

I would be interested to hear about your work with the youth in this area, and also whether you have any publications on these matters.

With respect and blessing.

## 6,158

B"H, 24 Teves, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Zos Chanukah,
