**This question:** A non-Jewish daughter to a Kohen.

## 6811

6811

By the Grace of G-d
6 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn

Rabbi, G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished, engaged in communal needs,
R. Eliezer Lipa, may he live.

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 23 Adar II, and subsequently the books you sent as per the list below, foremost among them your own book, *Otzar Maamarim Al HaTorah*.

Surely you will continue to act in this manner in the future, and I express my gratitude, my gratitude, both retroactively for the past and for the future to come.

May it be G-d’s will that for many more years you will work to disseminate the revealed and inner dimensions of Judaism among ever-widening circles, and may this be done with proper health—health in physical matters and health in spiritual matters. For if this endeavor was great at any time and in any place (see *Zohar*, Vol. III, 5a, that the concept of "greatness" is when the King and the Matronita, the Holy One, blessed be He, and the Community of Israel, are united through Torah and mitzvos in actual practice here in the physical world, specifically, as explained in many places), then how much more so is this true in the generation of the footsteps of Moshiach. And together with this, it is the final point for the arrival and revelation of "He who stands behind our wall, watching and peering."

May it be G-d’s will that this revelation occur *b'agala di'dan*—that is, in the very near future—in accordance with the ruling of our Sages (*Shemos Rabbah*, ch. 15:11) that in Nissan they are destined to be redeemed, and therefore joy was established in it: redemption with joy, in repentance and tranquility, and may He show us wonders.

With blessings for good tidings and a kosher and happy Pesach.

P.S.
As is my custom, and for the sake of affection, I will note at least a few points regarding your book. It is a pity that you did not collect at least some pearls from the treasury of Chabad Chassidus. It is a pity for several reasons, although I am certain there is room to judge favorably, that the selection was not dependent on you alone, and the wise will understand. Nevertheless, the reader who studies your book will ultimately not encounter those true pearls that reflect and illuminate in a special way various matters, as well as a worldview, etc. Surely for someone like you, there is no need to elaborate on this, especially since my hope is that through an expansion of your horizons, you will add your hand to publish more works such as this, and you will also fill in the area I have noted. In that case, there will be room for fulfillment in double measure. It is understood that I took note of the beginning of your book, where you brought teachings from Chabad, as well as the introduction by the Rebbe the Tzemach Tzedek, and this further strengthens the aforementioned judgment to the favorable side, for regarding the introduction and the beginning, he alone was the one who decided.

2) In the collection at the beginning of Parshas Mishpatim, p. 131, regarding the interpretation of the words of the *Beis Yaakov*: It can be said that his intent is to the law that when one comes before a *Beis Din*, one should not show honor to one party, whoever he may be, so that the arguments of the other party are not stifled. Therefore, in the Parsha as well, they concealed that the speech was specifically to Moshe.

3) Ibid., p. 199, from the compiler, based on *Tanya*, ch. 32. And *dapach"ch* (words of the living G-d). It should be noted that the matter discussed in *Tanya* is explained even further in *Likutei Torah*, Parshas Matos, in the discourse entitled *Hechalutzu*, and how this relates to the Name Havayah. For all 613 mitzvos are dependent upon the Name Havayah (Introduction to *Tikkunei Zohar*, 2a; ibid., 102). And through the permutation of the letters of *Mitzvah* via *Atbash*, *Mitzvah* is the Name Havayah (*Likutei Torah*, Vayikra, 2a).

4) Ibid., p. 269. From *Tanya*, ch. 5. Note the *Moreh Nevuchim*, Vol. I, ch. 54 (and the Commentary on the Mishnah, *Avos*, end of ch. 3).

## 6802

6802

B"H, 7 Nissan, 5719

Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the congregants of the "Maoz Aviv" synagogue,
and at their head, the esteemed gabbaim R' Pinchas HaKohen
Rubin, R' Yitzchak Martin, and R' Shalom Kornfein,
may you all be blessed.

Peace and blessing!

I have received your letter dated 3 Adar II, in which you write that, with the participation of Rabbi Yudasin (may he live and be well), you are proceeding to execute a plan to build a synagogue in place of the existing wooden shack. It is my hope that the synagogue will also serve as a beis medrash for studying regular shiurim in public, as you write in your letter.

In accordance with the verse, "I am a companion to all who fear You," I am enclosing a symbolic contribution, and may it be Hashem's will that you see good fruit from your labor.

With blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach.

## 6833

6833

B”H, 7 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the participants of the farbrengen in honor of the yom hahilula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, may his merit shield us, Nasi of Yisrael,
May Hashem grant them life,

Shalom u’vracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the aforementioned auspicious day, a letter of blessing.

I have received the blessing and I return it, that as it is written, “I will bless those who bless you,” each and every one of them shall be blessed with the blessing of the Ribono shel Olam.

A blessing which possesses two qualities: the quality of blessing and the quality of tefillah, as explained at length in the maamar beginning with the words *Amar Rabbi Yehuda bar Ilai* (5629).

And merit is brought about on meritorious days, the days of the month of Nissan, for all the days of this month are in the category of Rosh Chodesh, as written in the Shelah (Parshas Bo), cited in the maamar *HaChodesh Hazeh Lachem* (5626), that the month and all matters within it are referred to by the words, “This month shall be **for you**,” which, as our Sages of blessed memory state, indicates that Bnei Yisrael are uniquely distinguished in this, and this is well understood.

And may the merit of the Baal HaHilula, who said before his passing, “Ich gay in himmel un di kesavim loz ich ayach” (I am ascending to heaven, and the writings I leave for you), which emphasizes that his writings of Chassidus serve as a replacement and fulfillment for the fact that he is ascending to heaven—

And as our Sages of blessed memory have informed us, that tzaddikim are similar to their Creator, of whom it is said, “I have written and given My soul” (Shabbos 105a) [see Tanya, Chapter 47, in the name of the Zohar HaKadosh]—stand by all those who occupy themselves with his Torah. And great is study, for it leads to action: walking in his guidance and his ways, for an increase of hispaalus and hisorerus from the Nasi of Yisrael in all that they require, from soul to body.

With blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach, and for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6833***

Printed in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 17, p. 423.

## 6804

6804

B"H, 8 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, which was received with some delay, I am sending this reply in accordance with your request, without waiting for my turn.

In it, you write regarding the Yeshiva Achei Tmimim in [city name] and its administration.

The question and doubt regarding this well-known matter are surprising, and it is as follows:

It is known that when my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, established the central Yeshiva Tomchei Tmimim in Brooklyn, and subsequently established—through his shluchim—branches in other cities under the name Yeshivos Achei Tmimim...

He also established the order that his shluchim in those cities were to manage the Yeshivos Achei Tmimim in their respective locations, in conjunction with the activists and anash of that place, in the form of a board of directors or the like.

And regarding any difficult matter that could not be resolved on the spot by the shaliach and the committee, they were to turn to and consult with the central administration at the central Yeshiva in Brooklyn.

And if even that did not suffice, they were to turn to my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and he would provide his opinion.

All the Yeshivos Achei Tmimim in the United States have conducted themselves according to this order and have also succeeded. It is understood that no one has any desire to change this order, especially since it has seen actual success in the aforementioned conduct.

And since I am confident that the intention of all the activists of the Yeshiva Achei Tmimim in [city name] is for the goal set forth by my father-in-law, the Rebbe—to increase and glorify Torah through this holy institution, by providing its students with an appropriate education on the purity of holiness—they will certainly continue to conduct themselves in this manner from here on as well. The merit of the many will assist them in achieving great success in the establishment and development of the institution.

And surely, through this, there will be an increase in the blessings of Hashem and success in the personal affairs of all those who exert themselves and assist—whether with their bodies, their money, or their souls, or with all three together—for this holy institution.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned matters, with peace of mind, and for a kosher and happy Pesach.

## V'Teseh

B"H, 11 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To our brethren, the Children of Israel, wherever they may be,
May Hashem grant them life,

Peace and blessing!

As we approach the Yom Tov of our liberation, Chag HaMatzos, the season of our freedom, I send my blessing to Jews everywhere: that this Yom Tov should bring into the daily life of each and every one a true and complete liberation from all worries and obstacles, both material and spiritual. May you rise to the inner content of Yetzias Mitzrayim, as a prelude to Kabbalas HaTorah, realizing the Divine promise: "When you bring the people out of Egypt, you will serve G-d on this mountain" (Shemos 3:12).

* * *

Matters of Torah and mitzvos are, naturally—as the Ribono shel Olam who gave them to us—infinite and eternal. So too are the instructions that one can and must learn from Torah and mitzvos; they are valid in all times and in all lands. Especially such a mitzvah and general concept as the Yom Tov of Pesach—Yetzias Mitzrayim—which one is obligated to remember every day.

One of the lessons of the Yom Tov of Pesach is that a Jew possesses the strength, and can in practice, in a short time—in a mere moment—transform himself completely, from one extreme to the other.

In Tanach and the words of Chazal, the bitterness of the Galus of Mitzrayim and the deepest degradation of the Jews at that time are described in detail.

They were enslaved in a land from which not even a single slave could escape, under the absolute power of a Pharaoh who bathed in the blood of Jewish children. They were in the greatest poverty, broken by harsh decrees and *avodas parech*—labor that breaks both the body and the neshamah. And yet, Pharaoh’s power was broken; the liberation of the **entire** people arrived, and this liberation was "with a high hand and with great wealth" (Shemos 14:8).

The Geulah in the spiritual sense was similar—it also went from one extreme to the other: Jews were sunk in the forty-nine gates of tumah, down to the level of avodah zarah—and suddenly, the Ribono shel Olam revealed Himself to them in His glory, and within a matter of weeks, they all stood at Har Sinai, at the highest level of holiness and prophecy. The Ribono shel Olam spoke to each one of them directly, without any intermediary, not even Moshe Rabbeinu, and said: "I am Hashem **your G-d**!"

* * *

The lesson and instructions from this are:

Whatever the state of a Jew may be—as a collective or as an individual—and however the situation may appear according to human intellect, a Jew must **every** day remember Yetzias Mitzrayim. He must strive and act for complete freedom and Geulah—a Geulah "with a high hand and with great wealth"—from all manner of enslavements and from all obstacles on the path of his personal "Mitzrayim," until he becomes a "kingdom of priests and a holy nation" and receives the Torah—all "as in the days of your coming out of the land of Mitzrayim."

One must not stop or hesitate on the way; one must not be satisfied with initial achievements. One must go further, rising higher and higher, until one hears and feels the "I am Hashem your G-d."

* * *

These instructions are especially important and timely in our era, when a movement is noticeable—not only among individuals but also among entire groups and strata—to free themselves from spiritual enslavement and to walk on the path of true freedom: a complete liberation from the fear of **"what will the goy say."**

The "goy" in the simple sense of the word, the "goyish" talk of a wayward Jew, and the "goy" that resides within oneself—the yetzer hara.

To them is the call of Pesach: Do not stop, go further, rise higher, and—with a high hand.

And then it is certain that you will go out to freedom "with our youth and with our elders, with our sons and with our daughters," with great wealth.

With the blessing of a kosher and joyous Chag HaPesach, and that speedily in our days, the promise may be fulfilled: "As in the days of your coming out of the land of Mitzrayim, I will show him wonders" through Mashiach Tzidkeinu.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 6559

6559

B.H., 11 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Avraham Yitzchak HaKohen
Known as Prof. Katz

Greetings and blessings,

Many thanks for your letter and the enclosed clippings from periodicals, which are returned herewith as you requested.

I must state that I remain firm in my opinion that the proposal to resolve the question of registering children whose mother is not Jewish and has not undergone conversion by adding an additional title—whatever it may be—to the title "Jew" or the like, including the proposal to register them as "Jew not identifying" or similar, carries the greatest danger of causing a rift within broad circles of our people, the Children of Israel. This is in addition to other negative aspects, such as confusion and deception regarding marriage, if not in Eretz Yisrael (may it be built and established), then certainly in the Diaspora, etc., etc.

I have already hinted elsewhere that it is instructive that precisely during the ascent of the Children of Israel from the Babylonian exile to Eretz Yisrael, they took the most stringent measures to prevent any matter of assimilation, intermingling with foreign nations, etc., as recounted in the Book of Ezra. And although we have not yet merited the ascent from exile to redemption, the common denominator—and even more so—exists in our days, for not only did forty thousand ascend to Eretz Yisrael as was the case then, but many times that number. Such a mass ascent requires not the weakening of the barriers that distinguish between Israel and the nations, but on the contrary, their strengthening and fortification. This is not a matter for those who are religiously observant only, or in the religious sphere alone, but a fundamental point in the existence of the people of the Children of Israel in all areas, including the security sphere in the literal sense, and more.

Incidentally, regarding your note on the contradiction between the Law of Return and the question of registering those of mixed parentage, and likewise the contradiction in the opposite direction from this question to the Marriage Law: while you are, of course, one hundred percent correct, I intentionally and precisely avoided touching upon this matter in my letters responding to the question of "Who is a Jew." This is because even if the aforementioned contradictions did not exist and the question of "Who is a Jew" were at the top of the agenda, it would not change at all the simplicity and validity of the answer to this question.

Furthermore, regarding the difficulty that many ask and wonder about—how to reconcile the solution with the point of discrimination, "Heaven forbid to touch upon this"—I have never understood what one has to do with the other. After all, many Christians, Muslims, etc., live in Eretz Yisrael, and according to the legislation, they too are granted the right of non-discrimination; yet, it would never occur to anyone to register them as Jews, etc. In this, there is also an answer to what you mention in your letter—that security considerations and democratic considerations have an influence on the manner of resolving the question of "Who is a Jew"—which, in my opinion, is not the case, as stated above.

This is the place for me to reiterate my thanks for lending me the second booklet, which is returned separately, along with a list of the maamarim and letters contained within it that, to the best of my memory, have already been published. Surely, if you find any more such manuscripts in your possession, you will allow me to see and examine them. My thanks are given to you in advance.

With respect and blessing, and in particular, a blessing for a kosher and happy Pesach.

## 6807

B"H, 11 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn

Agudas N'shei U'Bnos Chabad, Cleveland Branch
May Hashem grant them life

Blessings and peace!

It was with satisfaction that I read the report of the annual luncheon, which took place this past 19 Adar Sheni.

I wish to express my thanks and blessing to all the participants, and especially to those who assisted in the success of this gathering, for the purpose of strengthening Yiddishkeit in all areas, and especially among Jewish women. They are the ones titled with the name *akeres habayis*—the primary founder and leader of the Jewish home—to bring happiness and joy to themselves, to their husbands, and to their children, *shichyu*.

I also hope that this gathering, seeing the good work, will provide you with even more energy and effort to continue this holy work in an ever-increasing measure, and consequently, to receive the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach in an ever-greater measure.

As we approach the Yom Tov of Pesach, this should also serve as a lesson, especially for Jewish women, to grasp the great *zechus* and, at the same time, the responsibility to be the ones—just as in the past—of whom it can be said: *B'zechus nashim tzidkaniyos nig'alu avoseinu mimitzrayim*—in the merit of righteous women, our ancestors were redeemed from Mitzrayim—and also from the current *galus*, through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, speedily in our days.

Your contribution for the *bosei sefer limlachah* in Kfar Chabad was also received, [amounting to] 620 (included in the 1000 from the Kinsman branch).

And surely the promise of the *Hakadosh Baruch Hu* will descend in actuality, that the *zechus* of *tzedakah* will stand by each of you and your households for whatever you are in need of, both in *gashmiyus* and in *ruchniyus*.

With a wish for a *kasher'n* and happy Yom Tov of Pesach, and to bring down that joy into all the days of the entire year.

## 6,808

6,808

B"H, 11 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, whose work is the work of Heaven, R' Ben Zion Moshe Yair, shlita, and his son, the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, R' Yosef Menachem, shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 7 Nissan, which arrived at the appropriate time. Thank you, thank you for fulfilling my request to deliver *michla d'meheimenusa* and *michla d'asvasa* to Kfar Chabad in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, where "Hashem commanded the blessing..."

May it be the will [of Hashem] that merit is brought about through those who are meritorious, in a way that is good, revealed, and manifest, for days of merit. For all the days of the month of Nissan are in the category of *Rosh Chodesh* (Shelah, end of Parshas Bo). Although work is permitted (the *sefirah* of *Malchus*), they are nevertheless not called "days of action," because they receive from Above, unlike the days of action (as explained at length in the *maamar* beginning *Machar Chodesh* by the Rebbe the Tzemach Tzedek, printed in *Sefer Ohr HaTorah, Bereishis*). Thus, may there be an increase below ten *tefachim* from above, and even higher, to the place of "For I am Hashem your Healer" (*Ki Ani Hashem Rofecha*), the acronym of which is *Arik*, as explained in the writings of the Arizal. And this is also akin to the concept of Pesach—a beginning from *mochin d'gadlus*.

With respect and double blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach, in the manner mentioned above.

#### ***6,808***

**The esteemed Rabbi... shlita:** Weinstock, Jerusalem. Additional letters to R' Y.M. — see above, Vol. 14, 4,877.

*Chof-Tes* 5,707.

## 6,809

6,809

B"H, 12 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write that you have been studying at the seminary for some time, yet despite this, you are unable to befriend the other students and find yourself lonely, which naturally affects your mood, etc.

In general, you should consult with your teachers as to which of the students would be most suitable to befriend, and subsequently make an effort to do so.

Broadly speaking, the advice to facilitate friendship is to contemplate the fact that no person is perfect in all virtues, and you are included in this—meaning that you, too, lack perfection. Therefore, one should not demand or be overly particular that one’s friends be perfect in all matters and in every detail.

Furthermore, our Sages of blessed memory have testified that the daughters of Israel are beautiful, and each one of them is called a daughter of Sarah, Rivkah, Rachel, and Leah. Certainly, each one possesses many virtues, and in time you will recognize them; through this friendship, your own perfection will also increase.

The more you explain this point to yourself, the easier it will be for you to form friendships, and your isolation will pass. As our Sages have already taught: "Acquire a friend for yourself."

Regarding what you wrote about a shidduch, that you have been offered some from time to time, but they did not come to fruition—since you still have a period of time to study at the seminary, surely when the time for completing your studies draws near, Hashem will provide you with the shidduch that is appropriate for you.

With blessings for good tidings and a kosher and happy Pesach,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
In my office, we do not write in French, which is why this response is in Lashon HaKodesh. However, you may continue to write in French, as I am able to read that language.

## 6,410

B"H, 12 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 5 Nissan, in which you write regarding the thought of traveling from [your location] for the final days of Pesach.

The wonder of the matter is understood, for this is the first Pesach in your holy community—the time of our freedom and the exodus from one extreme to the other. This is an auspicious time when the members of the community should anticipate a special influence and a special awakening. If this is true for the first days, it is certainly true for the final days, and for two reasons: because they arrive after the preparation of the first days and the days of Chol Hamoed, and furthermore, because Shevi’i Shel Pesach—the splitting of the sea—and Acharon Shel Pesach represent the concept of the final redemption and the coming of Moshiach, as is explained at length in the holy sichos of our Rebbeim, and as is also explained in the Haftorah of the day.

May it be the will of Hashem that, out of joy and goodness of heart over the great success granted to you and your wife, the Rabbanit—may she live—in spreading the wellsprings in your community, and from there may they spread to its surroundings as well, you will utilize every single day, especially the Yomim Tovim and particularly Acharon Shel Pesach, for the aforementioned purpose, and in the direction of "u'fartzta"—which is Moshiach—and especially during the Seudata D'Malka Meshicha.

With blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach, and for good tidings in general and in particular.

#### ***6,410***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 22, p. 290.

## 6,811

B”H, 12 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter regarding the question that there are women who wish to participate in the gatherings of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, and there is hope that through this they will gain much in yiras shamayim; yet, on the other hand, there are regular members who do not get along well with the aforementioned—what should be the solution to this problem?

To every member of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, and especially to the active members and the founder in particular, the supreme importance of bringing a Yid—man or woman—closer to Yiddishkeit must be explained. Consider how much the Nesi’ei Chabad sacrificed themselves for this, even to the point of literal mesiras nefesh. Furthermore, they gave of their time—time they could have utilized to ascend higher and higher—in order to bring a Yid who seemingly had no connection to even a minor mitzvah, and certainly to a major mitzvah.

The fact that the Nesi’im related this to us is surely not for the sake of a nice story, but so that everyone should learn from it how one is required to act in such matters. Although it is understood that there is no comparison between the sacrifices of the Nesi’im and those of others, it was nevertheless done in this manner.

I hope that after explaining the above, the members of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad will certainly set aside their grievances so that a Yiddishe neshamah may draw closer to Hakadosh Baruch Hu, His Torah, and His mitzvos. Not only should they agree to this, but they should recognize their gratitude to Hakadosh Baruch Hu for giving them such an opportunity—to set aside a grievance, etc., **for the sake of the holiness of His Name, may He be blessed.**

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above and for the expansion of the work of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, and for a kosher and happy Pesach,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6412

B"H, 12 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter, in which you write about the state of your health, the headaches, and also regarding the swelling in the neck area.

It is understood that you must consult with a specialist physician once again and follow their instructions.

Regarding what you wrote about the headaches, it can be said that this comes about because your cycle is not regular. Once it is regulated through appropriate treatments, the pains will, at the very least, be lighter.

At an auspicious time, I will mention your aunt, may she live, at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever she needs, in accordance with the content of your letter—and specifically regarding a suitable shidduch.

Your aunt, may she live, must firmly resolve that when she marries, she will conduct her home as is fitting for a kosher daughter of Israel, through the observance of Taharas HaMishpacha, the observance of Shabbos, keeping kosher, and so on. Hashem, who sees into the heart, knowing that she has resolved this firmly and sincerely and will indeed act accordingly, will provide her with a good shidduch.

The same applies regarding her sister, may she live.

With blessings for good tidings and for a kosher and happy Pesach,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6,413

6,413

B”H, 13 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt,
May Hashem keep them alive,

Greetings and blessings!

In connection with the Second National Convention of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt, which will take place this coming Sunday, the Rebbe shlita has seen fit to send a telegram, a copy of which is transcribed here:

Tzeirei Agudas Chabad Convention, Kfar Chabad.

My blessing is sent to the participants of the convention, and through them, to all members of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in the Holy Land and abroad, for lively, successful, and illuminating activity within ever-expanding circles, to disseminate traditional Judaism and its wellsprings. And through this, to instill in every single man and woman of Bnei Yisrael the recognition that they are counted among the legions of Hashem, and that their vital task is to go out and lead their surroundings from inner slavery and exile to true freedom, with a high hand and great wealth—in all your ways. And by doing so, you hasten the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu and our complete and true redemption.

Wishing you joyous festivals,

**Menachem Schneerson**

The telegram was surely received in due time, and you will certainly inform us here of everything that transpires, and regarding this too, it is said: whoever does more is praiseworthy.

With respect and blessings for a kosher and joyous holiday,

Secretary

#### ***6,413***

**Second National Convention:** See above, Vol. 14, Letter 5,171.

## 6414

B”H, 14 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

... I was very pleased to read in your letter about the summer camp for the daughters of Yisrael, may they live. The importance of this matter certainly requires no explanation to one such as yourself, and we have already spoken many times on various occasions about how one can see tangibly that in summer camps—and generally during the vacation days from studies—one can influence the boys and girls much more than during the school year. After all, at that time there is no distraction from secular studies; they are under the authority of the camp all day, and the parents, the environment, and the atmosphere at home do not interfere, etc. May it be the will [of Hashem] that you succeed in the aforementioned, and may the impression of the girls’ stay in camp be evident in the weeks and months that follow, in the improvement of their health in ruchniyus, in addition to the improvement in gashmiyus.

Regarding what you wrote about the name, since it has already been publicized, what is the reason for changing it?

I am sure you have publicized the above in the Lubavitch Yeshiva and in the other educational institutions in your vicinity, and I, too, will speak with anash at the appropriate opportunity.

## 6815

B"H, Erev Chag HaPesach, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Students of Beis Rivkah, class...
May Hashem grant them life.

Blessings and peace!

I was pleased to receive your letter with the contribution for Maos Chittim, to assist the needy with their Pesach requirements. Surely, the merit of this mitzvah will assist you all to ascend ever higher in your studies and in good conduct.

Certainly, each of you belongs to the category of a "wise child." Since there are three lesser categories, I hope that you will serve as a living example for Jewish children in general, and in particular for those with whom you come into contact, so that they too will observe "the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances that Hashem has commanded."

With blessings for health and success in all the above, and for a kosher and happy Pesach,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

## 6816

6816

Erev Pesach, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Eliezer Steinman,

Greetings and blessings!

It has been a very long time since I received any news from you, even though I inquire about how you are doing through Anash in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established.

As we approach the festival of Pesach, the time of our freedom, which is coming upon us and all of Klal Yisrael for good, I send you my blessing for true freedom—freedom, both physical and spiritual, from all matters that distract from the avodah of Hashem with joy and a good heart—and that you may draw this freedom and joy into the entire year ahead.

I take the liberty of enclosing a copy of my general letter for the festival of Pesach, which in truth is relevant all year round, and I hope it will be of interest to you.

With respect, esteem, and blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach.

Regarding what you wrote in your last letter—that you do not see any blessing in polemics, and that one must distance oneself from all controversy, even if it is for the sake of Heaven—you will surely forgive me if I do not entirely agree with you on this. It is not, Heaven forbid, that I am a proponent of controversy for the sake of Heaven; rather, in the present case, the discussion is not about that point. The proposal was regarding the negation of a conclusion that is entirely undesirable—a conclusion that a reader of your books might naturally draw when they see a certain name mentioned therein. As is the nature of people, both in the past and especially in our generation, the reader does not search or investigate the depth of the matter, nor do they examine every word and letter to see how much praise is contained in the mention of a certain person, or to discern whether there is a reservation included. Our Sages of blessed memory have already warned us in the Mishnah: "Be careful with your words, lest you be exiled to a place of poisonous waters, and the students who come after you will drink..." Every author and writer invests a part of his soul—or at the very least, ought to invest a part of his soul—into his books and compositions. Wherever the book goes, the author goes with it; and if it is a place of "poisonous waters," then the students are in that same category. Therefore, extreme caution is required. This is the case in our present matter.

The reason I am repeating this matter once again is simply—not, Heaven forbid, to be contentious—but to express my opinion regarding future opportunities of the type mentioned. Furthermore, it is possible that there will be an opportunity to clarify the reservation you wrote about in your letter, in an appropriate manner and in an appropriate place.

Regarding your further point that one should show gratitude to anyone from whom they have learned even one letter or one word, and that one should not deprive any creature of their reward: the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life, in this regard is that there is one exception to this rule, and that is in the realm of faith and the opposite of faith—incitement to another religion. How wondrous are the words of the great teacher, the Rambam, at the end of his work Mishneh Torah (Hilchos Melachim, end of Chapter 11), regarding Yeshu and Muhammad, that "they only serve to pave the way for Melech HaMoshiach and to prepare the entire world to serve Hashem together." Yet, at the same time, three times a day we mention them in the Aleinu prayer with the well-known epithets, and the aforementioned words of the Rambam have almost been forgotten (due to censorship, etc.). It is a great principle in the laws of Yisrael that one must not find merit in such matters. Anyone who contemplates the history of Yisrael throughout the time of its wanderings will see that it is precisely this zeal that has enabled us to "stand today, all of you, before Hashem your G-d," as the verse states.

#### ***6816***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 16, p. 561, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**Mr. Steinman:** Additional letters to him appear above in 6663, and in the references noted there.

## 6,717

6,717

B"H, 18 Nissan, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi Alter, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 11 Nissan, and I am certain that in the meantime you have also received my letter from that same date.

Regarding what you wrote about the booklets of maamarim that have dates from the years 5755 and so on, it is understood that I am interested in knowing these dates. This also applies to Torah Or and Likutei Torah, as you write that there are only very few for which the dates are currently known to me [although a search is still required in several old booklets, where the date is sometimes preserved, even though it seems the early Chassidim did not concern themselves with this at all. Furthermore, they did not even consider whose maamar it was, arguing: "What difference does it make?"—since there is no issue of a dispute where one must determine the halacha]. Ever since I began collecting information on this [which has effectively resulted, for the time being, in an index of the maamarim of Admur Moharash and an index of the maamarim of Admur Moharashab—may the memory of the righteous and holy be for a blessing, may their merit protect us].

With respect and blessings.

Due to the sanctity of the festival, the Rebbe shlita did not sign, and I am signing on his behalf.

Secretary

#### ***6,717***

**Rabbi Alter, may he live and be well:** Hilvitz, Johannesburg. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 16, 5,839, and in the notes indicated there.

## 6118

6118

B”H, 18 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of 2 Nissan, in which you write of your desire to be a teacher for the children of Israel—and surely your intention is to teach in an educational framework that is pure, based on our holy Torah, the Torah of life.

For it is only this that leads the son and daughter of Israel to their true happiness—happiness in material matters and happiness in spiritual matters.

And since it appears from your activities until now that you are successful in the field of education, this resolution seems very appropriate.

From this it is understood that regarding the examinations, you should choose those examinations that are suitable for achieving the aforementioned goal—that is, acquiring the knowledge and certifications that will further qualify you to fulfill the most important and lofty role mentioned above.

And the merit of your current engagement in drawing the hearts of your students to our Father in Heaven through our Torah, the Torah of life, and its mitzvos, will stand for you as an addition of blessing and success in your personal affairs as well.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above, and with an increase in your work at the Oholei Yosef Yitzchak Lubavitch institution,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
It was pleasing for me to see in your letter a request for a blessing for the students of your class. I will express my blessing in the context of these days, the days of the Festival of Matzos, which began on the first night with the conducting of the Seder. At its beginning, there are four sons, one wise and descending from level to level.

May it be the will of our Father in Heaven—regarding whom it is said, “You are children to Hashem your G-d”—that very soon all the children of Israel, of all the aforementioned types, will ascend from level to level until they are united in the most lofty category, the wise son. And so too regarding the students of your class: that they should all be, without exception, only of the aforementioned category of the wise son, through study with diligence and perseverance in our Torah, of which it is said, “For it is your wisdom and your understanding,” and through the observance of its mitzvos.

May it be His will that you explain all of the above in words that come from the heart, which enter the hearts of those who hear them.

## 6,319

[19 Nissan 5719]

...Regarding what you wrote about the book that was given to one of the students, and the content of the book shocked him, etc.—certainly, in an appropriate manner, you must ensure that such books do not enter the four amos of any of the students. However, this matter must be handled in a way that does not stimulate their curiosity, in accordance with the verse, "Stolen waters are sweet." Therefore, you should consult with the faculty in an appropriate manner, for it is said, "In the multitude of counselors there is salvation."

#### ***6,319***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 22, p. 403.

## 6,820

6,820

B”H, 20 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Blessings and peace!

After a very long interval, your letter of 9 Nissan was received, and it was a pleasure for me to read about your activities in organizing Shabbos gatherings, a girls’ club, etc. It is understandable that you feel regret regarding the weakness that followed in your activities, and in some of them, a cessation as well.

My hope is that when you bring this matter once again to the attention of the N’shei U’Bnos Chabad, there will be a change for the better. The matter is of the utmost importance and loftiness, and every effort and endeavor in this regard is worthwhile. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding what has been stated.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the reasoning that, as the new year approaches, you will leave your teaching position in the "Reshet" because you find it difficult to continue with the study required for concentration of thought, etc.:

It is understood that this is not correct at all. Precisely because of the reasons you write—that you feel talent and success in the aforementioned field—it is understood that you must prepare everything necessary for the success of your activities. We see tangibly that a small deficiency in preparation leads to a deficiency many times greater in the activity, the effectiveness, and the benefit of the teaching. Furthermore, the latter deficiency affects many others—I refer to the entire class. It is certainly superfluous to elaborate on such a simple matter; I have only come to add that you should persist in your own studies. We see tangibly that one succeeds in this more easily when studying in a *chavrusa* with one or two friends.

From the above, it is understood that in my opinion, you should prepare everything necessary for the coming school term (if preparation for this is required). In any case, you should continue with your studies with the necessary vigor, and may Hashem grant you success. It is understood that the above has no bearing on the manner and utilization of the upcoming vacation days, provided it does not affect the quality of the preparation for the next school term.

With blessings for good news in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6821

B"H, 23 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

We acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, along with your request for a blessing and a *pidyon nefesh*.

At an opportune time, it will be read at the holy gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It is understood that any increase in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*—and no matter how good the situation may be in this regard, there is always room to add more in matters of goodness and holiness—brings about an increase in the blessings of Hashem in all that a person and their household require.

Regarding what you wrote, that it seems to you that since you purchased the pharmacy, you have been experiencing events that are out of the ordinary:

It should be investigated whether there was any issue of *hasagas gevul* (encroachment on another’s livelihood) or the like in this purchase. If there is any doubt in the matter—and since a person is biased toward themselves—it is understood that you should clarify the *Daas Torah* on this with a Rav who is a *moreh hora'ah*.

May Hashem, who watches over each and every person with *hashgacha pratis*, lead you toward goodness, both materially and spiritually.

It is appropriate that you have your *tefillin* checked, and likewise the *mezuzos* in your home, to ensure they are all *kosher* according to the law. And she should maintain the good custom of virtuous Jewish women to set aside coins for *tzedakah* before lighting the candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6822

6822

B"H, 25 Nissan, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished Rav, a man of many accomplishments, Rabbi Yeshaya, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter from Chol Hamoed Pesach, along with the enclosure, in which you write about your time studying at the Yeshiva Achei Tmimim Lubavitch in the city of Worcester, under the administration of the man of fine character and many accomplishments, R' Yehuda Zvi Fogelman, may he live. You also mention that you remain in contact with him through correspondence, and that you distributed Matzah Shmurah to everyone possible.

May it be the will of Hashem that you utilize the talents with which you have been gifted, and the influence granted to you by Divine Providence, for the dissemination of Torah and Yiddishkeit in ever-expanding circles, and especially among the Bnei Yisrael who are serving in the military.

I have already spoken at greater length elsewhere that one should derive a lesson for one’s avodah to one’s Creator from every matter, and especially from the situation in which one finds oneself for several years. In this case, the foundation and existence of the military depend on absolute kabbalas ol—without raising difficulties and not even asking questions—but rather, first and foremost, to fulfill the order in its entirety.

Furthermore, there is a second point: the weakness of one member of the military in this regard is not merely a private matter for him alone; rather, it affects all his comrades in his unit, and through this, the military camp as a whole, and the defense of the country in its entirety.

And if this is true regarding orders given by flesh and blood, how much more so does it apply to the commands of the Supreme Commander of all of creation, the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He. Further elaboration on this is surely unnecessary.

With blessings that you succeed in all of the above, the inner point of which is to increase your own conduct in a manner of "moistening so as to moisten" (influencing others), and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

With blessings for good tidings.

P.S.
Surely you will continue to send what you have printed in the future, and thank you in advance.

## 6823

B"H, 26 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Thursday, in which you write that you are finishing school and share your thoughts regarding the continuation of your studies.

It is understood and simple that you should study in a yeshiva where our holy Torah—the Torah of life—is studied throughout the entire day. Our holy Torah is also a "Torah of life" in the literal sense, "the guide for a happy life in every detail, both materially and spiritually," as it is written, "If you walk in My statutes," which our Sages (cited in Rashi's commentary) explain to mean that you should be toiling in Torah, and [then] "I will give your rains," etc., and all the blessings enumerated in the parsha.

Regarding what you wrote—that some say one must know everything—it is generally impossible for a person to know everything. For even the most learned individuals, both among the Jewish people and among the nations of the world, know only a **small** fraction of what is possible to know. In any event, at your age, you need and are obligated to dedicate all your time [at the very least for several years] solely to the study of the Torah that was given to us by the Creator and Leader of the world. May it be His will that you study with consistency and diligence, and may you succeed.

It is appropriate that you maintain the practice of reciting the monthly Tehillim shiur—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—every day after morning davening, at the very least from this point forward.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6824

B"H, 26 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Mr. Yitzchak Edel
Shalom u’Bracha!

Following a very long hiatus, your letter was received, and subsequently the books you sent via Rabbi A. Ch. Glitzenstein.

Many thanks for this gift. Surely you will continue to send your publications in the aforementioned field here in the future, as the library here serves the benefit of the many.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the processing of Chabad nigunim in a way that preserves their inner and essential integrity:

I have requested that anash connect with you regarding this matter and its details. May it be the will of Hashem that, in the style of our Sages, "from him and from them, the Supernal Name is praised." It is well known how highly Chassidus Chabad values the matter of nigunim, as is also brought in various places in the introduction to the *Sefer HaNigunim* which you possess.

Since we have recently come from the festival of Pesach, the time of our freedom, and you mention in your letter—among the primary nigunim—the *U’fartzta* nigun, I have enclosed a copy of my general letter for that festival. May it be the will of Hashem that the intention of this festival—to draw its joy and its themes into the weeks and months that follow—be fulfilled as well.

With respect and blessing.

***

**Mr. Yitzchak Edel:** Another letter to him—see 6757.

## 6825

6825

B"H, 27 Nissan, 5719

Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the avreich Avraham Abush Mordechai, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your notification regarding your birthday:

You have surely followed the custom of anash in recent times regarding one’s birthday. May it be the will of Hashem that this be a year of success for you in the study of Torah and the performance of mitzvos with hidur.

With blessings for Torah study with yiras shamayim,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

Regarding your questions:

a) Concerning the distress of Ben Azzai that he did not serve Rabbi Yishmael (Chullin 71a), even though he is the one who said (Bechoros 58a) that all the Sages of Yisrael appear to him like a garlic peel, etc., except for Rabbi Akiva—aside from the fact that one could say his latter statement was made when he had grown in wisdom, etc., the difficulty is fundamentally non-existent. For Rabbi Yishmael was unique in the Mishnayos he had received and in his mastery of Gemara, etc. (see Eiruvin 13a) [even though he was also an "uprooter of mountains"—Toras Kohanim to Tazria 13:17]. And similarly in Avos, Chapter 2, Mishnah 9, see there.

b) Regarding your doubt as to whether you counted Sefiras HaOmer—see Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim 489:8, which is also transcribed in the Shulchan Aruch of the Alter Rebbe there. In Shu"t Shoel U'Meishiv (Mahadura Daled, Vol. 3, 27), he asks why we do not say that we are certain he did not count if thirty days have not yet passed, etc. Likewise, among the Acharonim (many of whom are cited in Orchot Chayim—the new one—489 there, and in Sdei Chemed, Asifat Dinim, Ma'arechet Brachos 1:19), there is a struggle with this, given the opinion of those (and they are many) who hold that a sfeik sfeika in brachos is also to be treated leniently. And although one can pilpul on their words, we do not deviate from a clear psak din in the Shulchan Aruch for the sake of pilpul.

#### ***6825***

**Avraham Abush Mordechai:** Porush-Glickman, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 16, 5791, and in the references noted in the footnotes there. Below, 6897.

## 6826

B"H, 27 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn

The Administration of Yeshivas Achei Temimim Lubavitch
in Rishon LeZion
May Hashem keep them alive

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the last day of Pesach—and the previous one—written by the esteemed Rabbi, the Chossid, man of many accomplishments, etc., R' Chaim Shaul, may he live, Brook.

At a time of favor, I will mention the holy institution with all its details—those you wrote of, as well as the other details—at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Based on the known mesiras nefesh of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, for these institutions, it is certain that this will add to the hisorerus of the students, and there will be an increase in the blessings of Hashem and success in all that pertains to the institution, even below ten tefachim, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

And in accordance with what has been discussed many times regarding the concept of "u'faratzta" (and you shall spread out) to the west, to the east, to the north, and to the south, and reaching an inheritance without boundaries—and this is connected especially to the time of our freedom from which we have just emerged, the essence of which is the exodus from Mitzrayim (Egypt) and its limitations—may it be Hashem’s will that this also manifest in the institution, visible to physical eyes: an inheritance without boundaries, both in quantity and in quality, in all its affairs. Likewise, may there be an increase in the blessings of Hashem in the personal matters of each and every one of you, may you live and be well; the merit of the many will surely assist you in this as well.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***6826***

**And in accordance with what has been discussed:** See also above, letter 6541, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 6,827

6,827

B"H, 27 Nissan, 5719

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 23 Nissan, after the longest of breaks.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in accordance with the content of your letter.

Regarding what you explain about your state of mind—the weakness in some of the activities of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad and Anash, specifically in...

It is understood, and indeed simple, that the opposite is true. It is similar to a person himself: when one feels weakness in one of his limbs, it is obvious that this is not a reason for him to bring weakness upon the rest of his limbs. On the contrary, one of the methods of healing the limb that has weakened is through strengthening and adding extra strength to the other limbs.

And surely all of Klal Yisrael is one complete stature, and likewise, stature within stature—Anash Chabad and Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in particular.

In other words, if you see (and surely everything is by Divine Providence) that in one point or more there is a weakness, it is certainly a directive from Above that you must arouse those who are connected to that weakness, and work on yourself—that you should double and triple the aforementioned activities in order to fill what is lacking and add more. And this is also the essence of what was said—that since "according to the camel is the burden," the demand for this addition is proof that you have also been given additional strength, and you have the ability to add much more than your activities until now. You only need to bring the aforementioned from potential into action, and this is easy to understand. The matter is so straightforward that it requires no further explanation. I will await good news regarding all of the above, as well as your efforts with your colleagues on the aforementioned point; and in this, too, "there is nothing that can stand before the will."

With blessings for good news,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

#### ***6,827***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 23, p. 462.

## 6828

6828

B"H, 28 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Abba, may he live, Halevi

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of 25 Nissan, I was pleased to read that even though, in your opinion, the matter of traveling for the purpose of *Ufaratzta* was difficult for you, you nevertheless proceeded and are continuing. Happy is your portion, that you immediately saw good fruit for your labor, as you spoke to those who were listening, for they heard the explanatory lectures and so on with special attentiveness. This is truly in the category of "You shall see your world in your lifetime." As is explained in Chassidus, the concept of the World to Come is the radiance of one’s Torah and *mitzvos* and the reward of the *mitzvos*. I am strengthened in my confidence that you will continue in the aforementioned, and not only that, but that you will increase in it. The magnitude of the necessity for this cannot be sufficiently explained or imagined, especially regarding the influence on the youth, which often affects the standing (*shtel*) of the entire home and the members of the household.

Regarding your question: Is it permissible to speak about matters of Chassidus in the form of a lecture or a conversation?

It is understood that not only is it permissible, but it is also necessary. The question itself is surprising, after what is explained in the letter of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (Rashab), may his soul be bound in the bond of life, that even regarding the study of Tanya, if it leads to *avodah*, the discussion is worthwhile, etc. (printed in *HaTamim* and in the supplement to *Kuntres Eitz Chaim*, published by Kehot), and it is well-known and famous among *anash*. If this was the case in those years, how much more so in our current era. Furthermore, an instruction has been added over time regarding such matters: that the test to determine the source of a particular question is to see in what way it affects *avodah* in practice (*Hayom Yom*, 23 Sivan).

The same applies to your question regarding whether it is permissible to add an explanation to a *maamar* by inserting (*areinshtellen*) in the middle of the *maamar* an explanation of a point from another *maamar*, in a place where the listeners are not knowledgeable in Chassidus; this, too, is desirable and correct.

At a time of favor, please mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may the merit of the righteous protect us, each according to what they need, appropriate to the content of your letter.

May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good tidings regarding yourself and each and every one of the residents of Kfar Chabad, for there Hashem has commanded the blessing.

With blessings for good news regarding all that was mentioned, and with a blessing in the style of my father-in-law, the Rebbe: *Tzu a gezunten zumer* (to a healthy summer),

**M. Schneerson**

***

**6828**

**R' Abba, may he live:** Levin, Kfar Chabad. Another letter to him — above, Vol. 13, 4377.

**Regarding *Ufaratzta*:** See above, letter 6141, and the references in the notes there.

## 6,829

B"H, 28 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Yitzchak HaLevi, may he live:

Shalom and Bracha!

After a very long interval, your letter of 27 Nissan, along with its enclosure, was received.

At an auspicious time, it will be read at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Surely, for someone like you, it is unnecessary to elaborate on the statement of our Sages that action alone is insufficient; rather, the action must be performed specifically in the proper manner. It is understood that my intent regarding you is that even after what you write—that when you find the time, you study Nigleh and Nistar, etc.—one who has been gifted with the power to influence others, it is clear that this is part of his inner mission. Furthermore, this must be done specifically in the "proper manner" mentioned above. If at all times and in all places the approach of "I have saved my own soul" was condemned (see Zohar, Vol. I, 106a), how much more so in our era and in the United States, where in these years a strong awakening is evident among the youth toward teshuvah to Hashem, His Torah, and His mitzvos. The only thing is that they need someone to show them the way. We see tangibly that when they hear these things not in their spoken language, or from someone who is decades older than them, it does not have as much influence as when they hear it in the language of the land and from someone who is close to them in age. It is clear, and there is no need to elaborate on such a simple matter.

May it be the will of Hashem that, at the very least, from here on out you will begin to influence others and increase this effort more and more, and the merit of the many will assist you.

I look forward to hearing good news regarding all of the above.

#### 6,829

**R' Yitzchak:** Levi, Long Beach.

## 6,830

[28 Nissan, 5719]

After a very long interval, your letter and *pidyon nefesh* for [Name] *shlita* were received.

At an *eis ratzon*, I will mention him at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever he requires, in accordance with the contents of your letter.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that the merit of the *tzedakah* (which you will find enclosed) and the merit of your increased involvement in matters of Torah and *mitzvos* in general, stand you in good stead for the improvement of your health and for all that you and your family *shiy* require.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that in all the matters you write about, you continue to hear good tidings—both regarding the public and, in particular, regarding yourself—good for Heaven and good for mankind.

#### ***6,830***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 434.

## 6831

B"H, 29 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chassid, G-d-fearing and learned, occupied with communal needs,
R' Ephraim Eliezer, may he live, HaKohen

Shalom u'Bracha!

Today I received your letter before I departed for the holy Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and I mentioned there all those whom you wrote about.

May it be the will of Hashem, since we are currently between the month of Nissan—the month of redemption and miracles—and the month of Iyar, in which the "radiant ones of the world" were born, and considering the well-known words of the Arizal that these days, when they are remembered (and also when they are "made" / actualized), there will be an increase in all matters, both private and general, in the spirit of freedom and redemption, to increase radiance in the world as well—radiance and illuminations from the avodah of our forefathers. As is explained in Tanya, all their limbs and actions were solely for the supreme will throughout their lives—the level of a *merkavah*.

With blessings for good tidings.

P.S.

Regarding the matter of the name Rachel, which you write was given without any specific reason, it is worth noting what is brought down as a halachic ruling in the *nigleh* as well (Noda B'Yehudah, Orach Chaim, section 26, subsection 20), that Iyar is an acronym for Avraham, Yitzchak, Yaakov, Rachel [the *merkavah*]. And regarding the freedom and redemption, it is said, "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders." This will come about through the fact that "Rachel is weeping for her children," which accomplishes that "So says Hashem... and the children shall return to their own border."

#### ***6831***

**R' Ephraim Eliezer:** Yalles, Philadelphia. Additional letters to him—above 6582, and in the references noted there.

## 6832

B"H, 29 Nissan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing man, a man of many accomplishments, etc., Aharon Nachman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

After a delay, I received your letter from Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Acharei, along with the enclosure. It is surprising that you are brief where you should have been expansive, specifically regarding the activities of the Matzah Shmurah campaign, etc. While there is room to judge this favorably—that you will surely see to it that others write about this with the necessary detail—may it be His will that you bring good news regarding the points you mentioned. You are certainly correct that for several of them, **now** is the opportune time to act successfully and without excessive difficulties. Regarding such matters, it is said: "One hour earlier is better," though, of course, it must be done with a prepared plan and in pleasant ways.

What is also evident from your letter is that, regrettably, it seems that this year as well, the Matzah Shmurah project was not utilized in an organized manner. This is one of the unique activities of Chabad, providing a way to reach every individual in a pleasant manner, while simultaneously creating the opportunity to share words of inspiration, and the rest is understood.

May it be His will that, since we have come from the month of Nissan and are entering the month of Iyar—the month of *ziv* (radiance), in which the "radiant ones of the world" were born—and knowing the words of the Arizal that when the days are remembered (and thus also) become actualized, may there be an increase in all matters, both private and communal, in the spirit of *tzedakah*, freedom, and redemption. May we increase the radiance in the world—the radiance and illuminations of the deeds of our forefathers, as brought in Tanya, that all their limbs and actions were solely for the Supreme Will throughout all their days.

With blessings for good news in both private and communal matters, and may it be soon.

## 6,833

[Nissan 5719?]

...Regarding what you wrote about the explanation of "You are standing today, etc." — there is no contradiction here, for "you were standing, etc., to enter into the covenant, etc." refers to the time of their entry into the Land, and this dimension exists every single year, which is on the day of Rosh Hashanah. See Likutei Torah, beginning of Parshas Tavo.

And regarding what you wrote about what is the obligation of *matanos la'evyonim* in addition to the *mitzvah* of *tzedakah* — it is simple, because it is a *mitzvah* from the words of the Sages to seek out the needy, and even [to give] to a poor person, etc., and it is not merely a good custom like [giving] *tzedakah* before *davening*, etc.

#### ***6,833***

The letter is to R' Tzvi Freidin, and was written on the margin of his letter dated 13 Nissan 5719.

## 6,834

6,834

B”H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Monday, along with the enclosure.

Regarding what you wrote about your daughters—may they live—and the reason they were not present at the farbrengen of Acharon Shel Pesach: seemingly, the reason is very valid, as it is one of the Ten Commandments to honor one’s mother. However, when taking into account that we are speaking of the farbrengen of Acharon Shel Pesach—the Seudata D’Malka Meshicha and the beginning of a healthy and happy summer—arrangements should have been made to fulfill the commandment mentioned in the Ten Commandments while also ensuring they were present at the farbrengen. The way to achieve this is very simple: by their mother—may she live—also being present at the farbrengen. This is especially true since the reason for the daughters to be present applies with equal, and perhaps even greater, force to the mother—may she live—and this is understood. And although our Sages said that one does not cry over the past, these lines were written because, surely, a question of this nature will arise in the future as well, and this is understood...

With blessings for success and for good news regarding all the above.

## 6,535

[2 Iyar, 5719]

Following a very long hiatus, your letter of 23 Nissan was received, detailing your activities.

It is understandable that there is great wonder and astonishment regarding this very long break. Especially since, from other sources, I have received several reports of your activities over the past months, which were successful and left a good impression—and at times, even a strong impression, etc. Why withhold the good news, especially when it is good for Heaven, good for people, and produces results, as our Sages of blessed memory state in Kiddushin 40a. From the above, it is also understood that your writing that there is no change for the better in your mood due to such-and-such, is certainly not in accordance with reality.

The saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, is already well-known: that the prohibition against lashon hara applies not only regarding others, but also regarding oneself. And the directive of my revered father, the Rebbe, is also known: just as one must know one’s own shortcomings, one must also know one’s own virtues. Furthermore, it should be noted that regarding work with others, sometimes recognizing one’s own virtues is more relevant than recognizing one’s own shortcomings, and the wise will understand.

At a time of favor, may you mention yourself and all your family members, may they live, at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be His will that you bring good tidings regarding each and every one of them.

And since we have come from the month of Nissan and are now in the month of Ziv (Iyar), in which the "radiant ones of the world" were born, and the words of the Arizal are known—that when the days are remembered (and thus also) become actualized, there will be an increase in both specific and general matters, in the service of Hashem, in freedom and in redemption, to increase radiance in the world—the radiance and illuminations of the deeds of our forefathers, as brought in Tanya, that all their limbs and deeds were solely for the Supreme Will throughout all their days.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6,836

6,836

B"H, 3 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in the needs of the community, R' Shlomo, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from Rosh Chodesh Iyar. I am surprised that you do not mention my letter for the holiday of Pesach; surely you will supplement this at the next opportunity.

I was pleased to read in your letter about the strengthening of "Beis Lubavitch" and the plans for further work.

It is surely unnecessary to emphasize that "Hashem is with us (consequently) do not fear them," despite the various difficulties you mention in your letter. As is well known, the ruling of the Heavenly Beis Din is that because of this *mesiras nefesh* (of the Alter Rebbe) for *Toras HaChassidus*, it was decreed in the Heavenly Beis Din that in every matter of Torah, *yiras shamayim*, and good *midos*, the hand of those who are connected to him and walk in his footsteps shall be uppermost (printed in *Sefer Kitzurim VeHe'aros LeTanya*, end of page 122). And if this was the case in all times, then certainly in this time of *ikvesa d'Meshicha* and the end of *galus*—where every matter of *Yiddishkeit* and *Chassidus* is **very** pertinent—one is surely granted the *kochos* to fulfill the mission.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that just as it is the slogan of this year, "And you shall spread out to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south, and unto an inheritance without boundaries," so too should there be this *u'faratzta* in this matter.

And fortunate is the portion of everyone who works in this, which also serves as a *tzinor* to bring an increase in the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach in that which one needs for oneself and one's family.

With blessings for extraordinary success, and especially to all those who have taken part until now—and will surely add more—to be blessed with an abundance of good, from soul to body.

Awaiting good news in all that has been mentioned,

**M. Schneerson**

***
