## 6,837

6,837

B"H, 3 Iyar, 5719

Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about purchasing a house in your location now, or renting an apartment—in general, purchasing is correct, but it seems appropriate to take into account the argument of "why should you make yourselves conspicuous."

Regarding what you wrote about your eldest grandson who is learning with you, shlita, and not in a yeshiva: I have known about this for some time, but since it is my policy not to intervene before I am asked, I did not write about it. However, it is understood that this matter is not correct or good at all, both regarding the boy himself, as well as regarding his brothers and his parents, shlita, in addition to the impression it makes externally. This is for several understandable and simple reasons, especially for one who knows of the mesiras nefesh of the Rebbeim regarding Tomchei Temimim; surely for one such as you, further elaboration on this is superfluous.

At a time of favor, may you mention him and all those you write about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, each one for what they require.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

## 6,838

B"H, 3 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Parshas Kedoshim, in which you write regarding a shochet who suddenly began to go with an uncovered head, asking if this affects the kashrus of his shechita.

Surely you are aware of the incident from several generations ago, where they actually forbade the shechita of a shochet because he wore a *kalashin* (a non-Jewish style of clothing), even though, of course, there is no prohibition in the matter itself. However, at that time, it was proof that he had strayed from the straight path. A shochet must be a *yirei shamayim* to the highest degree, as explained in the *Shulchan Aruch* of the Alter Rebbe and in the *Responsa* of his grandson, the Tzemach Tzedek, in the laws of *shechita*. The same applies to the case at hand; the matter of uncovering the head is incomparably more severe than wearing a *kalashin*, and this is obvious.

Regarding how to rectify the matter, it is understood that this depends on many details and minute particulars. Perhaps it would be worthwhile to inquire confidentially with the Rabbinical organization if there have been similar cases, and what methods and measures they employed. It is understood that the sooner action is taken in the aforementioned area, the easier the rectification will be.

With blessings for good tidings in all the matters about which you write, and that you may utilize every possibility for the dissemination of Yiddishkeit in general and the dissemination of Chassidus in particular.

#### ***6,838***

**Because he wore a kalashin:** See also above, Vol. 9, Letter 2,834.

## 6,839

6,839

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from 27 Nissan. May Hashem help that the parents raise each of their children and grandchildren—may they live—to Torah, the chuppah, and good deeds in comfort, and may you and your wife—may she live—derive from them much true nachas, that is, Jewish, Chassidishe nachas, with joy and goodness of heart.

Regarding what you wrote about the difficulties in the Chabad shul where you daven, which caused you to stop davening there for a time, and then you returned, and now the difficulties persist, etc.

In any case, even if we assume that all the claims are justified—including those you did not write about—leaving a Chabad shul is not the solution. The fact that there are fewer mispallelim does not contribute to the tikkun of the shul. On the contrary, if one considers that there are matters that need to be rectified, one needs not fewer mispallelim, but rather to attract more mispallelim. Then, the obstacles will become fewer, and it will become easier to elevate the ruchniyus and kedushah in that holy place. Surely, elaborating on this is superfluous, and may it be Hashem’s will that you bring good news in this regard as well.

And just as you have seen the kindnesses of Hashem in the past—especially in the improvement of the situation during the winter, as well as with your son and his family—may they live—who are here, and in other matters—surely you will continue to bless and give thanks to the Creator of the world and its Guide. For the offering of thanksgiving is meant to increase ruchniyus and kedushah in every place one can reach, and all the more so in the place where one davens for a significant amount of time. And there is nothing that can stand before the will.

Awaiting good news in all the aforementioned.

## 6840

6840

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Committee of the Association for the Construction of the Central Synagogue
Kiryat Shalom, Tel Aviv
May they live

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 25th of Nissan, with the good news regarding the construction of a central synagogue in your neighborhood.

May it be the will of Hashem that the desire of Hashem shall succeed in their hands, to establish a *mikdash me'at* — a place of *tefillah* and also a place for the study of Torah, with *shiurim* set daily, and with an increase on *Shabbos* and *Yomim Tovim*, as is explained regarding the greatness of this matter in the exposition of our Sages, of blessed memory, on the verse, "Hashem loves the gates of Zion," etc.

May it be the will of Hashem that this synagogue and *beis medrash* serve as a center for all matters of your neighborhood, a center from which our Torah — the Torah of life — and its *mitzvos*, the love of Hashem, the fear of Hashem, and *avodas Hashem* will spread into all the homes of the residents and into their daily lives.

With blessings for success in all the aforementioned.

P.S.

Regarding what you wrote about a letter of recommendation, this is not the custom of the Rebbe’s house.

However, in accordance with what is written, "I am a companion to all who fear You," I have enclosed my personal check as a symbolic participation in the aforementioned construction.

## 6,841

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write that you have become engaged, and that the *tenaim* will take place after the holiday of Shavuos, and the wedding in the year 5720.

Your letter is unclear regarding what you mean by "engagement," what the *tenaim* are, and why it is necessary to delay the wedding for so long. You are surely aware of my opinion on this from long ago. It is possible that by "engagement" you mean what is called *a vort* (a simple verbal agreement), and by *tenaim* you mean the writing of a document. Perhaps this is the custom in Jerusalem, may it be rebuilt and established speedily, and "when you come to a city, follow its customs."

In any case, may it be His will that it should be in a *sha'ah tovah u'mutzlachas* (a good and successful hour) and that you build a *Chassidishe* home.

I was pleased to read in your letter that you have been occupied with registration and that it has been successful. Surely your other colleagues in this work are also engaged in it with the necessary vigor, and may Hashem grant them success as well in magnifying Torah and glorifying it through the development of the school. This is certainly the channel and vessel for success in their personal matters.

At an *eis ratzon*, I will mention you and your mother, may she live and be well, at the *tziyun hakadosh* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need.

## 6842

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the participants of the annual dinner
of the Lubavitcher Yeshiva in Newark
May they live, Hashem be with them

Peace and blessing!

On the occasion of the annual banquet of the Lubavitcher Yeshiva in Newark, Sunday, Parshas **Behar**, I send my greetings and blessing to all the honored guests and participants. I hope that this seudas mitzvah will bring the Yeshiva more than the expected help and support—both spiritual and financial—through the greatest efforts of all the friends of the Yeshiva.

We are currently in the days of **Sefirah**, which awaken in us the experience of our forefathers as they left the exile of Mitzrayim in order to receive the Torah at Mount Sinai. They counted the days and weeks with longing and impatience toward that luminous day of Matan Torah. One must reflect upon this: for when the Jews left Mitzrayim, they did not yet know what Torah was, nor what Torah signified for the Jewish people and for the individual Jew. We, however, have since that time felt and become convinced of how the Torah—Toras Chaim—is our entire life; how Torah and mitzvos illuminate the daily life of every Jew and protect us during the most difficult times of galus. Surely, every Jew must now feel and demonstrate an even greater and deeper sense of love and devotion to the holy Torah.

In the parsha of the week of the banquet (Parshas **Behar**), the Torah speaks of the mitzvah to dedicate an entire year to Hashem, one year in seven, as a "Shabbos to Hashem." Jews were required to detach themselves from their personal interests and worldly occupations and dedicate a **full year** to Torah and holiness. Simultaneously, the holy Torah provides assurance that not only will the Jews lack nothing, but on the contrary, Hashem will send His blessing in a supernatural way, such that one year will provide sustenance for three years.

In this same manner, there is a supernatural blessing that Hashem sends for every activity and devotion in all matters of holiness, especially the support of such an institution of Torah *al taharas hakodesh* as the Lubavitcher Yeshiva in Newark—the pride of Newark and New Jersey Jewry.

With blessings for success.

6443

B”H, 5 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Dr. Yisrael Eldad-Scheib
Jerusalem, T”U

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 19 Nissan, in which you comment regarding my general letter for the festival of Pesach from the year 5717 (or 5718?), and express concern that it might be interpreted incorrectly, allow me to point out that the best proof that there is no room for such a mistake is the fact that although quite some time has passed since that letter was published, this is the first time anyone has pointed out this potential for error to me.

Naturally, you need not apologize for commenting on a matter that you feel leaves room for misunderstanding. For my part, I am always pleased to hear comments on such matters, especially regarding a letter published publicly, for of such things it is said: "Be careful with your words, lest you reveal..."

I am enclosing a copy of my letter for this Pesach, in which you will see my perspective on the issue you write about.

I would like to touch upon a point you raise in your letter, where you write that there may be a greater danger in the United States than in the Soviet Union. You are evidently referring to the danger of assimilation, since in the Soviet Union there is the enslavement of the body, which awakens the yearnings of the soul, whereas in the United States there is freedom of the body and the *yetzer*. In my opinion, for this very reason—to carry the parallel a step further—the danger in Eretz Yisrael is even greater. This is because there, they have invented matters of substitution and replacement that leave room for one to mistakenly interpret them as the freedom of the soul, while in reality, they are the lowest level of internal *galus*. I refer to the imitation and the pursuit of being "like all the nations of the lands." Since this occurs in the Holy Land and in the Holy Tongue, the danger of this dulling the soul’s yearnings for the true and **only** freedom is even greater. This is precisely as you write in your letter, in your own words: "It goes without saying that the path of the *geulah* leads from Mitzrayim through Har Sinai, and Yisrael were not redeemed from Mitzrayim until judgments were executed **upon the gods** of Mitzrayim."

Our Sages have informed us that all the *galuyos*—in other words, all the nations of the world—are called by the name Mitzrayim, because they distress (*metzarim*) Yisrael. This is exactly the case in our own time, where it goes without saying that the path of the *geulah* for every single Jew, and certainly for the entirety of Klal Yisrael, leads only through Har Sinai—which our Sages explained as the place from which hatred descended upon the nations of the world (upon all their values and culture). And Yisrael were not redeemed until judgments were executed **upon the gods** of Mitzrayim, meaning all the ideologies and values to which the nations of the world bow down.

It is difficult to elaborate on this most distressing matter, especially since I am certain that for someone like yourself, further elaboration is superfluous. May it be the will of Hashem that very soon we merit to see in actuality, as you continue in your letter, the fulfillment of the spiritual designation of a "holy nation" in the Holy Land. As with all matters of our language, the Holy Tongue, a word can have multiple meanings; for the definition of *kadosh* (holy) is separation and distinction. In the words of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe in *Tanya* (66a): "He has sanctified us with His *mitzvos*—that He has elevated us to the level of the Supreme *Kodesh*, blessed be He, which is the holiness of the Holy One, blessed be He, in His glory and essence, and *kedushah* is a term denoting separation," etc.

From time to time, I see your articles printed in various periodicals. May it be the will of Hashem that you utilize this influence of yours to disseminate traditional Judaism, to influence your readers with a longing and desire for the aforementioned designation—a holy nation wherever they may be—until Mashiach Tzidkeinu comes, gathers our exiles, and leads us upright to our Holy Land.

With respect and blessing.

## 6444

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday.

1) When I emphasize (underline) a certain word in a note containing questions asked of me, my intention is that the emphasized words are my answer, and not as you wrote, that "it would not hurt."

2) Regarding what I noted—that meditation before davening and during davening itself is explained in Tanya, etc., and that you will find several methods there—it is understood that for many of the methods brought there, the type of person to whom that method applies is explicitly stated, and it is not at all the type of a bochur who learns in a yeshiva and is within the walls of the yeshiva, etc. From all those methods you saw written, there will certainly be no doubt for you except regarding two or three of them; then, you should consult with your mashpia as to which of them to choose.

3) Regarding your writing that when you begin to meditate on a subject in Chassidus or a matter of learning in general, several distracting thoughts occur to you:

The advice for this is known: learn from the book itself. Even when you are meditating and your eyes are closed, the book should be open before you, as the letters hold the thought and diminish the distractions, and sometimes nullify them entirely.

4) Regarding your writing that you are "far" from the meditation in Tanya, Chapter 41, from its beginning until the word "HaMelech" on the second page—do not be impressed by these enticements of the yetzer hara. If you review the subject in your mind a few times, the claims of the yetzer hara will naturally be silenced.

5) Regarding what you wrote about the known matter—it seems you have already been answered that for the time being, you should divert your attention from the matter, and even from fixing it, as this causes the matter to diminish and weaken from time to time.

At a time of favor, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you need. And in the style of my father-in-law, the Rebbe: may you have a healthy summer and a good "tamid" (always), and may you hear good news regarding all the matters about which you write.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6845

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Iyar, Tiferes sheb'Tiferes, I was pleased to read that everything is going well, thank G-d. May it be His will that you always report only good news, both in matters of physical health in the literal sense, and in matters of spiritual health.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the matter of growing a beard:

It goes without saying that you are correct that this must be done in a manner that ensures you can maintain it, without being affected by others, as is stated at the beginning of the Tur and Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim, and brought in the Shulchan Aruch of Admur HaZaken: "And he shall not be ashamed before those who mock."

If this is with the agreement of both of you (i.e., also your wife, may she live), and you estimate—to the extent that a person can estimate—that you have the necessary strength, and we are, after all, in the days of Sefiras HaOmer, as it is written, "And you shall count for yourselves" (Vayikra 23:15)—and as is well known, the interpretation of Rabbeinu HaZaken, based on Sifrei Kabbalah, is that *u'sefartem* means to illuminate, and *lachem* means for yourselves.

May you make the resolution to do this and carry it out in practice, in a good and successful hour, and may the difficulties be far easier than you imagine.

Also, regarding the people you mention in your letter who you fear will react sharply: I hope that in connection with the good news soon to come in your family life—and which will certainly add the *tzelem Elokim* to the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach, and success in everything you need—the reaction of those mentioned above will also be much weaker than you imagine. May Hashem Yisbarach grant you success in reporting good news in this regard as well.

I reiterate my blessing that Hashem Yisbarach should fulfill your wife's pregnancy, may she live, properly and easily, and that she should give birth to a healthy child at the proper time and in a proper and easy manner.

With blessings for good news in all the above.

## 6846

6846

B”H, 5 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Thursday, in which you write about the sleep disturbances you are experiencing, etc., and that you have consulted a doctor, though as of now it has not helped you.

First and foremost, you should have the mezuzos in your home checked, as well as your tefillin, to ensure they are kosher according to law. Every weekday, before morning davening, set aside a few coins for tzedakah. After davening every day—including Shabbos and Yom Tov—recite the daily portion of Tehillim as it is divided for the days of the month. It would be very good if, in addition to wearing a tallis katan with checked tzitzis during the day, you also sleep in a tallis katan, in accordance with the words of the Arizal regarding this (cited by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe in his Siddur, Laws of Tzitzis). I have strong hope that your matters will then be resolved.

However, since everything must have a foothold in the natural order, and the doctor will surely give you some pills or the like, take them according to the doctor’s instructions. At the same time, divert your mind completely from the aforementioned disturbances. Then, as mentioned above, slowly but surely, over a short period of time, you will return to your full strength like any other person. The more you divert your mind from it and increase your bitachon in Hashem, the Creator and Guide of the world, the more you will be blessed by Hashem with success in this matter and in all your affairs.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

## 6,847

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Iyar, in which you write about thoughts that sometimes disturb you—thoughts that began several years ago—and you ask for my opinion on this.

It is understood and simple that it is clear that these thoughts have no substance or reality whatsoever. Surely, you yourself have witnessed this, for since these thoughts began, you have seen the kindness of Hashem and His blessing tangibly and concretely in the building of a Jewish home upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos. This is one of the most fundamental matters, and it is the essence of the life of a Jewish man and woman.

However, regarding matters that depend on thought, the primary advice is *hesech hada'as* (distraction). *Hesech hada'as* does not mean to fight against an undesirable thought, but rather to divert one’s thinking to another matter. How good it is to divert one’s thoughts to matters of Yiddishkeit, for then a little light pushes away much darkness. This is unlike what you write—that you strive to overcome the thought—for fighting in the aforementioned matter is the opposite of *hesech hada'as*.

It is appropriate that you have the mezuzos in your home checked, as well as your husband’s (may he live and be well) tefillin, if they have not been checked in the last twelve months. You should maintain the good custom of the daughters of Israel to set aside money for tzedakah before lighting the candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov, and you should also do so every weekday before reciting any morning tefillah. It is understood that all of this should be done without a vow.

It is also understood and simple that becoming accustomed to thinking about trust in Hashem Yisbarach, the Creator of the world and its Guide—[strengthening oneself in this must be done without any connection to the undesirable thoughts, from which one must divert one's mind, but rather from a traditional Jewish worldview]—adds to the blessings of Hashem and success in all that is needed.

You mention in your letter that you worked in the field of education as a seminary graduate. I hope that you are utilizing your talents in this area even now. If you do not have the time for a full-time or even part-time position, then at the very least, you should do so from time to time, and frequently, in the dissemination of traditional Yiddishkeit in general, and among the youth and children in particular, for this is the demand and necessity of the hour.

With blessings for good news in all the aforementioned.

## 6848

6848

By the Grace of Hashem, 6 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Rabbi, a man of great deeds and fine character, R' Nissan, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to receive your letter of 5 Iyar regarding the examination you conducted, together with your colleagues, the esteemed Rabbis, shlita, of the students of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim, which was founded and is directed by our holy Rebbes, may their merits protect us. I was happy to hear of the results of the examinations and your assessment regarding the knowledge of the students, may they live.

It is understood that I am particularly pleased with the main point you raised—that the back-and-forth of the discussion and the answers were full of life, which indicates, as you phrased it in your letter, that they are studying with love.

May it be the will of Hashem that for many years to come, you, as well as your colleagues, the Rabbis, shlita, will continue to share good news regarding the study of our Torah—the Torah of life—out of a love for Hashem and a love for Torah. This is especially true regarding the students of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim, for the founders of the Yeshiva established the goal of their education to be that they become candles to illuminate the darkness of the "small world" (the person himself) and also the world at large, by magnifying the light of Torah and glorifying it through the *ma'or* (the light) within the Torah—which is the *pnimiyus* of the Torah.

And just as you have provided much satisfaction, may it be that, in the measure of the Giver of the Torah—which is *middah k'neged middah*, only many times over—you will be satisfied and derive *nachas* from your descendants, may they live, and from yourself as well.

With respect and blessing, and regards to your colleagues, the Rabbis, shlita.

#### ***6848***

**R' Nissan:** Teleshevsky, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him appear above in 6774, and in the notes referenced there.

## 6,849

6,849

B"H, 6 Iyar, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Pesach, may he live.

Shalom and blessings!

In response to your letter, which is "beauty within beauty."

1) Your son, Yaakov Peretz, may he live, will turn three years old on Thursday, 5 Menachem Av. You ask when to perform the haircut (*upsherin*).

Based on the implications of the words of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, on various occasions, it appears that one should not cut the boy's hair before he has completed three years. On the other hand, since the birthday is at the beginning of the month of Menachem Av, if—G-d forbid—the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu is delayed until then, the haircut should be performed after Tisha B'Av. May it be at a good and auspicious time.

2) Lighting candles by young girls.

According to the custom in the Rebbe’s house, and in the homes of anash who are aware of this, they begin lighting once they have reached the age of *chinuch*. See the guidelines for *chinuch* in the Shulchan Aruch of our Alter Rebbe, Orach Chayim, section 343, paragraph 3. I believe that until marriage, they light one candle.

3) The manner of placing the *lechem mishneh* on Friday night and the following day.

On Friday night, they are placed side-by-side. On the following day, they should be placed such that the challah on the right is slightly higher than the second one, but only slightly.

4) Regarding the other matters you ask about, you should consult the elders of anash in your vicinity as to how they conduct themselves.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

***

6,849

Part of this was printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 11, p. 244, and part in Igros Kodesh, Vol. 24, p. 337; it was completed based on the secretariat’s copy.

**R' Pesach, may he live:** From Jerusalem.

## 6850

B"H, 6 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned R' Menachem, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

...Regarding your question as to the reason the Alter Rebbe did not include the recitation of "U'venucho Yomar" in his Siddur—I have not heard a specific reason for this, but it is understandable and simple in my opinion. For the verse warns (Koheles 5:1): "Be not rash with your mouth... therefore let your words be few" (see the commentary of the Ibn Ezra there). Furthermore, at the beginning of Shaar HaTefillah in Pri Etz Chayim, it states: "These later scholars who did not know the path of Kabbalah do not know... and they err..." Since the Arizal, to whom the paths of the heavens and the gates of prayer were illuminated, was meticulous regarding every letter and vowel in prayer, and since in his Siddur (both that of R' Y. Kapel and that of R' S. of Rashkov—a student of the Maggid of Mezeritch) he did not include these verses, we follow his lead. And who could come after the "living lion"?

#### ***6850***

**R' Menachem:** Of Lod, Bnei Brak. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 13, p. 279, and in the references noted in the footnotes there. Below, 6872, 6890.

## 6,851

B"H, 6 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

It is a great wonder that for a very long time there have been no letters or news from you. Although I have received news about you from others, it is, of course, not the same as receiving news directly, and I am certain you will make up for this at the next opportunity. I hope it is unnecessary to repeat what I have already written to you, that you must increase your efforts in our Torah and its mitzvos, both regarding yourself and regarding others. It is understood, and indeed simple, that the strength for this has been granted to you, as explained in Tanya—that it is an inheritance passed down to us from our forefathers. Even a day-old infant inherits it absolutely; how much more so one who has merited to study in the holy Yeshiva Tomchei Tmimim and heard Chassidus from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi Yisrael. This matter is eternal Above, and below it is awakened either through *is'arusa d'letata* or *is'arusa d'le'eila*. However, any sense of discouragement—and certainly despair, *chas v'shalom*—has an impure source.

Since we have come from the month of Nissan and are now in the month of Ziv—the time of the Exodus from Egypt and its limitations, and the preparation for the receiving of the Torah—may this also be for you a time of the beginning of freedom, its completion, and its ultimate fulfillment very soon. There is nothing that can stand before the will, especially when one is being assisted.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6,852

B"H, 6 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

... It was a pleasure for me to read in your letter the highlights of your activities over the past few weeks. I am certain you will continue to provide detailed reports in the future. The same applies to your wife, long may she live; although this is only the second time she has written, I hope this will be a good beginning and that her writing will also include the necessary details. This is especially relevant in these *ikvesa d'Meshicha*, as explained in the writings of the Arizal—a time when the merit of the women of Israel is comparable to the first redemption, the exodus from Egypt. And this is understood.

With blessings for good news regarding all that was mentioned, and for outstanding success in both communal and personal matters, with proper health and with joy and a glad heart.

And with the blessing of *u'faratzta*, etc.,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***6,852***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 351.

## 6,853

B"H, 7 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yosef, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

After the longest of intervals, your letter of 5 Iyar was received, along with the collection you organized for the benefit of Kolel Chabad in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established.

Although for someone like you there is certainly no need to express thanks, etc., etc., I will nevertheless express my gratitude for the great satisfaction and *nachas ruach* you have caused me personally. This is in addition to the magnitude of the matter and the *mitzvah* itself. Even though personal *nachas ruach* is incomparable to the *mitzvah* of the matter, I must express my feelings regarding my own experience—especially since good and joyous matters are not so common, which further increases the value of such an event, particularly when it is done through the *is'arusa* of the individual himself, without anyone prompting him to do so.

Since we have come from the month of Nissan and are now in the month of Ziv, in which the "radiants of the world" were born, and the words of the Arizal are known—that when these days are recalled (and also when they are "made"), there is an increase in all matters, both private and general, in *Torah, Avodah, and Gemilus Chasadim*, in freedom and redemption, to increase *ziv* (radiance) in the world—*ziv* and illuminations from the deeds of our forefathers. As is brought in *Tanya*, all their limbs and all their actions were solely for the Supreme Will throughout all their days.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, in good health.

#### ***6,853***

**R' Yosef:** Player, Chicago. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 17, 6,291, and in the references noted there.

## 6,854

B"H, 7 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yisroel Meir, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of 6 Iyar, I am writing in the briefest possible terms regarding the farbrengens on Acharon Shel Pesach. I hope that the matter of distributing matzah shmurah was also handled in the appropriate and desired manner, and that, finally, you will also instill in your camp the idea that the phrase "your springs shall spread outward" is precise in every detail—meaning that there must be *hafatzah* (dissemination), specifically, and that it must spread and reach even the "outside." It is understood that the fulfillment of this task rests upon those who are aware of the aforementioned Iggeres HaKodesh of the Baal Shem Tov, and especially those who have merited to hear it from our Rebbe and to study his Torah and sichos regarding the magnitude and necessity of this matter. And if you ask why this necessity was not so well known in their times, what is the benefit of questions and answers? For the main thing is the actual deed, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

#### ***6,854***

**R' Yisroel Meir, may he live:** Altien, Pittsburgh. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 3, 439, and in the notes indicated there.

## 6855

6855

B.H., Erev Shabbos Kodesh, Parshas Emor, 5719
Brooklyn

To our brothers, the Children of Israel,
May Hashem keep them and sustain them,

Greetings and blessings!

We find ourselves in the days of Sefira, which in their deeper significance are days of preparation for Kabbalas HaTorah. According to our Sages, the days of Sefira bring us to the time of Yetzias Mitzrayim, when the Jews, immediately following their physical liberation, counted the days and weeks with great inner yearning until the moment of their spiritual liberation at Mount Sinai. They did not yet know exactly what Torah was, nor what Torah meant for the individual and for the collective. It was enough to know that the entire purpose of Yetzias Mitzrayim was for the sake of Kabbalas HaTorah, for deep in their hearts there already sprouted the thought of Ahavas Hashem, Ahavas HaTorah, and Ahavas Yisrael, which our Patriarchs—Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov—had planted in every Jewish heart. And the great inner yearning, which expressed itself in daily life, brought it about that in the span of a very short time, the Jews reached the level of a "kingdom of priests and a holy nation."

Over the course of the 1,723 years since Yetzias Mitzrayim and Maamad Har Sinai—the greater part of which the Jews have endured in one exile after another—the Jewish people have become deeply convinced that the Torah is **Toras-Chaim**—in the literal sense of the expression—our life, both as individuals and as a people.

It is self-evident how great our yearning for the Torah must be. It is only that various circumstances sometimes dim the inner connection of a Jew with Torah and Mitzvos. Therefore, it is necessary, from time to time, to awaken the Jewish heart, which is in truth always vigilant (*Lev Yisrael Er He*).

The Torah says (in this week’s Parsha): **"Emor"** (Speak) to the Kohanim... **"Ve'amarta"**—speak once and a second time—**"Lehazhir gedolim al haktanim"**—to instruct the greater ones to influence the smaller ones.

All Jews are, after all, like Kohanim (*Mamlches Kohanim*), and although Kohanim are *zerizim* (diligent), not asleep, one must still awaken them often. And primarily, they must remember not only their own duty, but must also move others to fulfill their duty.

There are those whose greatness lies in the realm of talent, labor, and influence, and those whose greatness lies in the realm of money. Each person must act in the field where Divine Providence has placed him.

It is understood that for one who is great in all these areas, his duty is proportional, and the demand that he fulfill his duty is even greater and stronger.

* * *

I turn to you, our brothers, the Children of Israel, on behalf of the Central Lubavitch Yeshiva, "Tomchei Temimim," which currently finds itself in a critical financial state.

If the Lubavitch Yeshiva were being founded now for the first time and requesting your support, you would surely have to respond with the greatest warmth. Especially since the Yeshiva has proven, with Hashem’s help and with the *mesiras nefesh* of its students and Roshei Yeshivos, that it can endure over sixty years of one exile after another, and has clearly demonstrated its specific inner value, perseverance, and self-sacrifice for Torah and Mitzvos *al taharas hakodesh*.

Each and every one is included in the call to "instruct the greater ones regarding the smaller ones"; each and every one must respond, with *zerizus* and even *mesiras nefesh*, to the cry for help from the Lubavitch Yeshiva in its current critical financial situation.

In the merit of this, the "smaller ones" will become "greater ones," and the "greater ones"—even greater, in all respects. For "Great is Hashem, and to His greatness there is no end," and the Almighty will certainly not remain in debt, with children, life, and abundant sustenance—abundant in both material and spiritual terms alike.

With respect and blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6855

B"H, 9 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, active in communal affairs, R' Tuvia, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

After a long interval, I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 3 Iyar. At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, each according to their needs.

It is surprising that you are so brief—to the point of nothingness—regarding activities within the framework of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad, and specifically regarding the dissemination of the wellsprings. May it be His will that this is only a deficiency in writing, and not in the activities themselves.

Although I have already written that it is evident that the knowledge that one will eventually write about these activities to a certain person brings about an increase in the activities—whether in quantity, quality, or both together—may it be His will that you bring good news in this regard.

Especially since we are in the month of Ziv, in which the "radiance of the world" (zivtanei olam) were born. It is known from the words of the Arizal that when the days are remembered (and also become present), there is an increase in all matters, both general and specific, including—freedom and redemption—to increase the radiance and illuminations of the deeds of our forefathers. As is explained in Tanya, that all their limbs and deeds were solely for the Supreme Will, all their days.

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

***

6855

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 17, p. 469, and completed according to a photocopy of the letter.

**R' Tuvia, may he live:** Blau, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—above, 6636, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6857

B"H, 9 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 3 Iyar, in which you outline what you have been through and how you have now settled in, concluding with a question of wonder: why is it that when you desire to ascend in matters of Torah and mitzvos, you encounter various difficulties, while at the same time you see secular bochurim whose lives are without difficulties, without exertion or effort, etc.?

The answer to this is found in your own letter, in your description of your situation: that you must study your profession for three or four years, and now, in practical work like any other laborer, you earn only 110 Lira per month. In other words, the action itself is not enough; rather, it requires appropriate preparation through study and knowledge, and then the action itself also improves and becomes much more refined.

The reason is simple: the greater the achievement, the greater and more extensive the preparation must be; and the smaller the achievement—or in a matter where there is no achievement at all—the preparation is smaller, or not needed at all.

It is the same regarding lives that lack substance; they require no preparation, for whatever pleasure they contain vanishes the moment it passes. What wonder is it, then, that no preparation is needed for such things? That is to say, any person who contemplates his future, even for a brief moment, will strive as much as necessary for the best possible preparation over the course of several years, since through this, his life in the near future and in the future in general will improve many times over, for decades and decades. As our Sages of blessed memory succinctly and wittily put it, "According to the pain is the reward"—except that the reward is many times greater than the pain, the exertion, and the effort. That is to say, by multiplying the exertion and effort, the reward and the benefit increase many times over.

It is my hope that these few lines will suffice for you to contemplate their content and arrive at the true conclusion: that you must utilize these years and the energy of youth and bochur-hood for the preparation required for your entire future, so that you may be happy both materially and spiritually. There is only one path that leads to this: mental preparation, the study of our Torah—the Torah of life—and the fulfillment of its mitzvos, regarding which it is said, "and live by them." This is the fulfillment of the request, the encouragement, and the command: "Choose life"—true and happy life in our world as well.

May it be His will that you bring good news regarding all that has been mentioned.

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6,858

6,858

B"H, 11 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, the man of action, who performs his work as a task of Heaven,
R' Aharon Mordechai, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 4th of Iyar and those preceding it. My mind is set at ease, just as you have set my mind at ease—as is the case many times—with the good news regarding the success in Ir Ganim of the Oholei Yosef Yitzchak school, and that all the classes have already been separated. You will surely inform me as soon as possible regarding the other schools of the Reshet in general, and in particular those that are under your practical management, whether officially or unofficially—I refer to Menachem and Sdei Micha. May it be the will [of Hashem] that there, too, there will be good news, good for Heaven and good for people, and in the primary point: that they are growing both in quantity (the number of students) and in quality (the level of studies), and are imbued with yiras shamayim. Happy is the portion of all those who have taken part in this in any way, and surely, according to the teaching of the Mishnah, "a mitzvah leads to another mitzvah, and the reward of a mitzvah is a mitzvah," they will continue with joy and a good heart in the aforementioned activities and in similar ones, with great success.

There is a special reward and a special merit for the women—your wife, may she live, and likewise for the wives of the other directors, teachers, and activists of the Reshet schools—as per the ruling of our Torah, Toras Chaim. Furthermore, it is emphasized there that the promise that the Ribono shel Olam has promised to women is greater than that of the men, for they are tranquil and trusting.

It is surely unnecessary to point out [based on the teachings of Chassidus, beginning with the Baal Shem Tov, that every detail is by Hashgacha Pratis] that the aforementioned promise and merit is noted in Maseches Berachos, page 17a.

It is understood that it is within your authority and privilege to convey what is stated here, or even to explain it to the other wives of the Reshet activists, in addition to the fact that your wife, may she live, will surely read this letter of mine herself.

With blessings for good news and details regarding all the above, and with thanks, and a Mazal Tov for the birth of the firstborn son to his sister and brother-in-law, the Spigels, may they live. I look forward to good news.

#### ***6,858***

**R' Aharon Mordechai, may he live:** Zilberstrom, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 14, 4,999, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6,859

B"H, 12 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After a long hiatus, your letter from Motzaei Shabbos was received.

...Regarding what you wrote about the various strange reasons that caused the cancellation of several shiurim you had with other students:

The adage is already well-known: what is the use of excuses when the matter itself is not being done? It is well-known that even in a case of absolute *ones* (duress)—where the Torah rules that "the Merciful One exempts the one under duress"—we do not say "it is considered as if he did it." Consequently, there is no *hafatzas hamaayanos*, and not even anything less than that.

May it be the will of Hashem that you, along with all *anash*, recognize the concept of *hafatzah*. This is for the benefit of each and every person who spreads [the wellsprings] (even though in doing so he also does a favor for others). Consequently, one should approach this as one approaches matters of one's own personal benefit. This is along the lines of what was explained at the *farbrengen*, and in the vein of the witty observation that in our current era—a "topsy-turvy world"—we have inverted even what is discussed in *Torah Chassidus* in many places regarding the superiority of the *avodah* of "the closeness of Elokus is good" over the *avodah* of "the closeness of Elokus is **good for me**." Now, it is the exact opposite: one learns, understands, and grasps—and can even explain to someone on a lower level—how "the closeness of Elokus is good," but he does not connect the entire matter to himself, that he too is included in this, and that the closeness of Elokus is **good for him** as well. If only they would begin with the aforementioned explanation (a lower level, so to speak), surely the promise of our Sages would be fulfilled: "Through [doing it] not for its own sake, one comes to [do it] for its own sake."

It is understood that my intention is not, *chas v'shalom*, to preach morality. It is simple that I reject any sense of discouragement, etc.; rather, I am expressing my astonishment that *anash* are "slowly but surely" slipping away even from the matter of *hafatzah*, amidst a general withdrawal from activities concerning Chabad institutions, and even activities concerning public study. If only I could be given the good news that the tide has turned for the good—good for Heaven, good for the creations, and good for the one who produces fruit.

With blessings for health and for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6860

B"H, 13 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter in which you write of your concern regarding the occurrence of undesirable matters at times, and that you are saddened by this, etc.

Indeed, this has already been stated and explained in the holy Tanya and in many places throughout the teachings of Chassidus: that melancholy—and even sadness—is one of the enticements of the yetzer hara and one of its tactics of war to prevent a person from serving Hashem. And although it is clear that one must feel bitterness regarding an undesirable matter, the distinction between bitterness and sadness is explained there (see the subject index for the indicated places in Tanya), as is the fact that even for bitterness, there are designated times.

However, it is understood and also simple that you must repent with regret for the past and resolve with the necessary firmness, through a *kabbalah tovah* for the future, to choose life and the good of our Torah—the Torah of life, of which it is said, "I have given you a good teaching." And when a person sanctifies himself (even) a little from below, they sanctify him greatly from above. You should establish fixed times for study in the *nigleh* of Torah and in the teachings of Chassidus, with an increase in study on Shabbos and Yom Tov.

It is appropriate that you have your tefillin checked to ensure they are kosher according to the law, and that you observe the *takanah* of the three daily portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—as is known. Furthermore, every weekday before morning davening, you should set aside a few coins for tzedakah. It is understood that all of this should be done *bli neder*. Likewise, you should have the mezuzos in your home checked to ensure they are all kosher according to the law.

Awaiting good news regarding all of the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6861

6861

B"H, 13 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 10 Iyar.

At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for each according to their needs, as appropriate to the content of your letter.

It is already well known that in family matters and between a husband and wife, indirect efforts are more effective than direct ones, as is tangibly evident. Furthermore, there is the matter of not magnifying differences of opinion, etc. If there is any possibility of ignoring them, or at the very least minimizing them—how good and how beneficial this is, and how it draws peace closer. It is stated, "As water reflects a face to a face," which is the nature of every person. May it be His will that the statement of our Sages be fulfilled in them: "If a husband and wife are worthy, the Shechinah dwells between them."

Regarding what you wrote concerning your position—that your contact with people is only, etc.—you can certainly exert influence even on the substance of matters through your acquaintance with the officials, as that is part of their duties. Especially since, as you also mention in your letter, there are certainly times when you have the opportunity not only to speak with the people who visit, but also to influence them. It is a well-known saying of the Baal Shem Tov that everything a Jewish person sees or hears is certainly relevant to his task in the service of Hashem.

May Hashem cause your wife, long may she live, to complete her pregnancy properly and with ease, and may she give birth to a healthy child at the proper time, properly and easily.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

P.S.

It is appropriate that those pregnant women you wrote about have the mezuzos in their homes checked [if this has not been done within the last twelve months], so that they are all kosher according to the law.

## 6862

B"H, 14 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Rabbi Hecht shlita also writes to me that you are deliberating regarding the tallis that was used for your husband, a"h, as you are in doubt whether it was his.

If you know that it belonged to someone else, you can give the value of the tallis to that person. If, however, this is nothing more than an (unfounded) suspicion, then, as an extra measure, you can donate the value of a tallis to support a shul where people daven every day, or donate a tallis to such a shul, to be used by the chazzan or the mispallelim of the shul.

## 8,863

8,863

B"H, 16 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Chaim Zusha, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 9th of Iyar and those preceding it. May it be the will of Hashem that, in accordance with your letter, your efforts regarding the village as a whole will succeed, and consequently, there will be success in the personal affairs of each of the residents of the village, where "Hashem has commanded the blessing." This is especially true given that we are in a year of which it is said, "It is a Shabbos to Hashem." Just as on Shabbos there is a cessation from all distracting matters—as it is written, "And you shall call the Shabbos a delight"—so too, and in a similar vein, is the nature of this entire year in terms of *is'arusa d'leila*.

According to the order of the text, "When you come... the land shall rest, a Shabbos to Hashem" (even before "six years you shall sow your field, etc."), as explained in *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Behar*, in the *maamar* titled *V'shavtah*; in the *maamarim* of Admur Moharash from the year 5627; in the *Hemshech* of 5666; and elsewhere. From these sources, it is also clear that the activities of the "six years you shall sow, etc."—each person according to their standing and situation—add further to the "Shabbos to Hashem," until one reaches the level of "Shabbos Shabbaton." See there for further elaboration on this.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***8,863***

**R' Chaim Zusha:** Vilimovsky, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 17, 8,444, and in the notes indicated there.

**According to the order of the text:** See also the following letter.

## 6,864

6,864

B”H, 16 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To my dear friend, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc.,
R’ Shmuel Menachem Mendel, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter regarding your activities over the past few weeks. May it be Hashem’s will that these activities succeed in a manner of "increasing and continuing, increasing and adding light." It is certainly unnecessary to elaborate on the magnitude of the merit of those who exert themselves in this, and may it be His will that the promise be fulfilled in them: "The reward of a mitzvah is a mitzvah," and "one mitzvah leads to another," so that you may go from strength to strength in these aforementioned activities, until you "spread out to the west, east, north, and south," and reach an inheritance without boundaries.

Indeed, this year is a "Shabbos to Hashem," and when one acts within it in the spiritual sense of the matters hinted at in "six years you shall sow"—as it is known that their general theme is Torah and its study, and study leads to action—then one approaches an even higher level: from "a Shabbos to Hashem" to "a Shabbos Shabbaton," in the order of the verses at the beginning of Parshas Behar. See also Likutei Torah, Behar, the maamar entitled *V'shavtah*; the *Sefer Maamarim* of the Rebbe Maharash for the year 5627, the maamar entitled *V'shavtah*, and at its conclusion—that "Shabbos Shabbaton" is like the time to come, a day that is entirely Shabbos. And as is explained in the *Hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah 5666, that even in the time to come, this is the highest level of all.

With blessings for good tidings in a revealed and manifest good,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***6,864***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 12, p. 256; completed based on a photocopy of the original letter.

**R’ Shmuel Menachem Mendel, may he live:** Schneerson, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above, 6,739, and in the notes indicated there.

## 6865

B"H, 17 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the avreich Ezra Meir, may he live, HaKohen

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter.

At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, may his merit protect us, for whatever each of them requires.

Regarding your question as to why in many places in Chassidic discourse multiple analogies are brought for a single concept—even though every detail in Torah is precise, which implies that one analogy offers an explanation that another does not—nevertheless, the primary method of study is not to be overly precise with every single detail of the analogies. Rather, one should understand that just as there are multiple interpretations for every matter in Torah, so too can one find various analogies for every concept, or at least for many concepts in Torah.

In general, this is based on the principle, "And from my flesh I shall see Elokus." In most instances, the teachings of Chabad bring analogies from the powers of the soul. In many matters that were explained in seforim prior to the revelation of Chabad Chassidus using other analogies, for whatever reason, these analogies are sometimes retained.

This is similar to the matter of the hidden Sefiros, where sometimes the analogy of a coal and a flint is brought, and sometimes the analogy of the powers of the soul. This is also consistent with what is written in Eitz Chaim, Shaar 1, end of Branch 4, that supernal, spiritual matters should be illustrated through the image of a person or the image of letters, and that both are necessary; see there.

It is also understood that in many maamarim, the discussion concerns the general difference between one level and another without entering into the details. Therefore, the analogy is also for the general concept, even though when one distinguishes between the details within that very level, that specific analogy may be appropriate only for one detail and not for the others.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

*) In some places, one should add: In Torah Or in various places, including the end of Parshas Noach; Hemshech Mayim Rabim 5736, Chapter 4 and onwards; and others.

## 6866

B"H, 17 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the 6th of Iyar.

It is surprising that you repeat your request time and again, when I have already written to you that it is appropriate for your heart to be confident in the Healer of all flesh, Who performs wonders—for, in the words of the verse, "He has made everything beautiful in its time," which is precise in this regard: both matters, that it will be in its proper time, and that it will be beautiful.

May it be His will that the medical treatment be in its proper time and successful, as mentioned above, and may you bring good news from a state of good spirits regarding both your personal and general matters.

With blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***6866***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 464.

## 6867

6867

B”H, Lag BaOmer, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to the telephone message regarding the medical treatment that has begun:

May it be Hashem’s will that it be met with great success for your complete recovery.

We bring about merit on a meritorious day—the Hillula of the Rashbi, which is a day of great joy, as is explained in many places. The essence of his mission was to bring the hidden aspects of Torah into the revealed aspects of Torah. The result of this is to bring the hidden aspects of Yisrael into the revealed aspects of Yisrael, and from the inner vitality to the revealed vitality—the vitality of each and every limb—as we are commanded in our Torah, the Torah of life, for the health and wholeness of the body is among the ways of Hashem.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6,868

6,868

B"H, 18 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, active in communal affairs and a man of many accomplishments,
Sh. B. [Dr. Ullman], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter, along with a copy of what you wrote to Rabbi... may he live. Surely you are aware that several of the periodicals printed in Yiddish in the United States have published evaluations regarding Freud’s methodology similar to the one you mention in your letter.

I have already stated, based on one of the foundations of the Chassidic approach, that there is no evil in the world without some good. The superficiality that characterizes many of the readers of these periodicals, while an extremely negative trait—for superficiality is even more detrimental than a lack of knowledge, as it often leads people to grasp matters inaccurately and distort them—nevertheless, in the current state of affairs, holds a great deal of hidden good. Since, unfortunately, most of the authors of these periodicals and printed materials would be better off had they never seen the light of print and publication, and their harm is great while their benefit is highly questionable, this superficiality serves to minimize the damage and loss. In most cases, they perceive the printed material merely as a sensation, and especially in a case such as the one mentioned, it is human nature to seek out a different sensation the next day, and "you shall clear out the old to make way for the new."

It is surely unnecessary to point out that in the periodical where the aforementioned article was printed, there are other articles that stand in much greater opposition to traditional Judaism than the evaluation regarding the aforementioned subject...

With respect and blessing.

#### ***6,868***

**Dr. Ullman:** Additional letters to him appear in Vol. 14, p. 358, and in the notes indicated there.

## 6869

B"H, 18 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter in which you mention that at times you become preoccupied with thoughts regarding your future, and that this distracts you from your learning, etc.

It is surprising, for in this very letter you mention what I told you—that you should learn for a year, etc., and only then make a decision. It is understood, and indeed simple, that the study of wisdom in general, and specifically the study of our holy Torah—which is not merely "your wisdom and understanding," but is the holy Torah, the wisdom and will of the Ribono shel Olam—certainly has the power and efficacy to nullify worries and bring success, not only in the understanding and comprehension of one's studies, but also in all of a person's needs, especially in matters of primary importance.

You should learn with complete peace of mind, with *hasmadah* and *shekidah*, and the *Ribono shel Olam*, who watches over each and every one with *hashgacha pratis*, will assist you in your affairs now, and also after the aforementioned time of learning.

I trust you are keeping the known *Chitas* *shiurim* and are also involved from time to time in the activities of *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad*, and the like.

With blessings for good news in all that has been mentioned.

## 6870

6870

B"H, 19 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rav, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, who engages in the needs of the community,
R' Alter, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your note—that for some reason, the answer was omitted from my previous letter.

Regarding the Kuntres Eitz Chaim, Chapter 29, where it is written, "For R' Zeira also would conclude the halachic ruling, but he did so through *pilpul* (and this is also stated in Shaarei Orah there)," you suggest that in Shaarei Orah, it is not explained in this manner.

In my humble opinion, the intention is to what is written there in the *maamar* beginning with the words "B'Chaf-Hei B'Kislev," Chapter 56: "And in truth, one cannot say that a *charif* (sharp scholar) who poses questions is only one who poses questions without arriving at a halachic ruling, like Rabbah, who was *charif* and posed questions, etc." From this, it is understood that this applies to R' Zeira as well. This proof is strengthened by what is written further on there, providing examples of a *charif* and questioner who would not conclude the halachah, such as a certain student of Beis Shammai, etc., or like a student of R' Meir, etc.—except that the halachah does not follow them, which is not the case with R' Zeira.

Incidentally, and related to the above, when I was studying Shaarei Orah some time ago, I paused over the phrase "like Rabbah, who was *charif* and posed questions," for two reasons:

1) The expression cited at the end of Maseches Horayos regarding Rabbah is "an uprooter of mountains" (even though the content is the same).

2) Why bring the example of Rabbah, at the very same time that in the **original source** itself, the expression "sharp and poses questions" is found regarding R' Zeira?

Were I not afraid, I would say that this is a printing error [and one should check the early editions], and in place of "Rabbah," it should read in Shaarei Orah "R' Z" (R' Zeira). With this, both points mentioned above are understood simply, and furthermore, what is written in the Kuntres Eitz Chaim also follows simply.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6870

6870

B"H, 19 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the distinguished Rabbi, the distinguished Chossid, G-d-fearing and man of action,
R' Schneur Zalman, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter, in which you share the good news that you hope to begin heating the mikveh soon.

May it be the will of Hashem that this be done in a good and auspicious hour, and may the promise be fulfilled speedily in our days, that "purity leads to..." Eliyahu HaNavi—the herald of the coming of Mashiach Tzidkeinu.

Regarding your notification of your upcoming birthday: May it be the will of Hashem that for long, good years, you lead your congregation directly on the path that ascends to the House of G-d, and ultimately to the path of the King of the World (see Bava Basra 100b), in accordance with the will of our Rebbes, the Nasi'im, may their merits protect us.

The King of the World, but primarily (as written in the chapter of Tehillim that one begins to recite) the Shepherd of Israel. As explained in Chassidus, the concept is: "Baruch Atah" (the drawing down of the Essence is through) "Havayah" (Tzimtzum and expansion, drawing down and expansion) "Elokeinu" (our power and our vitality, of Bnei Yisrael, and through this) "Melech HaOlam."

May this be hastened speedily in our days, as mentioned above: "Shine Your countenance and we shall be saved" from all matters that conceal and hide, all with kindness and mercy and with the illumination of the countenance of the "Light of the countenance of the living King."

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6771

B"H, 19 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, in which you write that it is surprising to you that you do not have a "management" [hanhala].

I am surprised by this surprise, for the essence of a management is as its name implies: that it manages the institution, and does so well.

Even though it is understood that in matters of goodness and holiness there is always room to add—and consequently, one is obligated to add, especially since it is connected with Hashem, who is the true Ein Sof and Bli Gvul, and it is a mitzvah from the Torah to cleave to Him and to emulate His ways—nevertheless, since you are going from strength to strength in the development of the institution, as is clearly evident (comparing the institution to what it was a year or two ago), this is indeed a management. And as mentioned, my hope is that you will not be satisfied with the achievements reached until now, but will add even more, especially since you are in a favorable time to increase and expand Torah and mitzvos in general, and in the institution in particular, as mentioned, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that you bring good news very soon, in accordance with what was discussed with the dear friend, G-d-fearing, industrious, and energetic man of fine character, [Name]... regarding the necessity of a leadership that "spreads out to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south," and even to an inheritance without boundaries.

With blessings for success in your holy work and for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***6771***

**The necessity of a leadership that spreads out:** See also above, letter 6541, and the references in the notes there.

## 6,872

B"H, 20 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing, engaged in communal needs, our friend Menachem, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 17 Iyar, along with the enclosure. Thank you for your trouble in sending me a copy of the *maamar choizer*, etc., even though I believe I have it, but time does not permit me to search for it now.

Regarding what you wrote about finding in Tel Aviv the son of a Chassid who studied by Admur HaTzemach Tzedek, and that he possesses several *bichelech* (notebooks) of manuscripts or *hanachos* that his father heard—you should certainly take an interest in this and acquire them. For very often, in what the listeners themselves recorded, there are important additions, and this is understood. Thank you in advance for your efforts in all of the above.

My letter in response to your previous one was surely received in due time, and may it be His will that you bring good news regarding all those points that were written and discussed.

With blessings for good tidings.
