## 6,873

B"H, 21 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Wednesday, along with its attachment, in which you write regarding your good custom for the past 37 years of setting aside maaser for tzedakah. You ask for advice on how to conduct yourself, and whether or not you require hataras nedarim (annulment of vows), given that your income will be reduced in the future and you will be unable to continue setting aside the maaser.

It is a great wonder why you should enter into all this pilpul (legalistic debate), when the best solution and the advised course of action is to continue in your good custom, and to demand from the Creator of the world and its Guide that He grant you the ability to do so with peace of mind, in proper health, and with joy and a good heart. Regarding the halachic ruling in the Gemara concerning "turn from evil," our Sages said that once the majority of a person’s years have passed without sin, he will no longer sin. The same applies to "do good," for we follow the majority in Torah law, as is proven from the conclusion of the verse there and the conclusion of the sugya there (Yoma, end of Chapter 3).

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, and in the style of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: *Tzu a gezunten zumer* (a healthy summer).

Regarding the halachic aspect—what you wrote about not wanting a hataras nedarim because you do not wish, Heaven forbid, to regret your past conduct—I did not understand this, for the regret is not regarding the act itself, but rather regarding the aspect of the vow involved. It is also worth noting the text of hataras nedarim established by the Alter Rebbe in his Siddur. From there it is also understood that without hataras nedarim it is impossible, as it is obvious that it is no less significant than a good custom practiced three times.

Regarding what you wrote about whether one sets aside maaser upon the sale of a house or similar matters—you did not clarify your question or the doubt. If the house was purchased with funds from which maaser had already been taken, it is understood that you are not obligated to set aside maaser except from the profit, and not from the entire sum you will receive.

## 6874

6874

B”H, Sunday, Parshas Bamidbar, 5719
Brooklyn

To all the members of Agudas N’shei U’bnos Chabad everywhere, and to their representatives—the participants of the annual convention in particular,
May Hashem grant them life.

Blessings and peace!

In connection with the annual convention of Agudas N’shei U’bnos Chabad, and through the medium of this convention, I send my blessing to each and every one of you everywhere, and I also express my heartfelt greeting to the convention.

It is a greeting and a blessing for great success in the work of N’shei U’bnos Chabad in all its branches and details, and that this work should be carried out in good health and high spirits, with Chassidic vitality and joy.

And especially should this be the case in the work of the convention, which must determine and influence the program of work until the next convention—after having taken stock of the achievements of the time that has passed.

* * *

In the reading of the Torah in shul, and in its weekly study, learning it, and living with it in the home and everywhere—we are now beginning the parsha and the book of Bamidbar.

Right at the beginning, we are told about the instruction to count the Yidden (may they increase). In the later parshas of this book, we are also told about the countings of the Yidden at that time. And the Torah attaches a special importance to this—to such an extent that the entire book of Bamidbar is called *Chumash HaPikudim*—the Chumash of the countings.

* * *

What can we learn from this regarding our work of spreading Yiddishkeit, permeated with Chassidic light and warmth?

One of the main ideas in this is:

In countings—each one of those counted, no matter how great and important he may be, is still no more than **one**. And, on the other hand, no matter how small and fallen another may be, he is still no less than **one**.

One of the main obstacles to dedicated and warm work for another person is the thought: "What does it have to do with me?" or, regarding the other person’s fallen state, "What can actually be accomplished here?"

However, when one reflects on the aforementioned—that on one’s own, one is no more than **one**, and in order to attain the strength and importance of many, one **must** connect with other Yidden;

And when one reflects that, just as the other person is **one** like oneself, it is therefore very, very possible that, with the proper help, he could become equal to oneself in all respects—

One sees the incorrectness of the aforementioned obstacles, one sees the great achievements—for oneself and for the other—that can be reached, and one throws oneself into the work with renewed vitality and energy.

* * *

May the Ribono shel Olam bless your work so that it should be with much success in all respects.

Together with His blessing and success for each and every one of you and your families—may they live—in your personal matters.

And very soon, may we merit, together with all Yidden, to see the fulfillment of the Divine prophecy: "In the place (Eretz Yisrael) that is now desolate... the sheep (the Yidden) shall yet pass under the hands of the one who counts them (Moshiach)—says Hashem."

With blessings for success and for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6875

B"H, 23 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 9th of Iyar and thereafter. Regarding the educational institution, I have already replied officially to the administration in response to the letter that you also signed.

I would like to add that, to my great regret, the same situation exists regarding the other activities. Regarding what you wrote—that everything depends on the attitude and conduct of one individual—the astonishment is understandable: where there is an individual and a majority, the *halacha* follows the majority. This is not a *gezeiras hakasuv* (a scriptural decree), but a logical matter that corresponds to reality. However, it is understood that the majority must make their presence known regarding a specific matter or activity. This notification is achieved through **activity** and reporting about it. When both of these are lacking—at least to some extent—the *halacha* of "following the majority" is also lacking. Since, when the need arises, you all gather together—as is evident from the fact that you all signed the letter regarding the school—it is obvious that you must gather together to discuss the situation in its entirety. As you wrote, the situation has come about as a result of this, and it is not that "it cannot be changed for the better regarding the future."

Finally, it is understood that I must respond **strongly** to the conclusion of your letter. Firstly, I do not link minimal livelihood to activities in the vineyard of Chabad, etc., in addition to the primary point that the One who sustains and provides for all is **Hashem**, the Creator and Guide of the world. Secondly, it is also simple (and I hope no one suspects me otherwise) that my wish is that everyone should have abundant sustenance, in actual fact, *lematah me'asarah tefachim* (within the physical realm), and not to link this to **any** matters whatsoever.

What I wrote regarding the connection between the two matters was merely an expression of my opinion regarding the situation as I see it; it is not that I am the one linking them or setting conditions, *chas v'shalom*, not only regarding matters of livelihood, but even regarding abundant sustenance as mentioned above.

Since we are in the days of *Sefiras HaOmer*, where we count the days not with ordinal numbers—first, second—but with cumulative numbers—two days, etc.—meaning that the illumination of the previous day also joins the illumination of this day and its effects, may it be the will of Hashem that the good desire of each of *anash* in your camp, the good will, and the good deeds, when joined together, will certainly be able to nullify the concealments and obstructions and spread the *maayanos* in your camp and its surroundings—the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs—in circles that are ever-expanding, and with joy and goodness of heart, with expansion of mind and expansion of livelihood.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6,876

25 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn

Mr. Yitzchak Daniel
Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter, together with the question from your group of young men and women. Please accept my apologies for the delay in my response, which was due to the many pressures of the recent period, particularly the days and weeks before and after the festival of Pesach.

It is understood that the question itself cannot be answered exhaustively in a letter; therefore, I must limit my response to a few essential points. However, it is my hope that in several of these points, you will be able to add further explanation of your own, based on what is elucidated in our Torah, and particularly in the books of Chassidus.

It is also understood and simple that if you find any places or points in my letter that have not been sufficiently explained, I am always ready to answer further questions, or even challenges and contradictions, and I will endeavor to answer them to the best of my knowledge.

Regarding your question:
"Is there a way that we, as skeptics, can be convinced of the truth of the existence of Hashem — and with clarity, without any doubts or the possibility of doubts?"

Generally speaking, there are questions that appear simple and seemingly deal with simple matters, and are therefore phrased in simple, common terms. Yet, precisely such questions require extra caution, and the terms require precise definition. This is especially true for a question that has been discussed by many for hundreds and thousands of years, in various and broad circles, where it is impossible that everyone intends the exact same content in every detail. Therefore, it is a rule that one who comes with a question of this type must clarify his discourse and define the terms he uses.

This applies to your question as well—asking for proof of the truth of the existence of Hashem. The concept of "existence," and likewise what constitutes "proof" regarding something that exists, are terms whose meanings have become blurred precisely because of their frequent use, ranging from a small child to a researcher who is precise in all his matters to the ultimate degree; yet, you have not clarified your meaning in this regard.

To explain further: Some might say that for a small child, "existence" and proof of existence is only something he can touch with his hands. For a blind person, for example, is the entire existence of colors negated from his perspective, such that he must rely on another who tells him he sees colors and shades?

At a more developed level, "everyone" agrees with absolute certainty that every action necessitates the existence of a cause and source for that action. Therefore, when we see actions, these are absolute proof of the existence of the "force" that acts, even though there is no "direct" proof of it, and seemingly, room for doubt remains. A prominent example of this is the existence of electrical power. A human being is a creature of senses; his sense of sight verifies the existence of colors, just as his sense of hearing verifies the existence of sound, etc.—and these are considered complete and direct proofs. But a person has no sense to "see" electrical power. Rather, when he sees the effects—that a wire heats up, or that an electric meter moves, and the like—he arrives at the conclusion that there exists this force called electricity (which he will never see), and that it is the cause and factor behind the aforementioned actions. This, too, is considered **absolute** proof. The same applies to magnetic force, etc. I have chosen this example because the entire existence of electricity is so accepted and widespread without any doubt or shadow of a doubt.

Furthermore, at an even more developed level, it is now accepted as absolute truth—based on the assumption that every action has an actor—that the actor exists, even if this thing is "contrary" to logic.

An example of this is the "existence" of the force of gravity. The proof for this is the movement of physical objects, for which we see no cause; therefore, we accept the existence of the force of gravity, even though the existence of a force acting from a distance, without any medium in between, is a matter that sound logic does not accommodate. However, people are already accustomed to this doctrine from childhood, and it is repeated in textbooks until it becomes "simple," a matter beyond the realm of doubt and skepticism. The attempt to explain the force of gravity through the "subtle" material called ether requires this medium to have so many contradictory and opposing properties that it is even more refuted than the possibility of action from a distance without any medium—if there can be a greater absurdity than that.

I do not know in which field of science the young men and women who posed this question are engaged, and whether they are involved in what are called, so to speak, the exact sciences. But precisely in the field of these sciences, a new matter has recently emerged that is completely incomprehensible to sound logic, and yet it has been accepted by all practitioners of the exact sciences as an existence and phenomenon, and the matter has almost been verified even by the masses, even though it is entirely beyond the grasp of the intellect. This is the fact that matter is nothing but a special form of energy, and that it is possible to turn matter into energy and energy into matter. This has no place in logic at all, except that we see actions that have no other explanation, and if one accepts the aforementioned premise, they are explained—and this is considered scientific proof, accepted almost everywhere as clear proof without any doubts or possibility of doubts, as you phrased it in your question, even though from the perspective of logic, this is entirely absurd.

***

Since in the phrasing of your question you did not define at all what you would accept as proof for the truth of the existence of Hashem with clarity, without doubts, etc.—I assume that you would accept the same type of proof that you accept regarding your conduct in daily life.

From this premise, it is understood that there is proof of this type for the existence of the Creator, and not only that, but several proofs. As mentioned above, it is not a difficulty if one must say that this existence is not grasped by the intellect, or is even contrary to the intellect, because as stated, it is accepted by every person who thinks and contemplates what is happening around him in order to "explain" phenomena, that the intellect does not determine anything in this regard.

The course of the proof or proofs in this matter is along the lines of all the proofs that exist in the exact sciences, and even more—along the lines of the proofs that determine the daily lives of **each and every one** of us.

Anyone who contemplates his actions while sitting in his house, or walking on the way, or lying down and rising, will admit—and will not be ashamed—that no person demands of himself to personally examine the foundation of every action he performs or every behavior he adopts; rather, he accepts the testimony of others who have examined the matter. Only in a place where there is a suspicion that perhaps the testimony is forged, or that the witness was influenced by internal or external factors, or was not of sound mind and perceived matters in a distorted way, and the like, do we then demand more witnesses. And the more the number of witnesses increases, and the more they differ in their status, situation, and the circles to which they belong—all of which distances the possibility of error or conspiracy, etc.—the more the proof is strengthened as a scientific and absolute proof. On this basis, the individual and the collective perform many matters and actions, constantly and in order, with complete confidence that the matter is true and established.

And so it is in our case.

The matter of the Giving of the Torah at Mount Sinai has been verified generation after generation as a fact that occurred in the presence of six hundred thousand men, not including children, and if we include the women, as well as the men over sixty, etc., there were several millions of those who left Egypt, whose eyes saw and not a stranger. This is not testimony limited to one prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, or a small group. This testimony was transmitted from fathers to sons generation after generation, **and everyone** admits that there was no break in this tradition from then until now, and the number of witnesses never decreased from six hundred thousand and more people whose opinions are not similar to one another, etc. And even after they were scattered to the four corners of the world, all the versions that have reached us regarding the aforementioned historical fact and event correspond in every detail. Is there any testimony more faithful and precise than this?

A second type of proof is also based on the aforementioned assumption that when we see actions and phenomena and the conclusions drawn from them, this determines our conduct, even in a place where there is a fear of damage or loss, etc.

This is: when we see something organized, including several parts that are arranged and fit together with precise coordination, parts that have no control over one another, we conclude with complete certainty that there is a force outside of them that is the one connecting and unifying all the parts. The very fact that it is the one unifying and connecting the parts proves that it is greater and stronger than them and rules over them.

An example of this is when one enters a factory that operates entirely automatically, and one sees no person there. It would not cross one's mind, without any doubts, that there is not, somewhere, a great mechanic who encompasses in his mind all the machines and parts of the machines, who rules over them, and who assembles and unifies them among themselves and between them and the center of all the operations, etc. On the contrary, the more the human hand is absent in the aforementioned factory, and the more it operates automatically, the more this testifies to the wonder of the mechanic with greater intensity and power.

If this is the case regarding a factory, where we are talking about hundreds or thousands or even tens of thousands of parts, how much more so when one contemplates our world—a lump of wood or stone, or plant and animal, and there is no need to speak of the structure of the human body, and as the verse says, "From my flesh I shall see [Hashem]." Especially according to the explanation of science in our days, that everything is composed of billions of atoms, and every atom has several smaller parts, which seemingly should be ruled by confusion and disorder beyond measure—and yet we see that there is a wonderful order and amazing harmony between the small parts and the large and the very largest, and harmony between the parts of the small world (microcosm) and the parts of the great world (macrocosm), etc., etc.—it is clear without any shadow of a doubt that there is a "mechanic" responsible for all of this.

It is understood that the well-known formula that all of this is conducted according to the "laws of nature" has not escaped me. But I think it is superfluous to emphasize that this is an expression that contains no content of **explanation**, but rather a convenient **description** of the prevailing situation—namely, that the matters of nature are conducted according to certain orders. But to say that "the law of nature" is an independent existence in its own right, and that this existence rules over all of creation, and that there are thousands of such existences—corresponding to the number of laws of nature—is the greatest absurdity. And there is not a single scientist working in this field who would say this. The aforementioned formula is nothing but, as stated, a convenient and short expression to describe a situation, so that there would be no need to repeat at every step a long description of the most simple situations, so to speak. But it is obvious that it does not explain anything at all.

***

In essence, as stated, I assume that you are asking for proof according to which you will live your lives in terms of actual practice. And the aforementioned proof is many times stronger than all those proofs and evidences according to which you conduct yourselves every day. And what is more simple than this: when we go to sleep at night, we prepare everything for waking up in the morning, even though there is no logical basis that tomorrow morning the sun will shine again and all matters will proceed in nature as they proceeded yesterday and the day before. Rather, since the world has followed its custom for many days and years, surely these "laws" will rule also tomorrow and the day after. And on the basis of this "surely," one makes efforts and exerts labor and takes many pains to prepare everything for the conduct of tomorrow morning—all of which has no basis "in logic," unless there is a Master of this palace.

As stated above, one could elaborate on all that has been said much more, and explain some of the points even further, but I hope that this is also sufficient and provides enough material for contemplation and for the conclusion that those who say they need to search for proofs for the existence of the Creator are mistaken. The creation itself—its existence is beyond any doubt—at the very time that the opposite is true. On the contrary: according to the most recent scientific results regarding the existence of creation, and in what way to "describe" it, there is room for the greatest doubts. Especially since recently, contradictions from one scientific result to another are increasing in many areas, in addition to the greatest, primary, and fundamental scientific doubt: who can guarantee to me that the impression in my eyes or ears or brain in general has any connection with an existence outside of human senses and intellect? This is not the case regarding the Creator, or in other words, the Actor who acted and organized all of creation. It makes no difference whether there is an existence outside or only an impression of an existence, for the first axiom of a healthy person—according to which he lives his entire life—is that for every existence **in his world** that he knows about, there is a cause acting from within or from without.

One more remark I must add, which is that it is not a rare occurrence in human nature that when one brings a "simple" proof, it is difficult to accept it precisely because of its simplicity. But I hope that this is not the case regarding those who ask. For the aforementioned hesitation is not founded in logic, nor does it affect conduct—**in actual practice**, as is visibly apparent. And one of the foundations of our faith in the Creator of the world and its Guide, and in the event of Mount Sinai and the receiving of the Torah and its mitzvos, is that action is the essential thing.

I would be pleased to hear reactions to all of the above, and as stated in the attached letter, I hope that you will feel completely free to express your opinion even in places where you do not agree with what is written above.

With respect and blessing.

## 6,777

[25 Iyar, 5719]

In response to your letter of 27 Tishrei, in which you write that you heard from someone quite definitively—as is apparently implied by your letter—that it is a scientific conclusion that the earth revolves around the sun, etc., and not the opposite view, which has supposedly been refuted by various proofs, and you ask for clarification on this matter.

Since, as is apparent from what you say, you wish to know first and foremost what the scientific consensus is: in the last few decades, **all** scientists working in this field have concluded that there is no definitive determination as to which of the two—the sun or the earth—remains stationary while the other revolves around it. Furthermore, they have concluded, based on various **scientific** considerations, that science will **never** be able to provide evidence or proof in this matter. According to science, every person is free to hold whichever view they prefer: that the earth is stationary and the sun revolves around it, or the opposite, or that both revolve around one another. As is well known to those who follow the development of the sciences in our time, this is attributed to the theory of relativity.

As stated, this is the most recent and most absolute conclusion of all scientists in our generation.

Based on this, it is understood that all the verses in Scripture that speak of the sun’s movement around the earth, etc., are to be understood according to their literal meaning, and there is no difficulty with this at all.

#### ***6,777***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 18, p. 181.

## 6,778

B"H, 25 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the auspicious day of Lag B’Omer, the hillula of Rashbi.

It is certainly regrettable that the Tanya shiur with the boys has been discontinued, etc. My hope is strong that, knowing the nature of their souls, you will find an appropriate approach to renew the study. May it be His will that, since the deficiency created during the hiatus must be filled, the renewal should be in accordance with the instruction of Rabbeinu HaZaken in Tanya, Igeres HaTeshuvah, Chapter 9: "If he was accustomed to reading one page, he should read two pages," etc. From this, it is also understood that the results of the study—for study leads to action—should likewise follow this principle.

Regarding what you wrote about hesitating to continue your avodah for fear that it is not viewed favorably: such hesitation and doubt regarding this matter is very surprising. We have been commanded to engage in this [avodah] in many places in our Torah, the Torah of life. There is a beautiful allusion to this in the Tanya itself, whose conclusion and final section is in Igeres HaKodesh: "You shall surely rebuke" (*hocheiach tochiach*)—even a hundred times. Consider how profound the nature of this rebuke is, given that it is precisely in this Igeres HaKodesh that Rabbeinu HaZaken expresses himself with the words, "Gwald! Gwald!" The very order of the books of our holy Torah is itself Torah.

Regarding what you wrote about the lack of a mechitza between men and women—my hope is strong that with appropriate effort, through pleasant ways and words that come from the heart, you will succeed in rectifying this. This is of the utmost importance, as it will set a good and beautiful precedent for many settlements. Especially since even those who, for the time being, do not accept the explanation that the absence of a mechitza is contrary to the Shulchan Aruch, should be spoken to in a way that appeals even to the simple intellect of the nefesh habahamish. Surely, their intention in prayer is also to turn to One who is higher than them, however they may describe Him in their minds or feelings. Even according to their own perspective, prayer is a matter of "service of the heart" and kavanah. A direct result of contemplating, even for a brief moment, is that one must remove all matters that distract from kavanah and from connecting with the One to whom they are turning and praying. We see tangibly that the absence of a mechitza, even if they pray in separate sections of the room, provokes the men or women to look toward the other section, etc. It is simple that the above does not involve any belittling of anyone’s value. This is why the law (even though the relationship between a father and his children is, according to the Torah, as the verse states, "as a father has compassion upon his children," nevertheless the law in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 698) is that it is forbidden to kiss one’s children in the synagogue, and the reason for the law is based on the above.

With blessings for success in the dissemination of Yiddishkeit in general, and vitality in Torah and mitzvos in particular, and for good tidings regarding all that was mentioned above.

## 6879

6879

B"H, 27 Iyar, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, distinguished, and learned, whose work is the work of Heaven,
Rabbi Yosef E. (may he live and be well),

Shalom and blessing!

...I heard not long ago that his father (may he live and be well) **excels** in his Italian style. Perhaps there is room for him to dedicate some of his time to writing articles that are easy to understand (and easy in style) regarding the performance of practical mitzvos specifically, and about a traditional lifestyle in **day-to-day** life—for the **youth and young people** who speak Italian, and tailored to their soul’s characteristics.

I emphasized the matter of simplicity, etc., as mentioned above, because the two books of his father’s that I have—according to my knowledge of this language (which is insufficient, and it goes without saying that I am not an expert in it at all)—are suited for a **much** higher level, both in mastery of the language and in general knowledge.

#### ***6879***

**Rabbi Yosef E.:** Pacifici, Gibraltar. Additional letters to him—above 6550, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6880

B"H, 28 Iyar, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After the longest of intervals, your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh was received, along with the enclosure, and the receipt is enclosed herewith.

Regarding your question about one of the bochurim in the yeshiva who borrowed money from you and has not yet repaid it, even though the time he specified has already passed: you ask whether you should gift him the money so as not to cause him to stumble, or if it is possible that he will come [to pay] later.

You can certainly do as you suggested, provided it is done with a condition, and there are many such instances of conditions in the stories of Chazal.

Regarding what you wrote about the matter of bitachon, please study the Shaar HaBitachon in Chovos HaLevavos.

In connection with the upcoming Chag HaShavuos, the content for these days is enclosed herewith.

You do not mention your arrangements, and surely this is a sign that everything is in order.

With blessings for receiving the Torah with joy and with pnimiyus,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

## 6881

6881

B"H, 2 Sivan, 5719

Brooklyn.

To Mr. Eliezer Levna, may he live and be well,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I received your letter of 21 Iyar and read with interest the brief details regarding the new organization.

Regarding what you write—that there are those who are thinking about the separation of religion and state, and you emphasize that it is precisely your fellow *shomrei mitzvos* who are more extreme in this demand than the other members—from this very emphasis, it appears that your surprise is no less than my own. And the wonder is understandable. For the religion of Yisrael unites two extremes (which are only **seemingly** contradictory to one another): On one hand, it includes the most abstract matters, such as the unity of the Creator and the creation of something from nothing, etc. On the other hand, it determines the conduct of a person in their day-to-day life, in all its details and the details of those details, even the most simple and base ones. This is also evident in the content of the Ten Commandments, where the first utterance—"I am Hashem your G-d"—its inner meaning is to negate any true reality other than the reality of Hashem, and it concludes with "Do not murder," etc., in the realm of the most base instincts. This teaches that in the life of the children of Yisrael, no separation between religion and physical life is possible. On the contrary, unity and peace in the macrocosm, just as in the microcosm—which is man—are possible only through the integration of the two, meaning, by bringing spirituality into physicality and thereby refining the material.

From here comes the general motto of our Sages, of blessed memory: "It is not the study that is the essential thing, but the action." Even though they occupied themselves with the study of the secrets of Torah and the most abstract matters, they simultaneously emphasized meticulousness in practical matters that appear small, even in matters of eating, drinking, and other minute details.

And this is the touchstone for every person and every movement.

So too regarding the question of the separation of religion and state: first and foremost, one must estimate the **practical** consequences involved in this. It is easy to imagine the results if, for example, tomorrow this separation were to be realized and support for all religious matters in our Holy Land were to cease, etc. Would they truly think that through this separation, more educational institutions of pure holiness would open, more yeshivos would be established, and that rabbis, *shochtim*, and religious necessities would increase and become more affordable, etc., etc.? Given the simplicity of this point, it is surely unnecessary to elaborate that the slogan of the separation of religion and state demands the most absolute opposition from anyone who is religious and who sees what is coming—not what will come after several years, which is shrouded in fog, but what will occur tomorrow or the day after, literally.

Approaching the upcoming holiday of Shavuos, which, according to the phrasing established by the Men of the Great Assembly in the days of Ezra the Scribe, is the "Time of the Giving of our Torah," I will take the liberty of wishing you, and all those upon whom you have influence, that it be a time of both the giving of the Torah and the receiving of the Torah, and in the accepted phrasing—the receiving of the Torah with joy and with inwardness.

With respect and blessing.

## 6,882

B"H, 3 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the brilliant Rabbi, G-d-fearing and esteemed, a man of many accomplishments, R' Shmuel, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge with thanks the receipt of the book *She'eilos U'Teshuvos Zekan Aharon*, Part One, as a follow-up to our conversation when we met face-to-face.

I thank you for this gift, and may it be His will that for many more years you continue to engage in the dissemination of Torah, especially regarding matters of *halacha l'maaseh* in daily life.

For if in all places and at all times the study that leads to action is great, how much more so is the necessity for it in our current era and in this country, where the vast majority of our Jewish brethren reside.

Together with this, there are, unfortunately, many who mislead the weak by explaining to them that "the Merciful One desires the heart," and that all the rest is not so relevant. Consequently, the barriers between the Orthodox and the Conservatives, etc., are nullified. How great is the error of those who clothe the removal of these barriers in the mantle of *yiras shamayim*, etc. It is difficult to elaborate on such a distressing and painful matter.

May it be His will that, since we are currently in the days of which it is said, "And Israel encamped there before the mountain"—where all of Israel (the word *vayichan* is in the singular) were united before the mountain and its matters —through *Torah* and *mitzvos*, this situation will be rectified very soon.

With respect and blessing, in the style of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, for the receiving of the Torah with joy and inwardness.

#### ***6,882***

**R' Shmuel:** Walkin, Brooklyn.

## 6,883

6,883

B"H, 5 Sivan, 5719

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 27 Iyar, which was received, regarding its contents:

1) Whether your special donation should be included in what you are accustomed to donating annually to the annual campaign.

Since this donation is special, as mentioned and discussed, it is understood that one should not include in it donations that one is already accustomed to giving. Furthermore, it is obvious that this special donation should not detract from your regular tzedakah contributions, and this is simple.

2) Whether you can hold the funds until the time the new construction begins, since the intention is to donate them for this purpose.

You may transfer the sum in a manner that it will arrive at the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva in Montreal at whatever time you wish. However, regarding tzedakah in general, there is no benefit in delaying the money for any reason; on the contrary, one should remove the money **from one's possession** as quickly and as diligently as possible. In the words of Rabbeinu HaZaken in his Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 21: "Aside from the well-known greatness of the virtue of alacrity in all mitzvos... and the alacrity of Avraham Avinu, peace be upon him, is what stands forever... he did this with wondrous alacrity to show his joy and desire to fulfill the will of his Creator and to provide satisfaction to his Maker... and especially the act of tzedakah..."

3) You wish to transfer the funds through me. Do I agree to this?

It is understood that I agree that they be held in deposit with me until the time comes to transfer the sum to the aforementioned Tomchei Temimim Montreal fund.

As we find ourselves on the eve of the festival of Shavuos, the time of the giving of our Torah, may it be the will of Hashem—in the phrasing of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe—that you and your family, may they live, receive the Torah with joy and inwardness, and may you draw this into all your affairs throughout the entire year.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6884

6884

B”H, Isru Chag HaShavuos, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your notification regarding the scheduling of your wedding for Tuesday, the 24th of Sivan, upcoming.

I hereby express my blessing of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov, and may it be in a good and auspicious hour. May you build a home in Israel, an everlasting edifice founded upon the Torah and the mitzvos, as they are illuminated by the "light of Torah"—which is the Torah of Chassidus.

With the blessing of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov.

In response to your question—you should study (in a manner of completing them before the wedding) chapters 16-17 of Shaar HaKedushah in the sefer Reishis Chochmah. Also, the maamar "Lecha Dodi" in the Kuntres Drushei Chassunah (by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory).

## 6885

B"H, Isru Chag Shavuos, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the activists and friends of the Bais Rivkah schools in general, and to the participants in the seventeenth annual banquet of the Bais Rivkah schools in particular, may Hashem grant them life,

Shalom u’Bracha!

The banquet of the Bais Rivkah schools takes place this year during the month that stands under the influence of the Yom-Tov of Matan Torah and Kabbalas HaTorah. Consequently, all the activists and friends of the Bais Rivkah schools also stand under the impression of this Yom-Tov, whose inner content is **Na’aseh V’Nishma**.

Torah unites within itself two opposites, seemingly: on one hand, the Torah contains the deepest concepts regarding the oneness of the Creator, and so on, especially as this is explained in Pnimiyus HaTorah (Chassidus); on the other hand, the Torah connects with all the details of human daily life, even the smallest and most "mundane" ones.

This is also underscored in the **Aseres HaDibros**, which begin with **Anochi Hashem Elokecha**—the faith in the oneness of the Creator, may He be blessed, regardless of the multiplicity in creation, which demands the greatest depth; and they conclude with **Lo Tirtzach** and similar commandments, matters that are connected with the lowest instincts of man.

The significance of this is that just as the Torah unites these two matters from one extreme to the other, so too does it unite the Jew in his daily life—not by detaching himself **from** the world, but by bringing holiness **into** the world.

Therefore, Jews are called **Goy Echad Ba’aretz**—because they bring unity and holiness into earthly matters.

The aforementioned is reflected and realized in **Chinuch**—in the upbringing of Jewish children from their earliest youth. Although in their earliest youth children are not yet capable of delving into the deep concepts of Torah, the Torah nevertheless contains detailed instructions for them as well, regarding how children should be raised in all details, even the very simple ones, and this is relevant to the fulfillment of the entire Torah. Especially regarding **Chinuch HaBanos**, as every one of them must be the future **Akeres HaBayis** and the educator of both boys and girls in the way of Torah and Mitzvos—the life-necessity of this matter is clear. Therefore, I hope that each and every one will exert themselves to provide the greatest material assistance to the Bais Rivkah schools on the occasion of this banquet and throughout the entire year, and will also influence others to help in the support and expansion of the Bais Rivkah schools.

The Almighty will certainly not remain in debt, and will grant success to each and every one in their own personal and family matters as well, in both gashmius and ruchnius.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6886

B"H, Isru Chag of Shavuos, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Brownsville-East New York Ladies Auxiliary
For the Lubavitcher Yeshivos Tomchei Temimim
May Hashem grant them life

Blessings and peace!

It was with pleasure that I received the news regarding the upcoming annual luncheon on Tuesday, 10 Sivan. Since it is taking place during the days that still belong to the Yom Tov of **Kabbalas HaTorah with joy and with inwardness**—according to the wording of the blessing of my father-in-law, the Rebbe—I wish to hope that the inner joy of the Yom Tov of Shavuos will express itself in a fitting manner at the luncheon.

An expression of our Rebbes and Nesi’im, and especially the wording of a blessing, is precise—thought-out and clear:

Kabbalas HaTorah must be with both **joy** and **inwardness**, and the two are bound together. The joy that Hashem has chosen us as His holy nation and enabled us to draw near and attach ourselves to Him through Torah and mitzvos must awaken in us a yearning and an inner desire to accept the Torah in such a way that the Torah should permeate, penetrate, and animate all matters of our daily lives. Consequently, there can be no talk of viewing Torah and mitzvos as a burden, chalilah, or a learned habit lacking vitality and life; every matter of Torah becomes an internal and joyous experience, and all trials and difficulties disappear.

So too with every activity to strengthen and spread Torah and mosdos chinuch al taharas hakodesh, which must be done with joy and inwardness. And since this concerns an inner feeling, the Jewish woman distinguishes herself in this most particularly.

Therefore, I hope that each one of the members will continue their good work with increasing enthusiasm to help educate the young generation in chinuch al taharas hakodesh, in a growing measure, so that they may enjoy the blessing of Kabbalas HaTorah with joy and with inwardness to the fullest extent.

May Hashem Yisbarach grant you success in your work as well as in your personal matters, for you and your households, and may joy always prevail among you, in both gashmius and ruchnius alike.

With blessings for good tidings and success.

## 6,887

B"H, 8 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

After a very long interval, your letter from Wednesday was received, regarding your question about traveling to Eretz Yisrael (may it be built and established) for a few months.

It is highly questionable whether, even if you have the funds necessary for the travel expenses, etc., you should use them for this purpose rather than for matters of tzedakah, especially since the benefit of such a trip is not understood.

Furthermore, after a stay of thirty days in Eretz Yisrael, there is a halachic debate regarding leaving the land. Why should you involve yourself in such a debate, even if the conclusion were that it is permissible without question? Even though many do as you suggest, that does not nullify the doubts and the debate surrounding the matter.

From the content of your letter, it appears that the medical treatment for your wife (may she live and be well) is still ongoing. This only increases the wonder regarding the aforementioned initial thought. Moreover, you wrote that she is sad and of a broken spirit; as I believe I have already written to you, a Jewish person must serve Hashem with joy and a glad heart, and the service of Hashem applies to "in all your ways know Him."

I also believe I have already urged you regarding the necessity of establishing a regular study session in the teachings of Chassidus, especially in your specific case, which, as our Alter Rebbe states in his Iggeres HaKodesh, is a segulah for the birth of children (printed in the Siddur Meah Shearim under the heading "Segulah for the birth of children").

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6,888

6,888

B”H, 10 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Mr. Chaim, may he live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of 9 Sivan, in which you write regarding the matter of peace, etc.

Our Torah, the Torah of life, has already informed us of the true essence of peace, and how it is in the hands of every Jew to bring it about: through the dissemination of the matters of our Torah, which was given to create peace in the world. One should not be satisfied with conducting oneself in such a manner, but rather influence one’s entire environment. This also includes assisting in the establishment of yeshivos where Torah is studied with yiras shamayim, and the founding of new yeshivos in this spirit.

As for the establishment of an institute for world peace, etc.—aside from the fact that this matter is already being studied in several places, so to speak, and as of now, no benefit is apparent, other than a waste of money, energy, and vigor—every single penny that Bnei Yisrael give to the aforementioned institute will, at best, not cause harm, and certainly will not bring any benefit. A Jewish person should give his money to matters of Torah and mitzvos, where the reward is guaranteed and there is no loss.

It is surprising that you do not mention any of your own activities in the dissemination of Yiddishkeit, which, as stated above, is the primary task of every one of the Bnei Yisrael. My hope is that this is merely an omission in writing, and that you are doing everything that depends on you in the aforementioned area.

With respect and blessing,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

#### ***6,888***

**Mr. Chaim:** Herman, Lakewood.

**Institute for world peace:** A university for peace studies.

## 6,889

6,889

B"H, 15 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 8 Sivan, in which you write regarding the proposed match, noting that she is a G-d-fearing woman, etc.

Regarding what you wrote about the age difference, this is of no consequence whatsoever in the current matter.

And regarding what you wrote that you do not feel any connection, etc., you should examine the change that has occurred between the first time you met and the subsequent times you have met. If there has been any shift toward closeness, this provides grounds to say that this change will continue to grow and improve.

May Hashem, who oversees each and every person with individual providence, guide you on the path that is good for you, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6890

6890

B"H, 15 Sivan, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs, R' Boruch, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from Wednesday, along with the enclosed Supreme Court ruling and the victory.

May Hashem Yisbarach help that you should go from one victory to the next, and ascend ever higher in matters of Torah and mitzvos and authentic Yiddishkeit—not watered-down or amputated Yiddishkeit, but rather such as it was given to Moshe at Sinai. For it is precisely through this path, the path of the Giver of the Torah and the Commander of the mitzvos, that Hashem Yisbarach helps ensure that, ultimately, one succeeds in the strengthening and spreading of Yiddishkeit.

And it is self-understood that the stronger one is in bitachon, and the more heart, warmth—Chassidishe warmth—and mesiras nefesh one invests in this, the greater the results one sees, and soon, as "truth will sprout from the earth." "Hashem is with us, do not fear them," for "Hashem will fight for you."

With respect and blessing for good tidings, and according to the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, for a healthy summer in body and soul,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6,891

6,891

B”H, 16 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 11 Sivan, with the request for a blessing and a *pidyon nefesh* for the boy... *sheyichyeh*.

At an *eis ratzon*, I will mention him at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever he needs, in accordance with the contents of your letter.

Regarding the situation you describe in your letter, there is room to suggest that if the boy were occupied with a task where he could be a leader or an influencer of sorts (such as leading Shabbos gatherings for children a few years younger than him, and the like), it would add to his self-confidence and stability. This is because he would see that he is successful and that there are those who listen to his voice and guidance. Through this shift, it is possible that several of the disturbances would dissipate on their own. Surely there are many ways and means to organize a group as mentioned, and to draw him in (*areinzutziehen*) so that he will desire leadership. When one acts within the natural order, it serves as a vessel to receive the blessings of Hashem, may He be blessed, even beyond the natural order.

You will surely find the appropriate, explanatory words to explain to the parents (*sheyichyu*) that every increase in matters of Torah and *mitzvos* brings an increase in the blessings of Hashem, may He be blessed, for everything a person and his household requires.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6,992

B"H, 16 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you write regarding your conversation with the doctor treating your daughter, techiyah.

May it be Hashem’s will that you bring good news regarding this—not only that some slight change for the better is felt, but that this "slight" change may be great, and continue to grow ever greater.

It is a merit that you wrote your letter on Erev Shabbos Parshas Naso, and the response comes at the beginning of Parshas Beha'aloscha, regarding "lighting the lamps." Rashi explains: "until the flame rises by itself." This refers to the "lamp of Hashem, the soul of man," which must rise by itself specifically within the physical body. This is the soul’s task: to illuminate both in spirituality and in physicality simultaneously. In this state, the spirituality elevates the physicality, while the body refines, clarifies, and elevates it, as is explained in the *Likutei Torah* of the Alter Rebbe in the *maamar* beginning with the words "Beha'aloscha," see there. The root and source of his soul will assist him.

With blessings for good news in all the aforementioned.

P.S.
It is understood that if you inform me of any medical developments regarding the aforementioned, I will, G-d willing, acknowledge receipt of your letter.

#### ***6,992***

**And the response at the beginning of Parshas Beha'aloscha:** See also letter 7,002 below.

## 6893

B”H, 16 Sivan, 5719
Brooklyn

Blessings and peace!

Regarding what you write about your state of mind, and that you find it difficult to make decisions in any matter, and that you are in doubt as to whether you are acting properly, and the like.

Certainly, based on the upbringing you have received—which you mention in your letter—there is no need for special emphasis on the concept of *Hashgacha Pratis*. It is a foundation of our faith and our Torah, the Torah of life. The content of *Hashgacha Pratis* is simple: that Hashem Yisbarach created the world and leads it, and He oversees each and every person—not only in general matters, but also in one’s specific, personal affairs. From this, one understands the concept of *bitachon* in the Leader of the world, who is the essence of good; it is certain that all matters are also for the good, in the literal sense. This is understandable to the intellect of every believer, and its most immediate result is the absence of worry and the absence of confusion. For even at the time one is deliberating intellectually how to decide and what to do, even then Hashem Yisbarach is watching and helping all who desire what is good and upright. When one conducts oneself according to the instruction of the Torah, that is the good path, and this conduct itself assists a person to proceed in all their affairs in a way that is good for them.

As with all matters of faith, this point does not require intellectual hair-splitting or the bringing of complicated, deep, philosophical proofs. Every one of the Jewish people, both man and woman, feels in their soul that they are a true believer, even at a time when they are not thinking about whether this matter is correct or necessary according to the intellect. As our Sages have stated, all Jews are believers, the children of believers—meaning that the element of faith within them is present by their own power, and is also an inheritance from their ancestors who were believers. All traits that come from a person themselves, and those that come from inheritance, are strong in every respect and to the ultimate degree. And this is well understood.

It is my hope that these lines, though few in quantity, will suffice to awaken your thought and direct it to the true and inner point within yourself: that in the depths of your soul, you trust absolutely that Hashem Yisbarach is watching over you. You need only bring this thought from the depths of your soul into your daily life. There is nothing that can stand before the will. As mentioned, the way to achieve this is not through debate and intellectual depth, but rather by relying on your inner feeling that you trust in Hashem Yisbarach, and not by searching for doubts or creating questions that are not questions at all. In truth, they do not even confuse you; diverting your attention from the whole matter will certainly help you to easily rid yourself of the aforementioned confusion.

It would be appropriate that a few times a week, on weekdays, before morning *davening*, you set aside a few cents for *tzedakah*. It is best to do so on Mondays and Thursdays, and on Erev Shabbos Kodesh—it is understood that this should be done without a *neder*.

With blessings to strengthen your *bitachon* and for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

## 6894

6894

B"H, 17 Sivan, 5719

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Shlomo Chaim, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

After a very long break, your letter of 27 Iyar was received, along with the attached list of students.

It would be appropriate—if you have not already done so—to ask the bochurim who transcribe the sichos here during the farbrengens to provide you with the sichos regarding "u'fartzta" in learning. I hope you will be able to utilize them, as well as the general content of the Tomchei Temimim administration, to strengthen and increase learning. May it be the will [of Hashem] that these be words that come from the heart, for then they enter the heart and accomplish their effect.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that this endeavor, too, be in a manner of "u'fartzta"...

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.
