From this it follows that true and lasting peace, and genuine brotherhood and friendship, are founded and based upon our eternal Torah—the Torah of life—and its mitzvos, in accordance with the ruling of the great teacher, the Rambam: "The entire Torah was given to create peace in the world" (end of Hilchos Chanukah).

...As I am still under the impression of the farbrengen of the Day of Redemption, I will permit myself to conclude with a statement made by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, upon his arrival in the United States regarding his redemption. He said that his rescue then, which occurred in a miraculous manner, was not a private rescue, but rather the rescue of all the matters of Torah for which he fought, for that was the reason for his imprisonment. The conclusion in the United States must be: Torah education without compromise. And one must explain the connection: since the avodah was performed with mesiras nefesh—contrary to the calculations of the natural order, which requires the matter of mesiras nefesh—it is an avodah of the essence of the soul. This essence is indivisible; it stands equally and with full force in all parts of the soul and body. From this perspective, the very existence of compromise is negated. Compromise is possible only where there is a primary and a secondary, and one concedes the secondary for the sake of the primary. But when one reaches the essence within every detail, the "secondary of the secondary" becomes equal to the "primary of the primary." Or, regarding the virtue of the Jewish people, as in the language of the Alter Rebbe: **all** Jews are **literally** brothers, by virtue of the root of their souls in the one Havayah. By this analogy, regarding Torah and mitzvos, when there is a decree against the very existence of a mitzvah, one gives one's life even for a shoelace. By this same analogy, regarding Eretz Yisrael, by virtue of its essence—that it is a land upon which "the eyes of Havayah your G-d are always upon it"—there is no difference in time, from the beginning of the year to the end of the year, and similarly in place, from the Even Shesiyah in the Holy of Holies to the dust at the edge of the border. As it is said, "For your servants cherish its stones and favor its dust," as related by our Sages in Maseches Kesuvos, at the end.

With respect and blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 6,931

B"H, 14 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, engaged in communal needs, R' Michael [Lipsker]
May he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your question regarding what you wrote about the changes at Beis Rivkah, to change the age of the students, etc.

It is understood and quite simple that the reasoning for having classes for an older age group is very good, for several reasons. But at the same time, it is also understood and simple that there is no place to make a "business" out of this—that is, to send Beis Rivkah students to another educational institution. For the responsibility lies with the Beis Rivkah institution to ensure that even the younger ones are educated specifically in a chinuch al taharas hakodesh. There is no room here to swap one for the other; rather, one should expand and add older classes, and together with this, seek the means for the additional expenses incurred thereby. But it is simple that one should not send students away from the institution, regardless of their age. And this is clear.

With blessings for success and for good tidings.

Many thanks, especially for the list of the bochurim working in chinuch, etc., and in general for what you wrote regarding the success of the work. My hope is strong that there is even more—much more—to be joyful about regarding the fruits of your labor and that of the rest of anash who are in Morocco. And surely this will add to each one of them strength and courage, not only to continue in the holy work but to add more and more. The merit of the many assists them, and our Rebbeim, the Nesi'im, arouse abundant compassion for their success in the aforementioned, with health, joy, and a good heart, and for blessing and success in personal matters as well.

#### ***6,931***

**R' Michael:** Lipsker, of Meknes. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 14, 5,178, and in the notes indicated there.

## 8,932

8,932

B”H, 15 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, who is involved in communal needs, etc.,
R’ Yitzchak, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to the announcement regarding the engagement of his daughter, Mrs. Bruria, may she live, with her groom, may he live, in a good and auspicious hour.

May it be the will of Hashem that it be in a good and auspicious hour, and that it be an everlasting edifice built upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos, as they are illuminated by the "light of the Torah."

And may you and your wife, the Rabbanit, may she live, derive from them much true nachas.

With respect and with blessings of Mazal Tov and for good tidings.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the Shulchan Aruch of our Alter Rebbe, Hilchos Ona’ah, paragraphs 26-29: it seems there, on the face of it, that words which cause pain do not constitute lashon hara. This is contrary to the Rambam, Hilchos De’os, chapter 7, halacha 5: "One who speaks things that cause [harm] if they are heard, etc."

— And it should be added that in the *Mareh Mekomos* there, he references the Rambam in the aforementioned chapter —

In my opinion, the Shulchan Aruch there is dealing with the laws of *ona’ah* (oppression/deception). The laws of lashon hara (and cursing) were brought only incidentally.

(And the main point is to say that there is a prohibition of *ona’ah*—even in a case of lashon hara and cursing—and not to innovate that there is a prohibition of lashon hara, as is evident from the phrasing there.)

Therefore, he was brief regarding lashon hara and only brought what was clear to him. In the Shulchan Aruch of our Alter Rebbe, Choshen Mishpat, paragraph 156, section 10 and onwards—which is the place for these laws—he wrote at length and even copied the words of the aforementioned Rambam verbatim.

And see there at the end of the section: "However, a matter that has [a constructive purpose], etc., does not have [the prohibition of lashon hara], etc."

#### ***8,932***

**R’ Yitzchak:** Hutner, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 14, 4,925, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 6,933

B”H, 15 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

...For some time now, I have set my heart to consider why Hashem has done this to our people, the Children of Israel, as I contemplate the low state of affairs regarding the observance of the laws and statutes of *taharas hamishpachah*. As you also note, even though [the individual] is *kasher*, etc., it is especially difficult regarding the United States. It is tangibly evident that Divine Providence has brought the majority of the Jewish people here, and that Torah is spreading from here, etc. It is obvious that one cannot find solace in the words of our Sages that in the footsteps of *Mashiach*, insolence will increase and the son of a *niddah* will be—according to one opinion—impudent (*Kallah Rabbasi*, beginning of Chapter 2).

However, I have contemplated the law, which is clear according to all opinions, that the *tevilah* of a *niddah* requires no *kavanah* whatsoever. In the United States, the practice of bathing in rivers and oceans is extremely widespread—and not only in the summer months, but often in the winter months as well. This is in addition to bathing in places where the gathering of water is many times more than forty *seah*, and especially since the water channel from the river is often mixed with them, or with spring water.

Although it is obvious that the above should not be publicized—not only before the *amei ha'aretz*, but even before those who are on a higher level than them—at the same time, one must not, *chas v'shalom*, reject many sons and daughters of Israel from *shidduchim* with families who are *yirei Hashem* and tremble at His word, even if there were clear knowledge that they are not careful about *taharas mikveh* in public. This is certainly true in a case where there is only a conjecture, and *kal v'chomer*.

## 6,934

B"H, 15 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your note, along with your generous contribution, a portion of which is to be given to the chassan and kallah.

(See Zohar, Vol. I, 149a).

May it be the will of Hashem that this contribution, made at the beginning of the building of their home in Yisrael—a building that will endure forever—should bring about an expansion and increase in the blessings of Hashem and success in the building of their aforementioned home, a *bayis ne'eman*.

(See the maamar *Mizmor Shir Chanukas HaBayis* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, *Likutei Torah, Berachos* 98d, and in *Tanya*, *Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah*, Chapter 4).

And may you, from a place of *harchavas hada'as*, share good news regarding your general and personal matters, in a way that is good, revealed, and manifest.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6,935

B"H, 15 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

The Administration of Mosad Lubavitch in England
May Hashem preserve them and keep them alive

Shalom u’Bracha!

I was pleased to receive the letter of 7 Tammuz, written by the esteemed and energetic Chossid, active in communal affairs, R’ Shlomo Peryn, together with the key to the Beis Lubavitch building, which the shluchim delivered to me.

At an opportune time, I will mention you at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, that the Ribono shel Olam should fulfill your request, as you write, for success in your efforts to disseminate the wellsprings of Chassidus.

And may the Ribono shel Olam help that this Lubavitch house should become a merkaz—a central point—from which matters of Yiddishkeit, Torah, and mitzvos in general should spread and disseminate, permeated with warmth, hislahavus, vitality, and light. All of these are fundamental elements in the conduct of a Yid in daily life. For regarding every detail of Jewish life, it is said, "Know Him in all your ways," as it is ruled in Halacha in the Tur and Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, siman 231. And when one contemplates every matter of **"your ways,"** it leads to **"know Him"**—to recognize and become closer to the Ribono shel Olam, which must bring forth hislahavus and true light from the depths of the soul of a Yid.

May the Ribono shel Olam also grant blessing and success in the personal matters of each and every one who takes part in the aforementioned, so that they may be able to perform these tasks with tranquility, a good heart, and peace of mind, in a manner of "ascending in holiness," going from strength to strength, increasing and illuminating, until the promise of the Ribono shel Olam is fulfilled: "And you shall spread out to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south."

With respect and with blessings for outstanding success in fulfilling the purpose of Beis Lubavitch in all its matters, with joy, a good heart, and peace of mind.

#### ***6,935***

**Mosad Lubavitch in England:** See above, letter 6,909, and the references in the notes there.

6936

B"H, 16 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R’ Yitzchak, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

We acknowledge receipt of your letter from 13 Tammuz — the day of the redemption of the Rebbe, of blessed memory — along with the enclosure.

Transcribed here is what was recorded by the Rebbe on the letter and its enclosure:

1) The Tanchuma in Bamidbar explains that although for future generations one who disregards the words of a prophet is not liable for lashes, for the generation of the desert, this atonement (for death at the hands of Heaven) was granted.

If we wish to interpret the Tanchuma as offering a personal opinion or a novel idea that has no basis anywhere else, then the questioner’s difficulty is nullified from the outset. There is no need to search for novel interpretations; rather, we should interpret the Tanchuma according to its plain meaning: that he (the Tanchuma) holds that (in the generation of the desert) those liable for death are lashed and thereby achieve atonement.

In my opinion, it is puzzling to suggest that the Tanchuma would innovate that one who disregards the words of a prophet is liable for lashes, as this is the opposite of the fundamental logical principle that a negative commandment derived from a positive one is considered a positive commandment.

2) Regarding the entry of a dog into a shul.

One cannot bring proof from the initial thought (*kasa d’svara*) of the Sifrei to prohibit it in practice. One should not derive prohibitions from homiletical (*drush*) matters, even if you were to say that this prohibition is implied in the *Kli Yakar*. Specifically, as noted on the margin of the responsa you sent, there is no proof from the *Kli Yakar*, as the style is imprecise, etc.—a common occurrence among commentators, even when explaining the reasons for prohibitions.

3) Furthermore, it should be noted (and it is surprising that I have not yet found anyone who has addressed this) that the reasoning of the *Kli Yakar*, and also that of the *Sefer HaChinuch*, regarding the dog, is refuted by what is written (Yoma 21b) that in the Second Temple, the fire upon the altar crouched like a dog. If you argue that this is also proof that there is no prohibition in bringing a dog into a shul—

Seemingly, there would be room to interpret the above (albeit with difficulty) that it does not mean the fire was in the shape of a dog (or a lion, in the First Temple), but rather that they said this regarding the manner in which the fire crouched. In the First Temple, it was as it is written: "It crouched like a lion and like a lioness, who shall rouse him?"—meaning, it remained stationary and did not leap upward, which is not the case with a dog.

But this is incorrect, for the forms of a lion and a dog were indeed there, as Rashi explains *ad loc.*, and in the Zohar (Vol. III, 211a), in the Tikkunei Zohar (Tikkun 21, 62b), and elsewhere, it states that they saw the image of a lion and a dog.

4) At the end of the responsa, regarding the claim that there is no obligation to go to shul, etc.—see *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim*, siman 90, se'if 9 and onwards, and the sources cited there. It is explicitly stated in many places that one should make an effort, and it is a mitzvah to run, etc. Regarding what is written in the name of the Maharil—the *Magen Avraham* in siman 415, sub-paragraph 2, expresses doubt regarding the Maharil, but according to what is written, etc., it is certainly, etc. (In the new *Aruch HaShulchan* there, it notes that the *Responsa Chavot Yair*, sub-paragraph 12, and *Responsa R’ M. Ziskind*, at the end of siman 1, refute the words of the *Magen Avraham*, but these are not currently before me).

In such a case, there is another specific point, based on the ruling in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim*, end of siman 85, that it is a great distress that everyone gathers while they (the blind, etc.) must stand outside (although in the *Shulchan Aruch* it is only for two weeks a month, and even that is not always, unlike the case of a blind person, etc.). It is understood that one can arrange entry into the shul with the help of another person. However, the matter here is whether there is a point to his entry (whether due to distress or the importance of davening specifically in a shul), in which case one must seek ways to facilitate the entry. And this is well-understood.

* * *

You will surely acknowledge receipt of this letter. Thank you.

With respect and blessing,

Secretary

## 6,937

B"H, 16 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the distinguished Rabbi, the distinguished Chossid, a man of many accomplishments, a man of character and energy, etc., Moreinu Harav Chananya Yom Tov Lipa, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the day of redemption, 13 Tammuz—the redemption of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of the Jewish people—which was, in essence, a salvation and victory for the Torah and its mitzvos in general, as is understood and is also simple from the story of the arrest and the redemption, and the rest is understood.

In it, you write that you were asked regarding your efforts concerning a kosher and mehudar mikveh in several places, including those where the conduct regarding Torah and mitzvos requires fundamental repair, and perhaps even more than that, and you ask my opinion on this.

May his strength be fortified and may he increase his efforts to add to the strengthening and dissemination of purity among the Jewish people wherever his hand can reach. Regarding the aforementioned question, the approach to this is known, based on what my father-in-law, the Rebbe, heard from his father, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—what his father, the Rebbe Maharash, of blessed memory, answered him in yechidus (printed in Hayom Yom, 23 Sivan).

However, I would like to note that since the publicizing of one's participation in the aforementioned does not affect the kashrus of the mikveh, etc.—on the other hand, it can be said that whoever **wants** to err will err and will derive an undesirable meaning from this, even though it is the opposite of the intention. Therefore, in my opinion, one should minimize publicity as much as possible, to the extreme. This is also my own conduct in such matters, based on what I have seen—to do everything that depends on me to save any one of the Jewish people [and what are we, less than the type mentioned in Sanhedrin 44a? And Rashi’s words there are wondrous: "Their holiness is still upon them." And in the commentary of the Rashash—"The Merciful One calls them Yisrael"*] to save them even from a transgression of a minor rabbinic detail, and certainly regarding the foundations of the faith and matters that affect generations and generations of descendants. In this, the demand and the necessity are the greatest, and obviously also regarding the matter of transferring an inheritance, which is forbidden by Torah law, and even from bad to good, perhaps a worthy offspring will come from him. And the rest is understood.

Regarding what you wrote about staying in a hotel—we have seen from our holy Nesi’im that they would conduct themselves in this manner without any concern, even though it was known and publicized, since the number of those visiting them in the hotel was great. See Sukkah 32b: "**He is meticulous**... since it came from the mouth of Rav Kahana, etc."

With respect and a blessing for success in your holy work and for good tidings.

Regarding the aforementioned matter of staying [in a hotel]—it is the same regarding the photographs of people taken **by photography**—which we have seen our Rebbes use.

*) And the speech of Hashem is an act, and not only like an act (see Shabbos 119b).

## 6,939

B"H, the Fourth Fast shall be turned into joy and gladness, 5719

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday, along with its enclosure.

At an opportune time, I will mention you and your wife, may she live, at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding the contents of your letter.

Regarding what you wrote about surgery on the tubes — this does not seem advisable to me at all, as it almost permanently precludes the possibility of pregnancy. As for what you mention regarding other methods — presumably referring to preventing pregnancy — you must consult a Rav who is a *moreh hora'ah* (halachic authority) and detail the doctor's findings to him.

However, I would like to point out that often, even when there is what is called blood incompatibility, it does not affect the children at all. Furthermore, it happens that before the birth, the situation changes for the better, etc. In your specific case, since you write that your wife, may she live, has difficulties, etc., it is possible that the matters you describe can be explained by that, rather than by the blood. The practical difference is simple: by your wife strengthening her health, there is room to say that all the concerns regarding this matter will be nullified.

In every matter and at every time, we are commanded to ascend in holiness, to increase in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*, especially when one is in need of an additional blessing from the Giver of the Torah and the Commander of the *mitzvos*. Therefore, it is appropriate that you and your wife, may she live, increase in the above, in a manner suitable to your standing and situation. Primarily, I have come to arouse you regarding the necessity of establishing a time for the study of the inner dimension of the Torah, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus. If in all times and in all places this was a necessity, how much more so in our current era and in this country.

It is appropriate that you have your *tefillin* checked, as well as the *mezuzos* in your home, to ensure they are all *kosher* according to the law. Likewise, your wife, may she live, should observe the good custom of virtuous Jewish women to set aside money for *tzedakah* before lighting the candles every *Erev Shabbos* and *Erev Yom Tov*.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

#### ***6,939***

Blood incompatibility: When our firstborn son, may he live, was born, it became known that there is an incompatibility between my wife's blood and my blood, known as "Rh factor," and the illness resulting from this incompatibility can, G-d forbid, sometimes harm the children who are born.

## 6,939

B"H, 17 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Camp Gan Israel in Canada,
May Hashem protect them and may they live.

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Chag HaGeulah, the redemption of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi Yisrael, and our own redemption, may it be realized speedily.

It was a pleasure for me to read the details regarding the organization of the Gan and the day camp.

May it be the will of Hashem that these weeks be utilized to instill in the children of the Gan and the day camp a love for Hashem, which is the foundation for all positive mitzvos, and which includes the mitzvah of the fear of Hashem, which is the foundation for all negative mitzvos. May this be done in such a way that it leads to action, which is the essential thing—meaning, that from now on, all who see them will recognize that they are the seed which Hashem has blessed.

The magnitude of the reward for all those who involve themselves and exert effort in this—whether with their soul, their body, or their resources—is beyond measure, both in material and in spiritual terms.

May it be the will of Hashem that we soon merit the fulfillment of the prophecy, "The small one shall become a thousand and the young one [a mighty nation]," and that from these "days of distress" (bein hametzarim), we will merit—speedily in our days—that they be transformed into joy and gladness, for the fulfillment of the promise of an inheritance without boundaries.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 6,840

[15 Tammuz, 5719]

...Regarding the merging of the schools so that everything is in one location — it is understood that there is no place for this at all. One must absolutely avoid this, yet do so diplomatically, without offending anyone’s honor, or even anyone’s ego. There are several reasons for this: surely there is no demand or justification to abolish their community, Heaven forbid, and merge it into another. Similarly, in the aforementioned matter, one should not force the public into a single educational program for everyone. On the contrary, there should be several programs — all, of course, based on the holy Torah — so that each person can choose what is most suitable for them. (The proof is that in Kfar Chabad itself, they established a Talmud Torah, a Yeshiva, a vocational school, an agricultural school, and more, even though they are all Chabadniks. And all the more so in a large city like...). Everyone agrees that several synagogues can exist in one neighborhood if the *nusach* is different, and likewise in education: some prefer the study of Mishnah or Gemara, *bekius* or *charifus*, *dinim* or *Torah lishmah*, and so on and so forth. The less one dwells on the back-and-forth of this, and the less one highlights the principles involved, the better it will be for the sake of maintaining better and more comfortable relations in the future.

#### ***6,840***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 425.

## 6,941

[17 Tammuz, 5719]

We acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 15th of Tammuz, along with the enclosure. The astonishment and great wonder are understandable, as there is not a single word mentioned (regarding the content) of the farbrengen for the Chag HaGeulah of 12th and 13th Tammuz. If the head administrator of the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva for the entire country of... does not find it appropriate to mention this, what are those below him in status and position supposed to do? And this is understood.

It is a wonder that after thirty-two years have passed since the miracles and the redemption, it is still necessary to raise awareness about such matters, even if, on the face of it, there is room for an excuse or a pretext that "what difference does it make if it is reported, as long as the result is achieved." But we see tangibly—and as I have written to several of Anash—that the knowledge that one must report to someone regarding activities adds energy to the fulfillment of those activities. And this is understood.

May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good news in a way that is good, visible, and revealed, and that during these days of Bein HaMetzarim, we may merit, in our immediate future, that they be transformed into joy and gladness, and may the prophecy of "an inheritance without boundaries" be fulfilled.

## 6942

6942

B"H, 18 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Sunday, in which you write that you were told various things about Lubavitch, and you want to find out if they are true, and it bothers you greatly to know this.

Regarding the people you mention, from whom you heard these things, you surely understand that they have never been close to Lubavitch. Furthermore, as it appears from the way they speak to you, they are acting in opposition to the mitzvos of the Torah—and not just any ordinary mitzvah, but the *klal gadol* (great principle) in the Torah: "Love your fellow as yourself" (*Yerushalmi Nedarim* 9:4).

From this, it is also understandable that there is no room in logic to say that such people, of all people, know what is believed in Lubavitch—things that Lubavitchers themselves do not know. As for the things you write that they told you, I have never heard of them, despite being in Lubavitch far more than they (if they even **know** anything about Lubavitch at all).

What is far more painful is that they do not know what is written in many places in *Shas*, and what is ruled in the *Rambam* and *Shulchan Aruch*, etc. It would be worthwhile for you to convey to them that regarding what they say about *kavod kedushas* our holy Rebbes, *zatzukal-lah-h-n-b-g-m z-y-a*, there is a *psak din* in the *Gemara* (*Berachos* 19a) regarding their poor conduct (using a euphemism) [and the *psak din* in the *Yerushalmi* (*Peah* 1:1): "Lashon Hara is equivalent to them all," and likewise there, that one who does not speak *Lashon Hara*, etc.; *Shas Sotah* 42a; *Erchin* 15a and there at length; and in many other places].

And if, as it seems, they do not care for Lubavitch, they should at least care for the *Sefer Chofetz Chaim*. They should not keep it—the *sefer*—for others or for the bookshelf, but come to the realization that it is actually referring to them personally. May Hashem Yisbarach have compassion and mercy upon them and save them from their current state, and may it be His will that this happens with kindness and mercy.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of *kavod kedushas* Admur *shlita*,

Secretary

## 6943

6943

B”H, 18 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished chossid, who engages in communal needs, R’ Ephraim, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 14 Tammuz, along with the enclosure. Surely you will write regarding who is involved in the *farbrengen* for the days of redemption...

2) Regarding what you wrote about organizing a booklet of *pilpul*, etc.: Surely the matter has been conveyed in my name as well. Once you find at least one or two who are interested in this, it will surely provide a push for other students, as our Sages of blessed memory said: "The jealousy of scribes increases wisdom."

3) Regarding what you wrote about students from the *Reshet* schools: It has already been written—and I believe the *Hanhala* of Tomchei Temimim were also signatories—that they decided that the *Roshei Yeshiva*, *Roshei Mesivta*, or the *Mashgichim* of Tomchei Temimim should visit the *Reshet* schools, etc. It is understood that **this is** the most correct way to prepare for bringing *Reshet* students into Tomchei Temimim and vocational schools, etc.

4) Regarding arranging secular studies in the *Yeshiva*: It is understood that this is not the way. I believe we have already spoken about this several times: there must be a distinction between the institution where secular studies are taught and the *Yeshiva*. The institutions must be in different locations so that there is a clear partition.

I was very pleased to hear about the opening of the summer camp, and surely you have received my letter regarding this since then.

With blessings for success and for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

## 6944

6944

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter in which you write regarding the farbrengen and the idea of "three times a year," etc.

It goes without saying that you do not need to ask for forgiveness; rather, you should ensure that the matters you write about—which have come to your mind—should be brought down into actual conduct. The instruction of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, is well known: that any matter which leads to an increase in yiras shamayim and an increase in the fulfillment of mitzvos properly is worthwhile and correct. As it is written, "Fear Hashem and keep His mitzvos, for this is the whole man" (Koheles 12:13). And the fulfillment of mitzvos properly certainly includes the concept of *darchei noam* (pleasant ways), which is a law in the Torah, as it is written, "Its ways are ways of pleasantness, and all its paths are peace" (Mishlei 3:17). [This means] to utilize this influence within one's environment and upon others, to bring them closer to Torah and mitzvos through ahavas Yisrael and also with Chassidish warmth. And since this is demanded of us—especially from the residents of Kfar Chabad—it is certain that the strengths have been given to be able to fulfill this, both regarding one's environment and regarding oneself. And this is easy to understand.

Surely you are participating in the shiurim that are taught in public, in addition to the well-known shiurim of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, and you are also participating in the work of *hafatzas hama'ayanos* as required.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6,945

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 15 Tammuz, in which you write regarding the proposal to teach Gemara in a mixed class of boys and girls.

There are two issues involved here: a) teaching Gemara to girls, and b) teaching boys and girls together; and as is implied by the question, we are speaking of older students.

Regarding the first question — in general, one must be careful in such matters not to breach the established custom of the place, especially in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, upon which the eyes of Hashem are from the beginning of the year to the end of the year. If there is no established custom, one should act in accordance with the ruling brought in the Shulchan Aruch of our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, Hilchos Talmud Torah, end of Chapter 1, regarding the halacha that women are also obligated to learn, and choose a tractate that contains the relevant halachos.

Regarding the second question — it is more severe than the first from several standpoints. Therefore, one must strive as much as possible to ensure that they learn in separation. Even if one were to say that in their studies with other teachers there will not be the aforementioned separation, he must at least demonstrate, in his own area, the necessity of separation. This will serve as a protest on his part and will provide an opportunity for those who do not currently agree with separation to reflect on this issue, once they see the success in the place where they did separate; and the wise will understand.

It is surprising that you do not mention your participation in the farbrengen for Chag HaGeulah, 12 and 13 Tammuz, and surely you also spoke with those you influence about the episode of the imprisonment and the redemption as it relates to them.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
As per your request, the letter is being sent without waiting for the turn.

#### ***6,945***

**In separation:** See also above, letter 6,737, and the references in the notes there.

## 6,940

6,940

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Tammuz, in which you write of the logic in visiting the place of rest in the vicinity of Antwerp, and likewise to remain in Antwerp further in order to continue and complete the work of your visit from before.

This is a correct approach, and I have already stated that, very often, one can accomplish more in places of rest (kur-ort) than in the place where people reside—both regarding "gathering" physicality and regarding "sowing" spirituality. For there, they are more at leisure, and on the contrary, they are happy for news and new faces, and for various matters regarding their homes and businesses, which do not distract them in the place of rest.

May it be the will of Hashem that these days of Bein HaMetzarim be transformed, speedily in our time, into joy and gladness, and to the fulfillment of the promise of "an inheritance without boundaries."

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6,947

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of July 23, in which you write regarding the doctor’s opinion concerning your wife, *tichyeh*, that one of the tubes is supposedly blocked.

Surely the specialist doctor has explained to you that there are two tubes, and if only one is blocked, conception and birth are possible through the second tube. Furthermore, there are methods of treatment to open a blocked tube even without surgery; therefore, it is worthwhile to ask a specialist doctor again regarding this (treatment without surgery). May it be His will that you succeed in this.

It is understood that you must increase your good conduct in daily life, in accordance with the instructions of our Torah, the Torah of life, and in particular, exercise caution and precision in the matters of the laws of family purity (*niddah*, *hefsek taharah*, immersion in a *kosher* *mikveh* specifically, etc.).

It is appropriate that you have your *tefillin* checked, as well as the *mezuzos* in your home, to ensure they are all *kosher* according to the law. Your wife, *tichyeh*, should observe the good custom of virtuous Jewish women to set aside money for *tzedakah* before lighting the candles every *Erev Shabbos* and *Erev Yom Tov*.

When you write their names in the Holy Tongue (including the mother’s name for both you and your wife, *tichyeh*), we will mention them at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nishmaso b'eden, zechuso yagen aleinu*, for the aforementioned.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, *shlita*,
Secretary

P.S.
In my secretariat, we do not write in French, which is why this response is in the Holy Tongue. However, you may continue to write in French in the future, as I am able to read that language.

6,948

B"H, 22 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding the contents of your letter concerning matters of... it is understood and simple that since Hashem has graced you with talent in the field of chinuch al taharas hakodesh, and furthermore, you are active in this in a way that is constantly increasing, it is clear that you must continue in this, and even more so, with added effort and added exertion. This is certainly true—a fortiori—for those who until now have not been involved in this, or whose success is uncertain; they all have the obligation to utilize their strength for the salvation of the souls of Bnei Yisrael, to stand in the front line in conquering the souls of Bnei Yisrael, to reveal within them what is hidden and buried in their hearts—their awakening to Hashem, to His Torah, and to His mitzvos, in a revealed manner in daily life, in actual deed.

Regarding what you wrote about the financial difficulties in the general situation, and that therefore there is a commercial proposal—as a side point, I see slim prospects for the future of private commerce in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established), except perhaps for large concerns and foreign nationals, etc., for several reasons, the main one being the competition from various organizations in this area. I am writing this, as mentioned, as a side point, because even if there were prospects—my intention is regarding materiality and physicality—even then, in my opinion, there is no room for the aforementioned reasoning that you should leave your beautiful portion, chinuch al taharas hakodesh. And a second side point: according to all estimations of common sense, the field of chinuch in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) is a vast arena, not only for activity but also for compensation in the literal sense—a salary that is constantly increasing. This provides an answer to the second point, as written at length in my aforementioned letter, that it is appropriate according to the instruction of Toras Hashem to "ascend in holiness" and not, chas v'shalom, the opposite...

It is understood, based on what I have written to several of anash, that although the matter of kabbalas ol is the foundation and root of avodah, and its impression must be evident throughout the entire day, etc., and not only during the recitation of Modeh Ani and Hodu L'Hashem, nevertheless, for the success of the avodah, it is required that one should have in this a taste, pleasure, and personal satisfaction within the vessels of the intellect as well. Therefore, I suggested its place, provided that both of you accept this upon yourselves out of full agreement and not merely due to kabbalas ol alone.

At a time of favor, mention all those you write about at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in accordance with the content of your letter. It is understood that you are also included, that you should bring good tidings of your extraordinary success, in accordance with the aforementioned lines.

With blessings for good tidings in all that is mentioned.

## 6,949

B"H, 23 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 18th of Tammuz, in which you write regarding the farbrengen of the days of redemption, 12 and 13 Tammuz—the redemption of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and our own redemption, which was also a redemption for the many and a salvation for the Torah.

May it be the will of Hashem that your hand will bring good tidings regarding the above, as well as regarding your personal matters, and regarding the central point of *hafatzas hama'ayanos chutzah*. Through this, we hasten the fulfillment of the promise that from these "straits" (*mitzrayim*) we will merit, speedily in our days, to reach the "inheritance without straits" (*nachalah bli metzarim*).

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***6,949***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 13, p. 238.

## 6,950

B"H, 23 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated the 18th of the Month of Redemption, in which you write regarding the farbrengen of the days of the Chag HaGeulah, 12 and 13 Tammuz, and conclude with good news regarding...

May it be the will [of Hashem] that you continue to share good news regarding both general and specific matters, including the central point: that each and every one of us engages in *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah*. The *hafatzas hamaayanos* in general, and specifically during the days of *Bein HaMetzarim*, is a matter and an opportune time to awaken [the realization] that "because of our sins we were exiled from our land," etc. The *tikkun* for this is through *teshuvah* regarding the aforementioned, and by drawing down all matters from their root and source. Through the study of Torah, this is the drawing down of the Torah and the light of the Torah—from the "Light" within it, which in our generation has been revealed in the *Toras HaChassidus*.

With blessings for good news in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

#### ***6,950***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 18, p. 487.

## 6991

B"H, 24 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

... Regarding what you wrote about fasting on Tisha B'Av and Yom Kippur.

It is understood that one must fast the entire day, as we are commanded in our Torah, the Torah of life. By minimizing work on that day as much as possible—and most importantly, by being occupied with matters that will prevent one from thinking about the fast—one will be able to fulfill what is stated. Furthermore, as an additional measure, you should consult with a physician, as there are pills that reduce the appetite for several hours, and perhaps even longer.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 6,952

B"H, 24 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Pinchas HaKohen Peli, may he live and be well,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter, you surely know that I do not participate in surveys in newspapers.

I am taking this opportunity to express the spiritual satisfaction I felt when Rabbi Dulgow, may he live and be well, from Los Angeles visited me in his time. He is at the Brandeis camp, and he told me about the positive influence you had there and how impressed they were by your visit. Therefore, it is my hope that you remain in contact with those you influenced, especially since every young person is a potential *mashpia*—and it is certain that you will build a *bayis b'Yisrael*, and therefore every improvement in his affairs now is multiplied many times over for the future.

It is surprising that specifically in the *Panim el Panim* booklet from Friday, Erev 12-13 Tammuz—the days of the *geulah* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, the significance of which is surely known to you—there was no mention of these days. Surely many of the readers are interested in such events, the impact of which is felt to this day. One could even say the opposite: that for those who, for the time being, are not yet interested in such matters, an announcement regarding this is even more important, as is known from the parable of the sick person who does not recognize his illness, whose condition is more critical because of that.

With respect and blessing for good tidings.

It was pleasant for me to see your father-in-law, may he live and be well, at the *farbrengen* of 13 Tammuz, and he surely conveyed my regards to you.

## 6953

B"H, 27 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the participants of the 3 Tammuz assembly,
May Hashem protect them and grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your report from the aforementioned assembly, and of the proposals and resolutions regarding the dissemination of the wellsprings.

May it be the will of Hashem that these resolutions be fulfilled in practice, in a manner of "increasing and continuing, increasing and illuminating," in accordance with the precise language of the Baal Shem Tov’s Iggeres HaKodesh: that they be disseminated [outward].

And through this, may the verse "She has gone into exile" (*Galta*) be fulfilled—referring to the revelation (*gilui*) of *Yehudah*, the concept of acknowledgment (*hoda'ah*) throughout the entire structure of the *komah* (both individual and collective, once the wellsprings reach even the "outside" through this dissemination). This will be from the "affliction of the intensity" of *iskafya*, and from the abundance of *avodah*. For "she dwelt among the nations," and therefore their *avodah* is with extra intensity; and because "she found no rest," her reward for not finding it—as she is pursued by all, as it is written, "Goodness and kindness shall pursue me"—is that they overtook her "between the straits" (*bein hametzarim*), from the narrow straits (*min hametzar*), and so on, until the ascent to that which "no thought can grasp," as explained in the commentary of the Tzemach Tzedek in *Reshimos* on *Eichah* 1, 3.

With blessings for success in your holy work and for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***6953***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 4, p. 1333, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

## 6954

B"H, 27 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

The Spiritual Committee of Kfar Chabad, Holy Land, may it be built and established,
Founded and directed by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory,
May Hashem grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 22 Tammuz along with its enclosure. I will address the points in order.

1) Regarding the fact that you have not received a response from here to your letters:

I have already written to several of anash, and many times over, that I have requested—doubly and triply—not to take the delay in response into account. Even when there is no response, it is due to an abundance of pressures. If this applies generally, it applies all the more so to a Spiritual Committee, for according to the law of our holy Torah, a judge has only what his eyes see on the spot, and in matters of Torah and mitzvos, "one hour earlier is better."

2) Regarding the matter of Shemitah and the cooperative store:

Although, first and foremost, the matter of caution in these areas concerns the cooperative store, since most of the food reaches Kfar Chabad through it, it is understood that a general list of foods permitted without any doubt, etc., must be publicized. For certainly, many families and many of anash purchase items not through the cooperative store, and this is obvious.

And since there are several differences of opinion regarding the aforementioned kashrus, it is incumbent upon the Spiritual Committee, led by the Mara D'Asra, to clarify the Da'as Torah and publicize it...

May it be the will that each and every one, within the totality of the Temimim and anash, fulfills their role in spreading the wellsprings outward, through which we hasten the fulfillment of these days, when from the "narrow straits" (Bein HaMetzarim) we will arrive at an inheritance without straits, and even to the essential expanse (see Reshimos of the Tzemach Tzedek to Eichah 1:3). May this be speedily in our days through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings for good tidings.

The sending of this letter was delayed, and in the meantime, I received your letter regarding the proposal to build a mikveh in the old shul, and I have already answered R' Y.M. שי' Lis in the negative.

## 6,955

B"H, 28 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Mordechai, may he live, Hakohen.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 23 Tammuz.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you once again at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding the contents of your letter.

And since you were privileged to participate in the most exalted activities for the rescue of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in those days and at this time, may it be His will that this stand you in good stead for everything you require, both spiritually and physically. This includes, first and foremost, proper health, an expansion of your livelihood, and hearing good news in a revealed and manifest way in all matters.

And from the specific matters to the general: may these days of Bein HaMetzarim soon be transformed into joy and gladness, and may we merit the fulfillment of the promise of an inheritance without boundaries.

With respect and blessing.

## 6,950

B"H, 28 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the Chassid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Gershon, may he live, and his wife, the Rabbanit Gessie, may she live.

Peace and blessing!

After a long interval, your letter was received on Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you write very briefly regarding the farbrengen of the Chag HaGeulah, 12-13 Tammuz. May it be His will that the impression of the farbrengen continues throughout the days and months that follow.

At an opportune time, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need, in accordance with the content of your letter.

As we find ourselves at the conclusion and seal of the Chag HaGeulah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi Yisroel, may his merit increase success in all matters, both general and personal, for each and every one of us, in all that we need, and with joy and goodness of heart.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

P.S.
Regarding what you wrote concerning *Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah*, "You give life to all" — it is not merely "giving life," but "bringing into being" (*mehaveh*). I believe I wrote to you some time ago regarding this, that it is also in the beginning of *Shaar HaKedushah*, end of Chapter 7, in the name of our Sages, of blessed memory, and similarly in *Pardes*, *Shaar* 6, Chapter 8; *Shelah*, page 48b, and elsewhere.

#### ***6,950***

**R' Gershon:** Chen, Haifa. Additional letters to him — see above, Vol. 17, 6,412, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 6,957

B”H, 28 Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of July 12, in which you outline the events you have experienced and express your aspiration for a religious life and a desire to dedicate yourself to the education of Jewish children in the spirit of our ancestors, yet you express concern regarding the influence of your surroundings.

Now, in our Holy Land—may it be built and established by Moshiach Tzidkeinu—there are many possibilities to live in a religious environment. Indeed, the command of our Torah, the Torah of life, as instructed by the great teacher, the Rambam, is that a person must attach themselves to the righteous, sit constantly with the wise, etc., and distance themselves from the wicked, etc., and go to a place where the people are righteous and conduct themselves in the way of the good. Before this, he explains the reason: because it is the nature of a person to be drawn in their opinions and actions after their friends and acquaintances.

The same applies regarding involvement in religious education, as there are many religious institutions seeking teachers in the aforementioned spirit, with appropriate salaries and suitable conditions.

Since you already have experience in the field of education, surely with some effort you will find a suitable place to utilize your talents in an education that draws the hearts of the children of Israel to our Father in Heaven. Certainly, the merit of this holy work will stand by you in your personal matters, for inner tranquility—true tranquility—and for a suitable arrangement in life in general, and specifically regarding the central point: a good shidduch in all its details.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 6958

B"H, End of Tammuz, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Eliezer Aryeh HaLevi, may he live,
Known as Dr. Finkelstein

Greetings and Blessings,

Under separate cover, you will receive our latest publications, and it is my hope that they will be of benefit to the many.

On this occasion, I would like to address one point in your letter, where you write, "I am not a person of influence in any way. In our circles, there is no power of influence," etc.

There is a well-known adage from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding such matters: just as it is incumbent upon every person to know their own shortcomings—for only then can one correct them and live one’s life as is proper—to the same degree, one must also know one’s own talents, capabilities, and virtues.

Surely, especially in these days, there is no need to elaborate with proofs that you are a person of influence. According to my information, your influence has succeeded in causing an important shift to the right in the circles over which you have influence.

And as it has been reported to me, were it not for your intervention, certain matters would have turned out in a completely undesirable fashion.

It is understood that I do not know the details of these matters, but the words of truth are recognizable.

It is surely also superfluous to elaborate on the point that any success in a good action must certainly cause an increase in effort in this direction. As our Sages of blessed memory said, "He who has one hundred wants two hundred"—by adding another hundred; "and he who has two hundred wants four hundred"—by adding two hundreds. And—if you will forgive me—these are fundamental matters in the foundations of our faith; we expect and yearn for a fundamental change among those you influence.

Regarding what you write, that there is only one way to exert influence—through an increase in Torah study, in the hope that the "light" within it will return even those who are distant to the proper path: although the mitzvah of Torah study is equivalent to them all, and the light within the Torah has the power to return one to the proper path, we are not permitted, nor does the time allow us at all, to stand from afar and wait for the aforementioned fulfillment. Especially when we are speaking of those who are influenced, who themselves are also influencers in a circle of greater or lesser size. Furthermore, we have a clear ruling and instruction in Pirkei Avos that in order for one's wisdom to endure (and the intent of the Mishnah is certainly the wisdom of Torah), one's fear of Heaven must precede it. When one observes the nature of the souls of the youth of our people in the United States, one sees that they are ready—even more so than those of middle age—to accept true opinions without compromises. On the contrary, every compromise weakens the power of the influence. Surely, here too, it is unnecessary to elaborate that the meaning of "fear precedes wisdom" is to be taken literally: that the study then leads to practical mitzvah observance. As the wisest of all men concluded after all his investigation and inquiry: "Fear G-d and keep His commandments, for this is the whole of man."

The month of Tammuz is marked for us by the auspicious days of the 12th and 13th, the days of the liberation of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, from his imprisonment in the Soviet Union. The history of the leaders of Israel should serve as a living moral lesson, and the inner point of these days of liberation is that one who truly fights for the truth, even to the point of actual mesiras nefesh—especially in a matter that concerns the many—can, even as a single individual, stand before and against a mighty regime and even overcome it.

With respect and blessing.

6959

By the Grace of G-d
1 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished scholar, engaged in communal affairs, R’ Yitzchak, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha,

Following a long interval, I received your letter from Sunday, along with the enclosure confirming receipt of my two letters. I am certain that as this matter continues, you will write with the appropriate level of detail. Thank you in advance.

Further to the above, I also intend to hear your own observations, as you wrote in your letter that you were reporting the matter without additional comments, as well as the back-and-forth of the inquirers themselves. Surely, they are more open with you in this area than they would be with others.

Allow me to add that my intention was not only that they receive the two letters separately, but also regarding you. However, in your case, my intention was fulfilled, as there was an interval of several days in between. This is not the case for them; the style of your letter implies that they read both letters one after the other, without an interval for the mind to "digest" the content of the first letter, which is a pity. My reasoning for this is understandable: by human nature, in order for a message to be received more deeply and effectively by the emotions, the appropriate preparation is for it to be preceded by a disappointment in the realm of the intellect—or, at the very least, a sense of incomplete satisfaction in the intellectual realm. As I hinted and emphasized in my letter, even though they ask for an answer based on intellect, the nature of the question—and the core of it—is that the pressure and the demand for a solution stem primarily from the emotions, not the intellect. Therefore, it is impossible for an intellectual answer, even a perfect one, to quench that thirst. It is possible that in your aforementioned observations, if you write some of what you felt after reading the first letter and before receiving the second, the aforementioned point will also be reflected.

I will take this opportunity to answer your question from some time ago regarding your conversation with some of Anash, where there was a doubt as to whether the reality of the physical world will change in the time to come—meaning, whether one’s understanding or feeling in matters of Elokus will change—or if the things brought in this regard are only by way of metaphor and allegory.

According to what is explained in Chassidic teachings, the verse regarding the promise for the future, "And the glory of Hashem shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see together," is to be understood literally: the flesh itself will see (and in those creatures that possess intellect, they will also understand) that the mouth of Hashem has spoken. This is based on the statements of Chazal in the Gemara and Midrashim*, and it is also found in the saying quoted in *Hayom Yom**: "A stone shall cry out from the wall," etc.

I received the "Kemper" booklets and saw your article printed there, "On the Foundation of Emunah and Mitzvos." I was pleased that through it, these words of inspiration reached the readers of that publication as well.

As is the custom among the Jewish people, one cannot refrain from a comment. I am sure you will forgive me for what follows: it concerns the "inappropriate" impression created by grouping together, in one breath, the words of the Prophets, Nachum Ish Gam Zu, and then the authors of our own generation—and a word to the wise is sufficient. Even though we have heard many times from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, that one should not say "lehavdil," G-d forbid, between one Jew and another, it is obvious that this does not mean that all boundaries are erased.

It is understood that I also remember what is brought in several places [apparently based on the Rambam in his introduction to Chelek], "Accept the truth from whoever says it." Nevertheless, after asking your forgiveness, the sequence of the aforementioned names, one after another, leaves the impression mentioned above.

You will surely suspect—and you would be aiming at the truth—that my intention in writing this is the hope that perhaps, through this, such a matter will be avoided in the future.

We conclude on a positive note, with thanks for your assistance with the teacher training course organized by the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchak Lubavitch. I am certain that you will continue to do likewise in the future.

May it be Hashem’s will that, as we are approaching the anniversary of the Geulah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe—which was a redemption for the many and the salvation of Torah at that time and for the years that followed, and for which he sacrificed himself to strengthen Yiddishkeit in general and to disseminate the wellsprings of Chassidus in particular—may every person who is aware of this strive with energy to disseminate the wellsprings outward. Through this, we hasten the fulfillment of the promise (that these narrow straits will soon be transformed into joy and gladness, in our days, through the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu, and we will merit) an inheritance without boundaries.

With respect and blessing for good tidings, and with the hope that you, may you live, are well.

P.S.
Regarding what was written above—the response concerning the letters—it is understood that you have permission to share this with the inquirers as well, naturally after they have responded or will respond to my letters as they are, without adding the impression from what was written above.

Regarding the publication of the aforementioned letters, which you wrote about—I rely on your judgment.

In your classes for the study of Tanya, you surely season your words with matters of *arum fun Chassidus* (surrounding topics of Chassidus), as has been the custom in such matters for some time. There is a rich and varied amount of material available for this in the Sichos and Likutei Dibburim that have been printed.

*) See Midrash Tehillim, end of Psalm 73; Yerushalmi, Avodah Zarah, Chapter 4, Halachah 7; and elsewhere [and this was also the case before the sin (Avos D'Rabbi Nosson, Chapter 1, 6)]. And in many places in Chassidus. For example, in Torah Or by the Alter Rebbe, the maamar beginning "Tipol Aleihem," section 17.
**) 15 Adar. And see also Taanis 11a.

## 6860

6860

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter, in which you outline what you have been experiencing and your current state of mind—that you feel you are in a state of decline, G-d forbid, experiencing a lack of feeling for the people in your environment, and a lack of alignment between your actual conduct and your desires, etc.

First and foremost: since a person is naturally drawn to their environment and learns not only from their conduct but also from their knowledge and worldview—and since even within that environment, views are not identical—you must arrange to be in an environment that is appropriate for the path you have chosen in life: the path of true happiness, the path of our Torah, the Torah of life. Within that environment, you should draw closer to those who are most aligned with a Torah-based outlook on life. It is the Torah that brings peace between the various emotions within the soul, between the person and their environment, and, in a general sense, to the world outside. In the phrasing of our Sages, the Torah is what makes peace in the heavenly retinue and in the earthly retinue.

Regarding what you wrote about a state of decline: it is understood and simple that even if this assessment were correct, G-d forbid, the remedy is not to be found in feelings of discouragement or inferiority, etc. On the contrary, one must summon additional courage and bring hidden inner strengths to light in order to overcome the decline, even to the point of surpassing the level you were at before the decline. This is the task of a person, who must spend all the days of their life going "from strength to strength," moving forward. Since this is the task of a person, surely the Creator of man gives him, from the outset, the strength to fulfill this task as fully as is required.

An important point is missing from your letter—and it is possible that therein lies the solution, at least to some of the undesirable phenomena you write about—and that is your relationship with your family and those closest to you, as well as regarding the area of shidduchim. I am sure you will provide information on this as well at the next opportunity.

As for your question at the end of your letter regarding the domains of the intellect and the emotions: you are correct in the assumption that one should not suppress the emotion, but rather, as with all human matters, one must work with it in order to ascend to the necessary perfection. However, the operation of the emotion must be under the supervision of the intellect. In other words, in the phrasing of our Sages, the brain must rule over the heart. There are two points of precision here: a) the brain must be the ruler, and b) the heart must still function, but under the rule of the brain.

It is understood that one cannot set hard-and-fast rules for all people and all situations, etc.—that "up to here is the domain of the intellect, and from here is the domain of the emotion," or vice versa—because it differs from one person to another, and it differs from one situation to another. As with all human matters, one must always find guidance in the Torah as to how a specific person should behave in a specific event and how to define the boundaries in the aforementioned. In a place of doubt, it has already been said, "In the multitude of counselors there is salvation," with acquaintances and friends—those whose own worldview is based on the true foundation, the foundations of Torah and mitzvos.

You do not write about your study program. Surely you have fixed times for study, which is necessary in order to know the deeds you should do and those you should not do, as well as study that relates to establishing a worldview consistent with the tradition of our ancestors.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 6861

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter, as well as the *pidyon nefesh* that you transmitted through the traveler who came here from our camp.

Regarding what you write about traveling and visiting here:

It is already well-known and publicized that I am not at all pleased with this, for several reasons. In addition to the fact that such a trip involves many burdens and expenses—expenditure of energy, expenditure of money, and expenditure regarding health—there is also the fact that there is a vast field for every one of *anash* in Paris and its surroundings to act in accordance with the purpose of man. As the *Mishnah* states, "I was created to serve my Maker"—meaning, progressing from strength to strength in Torah and *mitzvos*, both regarding oneself and regarding influencing others.

Furthermore, when I observe those who have come here and the change that has occurred in them compared to their way of life before this in Paris, I must say that for most of them, their *pizur hanefesh* (distraction of the soul) has increased significantly. Much of their time is given over to matters that, in their way of life before coming here, were not at all necessary. They could have dedicated this time to spirituality, or at the very least to matters of substance, rather than wasting time on trivialities—matters of fleeting life, and even less than that. And if our Sages have generally informed us that living in large cities is difficult, how much more so is visiting a city in the United States, which is a country famous for *pizur hanefesh*, the vanities of the world, and the *tumel fun der gas* (the commotion of the street). While it is understood that within the *daled amos* of *Bnei Yisrael* the "street" is permissible, it nevertheless blurs and lowers a person from their level in many, many areas. I wish that most of the *anash* who came here from France would listen to my voice and, with kindness and mercy, in joy and goodness of heart, return to Paris and its surroundings to fulfill their *shlichus* there, to strengthen Yiddishkeit in general and to disseminate the *maayanos* in particular.

All of the above applies also to those who visited here for a few weeks, as experience has shown that they wasted their time and energy as mentioned above. The main point is their distractions here; a *Chossid* must devote his time and strength not only to the affairs of the *nefesh habehamis*, but even to things lower than that—to matters that would not have even crossed their minds before coming here. There is no need to elaborate on this most distressing matter.

I wish that these words of mine were publicized in a way that they would also influence others, and also those who have come here, to do everything mentioned above.

With blessings for proper health, and that you may engage in Torah and *mitzvos* in your current location with tranquility, joy, and goodness of heart. May you serve as an example to all of *anash* who are there, and also those outside of there, that they too should do likewise. It has already been said: "G-d made man upright, but they have sought many calculations."

With blessings for good tidings in all that is mentioned.

## 6862

6862

#### ***6862***

This letter is addressed to Rabbi Mordechai Mentlik, and was written on the margin of his inquiry dated Friday, 6 Menachem Av, 5719:

In the sicha of Simchas Torah 5718... [it is explained that] the entire twenty-four-hour period of Shabbos, including the *tosefes Shabbos* (the additional time added to Shabbos), is a single, unified category...

Proof for this is from the words of the Mechilta... However, in the sicha of 13 Tammuz of this year, this matter was also discussed in the name of the Rogatchover, and the Mechilta was also cited. It was explained there that if we did not have the teaching from the verse, we would have thought it was a single, unified category, and regarding this, the verse reveals to us that it is a composite category.

See also regarding this in Volume 2 of the Igros, letters 176 and 183, and at length in Likkutei Sichos, Volume 8, page 50 and onwards.

## 6,862*

*[6 Menachem Av, 5719]*

In the Mechilta (Tissa), it can be explained in **two** ways:

1) That the verse teaches us that Shabbos is joined, contrary to the initial assumption (*hava amina*).

2) That the verse teaches us that **even though** Shabbos is a point (*nekudah*), nevertheless there is a desecration upon desecration, since it says "those who profane it" (*mechaleleha*), in the plural.

It should be noted that the Sefirah of Malchus (Shabbos) indicates: the letter Yud (a point directed upward) and the letter Hei (the **expanse** that receives).

## 6,863

B"H, 6 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Shabbos, as well as the previous one, and I trust you are aware that I have written to all those you suggested I write to.

Regarding what you wrote concerning "smallness" (katnus): It is puzzling that you are searching for rationalizations as to why you should disregard what has been said, especially after this has been discussed so many times and is founded upon the holy words of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im—it is a matter of how one positions oneself, and so on.

It is also surprising that from letter to letter, you once again raise "straits" (mitzrayim) and limitations, and once again it is necessary to repeat the ruling that a King—*man malchei rabbanan* (our Nesi'im and their affairs and institutions)—breaks through boundaries. As was discussed at the farbrengen, citing the explicit words of the Tzemach Tzedek (who is the *posek* in our matters, and *b'asra d'rav nahig k'rav*—in the place of the Rav, one follows the Rav), it is precisely from the straits that one arrives at the true expanse, to an inheritance without boundaries. See the Reshimah on Eichah 1:3: "And for praise, one can say," etc.

With blessings that we will soon merit the fulfillment of the prophecy that these days will be transformed into gladness and joy.

## 6864

B”H, 6 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of the Beis Medrash for Teachers, Beis Rivkah,
And the Beis Rivkah School in France,
May Hashem protect them and may they live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 3 Menachem Av, along with the enclosure: the curriculum for the first academic year of the Beis Medrash for Teachers.

It was a pleasure for me to read the details of the program. Regarding the matters mentioned therein, a letter will be sent from the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch if necessary.

I would like to add that, surely, even on Erev Shabbos, the day should begin with Kodesh studies in addition to davening—at least for a few moments. This is especially true regarding matters relevant to that day, i.e., the parsha of the week. As stated in the Shulchan Aruch, one should read Shnayim Mikra V’Echad Targum on that day. Furthermore, this is relevant to these weeks, as we must provide the students with provisions for the journey—I refer to the vacation days. On the contrary, these months (and especially Tishrei) require an increase in all matters of Yiddishkeit, our Torah, and its mitzvos, as they are replete with halachos and minhagim, etc., beyond the rest of the year. To the extent possible, it would be worthwhile to find a suitable framework that does not interfere with the concept of vacation, yet simultaneously fills these days with the aforementioned—out of their own good will, rather than through the compulsion of the Beis Medrash and school. We see tangibly that these months are days of ratzon, especially conducive to good absorption of matters of Yiddishkeit. This is also understandable through human intellect, as the study is combined with actual practice of the Yom Tov and the days preceding and following it, and their influence is felt throughout the entire year. As is also true in the pnimiyus of these matters, it is known that every Yom Tov and moed is a general principle for the entire year in its respective areas: Rosh Hashanah for the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, Yom Kippur for the matter of teshuvah, Sukkos for the simcha of a mitzvah, etc.

In general, regarding textbooks about which there is doubt: in such a case, one should rule stringently. We are dealing with the chinuch of youth, where one must be careful even regarding a small deficiency—all the more so, as is known by the example that a small crack in a seed can ruin the entire growth of the tree, chas v’shalom, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

To the above, one cannot bring proof for leniency from the use of a book that contains excerpts from several authors without mentioning the author’s name, where only a few of their concepts are gathered. This is not the same as a complete book by a specific author, which constitutes giving a hechsher to all of his views.

During these days of Bein HaMetzarim, regarding which our Sages said (Shabbos 119b) that Jerusalem was destroyed only because the chinuch of children was neglected, may each one of you increase your efforts in rectifying this matter by adding and exerting extra effort in chinuch al taharas hakodesh for the children of Yisrael. It is obvious that the chinuch of girls is included in this (as brought in the poskim, among them the Hilchos Talmud Torah of our Alter Rebbe, end of Chapter 1: women are also obligated to learn the halachos they require, etc., all positive mitzvos that are not time-bound, and all negative mitzvos of the Torah and Rabbinic origin). This, too, will hasten the fulfillment of the prophecy that these days will be turned into joy and gladness, and the promise of an inheritance without boundaries will be fulfilled.

With respect and blessing for success.

6,965

B"H, 8 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 5th of Menachem Av, in which you outline the events you have experienced recently. It is my firm hope that wherever you may be, you do not, G-d forbid, lose sight of the task incumbent upon every member of the Jewish people, as the Mishnah states: "I was created to serve my Maker." As is explained in many places, this avodah is a daily way of life, following the path of Torah and mitzvos. Within this is the great principle founded upon "Love your fellow as yourself"—to influence those around you so that they, too, conduct themselves in the aforementioned manner. Our Sages have already promised that words which come from the heart enter the heart of the listener. This is especially and primarily true because, in essence, the heart of every Jew is awake to Hashem, His Torah, and His mitzvos; it is only that for some, the inner core of their heart is covered by a layer of dust, mud, etc. When someone helps remove this layer of concealment and hiddenness, the one being helped also assists from the depths of his own heart—whether he is aware of it or even if he is not. This is also understood, of course, from what is explained at length in the holy Tanya regarding the hidden love in the heart of every Jew, which is an inheritance from our forefathers.

Especially at this time, one should not wait to act on behalf of others until one has reached personal perfection. Rather, these matters must be pursued simultaneously; indeed, each type of activity assists the success of the other, as is also evident in tangible experience. Since you are now living a life founded upon discipline and kabbalas ol, you surely draw the a fortiori conclusion for yourself: if this is the case regarding the commands of flesh and blood, how much more so regarding the command of the Creator of man, the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He.

Regarding what you wrote about your father, shlita, and whether he should change his profession: the general rule in such matters is not to abandon a source of livelihood until there is a well-founded expectation that in the profession one wishes to pursue, a livelihood will be available with a degree of certainty bordering on the absolute.

At an auspicious time, we will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in accordance with the contents of your letter. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding all the above, and in this, of course, is included the matter of spreading the wellsprings, through which we hasten the fulfillment of the promise that these "straits" (mitzrayim) will be transformed, in our immediate time, into joy and gladness, into an inheritance without limits (bli mitzrayim).

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

A shidduch with someone whose attitude toward Torah and mitzvos is not as it should be—it is understood that this is connected with many complications, which cannot always be assessed in advance.

## 6866

6866

B"H, 13 Menachem Av, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, whose work is the work of Heaven, R' Y. [Grossman] shlita,

Shalom u’Bracha!
