If it is at all possible, my request is twofold: to send them to me for at least a few weeks. It is understood that I will return them if that is a condition that cannot be changed [though perhaps they can be exchanged for printed versions published by Kehot in that same subject area].

This is because with the aforementioned Maamarim, it is difficult to identify them based solely on the beginning of the Maamar, since they were sometimes repeated two or three times **with the addition** of various points or notes and emendations within the body of the Maamar.

Regarding the Maamar beginning with the words "Lama Korin L'Yom HaKippurim" etc., and the Maamar beginning with the words "She'amar L'R'M Meislish" etc., these have already been printed in various places.

Regarding what you mention about the Maamar of 195 chapters, the Maamar beginning with the words "Mayim Rabim," it is by the Rebbe Maharash, may his merit protect us, and it was printed long ago in the United States.

I have requested that a Kehot catalog be sent to your honor; it includes a list of the printed Maamarim of Chassidus.

Awaiting good news regarding the above, and signing with respect and with the blessing of Ksivah V’Chasimah Tovah.

#### 6,996

**Rabbi Dovid Shlomo:** Shapiro, Milwaukee. An additional letter to him—above, Vol. 18, 3,133.

## 6997

6997

By the Grace of G-d
11 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and dedicated to the work of Heaven,
R’ Shlomo Chaim, may he live and be well,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the beginning of the month of Elul, along with the enclosure, as well as your previous letter, in which you write at length regarding the situation in the holy Yeshivah, where Divine Providence has granted you the merit and success to serve as a mashpia.

It is understood and self-evident that what I wrote from here regarding the great importance of education in purity—*al taharas hakodesh*—is by no means a reason to cancel classes or to abandon the overall structure. This is especially true given that only a very small percentage are capable of the ideal form of education, and naturally, only a small percentage are approved by the relevant offices, etc.

Similarly, regarding what you wrote about the distinction between those sent from here to the outlying cities versus the situation in Eretz Yisrael (may it be built and established), this distinction should be explained to the aforementioned individuals, not to me. It is the primary duty of a mashpia to ground the bochurim in the true intent of our Rebbes, the Nesi’im, regarding each and every one of them. The main thing missing from your letter is that there is no sign of an effort to root out and eradicate the assumptions held by some of our Sephardic brethren, may they live, whose past circumstances forced them to involve their children at a young age in matters of livelihood, until this habit became a second nature. The magnitude of the effort and exertion required to uproot such a natural tendency is understood, and this, too, is the duty of a mashpia of Chassidus.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the booklet of student pilpul—that they are not suited for *chiddushim* (original insights), etc.—I do not know the basis for the idea that the booklet must contain *chiddushim*. Surely you have seen the booklet printed here, where the main emphasis is on *hasbarah* (explanation) and not on *chiddushim*. If only this were the case in the revealed part of Torah as well, and not just in the part of Chassidus. As for your statement that it is enough for them to understand the concepts simply and that one should not demand more than that, the distress is understandable if the situation is as you describe. However, my hope is that the situation is not so, and that one should demand much more from them than what you wrote in your letter—except for the exclusion of the aforementioned *chiddushim*. This is because that is not the correct way of learning, nor is it the way in the revealed Torah, as explained in *Kuntres Eitz Chaim*, and all the more so in Chassidus. If only they had been printing explanations of Chassidic concepts since then; it would have given a special desire to the students and to those overseeing them, and primarily, it would have drawn many more to the study of Chassidus as it should be, and not merely because the Yeshivah schedule compels them...

I am sending this letter with urgency, especially for the sake of the last point, because as mentioned above, it is imperative that the aforementioned booklet be published as soon as possible, and that the mashpia dedicate himself to this as well, even though the main thing is the actual deed.

May it be the will of G-d that the days of Elul, a time of favor, be utilized—as the Alter Rebbe says, that the Thirteen Attributes of Mercy shine during this time—so that the students will be aroused to study the revealed Torah and Chassidus specifically in the manner described, with depth and *iyun* (analytical study). Then, surely, many, many topics will be available for printing in the aforementioned booklet...

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good.

## 6,998

6,998

B"H, 12 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding your second question concerning moving to... at the request of your wife, tichyeh.

You are correct in your contention that it is difficult to uproot one’s place of residence from a place where one has lived for approximately fifty years, especially since your livelihood is there and your business is there. However, since we are especially warned against causing distress to a wife (see the Shulchan Aruch of Rabbeinu HaZaken, Hilchos Ona'ah, at the end) because they are sensitive by nature, etc., you must appease the mind of your wife, tichyeh, through other matters, and not rely solely on convincing her through intellectual arguments of the mind alone.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good.

## 6,999

B"H, 12 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 7 Elul, in which you write regarding your brother, *sheyichyeh*, who attends gatherings of those who incite and lead others astray, *r"l*, etc.

It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding *shidduchim* for your brother—whether you have been involved in this, what the results were, etc.—and likewise, what he is occupied with regarding *parnassah* and the like as a steady pursuit. These two points can be utilized to influence his speech and his perspective on the various matters he discusses. For, as is evident from your letter, there is not much benefit in intellectual debate; rather, the goal should be to draw him into a suitable lifestyle where, for example, he will not encounter these inciters, etc. On the contrary, he should be in an opposite environment, or at least a different one, where people are not preoccupied with philosophical inquiry, etc. One must take a special interest in finding suitable employment for him, for he surely requires funds for various expenses and the like. Once he feels that he is earning and making money, there is room to say that this will occupy him, although, of course, it should be arranged that the income be minimal, so that he does not get the feeling that he is independent, etc.

It is appropriate to check the *mezuzos* in your home to ensure they are all *kosher* according to *din*, and likewise your brother’s *tefillin* [even if we assume there are days he does not put them on, they still belong to him]. Once they are *kosher*, he will surely find a favorable moment to put them on, at least for a few moments. Great is the power of *tefillin*, for as our Sages have ruled (*Menachos* 44a), they draw down life—which includes life in spirituality (see *Tur Orach Chaim* 287).

At a time of favor, you should mention him at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, navgam, zya*, in accordance with the content of your letter.

And since everything is by *hashgacha pratis* and you have turned to me, I will urge you and his father, *sheyichyeh*, regarding the necessity of setting times for the study of *pnimiyus haTorah*, which in our generation has been revealed through the teachings of Chassidus. Regarding this, Moshe *Reiya Mehimna* stated (*Zohar* Vol. III, 124b) that there, on the side of evil, there is nothing; on the contrary, it brings about that "I will remove the spirit of impurity from the land." If in all times and in all lands this was a necessity, how much more so in our era, and in this country in particular.

May it be the will of Hashem that just as you brought this request, you will bring good news regarding this.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

On behalf of the Rebbe, *shlita*,
Secretary

## 6,600

6,600

B"H, 13 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

...Regarding your previous question as to which seforim of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, you should prioritize acquiring for your library: begin with *Likkutei Dibburim* (recently published in four volumes. They will be sent to you now from here, as it is unknown whether they are already available in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established), *Sefer HaSichos*, and the *Kovetz* publications.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the words of the Rambam (*Hilchos Teshuvah*, end of chapter 9) that in the days of Moshiach the world will continue according to its natural order—he wrote the same in *Hilchos Melachim*, beginning of chapter 12. See also *Avodas HaKodesh* (by R. Meir ibn Gabbai), Part 2, chapter 38. Even based on the literal phrasing of the Rambam on this, he **clearly** expressed his **absolute** opinion regarding the events that will take place **before** the revelation of Moshiach and their order—that is to say, **before** the Geulah begins. His words in *Hilchos Melachim*, chapter 11, are: "The King Moshiach is destined to arise and restore the kingdom of David to its former state... and he will build the Beis HaMikdash and gather the dispersed of Yisrael... whoever does not await his coming... denies the Torah..." [and he continues to explain his nature and the **order** of the events:] "A king from the house of David who studies Torah and engages in mitzvos like David... and he will compel **all** of Yisrael to walk in its ways... and he will fight the wars of Hashem... and he will be victorious over all the nations surrounding him."

If you are writing in French or German, you should inquire about printing the maamarim you mention in your letters in the periodical (monthly?) that appears there. However, I do not know in detail what material is suitable for the readers of that periodical. You can contact Dr. Ehrmann there regarding this, and it is understood that you have permission to add that you are approaching him at my suggestion, as I have known him as a friend of Lubavitch for a long time.

#### ***6,600***

The letter is to R. Dovid Hellman, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him—above, 6,880, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**To Dr. Ehrmann:** See above, Vol. 17, letter 5,874—to him.

## 71

B"H, 13 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 12 Elul.

Thank G-d for the improvement in your health; this in itself should increase your hope and confidence that you will continue to go from strength to strength. The more you strengthen your trust in Hashem, the sooner you will see the blessing.

Regarding what you wrote about the difficulty in focusing on your learning, etc., this too will surely improve over time. However, it is understood that a total lack of learning is not good. It will certainly be easier for you to learn for at least short periods of time. It is good to learn in a group, and if you are reviewing your studies and do not have a study partner, you should specifically learn from inside the text. It is also very beneficial to articulate the words aloud rather than relying on thought alone. Furthermore, do not pressure yourself to immediately begin learning for long hours; rather, start little by little. Surely, if you discuss this with the yeshiva administration, they will assist you in this.

I trust you are keeping the monthly Tehillim schedule—as Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—to be recited every day after morning davening, in addition to the known daily Chumash portion.

With blessings for good news regarding all the above, and for a *ksiva v'chasima tova*.

## 702

B"H, 14 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

To the energetic and industrious *avreich*, Yitzchak Tzvi, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

After a long interval, your letter of 7 Elul was received, in which you write of your activities over the past weeks.

May it be the will of Hashem that in all the matters you write about, there be not only consolidation, but also development and expansion—both in quantity and in quality. It is understood and simple that this depends on effort and engagement, for the verdict of the Heavenly Beis Din is well known: that because of this *mesiras nefesh* (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, they decreed in the Heavenly Beis Din that in every matter of Torah, *yiras shamayim*, and good character traits, the hand of those who are *mekushar* to him and follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost. This is especially true as we see tangibly in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, the success of Chabad activities in every place where they are engaged in **as they ought to be**—that is, with *mesiras nefesh*, with pleasant ways, and with peaceful ways, albeit with the appropriate firmness. It is understood that this is addressed to all his colleagues, may they live, in the school about which you write, and within the entirety of those who are engaged and striving in the Reshet schools and Chabad institutions in general. May it be the will of Hashem that following this letter of yours, detailed and joyous news will be received very soon in all the aforementioned directions.

Regarding your place of work, surely you will consult among yourselves, and it has already been said, "In a multitude of counselors there is salvation."

It is certainly very good that you write that you are traveling to the place where you were appointed, for "we do not know what to do," and as per the instruction of our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im*, one must engage with *mesiras nefesh*, and surely Hashem will grant success.

The Alter Rebbe is guaranteed in his promise that one's mind and heart become a thousand times purer, and it is understood from the glosses of the Tzemach Tzedek that it is literally a thousand times, as explained in *Likutei Torah* regarding the three *parshiyos*. This serves as an answer to the conclusion of your letter, that you have no time, etc.

With blessing for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

**M. Schneerson**

***

702

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 20, p. 616, and completed based on a photocopy of the letter.

**Yitzchak Tzvi, may he live:** Eisenbach, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above, Vol. 14, 4816, and in the references noted there.

## 73

B"H, 14 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter of 5 Elul, regarding your question as to whom you should turn for guidance in the study of Chassidus.

It seems to me that from time to time, Chabad Chassidim visit your place of residence. Furthermore, you yourself often come to Jerusalem—may it be rebuilt speedily—or to Tel Aviv, where several of the aforementioned reside. In general, however, with appropriate effort, I am confident that you will come to understand on your own as you study, provided your heart desires it. After all, these matters are explained thoroughly in many places. As is well known from the statement of our Sages regarding every part of our holy Torah: "Words of Torah are poor in one place and rich in another." To this end, a subject index has been printed at the end of Sefer HaTanya, Torah Or, Derech Mitzvosecha, and others, so that one may find an explanation of a subject in every place, where one adds clarity to the other.

Regarding the study of Tanya itself, my advice in such a case is to begin with the section Igeres HaTeshuvah, followed by Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah, and only afterwards the first section. It is also appropriate that you obtain the Kuntres U'Maayan and the Sefer Derech Mitzvosecha by the Admur the Tzemach Tzedek, as their style is more suited to one who is not yet so accustomed to the books of Chassidus. But as mentioned above, the main thing, first and foremost, is that it should be in accordance with the advice of our Sages: that your heart desires it, and that the study be accompanied by effort, in accordance with their promise: "You have labored and you have found."

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above, as well as in general matters, and for a *kesivah vachasimah tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 74

B"H, 17 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 5 Elul, in which you write regarding your order of study: It is somewhat surprising, as I had suggested that your Gemara study consist of those tractates that are practically relevant, and ideally those that pertain to daily life. Through this, the question you raise regarding your study schedule would resolve itself; yet, in your letter, you mention a different tractate.

It is likely that the explanation for this is that you are not the one choosing the area of study, but you also do not mention any effort on your part to change this. Such an effort is supported by pedagogical reasons as well, the content of which is as mentioned above—that the tractates of Seder Moed and the like contain the laws that are necessary, etc. See also the Laws of Talmud Torah by the Alter Rebbe regarding the order of study for one who does not have much time at his disposal, as well as the introduction by the sons of the Alter Rebbe to the Shulchan Aruch regarding the order of study, and several instructions from the Gedolei Yisrael on this matter. It would be worthwhile for you to consult with the elders of Anash on this, after explaining to them that this does not concern a yeshiva, where the hours of study are many, etc.

Regarding what you wrote about studying under duress: It is my hope that by adding to your study the order of questions and answers of the Tanya and similar works, within a short time the matter of "against his will" will be nullified, and you will increase in these matters, going from strength to strength. This is especially true if you can realize these studies through examples from stories found in Likutei Dibburim and sichos, etc.

May it be Hashem's will that you bring good news regarding all of the above, and that you see good fruit from your labor. May you merit and succeed in being one who is "moistened in order to moisten others" in matters of Torah and mitzvos, for the merit of the many assists one in the success of his efforts and toil in this.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 75

B"H, 17 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After a long interval, your letter of 13 Elul was received, in which you write regarding your work in the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchak Lubavitch in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in accordance with the content of your letter.

You should certainly increase your courage and strength in your work at the schools of the Reshet, which are named after the one who rests at that holy gravesite. May this serve as a conduit and vessel for an increase in the blessings of Hashem in whatever you and your household require. You should certainly do this with joy and a glad heart, for through this, the conduits are widened in every respect, and as is known, joy breaks through barriers.

Regarding what you wrote about fatigue, etc. — see the Shulchan Aruch of our Rebbe, the author of the Tanya (who is the authority in the hidden dimensions of Torah) and the Shulchan Aruch (the authority in the revealed dimensions of Torah) in Orach Chaim, section 46, paragraph 6: every day a person’s strength is renewed. The Geonim saw fit to institute a blessing for this great kindness. As is explained in Iggeres HaTeshuvah, chapter 11, there is certainty and decisiveness in every matter for which one recites a blessing. This is all the more so when the blessing is constant, in which case there is no doubt or uncertainty in the world regarding it.

With blessings for good news soon, specifically regarding the expansion of your activities in the aforementioned schools and the Reshet as a whole, for there is nothing that can stand before the will.

And with blessings for a *Kesivah V'Chasimah Tovah*.

## 716

B"H, 17 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter in which you ask my opinion regarding your study schedule for the near future.

My opinion on this is well-known, detailed, and established since long ago: that every bochur and avreich in this country must dedicate **at least** a few years to studying our holy Torah without secular studies, *lehavdil*. Since it is apparent from your letter that until now you have always studied with a mixture of both, my opinion is clear based on the above: that you should arrange your studies in the future in such a way that for at least two or three years, your time will be dedicated to the study of Torah with *hasmadah* and *shekidah*, without other studies. And there is nothing that can stand in the way of willpower.

It is appropriate that you have your tefillin checked, and also that every weekday, after morning davening, you recite the daily portion of Tehillim—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good.

On behalf of the Rebbe, *shlita*,

Secretary

## 77

B"H, 18 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After a very long interval, I received your letter of 13 Elul, along with the *pan* enclosed with it, which will be read at an *eis ratzon* at the holy *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nevg"m, zya".*

In it, you write about what you have experienced over the past few months and your decision—which you have already put into practice—to be at Tomchei Temimim in...

It is understood that this is very good and that you have acted correctly. My opinion on this has been known for a long time, and it is firm: every single one of the children of Israel must prepare himself (before) going out into the affairs of the world by dedicating his time solely and exclusively to the study of our holy Torah—the Torah of life—studying holy subjects without mixing in other matters, not even business affairs. Naturally, this applies to those who will be businesspeople and the like; it is different for those whose desire is to be "dwellers of the tent," as their entire lives are dedicated to the aforementioned, as the Rambam brings at the end of *Hilchos Shemittah V'Yovel*. This preparation is like a foundation for their entire future life; the existence of the entire structure built upon it depends, first and foremost, on the strength of the foundation. Just like a building, there is no benefit in improving the walls or the furnishings if one is not assured that the foundation is as strong as necessary. From this, it is understood that the years one dedicates solely to the study of Torah in the present are not merely for the sake of the World to Come, but are the proper preparation for life in this world, and *kal v'chomer* (it is understood).

May it be Hashem’s will that you fulfill this decision of yours with joy and a glad heart, and that you succeed in your studies and in the performance of *mitzvos* throughout the aforementioned period. This will also add to your success for all the days of your life.

From the above, it is also understood that preparing for only a few months is not sufficient at all. If it is not possible to spend several consecutive years, one should arrange it so that the total amount of time is at least the amount mentioned, and "whoever adds, they add to him."

Regarding what you wrote about funds for *tzedakah*, and your desire to fulfill this through *matan b'seser* (giving in secret), it is appropriate that you give this to the *kupah* of Tomchei Temimim in... It is possible to give this without disclosing to whom the money belongs.

With blessings for good tidings, success in the above, and for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

## 78

B"H, 19 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter—which is undated—in which you write regarding your state of mind, etc.

It seems to me that I have already written this several times: in my opinion, and as I have also seen tangibly, every person without exception is by nature a social being, although naturally not everyone to the same degree. When one tries to act against this nature, it is understood that this leads to complications and the like. For those who, for whatever reason, find it difficult to interact with people, there is no other advice but the analogy of learning to swim in water. This is impossible to do before entering the water, even if one is standing right at the riverbank; one must jump into the water, and then, through the actual experience, one will begin to learn the art of swimming and will eventually master it. All the extensive thinking while standing on the riverbank—how, what, and in what manner one will learn to swim—is of no use, because that learning is impossible except in the water.

With your permission, this is exactly the case regarding you. In your letters, you bring arguments for and against an endeavor that would involve being in the company of people, yet the deliberation on this takes place while you are sitting in your room or within your own four cubits.

It is understood and simple that my intention is not to preach, but to try once more—perhaps my words will be effective this time—that you should jump into some activity where, against your will (at least in the first few days), you will be among people outside of your home. My hope is strong that, within a short time, you will no longer need to do so against your will, and you will see the great importance and the great benefit, even for others, of human company, for it is not for nothing that man was created by nature to be a social being.

The words of our Sages are wondrous, serving as a directive for practical application in daily life: that every holy matter requires specifically a *minyan* of ten—not just a general multitude, but a multitude in its completeness. It is also a well-known Chassidic adage, stated in the name of several of the greats of Chassidic teaching, that it is worse to be alone in *Gan Eden* than to be with other Jews.

As we find ourselves at the end of the month of Elul, the month of mercy—the mercies of the *Ribono shel Olam* which are without limit—may it be His will that you begin a definitive shift for the good, and that you bring good tidings in this regard.

With blessings for good news and for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*.

## 19

B"H, 19 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Blessings and Peace!

...Regarding what you mention in your letter about America — I am very surprised, and I do not understand the conduct of the anash who are located here. It appears that they are concealing what is happening with them here, to such an extent that it can lead others into error and bring about highly undesirable results.

In short: of the anash who have come here, aside from a **counted few**, they are **all** struggling — not only in gashmius, which is already a given, but also in ruchnius. That is to say, they are unfortunately not in the same state as they were before coming here. Although there are many excuses, such as "it is America," they **themselves** fought against all these excuses with mesiras nefesh while they were in the previous country and before coming to America.

And if they would only listen to me, they should have packed their bags tomorrow and left America, whether for France or England or similar places, where, hopefully, things would improve — not only in ruchnius, but also in gashmius. And as stated above, this is not just the case for individuals (one might say, "this person is a batlan," or "not energetic enough"), but for almost **everyone**, with the exception of literally a handful. It is possible that they do not feel comfortable writing the aforementioned **reality**, but unfortunately, that does not change the factual situation as it stands. And it goes without saying that this should serve as a good warning for those who are looking for a country in which to settle.

And Hashem Yisbarach, who oversees each and every one with hashgacha pratis, will surely lead you as well in a way that you will settle, specifically in a good place, and specifically in a good shlichus, both in gashmius and in ruchnius.

With blessings for good tidings and for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*.

## 17

B"H, 21 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the members of the Executive Committee of the Provisional Committee, Bogota, may they be preserved,
May Hashem keep them alive.

Peace and blessing!

Your letter regarding the farbrengen with our shluchim, may they live, and your request to send a guide for your community to establish a Yeshiva Ketana and base your community’s education on the purity of holiness, was received in due time.

Please forgive the delay in my response, as I wished to clarify the possibilities of fulfilling your request. Regrettably, the guide you require has not yet been found, due to various reasons, and I have decided not to delay my letter any further, in case you have another way of finding a suitable guide.

On the other hand, it is my duty to point out the magnitude of the responsibility resting upon the members of the community, and especially upon the communal activists and all whose hearts are sensitive to the vital needs of our brethren in your community. First and foremost—education on the purity of holiness—you must find, in the meantime, people among the members of the community who will address the practical solution to this vital issue. I refer to the organization of Torah studies, both for children and youth and for adults, until a suitable guide is found to take upon himself the responsibility for education.

According to our Sages, of blessed memory, Hashem does not deal tyrannically with His creatures; since He demands proper education on the purity of holiness, it is certain that there are possibilities to fulfill what is required through appropriate effort and exertion.

It is my hope that during these auspicious days, when we recite Psalm 27 of Tehillim twice daily—"Seek My face; Your face, Hashem, I will seek"—one who seeks closeness to Hashem with inwardness is guaranteed success. May it be His will that you report good news in this regard.

With blessings for a *kesiva v’chasima tova* for a good and sweet year for each and every one, their families, and all the members of their community among the entirety of Klal Yisrael, may they live, in both material and spiritual matters alike.

## 711

B"H, 21 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the members of the "Adas Yisrael" community, Havana, Cuba
May Hashem grant them life

Greetings and blessings,

I have received your letter of 12 Menachem Av, in which you request that I send you a guide—preferably one ordained in *hora'ah*—so that he may possess the necessary authority to serve as a leader, and so on.

My response has been delayed because I wished to investigate the possibilities of finding a suitable candidate. Unfortunately, up until now, no such person has been found who is sufficiently suited to the conditions and requirements of your community, due to various circumstances. I have therefore decided not to delay my response any longer, for perhaps you have other ways to obtain a suitable candidate.

I must add, however, that if it will take more time until you find a suitable candidate, you must ensure that the vital needs of the community do not suffer in the interim. I am referring specifically to *chinuch al taharas hakodesh*.

Our Sages, of blessed memory, assure us that the Ribono shel Olam only demands that which is within our capacity to fulfill. Since *chinuch al taharas hakodesh* is among the most elementary and highly necessary obligations, as is *limud haTorah* in general—both for adults and the elderly—there rests a holy duty upon the community to find, within the community itself, several individuals who will together fulfill these aforementioned functions until such time as a suitable candidate is found to take on the entire responsibility. And, as stated, since this is a vital obligation, you will certainly find ways to fulfill it if you make the appropriate efforts to do so.

In these current days of the month of Elul, the month of mercy, and as we approach Rosh Hashanah, it is to be hoped that your community in general, and each individual in particular, will be permeated with a feeling of *hisorerus* and spiritual revival to strengthen the foundations of *chinuch hakasher*, the dissemination of Torah, and the performance of *mitzvos* in your community.

I hope that you will soon be able to share the good news that you have begun this work and that it is proceeding with *hatzlacha*.

I wish you all, collectively and individually, a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

With respect and blessing,

## 612

B"H, 22 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter requesting a blessing and a *pidyon nefesh*.

At an opportune time, I will mention you and all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may his merit protect us, in accordance with the content of your letter.

Regarding what you wrote about changing the knot of the *tefillin* to the Chabad knot—it is understood that this is the correct thing to do.

Regarding what you wrote about changing to the Chabad *nusach* (prayer rite), this is very correct. As explained in other places, it is permitted to change from *Nusach Ashkenaz* to *Nusach Sefard*, and from *Nusach Sefard* in general to the *Nusach* of the Arizal—which is the *nusach* that Chabad Chassidim pray with—but not the other way around. Therefore, if you decide to change your *nusach* **permanently**, it is the correct thing to do, as mentioned. This is especially true given that, according to Chassidic tradition, our Alter Rebbe selected his *nusach* from sixty different *siddurim*.

Since you are a *bochur yeshiva* and you are the one making this request, it is understood and simple that you must increase your consistency and diligence in the study of Torah and the performance of *mitzvos* with *hiddur*. Beyond the inherent greatness of this matter, through this, the blessings of Hashem will be increased in the areas you require, and your heart’s desires will be fulfilled for the good.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

#### ***612***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 18, p. 456.

**As explained:** See also above, 604, and the references in the notes there.

## 1713

B"H, 25 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 12 Menachem Av, along with the enclosure, in which you write regarding the good resolutions to increase in matters of Torah, avodah, and gemilus chasadim, etc.

Certainly, every increase in matters of Torah and mitzvos is worthwhile to the greatest possible extent. Furthermore, we are commanded regarding this, as per the instruction of Chazal: "We elevate in matters of holiness," and "Whoever adds, is added to."

However, at the same time, it is not always necessary to define every single increase with a new name and a new organization. The aforementioned command is sufficient to inspire those whose hearts are awake to such an increase, and there is no specific need for a new name, etc. Since there is no need for this, at least in the initial period, it should not be done, as it could lead to division among the chaverim, and consequently, to a diminution of the concept of ahavas re'im—matters which, on the contrary, one must strive to increase significantly.

Therefore, although you should be blessed for fulfilling the aforementioned resolutions until now—and surely you will continue to fulfill them in the future—nevertheless, for the time being, as mentioned, there is no need for a special organization or a special name. There is still time for that; once a certain amount of time has passed and the number of participants has increased, the matter can be discussed again.

With blessings to go from strength to strength in all three of the aforementioned pillars, which constitute the entirety of Torah and mitzvos, and to be inscribed and sealed for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## Z'id

By the Grace of Hashem, 25 Elul, 5719, the Year of Seven
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the sons and daughters of Israel, wherever they may be,
May Hashem keep them and grant them life,

Greetings and blessings!

These current days, the end of the old year and standing at the threshold of the new year—which is approaching for us and for all of Israel for good and for blessing—are a time for *cheshbon hanefesh* regarding the year that has passed, and, in accordance with the summary of that accounting, for making the appropriate resolutions for the new year.

This accounting can be accurate only if the assessment of one’s own strengths and capabilities is also accurate. The greater the capabilities one possessed but failed to utilize, the greater the regret, the *aveirah* of not utilizing them, and the stronger must be the resolve—from now on and into the future—to utilize these capabilities to the fullest extent.

The time before Rosh Hashanah, and Rosh Hashanah itself, is not merely an opportunity that calls for *cheshbon hanefesh* in general; it also reminds and demands a deeply internal assessment of the immense powers that one possesses—as a human being, the *bachir hanivra'im* (the choicest of created beings), and as a Jew to whom the *Borei Yisbarach* has given the divine *Toras-Chaim*—for Rosh Hashanah is the day when man was created.

***

When man was created, the Creator immediately indicated to him what his powers were and what his task in the world is:

"Fill the earth and conquer it, and rule over the fish of the sea, the birds of the heavens, and every living thing that creeps upon the earth" (*Bereishis 1:28*).

Man was given the power to conquer the entire world and to rule over it—on land, in the sea, and in the air—and he was instructed that he must do so; this is his task.

What is the path to this mastery of the world, and what is its purpose and content? Our Sages tell us and teach us:

When the *Ribono shel Olam* created Adam HaRishon, his soul—the *tzelem Elokim*—permeated and illuminated him. Through this, he ruled over the entire *briah* (creation). All creatures gathered to crown him as their creator. Adam HaRishon pointed out their error to them and said: "Come, let us bow down before Hashem, our Maker" (*Tehillim 95:6*).

***

The mastery of the world that was given to man as his task and mission is to elevate his surroundings—the entire world, including the animals and beasts—for the benefit of true humanity, a humanity that is permeated and illuminated with the *tzelem Elokim*, with the *neshamah*, a *chelek Eloka mima'al mamash*—for this brings the entire *briah* to the recognition that "Hashem is our Maker."

Self-evidently, before a person occupies himself with mastering the world, he must first master himself, mastering the earthly and animalistic aspects of his own nature. This is achieved by carrying out the instructions of the Torah, *Toras Chaim*—the guide for daily life—so that the material and the *chomriyus* (corporeality) become permeated and illuminated by the light of "Hashem Elokeinu Hashem Echad."

The *Ribono shel Olam* created one man and gave that one, singular human being the aforementioned duty and task. Here lies the deep but clear indication that one person—every person—has the power to "master the world."

If a person does not fulfill his task, if he does not utilize his invaluable divine powers, it is not only a tremendous personal loss and failure, but it affects the destiny of the entire world.

***

In these days of *cheshbon hanefesh*, when one considers that each one of us—by following the instructions of the **Borei HaOlam** in His Torah—has the possibility to conquer worlds, everyone must ask themselves how much they have done in this direction during the past year—which is the divine mission and the purpose of his life—and what he has left undone, and then make the appropriate resolutions for the new year.

And Hashem, who looks into the heart, seeing the firmness of the good resolutions in this regard, will grant His blessing that one should carry them out to the fullest extent—with joy and a good heart, with expansion in both material and spiritual matters.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year.

**Menachem Schneerson**

**The *aveirah* of not utilizing**: See also *Kesuvos 67a*.
**Our Sages tell us**: See *Tikkunei Zohar*, Tikkun 56; *Pirkei D'Rabbi Eliezer*, ch. 11.
***Mima'al mamash***: See *Tanya*, ch. 2; the *Hagahos* of the *Tzemach Tzedek* there (in *Sefer Kitzurim v'He'aros*, p. 72).
**Hashem Echad**: See *Sefer Mitzvos Katan* (SMaK), cited in *Beis Yosef* (*Tur Orach Chaim*, sec. 61).
**Has the power**: See *Sanhedrin*, ch. 4, *Mishnah* 5; *Kiddushin 40b*.

#### ***Z'id***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 9, p. 425.

## 1517

B"H, 27 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you write about what you have experienced and your current daily routine, concluding with a list of several problems:

1) Sometimes you feel apathy regarding spiritual matters, to the point of struggling with the *yetzer* [hara], etc.

The instruction of our Sages is well-known: a person’s heart should not fall because of such phenomena. These matters are explained in many places; see also the holy *Tanya*, in the places indicated in the index at the end, among them Chapter 27.

2) How to repel unwanted thoughts.

In general, the advice for this is through *hesech hada'as* [diverting one’s attention]—and not by fighting the thought, as that requires focusing on it, which is the opposite of *hesech hada'as*. Rather, since the mind is always wandering, *hesech hada'as* is achieved by occupying the mind with a completely different subject, and how much better if it is a subject of Torah and *mitzvos*. And if a little light repels much darkness, then how much more so does a great deal of light.

3) A beginning and order for refining character traits (*tikkun hamidos*).

It is understood that one cannot set hard-and-fast rules for this, as it depends greatly on the nature of each individual’s soul, and no two people are alike. Therefore, you should consult with the elders of *anash* in your vicinity who know you, or detail your matters to them, and they will guide you. A general point regarding the aforementioned *tikkun* is to proceed step-by-step, from level to level, and not by skipping and jumping, for such things are only temporary measures for specific times. Of course, this applies only to boundaries and abstentions in the aforementioned area, for what is forbidden is forbidden to do, and one should not wait for days until he reaches a level where he understands the prohibition, etc. In that regard, the order is not just skipping and jumping, but a complete uprooting from the first place to a new place—and the distance between the places is understood to be from one extreme to the other. According to what is explained in *Tanya*, one who transgresses even a minor detail of *divrei sofrim* is forbidden and is bound by the *chitzonim*, *rachmana litzlan*, while one who observes even a minor detail of *divrei sofrim* becomes bound and united through this with the *Ein Sof*, *baruch Hu*; see there.

4) Regarding what you wrote about whether to change your place of residence, and where.

The decision regarding this depends on many details. Likewise, regarding the home—the opinion of your wife, *tichyeh*, is of the utmost importance, for the woman is the one called the *akeres habayis* [the foundation of the home].

May the merit of your involvement in spreading Yiddishkeit in your surroundings—which you write about, and in which you will surely increase more and more, as it is a necessity and a demand of the hour—stand by you to add to the blessings of Hashem, may He be blessed, in what you and your household, *shiy*, require.

With the blessing of *kesivah vachasimah tovah* and for good tidings in the aforementioned.

#### ***1517***

**The opinion of your wife is of the utmost importance:** See also above, Vol. 17, Letter 6,414; Vol. 17, 6,147.

## 716

B"H, 27 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the participants of the Melaveh Malkah farbrengen,
The *Seudah* of Dovid Malka Meshicha,
And to those who signed the general *Pan*,
May Hashem grant them life,

Shalom Rav and Bracha!

At an auspicious time, the aforementioned will be read at the holy *tziyun* of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nbg"m, zya*.

While our Sages of blessed memory have always extolled the matter of prayer at the graves of *tzaddikim*, this is all the more true in our current era—an era of the revelation and dissemination of *pnimiyus haTorah*, which in turn brings about the revelation of the *pnimiyus* of the world.

For our Torah, the Torah of life, determines the reality of the world, as is well known from the ruling of our Sages regarding the verse "To the Kel who completes for me" (*Yerushalmi Kesubos*, Chapter 1, Halacha 2).

It has already been explained elsewhere that there is no contradiction between this and the well-known teaching that from generation to generation, hearts diminish and darkness grows, to the point that "there is no day..." (*Sotah* 49a). On the contrary, it is precisely this that provides the strength: when the darkness intensifies, the assistance from Above is increased, for we have been promised that the Holy One, blessed be He, helps him and he can overcome it.

The importance and the magnitude of this matter—and its results—are even greater in these days, when every day we double (see *Nedarim* 36a and the *Shulchan Aruch* there) the plea, "Seek My face; Your face, Hashem, I will seek"—a commitment to seek and pursue the *pnimiyus* specifically. This is especially true during the Ten Days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, when there is a drawing near of the Luminary to the spark, as it is written, "Seek Hashem when He is found... He is near."

This is particularly so according to the well-known interpretation of our Alter Rebbe, the author of the *Tanya* (the authority in the *nistar* of Torah) and the *Shulchan Aruch* (the authority in the *nigleh* of Torah), regarding the conclusion of the aforementioned teaching of our Sages: "here" refers to the individual, which is the level of *teshuvah* of the *yechidah* (within the soul) to the *Yechido shel Olam* (the Unique One of the world).

May it be the will of Hashem that just as you have detailed your heart's desires for good in the letter and the *Pan*, so too may you share news of the fulfillment of those requests, with joy and goodness of heart.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’Chasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year, in body and soul,

**M. Schneerson**

## 617

B"H, 28 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Monday, in which you write regarding questions that are asked of you from time to time concerning the Jewish religion and its matters, and you request answers for them.

In general, this is not the way to approach such questions via letters from overseas, especially since there is no need for it. You should connect with the mashpi'im of anash and the elders of anash—whom you surely know, since as you wrote, you studied in the Chabad Yeshiva in Eretz Yisrael—and they will instruct you. It is surely superfluous to add that this is not the way of a person in his world: to rummage after difficulties and search for them. For man was born to toil, to do good in his world. If one begins to search for difficulties as mentioned, he will spend all his time, days, and years on this, and even then, he will not manage to resolve all the difficulties in the world, since man is limited—his intellect is limited, his days and years are limited, etc.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that you walk in integrity on the path of Torah, which is the true path of life, for the Torah and its mitzvos are our life and the length of our days. This conduct itself will nullify doubts and difficulties, similar to a person who is hungry and thirsty, whose mind is confused and who does not grasp matters with clarity, unlike when he is not hungry or thirsty. It is the same regarding the hunger and thirst of the neshamah concerning Torah and mitzvos.

It is appropriate that you keep the takanah of reciting the monthly Tehillim shiur—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—each day after morning davening, and on every weekday before morning davening, you should set aside a few coins for tzedakah. And most importantly, do everything within your power to spread Yiddishkeit among your friends and your surroundings in general, in a manner of "increasing and continuing."

With blessings for a good and sweet year and for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 718

B"H, 28 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your two letters and in continuation of the blessing for the year which you have surely already received, in which you write regarding the education of your son, shlita. You mention that a doctor is suggesting a schedule that would reduce his holy studies so that he can increase his participation in gymnastics and other studies related to physical development.

Since our Torah is called "Torah of life"—even in the literal sense of a life in this world, and a full life, meaning healthy, complete, and good—it is understood that it is not advisable to change the schedule in a way that would reduce the time dedicated to Torah study, especially since this is also connected to matters of yiras shamayim. Rather, you should speak with the doctor to arrange that the activities beneficial for his development, according to the natural way, be scheduled during times that are already free from the aforementioned studies.

It is understood and simple that you must explain the matter to your wife in an appropriate manner. The main thing is that her heart should be secure in Hashem, who oversees each and every person with hashgacha pratis, and that by not changing or reducing the time your son, sheyichyeh, spends within the framework of the cheder, he will certainly increase in his health, even in the literal sense.

Furthermore, you should explain to the doctor that the school where they wish to enroll your son, sheyichyeh, is certainly mixed with non-Jewish children (and perhaps they are the majority), and there is cause for concern regarding complications in the child’s soul if he spends the majority of his time and education there. This is especially true despite what was said about there being no place for antisemitism there; unfortunately, it is found everywhere, and especially among children who do not hide the matter as much, and a word to the wise is sufficient. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding all of the above, as well as regarding the health of your wife, tichyeh l'arichus yamim tovim.

All of the above applies provided that you can secure the full agreement of your wife, tichyeh, through pleasant ways.

In any case, it is worthwhile to explain all of the above to the doctor and to express, in a diplomatic manner at the very least, a slight wonder that she would suggest such things to your wife before speaking with you first to clarify the **entire** matter. One cannot always know what is between a husband and wife, and certainly, a lack of this knowledge can lead to complications between one person and another, and not only physical ones. It can also cause complications in the soul of your wife herself, since by her nature and upbringing she is religious, and it is possible that in a short time she will be regretful and in doubt as to whether she did the right thing by changing his studies and education, etc.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good.

## 617

B"H, 28 Elul, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the auspicious day, 18 Elul—the day upon which the two great luminaries were suspended, the birthday of our teacher the Baal Shem Tov and the birthday of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe.

In it, you write at the request of parents regarding their son, may he live, who stopped speaking. After several treatments that were ineffective, they underwent a treatment involving the bones of the neck, and an improvement is visible. You ask whether to continue with this, etc.

Now, as it appears from the few words you write regarding the treatment, this is a method that several doctors have begun to use recently, which they call chiropractic. That is to say, there is no *segulah* aspect to it, etc., even according to their own opinion, and likewise according to the opinion of those who disagree with them. The difference of opinion is only regarding the extent of the benefit of the matter, and nothing more. In this, seemingly, there is an answer to the question of whether it is permitted to go to the one who administers this treatment.

Nevertheless, it is not my place to issue halachic rulings, especially in the absence of several details. Therefore, you should turn to a Rav, a *moreh hora'ah* in your vicinity, after he is made aware of the details regarding the treatment.

Regarding the matter itself, as to whether it is permitted and whether it is worthwhile to go again: since you see an improvement, it is worthwhile to continue with it.

When you provide the name of the son along with the name of his mother, I will mention him at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever he requires. May it be His will that just as you brought the request, so too may you bring good news regarding this.

Surely you will find appropriate words to explain to the boy's parents that they must increase their involvement in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*. Even if the situation is already good in this regard, there is always room to add more, since they are connected to *Hakadosh Baruch Hu*, who is infinite, and through this, the blessings of *Hashem* are increased in whatever a person and his household require.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
Surely you have already received the letters of blessing for *Rosh Hashanah*.

## 227

B.H., Days of Selichos, 5719
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Eliezer Aryeh Halevi,
known as Dr. Finkelstein

Shalom u’Bracha,

As we approach the New Year, which is coming upon us and upon all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all that is yours: a blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a year that is good and sweet, both materially and spiritually.

On this occasion, I wish to address the content of your recent letter, in which you write regarding the greatness of Torah study as the sole positive influence capable of changing a person’s nature, and so forth.

Indeed, there is no dispute that *talmud Torah k’neged kulam* (Torah study outweighs them all), and that "great is study, for it leads to action"—a matter that requires no elaboration or substantiation.

However, the difficulty is that in our times, in all places—and particularly in the United States—people are generally more practical than they are contemplative. In this country, in particular, the number of those engaged in contemplative matters is exceedingly small by comparison.

It is understood that I am not here to evaluate the merits or drawbacks of this, as this is not the place for it; I have only come to note the reality.

Therefore, from an educational perspective, one cannot rely solely on the study of Torah, assuming that the student will derive all the necessary conclusions from his study and that the "light" within the Torah will return him to the right path. All of this requires great contemplation and a great deal of time. As is the nature of people, the enthusiasm and pursuit of any matter that requires significant time gradually evaporate, until the entire opportunity is—in the passage of time—lost.

From this point, here in our country, more than anywhere else, reality dictates that simultaneously with the study of Torah, there must be an awakening and an appropriate influence to align one’s actual conduct with one’s study. I have already spoken at length on this on another occasion: those who have influence in the field of education are obligated to recognize the character of the youth and the young people being educated, and to place more trust in their inner strengths. They should not fear that if they provide them with full influence, they will be unable or unwilling to receive it; for in truth, their capacity for absorption is greater than one might estimate. This is also the point of my General Letter for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah, a copy of which is enclosed.

If you would say that this is the foundation of our faith—the faith of our ancestors—our Torah states that "the righteous are like their Creator," to the extent that it is said elsewhere: "Just as the Holy One, Blessed be He, creates worlds, so too did Yaakov create worlds." How much more so, then, are they able to create a single Jewish soul, which is an entire world—meaning, to create a new creature, to change him from one extreme to the other. And according to the explanation of Chassidus, that a *mitzvah* comes from the word *tzavta* (connection/bond), and that through the performance of a *mitzvah* one fulfills the verse, "And you who cleave to Hashem your G-d," it is understood even more clearly how they are "like their Creator." When one observes a student and sees how he appears to be extremely limited, yet one influences him to align his life with his study of the Torah of Hashem through a lifestyle of Torah and *mitzvos* in practice, one reveals within him immeasurable soul-powers—the powers of the *neshamah elokis* that is bound and attached to the living G-d.

To put it wittily, it would be appropriate to attempt this method, even if only on the basis of a *safek sfeika* (a double doubt), as a matter of *pikuach nefesh* (saving a life). But in my opinion, the matter is not in the category of a doubt at all; rather, it is a complete certainty.

With respect and blessing for good tidings.

## 721

B"H, Yemei HaSelichos 5719
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, Torah-learned, and pious man, who engages in communal needs, etc., our teacher Rabbi Shlomo, may he live:

...The shipment was delayed due to circumstances, and in the meantime, we have met.

Regarding what you noted in Bechoros 41a—based on what is written in Rabeinu Gershom Meor HaGolah there, the proof is from the fact that *cheres* (earthenware) implies *charutz* (a shard), as the letters *samech* and *tzadi* are interchangeable.

Based on this: 1) We can understand the statement in the Gemara: "Since the Master said, 'A *gerev* is a *cheres*'" (for seemingly, what is the superiority of *cheres* over *gerev*?). 2) It must be that *nega* (a plague/spot) is, etc.—this comes to prove that there is room here for an interchange, since in all *nega'im* it must be "deeper than the skin" (even though for the sign of impurity, it is **sufficient** if its appearance is deep, not necessarily its physical texture). See also Torah Temimah on Tazria 13:3 regarding this.

It is my hope that despite the hesitation during our conversation, you will speak in the appropriate settings regarding the matter of "**all** the glory of the king's daughter is within," and that she does not depart from this matter. After all, even regarding material profit—and only for an individual—there is the promise, "And I will bless those who bless you." Consider Rav, etc., regarding why Rav Huna did not speak up about the *shema* (even though it was not his responsibility at all), as per Rashi in Megillah 27b. How much more so, and how much more so, etc.

***

**Rabbi Shlomo, may he live:** Goren, Tel Aviv. Another letter to him appears in Vol. 17, 6,502.

## 722

[Days of Selichos, 5719]

To the esteemed Rabbi, the pious and G-d-fearing, distinguished in Torah and service, etc., our teacher Rabbi Meir, may he live:

...Your book, *Chumash Torah Meirah*, Vol. 1, has been received. Many thanks, many thanks.

...To show appreciation for the work, I have transcribed a few notes here (though very briefly, due to the pressure of time).

Our Sages, of blessed memory, said: "Atah Vahav in Sufah..." [referring to the study of Torah].

**Introduction, at the beginning**: "He would explain..." — this refers to the interpretation of the Baal Shem Tov brought in the sefer *Meor Einayim* at the end.

And furthermore:

**Tosafos, at their beginning** (9a-b): Although the surface reading of the *Midrash HaNe’elam* (Zohar Chadash, beginning of Parshas Bereishis) implies that the opinion that the world was created in the evening and the opinion that it was created in the morning are in dispute,

The consensus of the Mishnah and our Sages, of blessed memory, in many places proves that one master says one thing and the other says another, and they are not in dispute; rather, they are speaking of different levels in the revelation of Creation. See *Avodas HaKodesh*, Part 4, Chapter 4, and elsewhere.

For the sake of completeness (in your Tosafos), there would be room to also bring the words of our Sages, of blessed memory, regarding the "two" in the creation of the world (Yerushalmi, Chagigah 2:2), and the "thought" of the creation of the world (Zohar Chadash, Vol. 3, 20a).

**Sources for Rashi, at their beginning** (9a): "15" (Bereishis Rabbah, Chapter 12) — it is not there. In *Torah Sheleimah*, section 9, it notes: "To teach us, brought in *Toras Torah*." It is not at hand.

**Tosafos, at their end** (342b): "In Charan, the Nun is inverted..." — from the interpretations brought, only [the letters] Alef, Dalet, and Vav are mentioned regarding the inverted Nun. The rest [refer] to the word *Charan* (see Bereishis Rabbah, Chapter 68:13: *Charanah*, etc., "His wrath" (*Charon Apo*), and elsewhere).

***

**Rabbi Meir:** Meiri, London.

## 7,223

[Days of Selichos, 5719]

Regarding your question about the source for what is written in Torah Or (32b) and Likutei Torah, Parshas Beha'aloscha (29c), that Aharon is one of the Seven Shepherds, whereas in Sukkah (52b) he is not mentioned:

The same is found in other places in Torah Or, as noted in the index.

The source for this can be explained in two ways: 
1) In Zohar Chadash (at the end, Tikkunim 104a): "The faithful shepherd (Moshe) stood on his feet and said: 'Holy shepherds: Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov, and Adam HaRishon, and Aharon, and David, and Shlomo.'" Based on this, the following is also resolved: 
    1) In Torah Or (82a), where Avraham is the first shepherd, while in Sukkah it is Adam HaRishon. 
    2) In Torah Or (33c), it is implied that Yitzchak is also included among the shepherds.

2) In Zohar Chadash II (26b): "He (singular) is Aharon and Moshe"—to include one within the other. Note the reference in Taanis (8a). It may be that this is why, in the aforementioned sources, only one of them is listed—the one primary to that specific subject. And this is understandable.

Now, there is a further difficulty to be raised here, which is the main point. In Torah Or and Likutei Torah, the concept of the Seven Shepherds is explained as those who bestow Elokus upon Knesses Yisrael, etc.—and it is not understood at all what the role of Sheis and Metushelach is in this.

Now, the Tzemach Tzedek, on the verse in Michah (5:4), notes that there are two interpretations for the word "v'hakimonu" (and we will raise up): 
a) Against Ashur. 
b) Concerning Bnei Yisrael (as per the Targum)—and in that way, Ashur will automatically be defeated.

Based on this, it seems to me that we can resolve the aforementioned issue: that the Gemara in Sukkah follows the first interpretation, while the Zohar Chadash follows the second. And were I not afraid, I would suggest checking the early printings of the Zohar Chadash to see if, perhaps, instead of Adam HaRishon, it should read Yosef—along the lines of the Ushpizin of Sukkos.

However, standing against this explanation is the fact that in Torah Or (33d) and in the Tzemach Tzedek’s Sefer HaMitzvos, Mitzvas Chanukah, he quotes from the aforementioned Sukkah and explains the concept of the shepherds as mentioned above. But see there, where he brings from the Seven Shepherds only Avraham, Yaakov, and Moshe. This requires further study. And this is not the place to elaborate.

## 724

[Days of Selichos, 5719]

...Regarding your note on the apparent contradiction in the Shulchan Aruch of the Alter Rebbe—between what is written in section 192, paragraph 2, and what is written in section 198, at the end of paragraph 1, regarding the *chatzas* (half-recitation) of the *zimun*: The statement in section 192, "And not... of the one who is reciting the blessing," refers to what was mentioned immediately prior, namely, the one who is listening from among those seated at the meal. Based on this, we can understand:

a) The lengthiness of the language there. According to your explanation, it should have said, "He did not say... except for those seated at the meal." But based on the above, it is understood that it is teaching us that there are two reasons why those seated at the meal should not answer *Amen*: 1) Because they only said [the blessing] when one enters from the street (since those seated at the meal must have *kavanah*. The practical difference being that one of those seated at the meal who finishes before his companions does not answer *Amen* after them). 2) Because the essence of the *zimun* blessing is [the obligation of] those seated at the meal. Therefore, the one who answers (meaning, the one entering from the street), for whom there is a choice, must answer after those seated at the meal and not after the one who is reciting the blessing.

b) The source cited in section 192 is the *Levush*—who is the author of the ruling in section 198. This requires further study in the *Levush*, which is not currently before me.

Enclosed is what I wrote to someone regarding the Alter Rebbe’s text for *v’anachnu*, as I have seen what you wrote on this matter.

***

724

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 381.

The letter is addressed to Rabbi Yaakov Verdiger, author and publisher of the *Siddur Tzelusa D’Avraham* and other works. Another letter to him appears above in Vol. 17, 645.

**The contradiction:** Regarding the answering of *Amen* when one happens upon a place of a meal and hears the one leading the *zimun* repeating, "Blessed is He of Whose we have eaten and by Whose goodness we live."
